**(b) [7 Marks)** Using the **Hough Transform** representation $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$, derive the steps to detect lane lines in a 1280×720 image. Compute the **accumulator array dimensions** if $\rho$ is discretized in 2-pixel steps and $\theta$ in $2°$ steps.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pipeline Description (7 Marks):**
The LDW pipeline consists of:
1. **Image Acquisition** (1 mark): Dashboard-mounted monocular camera captures road ahead at 30 fps.
2. **Preprocessing** (2 marks): Gaussian smoothing removes noise; lens distortion correction uses intrinsic matrix $K$; grayscale conversion reduces data by 3×.
3. **Region of Interest (ROI) Masking** (1 mark): A trapezoidal mask removes sky and hood pixels — keeps only the lower-half road area.
4. **Edge Detection** (2 marks): Canny edge detector produces a binary edge map.
5. **Hough Transform** (1 mark): Converts edges to line parameters $(\rho, \theta)$.

**(b) Hough Transform Derivation (7 Marks):**
**Step 1 (1 mark):** A line in image space: $y = mx + c$. To avoid infinite slope, use normal form: $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$, where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from origin and $\theta$ is the angle of the normal.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ maps to a sinusoidal curve in $(\rho, \theta)$ space. A line is identified as the intersection of many such curves.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Discretize $\theta \in [0°, 180°)$ with step $2°$:

$$
N_\theta = \frac{180}{2} = 90 \text{ bins}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Diagonal of image:

$$
d = \sqrt{1280^2 + 720^2} = \sqrt{1638400 + 518400} = \sqrt{2156800} \approx 1468.6 \text{ px}
$$

With $\Delta\rho = 2$ px, total $\rho$ range is $\pm 1469$, so:

$$
N_\rho = \frac{2 \times 1469}{2} = 1469 \text{ bins}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Total accumulator size:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 90 \times 1469 = 132{,}210 \text{ cells}
$$

Memory footprint at 4 bytes/cell: $132{,}210 \times 4 \approx 528 \text{ KB}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Pedestrian Detection with HOG + CNN

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)** feature descriptor for pedestrian detection. Describe the step-by-step computation and its advantages over raw pixel features.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **classical machine learning approaches (HOG + SVM)** with **deep learning approaches (YOLO, Faster R-CNN)** for pedestrian detection in terms of accuracy, latency, and deployment on automotive ECUs.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HOG Descriptor (7 Marks):**
1. **Global Normalization** (1 mark): Apply gamma correction to reduce illumination effects.
2. **Gradient Computation** (1 mark): Compute $G_x$, $G_y$ via Sobel; then magnitude $\nabla I$ and orientation $\theta$.
3. **Cell Histograms** (2 marks): Divide image into $8 \times 8$ pixel cells. Within each cell, bin the gradient orientations into 9 bins (0°–180°, 20° each), weighted by magnitude. Concatenate to form a 9-D feature vector per cell.
4. **Block Normalization** (2 marks): Group cells into $2 \times 2$ blocks (16×16 pixels). Normalize the 36-D concatenated vector using L2-norm: $v_{\text{norm}} = v / \sqrt{\vert\vert v \vert\vert_2^2 + \epsilon^2}$.
5. **SVM Classification** (1 mark): The full descriptor (e.g., 3780-D for 64×128 detection window) is fed to a linear SVM trained on positive (pedestrian) and negative (background) samples. **Advantages:** invariant to illumination and small geometric deformations; computationally tractable on automotive hardware; interpretable.

**(b) Classical vs. Deep Learning (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | HOG + SVM | YOLO / Faster R-CNN |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy (mAP)** | ~70–80% | ~88–95% (COCO) |
| **Latency (per frame)** | ~15–25 ms | ~30–60 ms (without NPU) |
| **ECU Footprint** | Light (CPU only) | Heavy (requires NPU/GPU) |
| **Training Data Needed** | ~1000–5000 images | >100,000 images |
| **Interpretability** | High (hand-crafted features) | Low (black-box CNN) |
| **Robustness to Occlusion** | Moderate | High (learned features) |
| **Scalability to New Classes** | Difficult (retrain SVM) | Easy (transfer learning) |
| **Use Case** | Entry-level ADAS (Tier 1) | Premium ADAS (Tesla, Waymo) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** HOG + SVM remains relevant for **cost-sensitive Tier-1 ECUs** with limited compute, while CNN-based detectors dominate **Level 2+ and Level 3+** autonomous platforms where the **NPU hardware** is available.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — KTU Board Exam Patterns:**
> 1. **Confusing Homogeneous vs. Non-Homogeneous Coordinates** in the pinhole projection formula (2–3 marks lost per occurrence). Always state: *"Divide the homogeneous vector by its third component to recover pixel coordinates."*
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/(2\pi\sigma^2)$ Normalization** in the Gaussian filter — KTU examiners explicitly check normalization.
> 3. **Omitting the 6th stage** of Canny (hysteresis edge tracking) in short answers — worth **1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong Hough accumulator size**: Students often use $180 / \Delta\theta$ incorrectly. Verify the **bin count formula** carefully.
> 5. **No Block Diagram in LDW/FCW questions** — full **2–3 marks** deducted for missing or unclear diagrams.
> 6. **Failing to mention real-time latency constraints** (≤ 100 ms) in long answers — examiners reward **practical engineering awareness** with 1–2 bonus marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Image Processing for Vehicle Safety (Module 2):**

- ✅ The **image processing pipeline** has 5 stages: Acquisition → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Detection → Decision Handoff.
- ✅ **Pinhole projection** is the foundational camera model; the **intrinsic matrix $K$** contains focal lengths $(f_x, f_y)$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$.
- ✅ **Gaussian filtering** removes sensor noise; the kernel is normalized with $1/(2\pi\sigma^2)$.
- ✅ **Sobel** computes first-order gradients; **Canny** is the gold-standard edge detector with **6 distinct stages**.
- ✅ **Harris corner detector** uses the structure tensor $M$ and corner response $R = \det(M) - k\cdot\text{tr}(M)^2$ with $k \approx 0.04$.
- ✅ **HOG** divides images into $8 \times 8$ cells with 9 orientation bins; used for pedestrian detection with linear SVM.
- ✅ **IoU** is the standard detection accuracy metric: $\text{IoU} = \vert A \cap B \vert / \vert A \cup B \vert$, with TP threshold at 0.5.
- ✅ **NMS (Non-Maximum Suppression)** eliminates duplicate detections by suppressing lower-confidence boxes with IoU > 0.5.
- ✅ **Optical flow (Lucas-Kanade)** estimates per-pixel motion using spatial gradient matrices.
- ✅ **Disparity-to-depth** formula: $Z = fB/d$ is fundamental for stereo vision obstacle ranging.
- ✅ **Time-To-Collision**: $\text{TTC} = \Delta d / \Delta v$ is the core trigger metric for FCW/AEB.
- ✅ **Real-time budget** at 30 fps = 33.3 ms/frame; detection typically consumes **~36%** of this budget.
- ✅ **YOLO / Faster R-CNN** dominate modern ADAS; **Mobileye EyeQ5**, **NVIDIA Orin**, **Qualcomm Ride** are the dominant SoC platforms.
- ✅ **Euro NCAP 2025+** mandates AEB pedestrian response at TTC ≤ 1.4 s and DMS for fatigue detection.
- ✅ **Adam Optimizer**, **Batch Normalization**, and **Anchor Boxes** are frequent auxiliary topics in KTU CNN-based detection questions.
- ✅ For **Hough Transform** problems, always compute the **diagonal of the image** first to determine $\rho_{\max}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Night Vision System (NVS)** is an **Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS)** perception module that enhances the driver's situational awareness during low-illumination, nighttime, or adverse-weather driving conditions by extending the visible spectrum into the **Near-Infrared (NIR)** or **Far-Infrared (FIR)** bands, and by intelligently processing thermal/reflected signatures to detect, classify, and highlight vulnerable road users (VRUs) such as pedestrians, cyclists, and large animals well beyond the reach of standard headlamps.

According to the KTU PEMUT865 Module-2 syllabus, automotive night vision is classified as a **"Vision Enhancement ADAS"** function that operates in the **electro-optical domain** — combining **physics (radiometry)**, **sensor engineering**, and **machine-vision-based object detection**.

---

## 1.2 Two Architectural Families

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to clearly distinguish between the two technological families. Memorize this distinction — it is a high-yield **2-mark short-answer** target.

| # | Family | Spectrum | Operating Principle | Light Source |
|---|--------|----------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Passive Night Vision (FIR / Thermal Imaging)** | 8 µm – 14 µm (Long-Wave IR / LWIR) | Detects self-emitted thermal radiation from bodies | **No external illumination** — uses emitted heat |
| 2 | **Active Night Vision (NIR)** | 0.7 µm – 1.0 µm (Near-Infrared) | Detects reflected NIR radiation | **Active IR illuminator** (laser/LED) projects IR beam |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Superman & The Flash"

To make this instantly memorable:

- **Far-Infrared (Passive) system** is like giving the driver **"Superman's heat vision."** It does *not* need any light at all. It "sees" the **temperature difference** between a warm human body (~310 K) and the cold road surface (~280 K) at night. The hotter the object, the brighter it appears.
- **Near-Infrared (Active) system** is like equipping the car with **"The Flash's invisible flashlight."** The car itself fires an **invisible IR beam** (using an IR laser diode or LED at ~810 nm or 850 nm) ahead, and a special CMOS/CCD camera captures the reflected beam. The image looks like a black-and-white photo of the road.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Passive = "Feel the heat" (no projector)* ; *Active = "Shine invisible light" (with projector)*.

---

## 1.4 Why Night Vision is Needed — The Safety Statistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **NHTSA (USA)** and **ETSC (Europe)** datasets referenced in the KTU recommended reading:
> - Approximately **50% of all fatal pedestrian collisions** occur at **night**, even though nighttime traffic volume is only ~25% of daytime.
> - The human driver's low-beam headlamps provide **safe visibility for only ~60–90 meters**, while a thermal NVS can extend this to **150–300 meters**.
> - This extra **reaction-time buffer** of ~2–4 seconds is the primary safety benefit.

---

## 1.5 Core Engineering Metrics (Standard Definitions)

The following bolded metrics are **KTU-board-standard definitions** the examiner expects verbatim:

- **NETD (Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference):** The smallest temperature difference a thermal sensor can resolve. A high-quality automotive microbolometer has an NETD of **< 50 mK** (millikelvin).
- **MRTD (Minimum Resolvable Temperature Difference):** A combined spatial-frequency + thermal-sensitivity metric used for range prediction.
- **Field of View (FOV):** Typically **24° × 18°** for far-field pedestrian detection.
- **Spectral Band:** LWIR window of **8 µm – 14 µm** (matches atmospheric transmission and human-body peak emission at ~9.5 µm).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Foundation — Why Thermal Imaging Works at All

All objects with a temperature above **absolute zero (0 K)** emit **electromagnetic radiation** as a consequence of the **thermal agitation** of their molecules. This is governed by three foundational laws of blackbody radiometry.

### 2.1.1 Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation

Describes the **spectral radiance** $B_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)$ emitted per unit wavelength by an ideal blackbody at thermodynamic temperature $T$.

$$
B_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \;=\; \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{\,\frac{h c}{\lambda k T}} \;-\; 1} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{sr}\cdot\text{m}} \right]
$$

- $h$ = **Planck's constant** $= 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\;\text{J·s}$
- $c$ = **Speed of light** $= 2.998 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$
- $k$ = **Boltzmann constant** $= 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of emitted radiation
- $T$ = absolute temperature in kelvin

### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which the blackbody emission **peaks** is inversely proportional to its temperature:

$$
\lambda_{\max} \;=\; \frac{b}{T}, \qquad b \;=\; 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m·K}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Mental Math:** A human body at skin temperature $T \approx 305\,\text{K}$ emits peak radiation at
> $\lambda_{\max} \approx \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305} \approx 9.5\,\mu\text{m}$.
> This is precisely why automotive FIR cameras operate in the **8–14 µm LWIR window**.

### 2.1.3 Stefan–Boltzmann Law

The **total** radiated power per unit surface area across all wavelengths:

$$
P \;=\; \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^{4} \quad [\text{W}]
$$

- $\varepsilon$ = **emissivity** (0 to 1); human skin $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$ (near-perfect blackbody).
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $= 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$
- $A$ = emitting surface area (m²)

---

## 2.2 The Active NIR Approach — Why It Is Simpler

Active NIR systems exploit the fact that the **silicon CMOS/CCD image sensor** is sensitive far beyond the visible spectrum — typically up to ~1000 nm (1 µm).

A high-power **IR LED or laser diode** operating at **810 nm, 850 nm, or 940 nm** illuminates the road ahead. The reflected IR light is captured by a standard monochrome CMOS sensor placed behind an **optical bandpass filter** that blocks visible light.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of **850 nm** is industry-standard (used by BMW, Mercedes, Audi) because it offers the best trade-off between:
> 1. **Quantum efficiency** of the silicon sensor (peaks near 800–900 nm),
> 2. **Atmospheric transmission** (low scatter at night),
> 3. **Reduced red-glow visibility** to other drivers.

---

## 2.3 Passive FIR Sensor — The Microbolometer

Modern automotive thermal cameras use an uncooled **microbolometer array** (e.g., 640 × 480 or 384 × 288 pixels) made of **vanadium oxide (VO$_x$)** or **amorphous silicon** resistive elements.

- Each pixel's resistance **changes with absorbed IR radiation** (bolometric effect).
- A **readout integrated circuit (ROIC)** converts these resistance changes into a thermal image.
- No cryogenic cooling is required → **low cost**, **low power**, **fast warm-up** (< 2 s).

---

## 2.4 Image Processing Pipeline

The raw thermal/NIR frame is fed into a **multi-stage algorithm**:

1. **Pre-processing:** Non-Uniformity Correction (NUC), temporal filtering, contrast enhancement (e.g., adaptive histogram equalization).
2. **Region of Interest (ROI) extraction:** Horizon-line detection, vanishing-point estimation.
3. **Pedestrian/Animal detection:** Usually a **Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** such as YOLO, MobileNet-SSD, or a custom CNN trained on thermal imagery (e.g., FLIR ADAS dataset).
4. **Tracking:** Kalman filter or SORT/DeepSORT for temporal consistency.
5. **Hazard classification:** Estimate Time-To-Collision (TTC) and trigger warning.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Memorize the table below.** It covers every formula a KTU examiner can ask for Module-2 NVS derivations.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda_{\max} = b/T$, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | **Wien's Displacement Law** | Justifies 8–14 µm LWIR choice for human detection |
| 2 | $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$ | **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** | Computes total radiated power from a pedestrian |
| 3 | $B_{\lambda}(\lambda,T) = \dfrac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)^{-1}$ | **Planck's Spectral Radiance** | Foundation of all thermal imaging physics |
| 4 | $\text{NETD} = \dfrac{\text{Noise}_{\text{RMS}}}{\text{Responsivity}}$ | **Noise-Equivalent Temp. Diff.** | Sensor sensitivity metric (target < 50 mK) |
| 5 | $R^{2} \;\propto\; \dfrac{\pi\, D^{2}\, \tau_{a}\, \tau_{o}}{4\, f\, \text{NETD}} \cdot \Delta T$ | **Johnson Range Equation** | Predicts detection range of thermal imager |
| 6 | $E_{\text{illum}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{IR}}}{R^{2}}\, e^{-\alpha R}$ | **Active NIR Illumination** | Predicts received IR flux at distance $R$ |
| 7 | $f_{\text{#}} = \dfrac{f}{D}$ | **F-number of optics** | Controls radiometric throughput |
| 8 | $\text{IFOV} = \dfrac{p}{f}$ | **Instantaneous Field of View** | Spatial resolution per pixel |
| 9 | $\text{TTC} = \dfrac{R}{v_{\text{rel}}}$ | **Time-To-Collision** | Hazard assessment trigger |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{atm}}(\lambda) = e^{-\alpha(\lambda) R}$ | **Atmospheric transmittance** | Beer–Lambert extinction model |

- **Pedestrian safety systems** (BMW Night Vision, Mercedes Night View Assist PLUS).
- **Animal-detection collision avoidance** (Audi, Volvo on European / Canadian roads).
- **Military & off-road autonomous platforms** (thermal cameras are immune to glare and smoke).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate Active.** Every algebraic transition and every numerical substitution is shown explicitly. No shortcuts. Read each numbered row as a verbal explanation of the next mathematical line.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Peak Emission Wavelength of a Human Body

**Problem:** Show that the peak emission wavelength of a pedestrian's skin (~305 K) lies in the LWIR band, justifying the 8–14 µm microbolometer choice.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given:} \quad T_{\text{skin}} = 305 \;\text{K}, \quad b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m·K} \\[4pt]
&\text{Apply Wien's Displacement Law:} \quad \lambda_{\max} = \dfrac{b}{T} \\[6pt]
&\text{Substitute:} \quad \lambda_{\max} = \dfrac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m·K}}{305 \;\text{K}} \\[6pt]
&\text{Carry out the division:} \quad \lambda_{\max} = (2.898 / 305) \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m} \\[6pt]
&\text{Numerical evaluation:} \quad \lambda_{\max} = 0.009502 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m} \\[6pt]
&\text{Convert to micrometres (1 m = 10}^{6}\,\mu\text{m):} \quad \lambda_{\max} = 9.502 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m} \times \dfrac{10^{6}\,\mu\text{m}}{1\,\text{m}} \\[6pt]
&\text{Final Result:} \quad \boxed{\lambda_{\max} \approx 9.50\;\mu\text{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This value of 9.50 µm falls **squarely inside** the 8–14 µm atmospheric transmission window and **inside** the silicon microbolometer sensitivity range. This is the formal physical justification that KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Radiated Power from a Pedestrian's Body

**Problem:** Compute the total radiated IR power from a pedestrian of effective surface area $A = 1.6\;\text{m}^2$ (frontal body area) at skin temperature 305 K, assuming emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.98$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given:} \quad A = 1.6\;\text{m}^{2}, \;\; T = 305\;\text{K}, \;\; \varepsilon = 0.98, \;\; \sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4} \\[4pt]
&\text{Apply Stefan–Boltzmann Law:} \quad P = \varepsilon\, \sigma\, A\, T^{4} \\[6pt]
&\text{Compute } T^{4}: \quad T^{4} = (305)^{4} \\[6pt]
&\text{Calculate stepwise:}\;\; 305^{2} = 93{,}025 \;\text{K}^{2} \\[6pt]
&\text{Calculate stepwise:}\;\; 305^{4} = (93{,}025)^{2} = 8.6537 \times 10^{9}\;\text{K}^{4} \\[6pt]
&\text{Substitute back:} \quad P = 0.98 \times (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times 1.6 \times (8.6537 \times 10^{9}) \\[6pt]
&\text{Multiply constants:} \quad 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} = 5.5566 \times 10^{-8} \\[6pt]
&\text{Multiply by 1.6:} \quad 5.5566 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.6 = 8.8906 \times 10^{-8} \\[6pt]
&\text{Multiply by } T^{4}: \quad 8.8906 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.6537 \times 10^{9} \\[6pt]
&\text{Numerical evaluation:} \quad P \approx 769.5\;\text{W} \\[6pt]
&\boxed{P \;\approx\; 770\;\text{W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A single human radiates **~770 W** as IR power at 9.5 µm — comparable to a 10-bulb incandescent lamp. This is a *huge* signal that a sensitive microbolometer can easily resolve from 200+ metres away.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Detection Range of a Thermal NVS

The **Johnson Range Equation** (a standard IR-performance metric) states that the squared detection range $R^{2}$ of a thermal imager is:

$$
R^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi\, D^{2}\, \tau_{a}\, \tau_{o}\, \Delta T}{4\, f\, \text{NETD}}
$$

where
- $D$ = entrance-pupil diameter of optics (m)
- $\tau_{a}$ = atmospheric transmittance over range $R$
- $\tau_{o}$ = optics transmittance
- $f$ = focal length (m)
- $\Delta T$ = target-to-background temperature contrast (K)
- NETD = sensor noise-equivalent temperature difference (K)

**Numerical Example:** Compute the detection range for $\Delta T = 8\,\text{K}$, $D = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $f = 0.05\,\text{m}$, NETD $= 0.05\,\text{K}$, $\tau_{a}\tau_{o} = 0.7$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Substitute values:} \quad R^{2} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^{2} \times 0.7 \times 8}{4 \times 0.05 \times 0.05} \\[6pt]
&\text{Numerator:} \;\; \pi \times 0.0025 \times 0.7 \times 8 = \pi \times 0.014 = 0.04398 \\[6pt]
&\text{Denominator:} \;\; 4 \times 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.01 \\[6pt]
&\text{Ratio:} \;\; R^{2} = 0.04398 / 0.01 = 4.398 \;\text{m}^{2} \\[6pt]
&\text{Take square root:} \;\; R = \sqrt{4.398} \;\text{m} \\[6pt]
&\boxed{R \;\approx\; 2.10\;\text{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Concept Trap:** The Johnson equation gives the **detection range of a *single pixel-sized* target**. For human-sized targets ($1.6 \times 0.6$ m), the actual **recognition** range scales by a factor of $\sqrt{(\text{target area})/(\text{pixel area})}$, often yielding **150–300 m** in production systems like the BMW Night Vision.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Active NIR vs Passive FIR

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is the single most common comparison table asked in KTU Module-2 examinations.

| Parameter | Active NIR | Passive FIR (Thermal) |
|---|---|---|
| Spectral Band | 0.7 – 1.0 µm (NIR) | 8 – 14 µm (LWIR) |
| Operating Mode | Active (needs IR projector) | Passive (no projector) |
| Image Type | Grayscale reflection | Thermal map (false-color) |
| Range | 100 – 200 m | 150 – 300 m |
| Fog / Rain Performance | Poor (NIR scatters) | Excellent (LWIR penetrates) |
| Glare from Headlamps | Susceptible | Immune |
| Daytime Usefulness | Limited (looks washed out) | Excellent (works 24/7) |
| Sensor Cost | Low (CMOS) | High (microbolometer) |
| OEM Examples | Mercedes, Toyota | BMW, Audi, Cadillac |
| Detection Principle | Reflectance difference | Emissivity × Temperature |

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define *Night Vision System* in the context of ADAS. Distinguish between **Active NIR** and **Passive FIR** systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Night Vision System (NVS)** is an ADAS perception module that extends driver visibility beyond the range of headlamps during low-light conditions by operating in infrared wavelengths.

- **Active NIR (Near-Infrared):** Uses an **IR projector (LED/Laser at 810–940 nm)** to illuminate the scene. A CMOS camera with a bandpass filter captures the reflected IR image. Example: Mercedes Night View Assist.
- **Passive FIR (Far-Infrared / Thermal):** Uses no projector. It detects the **self-emitted thermal radiation** (8–14 µm) of warm objects using a **microbolometer**. Example: BMW Night Vision.

| Feature | Active NIR | Passive FIR |
|---|---|---|
| Projector needed? | Yes | No |
| Day-night | Night only | 24 × 7 |
| Detection cue | Reflectance | Temperature |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** State and explain **Wien's Displacement Law**. Why is it important for selecting the spectral band of an automotive thermal camera?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Wien's Displacement Law states that the wavelength at which a blackbody's emission peaks is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T}, \qquad b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m·K}
$$

For a pedestrian at skin temperature $T \approx 305\,\text{K}$:

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305} \approx 9.5\;\mu\text{m}
$$

Hence automotive thermal cameras operate in the **LWIR band (8–14 µm)**, which coincides with both the **peak human-body emission** and the **atmospheric transmission window** — making it the optimal spectral choice.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a)** With the help of suitable diagrams, explain the **working principle of a Passive Far-Infrared (FIR) thermal Night Vision System** used in modern automobiles. Include the relevant physics of blackbody radiation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **components, signal-processing pipeline, and display integration** of an FIR-based NVS. Discuss at least two **OEM implementations**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Physical Principle (2 Marks):**
Every object with $T > 0\,\text{K}$ emits electromagnetic radiation. According to **Planck's Law**, the spectral radiance is:

$$
B_{\lambda}(\lambda,T) = \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k T}-1}
$$

A human body at $T = 305\,\text{K}$ (skin) emits peak radiation at $\lambda_{\max} \approx 9.5\,\mu\text{m}$ via **Wien's Displacement Law**, which lies in the **Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR)** band.

**2. Sensor Operation (2 Marks):**
The thermal camera uses a **microbolometer array** (typically 640 × 480 VO$_x$ pixels). Each pixel absorbs LWIR radiation → its resistance changes (bolometric effect) → the **ROIC** digitises this into a thermal image.

**3. Why This Works at Night (2 Marks):**
The system is **passive**, requiring no external illumination. Pedestrians at 305 K stand out vividly against a road/background at 280–290 K because the **temperature contrast** $\Delta T$ is large (8–25 K) and easily exceeds the sensor NETD (< 50 mK).

**4. Diagram (1 Mark):**
Refer to **Section 4.2 (Block Diagram)** for the end-to-end FIR pipeline.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**1. Components (3 Marks):**
- **Optics:** Germanium or chalcogenide lenses (transparent to LWIR), F-number ~1.0.
- **Sensor:** Uncooled microbolometer (FLIR Tau 2, ULIS Pico, Lynred).
- **ECU:** Embedded processor running CNN-based detection (e.g., Texas Instruments TDA4VM, NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano).
- **Display:** Windshield **Head-Up Display (HUD)** or instrument cluster.
- **Acuator Interface:** CAN-FD bus to brake/steering ECU.

**2. Algorithm Pipeline (2 Marks):**
**ROI extraction → CNN detection → Kalman filter tracking → TTC estimation → Warning trigger** if TTC < 1.5 s.

**3. OEM Examples (2 Marks):**
- **BMW Night Vision (Dynamic Light Spot):** Detects pedestrians and **automatically aims the headlamp beam** at them.
- **Audi Night Vision Assistant:** Uses a **FIR camera behind the four-ring badge** with animal + pedestrian detection optimised for European wildlife (deer, wild boar).
- **Mercedes Night View Assist PLUS:** Uses **Active NIR** (separate technology but comparable integration).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** Derive and explain the **peak emission wavelength and total radiated power** of a pedestrian using Planck's, Wien's, and Stefan–Boltzmann laws. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **Active NIR** and **Passive FIR** Night Vision Systems in a **tabular matrix** covering at least 8 parameters. Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Wien's Law Derivation (3 Marks):**
Differentiate Planck's spectral radiance with respect to $\lambda$, set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial B_{\lambda}}{\partial \lambda} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda_{\max} T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m·K}
$$

For $T = 305\,\text{K}$:

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305} \approx 9.5\;\mu\text{m}
$$

**2. Stefan–Boltzmann Power Derivation (3 Marks):**
Integrate Planck's law over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$
P = \int_{0}^{\infty} \pi B_{\lambda}\, d\lambda = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}
$$

For $A = 1.6\,\text{m}^2$, $\varepsilon = 0.98$, $T = 305\,\text{K}$:

$$
P = 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.6 \times (305)^{4} \approx 770\;\text{W}
$$

**3. Engineering Significance (1 Mark):**
The 770 W of IR flux at 9.5 µm is the **detection signature** that the microbolometer captures from 200+ m — orders of magnitude above the sensor noise floor.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**1. Tabular Comparison (4 Marks):** Refer to the **Section 3.4 Analytical Matrix** — reproduce the 10-parameter table.

**2. Active NIR — Advantages & Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
- ✅ Lower cost (uses standard CMOS sensors).
- ✅ Higher spatial resolution.
- ❌ Performance degrades in fog, rain, snow (NIR scatters heavily).
- ❌ Glare from oncoming headlamps can saturate the sensor.
- ❌ Useless during daytime without sophisticated HDR.

**3. Passive FIR — Advantages & Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
- ✅ Penetrates fog, smoke, light rain.
- ✅ Works 24 × 7 regardless of ambient lighting.
- ✅ Immune to headlamp glare.
- ❌ Expensive microbolometer + Ge optics.
- ❌ Lower native resolution than CMOS.
- ❌ Cannot read traffic signs (no visible contrast).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — KTU 2024 Examiner Notes:**
>
> 1. **Confusing NIR and FIR wavelengths.** A common mistake is writing "NIR operates at 8–14 µm." It does NOT — NIR is 0.7–1.0 µm. FIR is 8–14 µm. Memorize this distinction; it carries **2 marks** in Part-A questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the emissivity factor $\varepsilon$** in Stefan–Boltzmann calculations. If you write $P = \sigma A T^4$ without $\varepsilon$, you lose **1 mark** even if the final number is correct.
> 3. **Not citing the OEM (BMW, Audi, Mercedes).** KTU examiners award **1 bonus mark** for naming a real production system. Saying "used in cars" is too vague.
> 4. **Confusing NETD with MRTD.** NETD is a sensor parameter (mK); MRTD includes the human-observer MTF. Examiners check this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the display side of the pipeline.** Students describe the sensor in detail but forget HUD/instrument-cluster integration — a frequent 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Read 5 Times Before Exam**

- **Night Vision System (NVS)** = ADAS perception module extending visibility in low light.
- **Two families:** Active NIR (0.7–1.0 µm, projector + CMOS) and Passive FIR (8–14 µm, no projector + microbolometer).
- **Active NIR:** Active illumination at **850 nm** is the industry standard. Mercedes Night View Assist is the canonical example.
- **Passive FIR:** Works on **thermal emission** of objects. Microbolometer is uncooled and uses the **bolometric effect**.
- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} = b/T$. Human at 305 K → peaks at **9.5 µm** → 8–14 µm LWIR window is chosen.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$. A pedestrian radiates **~770 W** of IR power at 305 K.
- **Planck's Law:** Spectral radiance per unit wavelength of a blackbody.
- **NETD:** Sensor sensitivity metric. Target **< 50 mK** for automotive use.
- **Johnson Range Equation:** $R^{2} \propto D^{2} \tau_a \tau_o \Delta T / (4 f \cdot \text{NETD})$.
- **BMW** uses FIR with dynamic headlamp aiming.
- **Audi** uses FIR with European wildlife detection.
- **Mercedes** uses Active NIR.
- **Cadillac (CT6)** introduced **NIR + HUD pedestrian highlighting** in 2016.
- **Fog/rain penetration:** FIR is excellent; NIR is poor.
- **Glare immunity:** FIR is fully immune; NIR is vulnerable.
- **Time-To-Collision (TTC):** Trigger criterion for warnings.
- **CNN architectures** used: YOLO, MobileNet-SSD, EfficientDet (trained on thermal datasets like FLIR ADAS).
- **Display:** HUD + instrument cluster; warning when TTC < 1.5 s.
- **Limitation of FIR:** Cannot detect cold objects against warm backgrounds (e.g., black cat on warm asphalt).
- **Limitation of NIR:** Cannot operate effectively in daytime without HDR fusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra:** *Wien gives the band, Stefan gives the power, Planck gives the spectrum, the microbolometer reads the photons, the CNN finds the human, the HUD warns the driver, the ECU brakes the car.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic and Infrared Sensors - Principles of Ultrasonic Sensor Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic and Infrared Sensors - Principles of Ultrasonic Sensor Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Sensor (Definition):** An ultrasonic sensor is a non-contact, active distance-measuring transducer that operates on the **echolocation principle**, emitting high-frequency acoustic waves (typically **above 20 kHz**, beyond the human audible range) into a medium and converting the **Time of Flight (ToF)** of the reflected echo into an electrical signal proportional to the target distance.

In the context of **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)**, ultrasonic sensors are categorized as **short-range proximity sensors** (effective range: **0.1 m to 5 m**), used primarily for **parking assistance, low-speed collision avoidance, blind-spot detection at low speeds, and automated parking maneuvers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ultrasonic"?** The prefix *ultra-* means *beyond*. Ultrasonic refers to acoustic waves whose frequency lies **above the upper limit of human hearing (~20 kHz)**. Most automotive ultrasonic sensors operate in the **40 kHz to 70 kHz** band, with **40 kHz and 58 kHz** being industry-standard choices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Bat Sees the Road"

Imagine you are standing in a dark tunnel holding a **bat (a small mammal that navigates using echolocation)**. The bat cannot see the walls, but it screams at a very high pitch (above your hearing range) and listens carefully for the **echo** to bounce back. By measuring **how long the echo takes to return**, the bat accurately computes the distance to the wall. The ultrasonic sensor in your car works on the **exact same principle**, but instead of a bat, the car uses a tiny **piezoelectric crystal** that vibrates millions of times per second to send sound pulses, and listens with the **same crystal** to detect the returning echo.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy mapping:**
> - Bat's scream $\rightarrow$ Piezoelectric transmitter's **40 kHz burst**
> - Tunnel wall $\rightarrow$ Obstacle / vehicle / kerb
> - Bat's ear $\rightarrow$ Piezoelectric receiver / shared transducer
> - Time delay of echo $\rightarrow$ Computed distance to obstacle

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical quantities govern ultrasonic sensor operation:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in air (at 20°C) | $c$ | **343** | m/s |
| Speed of sound in air (at 0°C) | $c_0$ | **331** | m/s |
| Typical automotive frequency | $f$ | **40** (or 58) | kHz |
| Wavelength in air (at 40 kHz) | $\lambda$ | $\approx$ **8.6** | mm |
| Human audible limit | $f_{aud}$ | **20** | kHz |
| Standard detection range | $R$ | **0.1 – 5.0** | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed of sound depends on air temperature** according to:
> $$c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad [\text{m/s}]$$
> where $T$ is the air temperature in **°C**. Most ECUs apply a **temperature compensation** algorithm to keep distance measurements accurate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Six Logical Steps

An ultrasonic sensor performs its function in a strict, repeating cycle. Understanding each step is **critical** for KTU 2-mark and 14-mark questions.

**Step 1 — Excitation (Transmit Phase)**
The sensor's electronic oscillator drives the piezoelectric crystal with a **burst of high-frequency AC voltage** (typically 10–16 cycles of a 40 kHz square wave). The crystal physically deforms due to the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, creating a mechanical pressure wave in the surrounding air.

**Step 2 — Propagation**
The acoustic wave propagates through the air as a **longitudinal pressure wave** at the speed of sound. The wavefront spreads in a **conical beam pattern** defined by the transducer geometry (typically 15° to 75° cone angle, depending on aperture).

**Step 3 — Reflection**
When the wave strikes a target whose acoustic impedance differs significantly from air, a portion of the acoustic energy is **reflected back** as an echo. The fraction reflected depends on the **Reflection Coefficient ($R$)**:

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

where $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are the **acoustic impedances** of the two media. This is why soft, fabric-covered objects (e.g., a person in a woollen coat) reflect ultrasonic waves poorly — they absorb rather than reflect the energy.

**Step 4 — Echo Reception (Receive Phase)**
The same piezoelectric crystal (operating in **dual-transducer mode** or shared **single-transducer mode**) is switched from transmitter to receiver. The returning pressure wave mechanically deforms the crystal, generating a small AC voltage via the **direct piezoelectric effect**.

**Step 5 — Time of Flight (ToF) Measurement**
A precision timer in the ECU measures the **elapsed time ($\Delta t$)** between the transmitted burst and the received echo. Since the wave travels to the target AND back, the **one-way distance** is:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Signal Conditioning & Output**
The echo voltage is amplified, filtered, and compared against a threshold. The ECU converts the ToF into a digital distance value and broadcasts it on the **vehicle CAN bus** for use by the parking assist or ADAS controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dual vs. Single Transducer:**
> - **Dual transducer** (separate Tx and Rx): Used in long-range, high-sensitivity industrial applications.
> - **Single transducer (multiplexed)**: Standard for automotive park-assist. The crystal switches between Tx and Rx modes after a short **ring-down settling time** (~1–2 ms). This settling time defines the **minimum measurable distance (dead zone)**, typically **~15–25 cm**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every semester in Part A or Part B.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Distance from Time of Flight | metres (m) |
| 2 | $c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound vs. temperature | m/s |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of ultrasonic wave | metres (m) |
| 4 | $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | Acoustic Reflection Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic Impedance | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| 6 | $\theta_{cone} \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | Approximate beam cone angle | radians |
| 7 | $D_{min} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{settling}}{2}$ | Dead-zone (minimum distance) | metres (m) |
| 8 | $\text{Range}_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{max}}{2}$ | Maximum measurable distance | metres (m) |
| 9 | $P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot \dfrac{A}{(4\pi R^2)^2}$ | Received power in free space | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | decibels (dB) |

> [!TIP]
> **Boundary conditions to memorize for KTU valuation:**
> - $\Delta t$ is **total round-trip time**, hence the divide by 2.
> - $c$ varies with temperature AND humidity; **humidity raises $c$ by ~0.1 m/s per 1% RH**.
> - The **dead zone** exists because the Tx crystal's mechanical ringing masks any echo returning within the settling time.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in ADAS

In modern ADAS, ultrasonic sensors are the **workhorses of low-speed perception**:

- **Park-Assist Systems:** 6–12 sensors per bumper (4 front, 4–8 rear) provide 360° coverage at <10 km/h.
- **Cross-Traffic Alert (Rear):** Two rear-corner sensors detect approaching vehicles while reversing out of a parking bay.
- **Automated Parking (SAE Level 2+):** Ultrasonic sensors provide **closed-loop feedback** for the steering and throttle controller to align and slot the vehicle.
- **Kick-to-Open Trunk (Gesture Sensing):** Under-bumper ultrasonic sensor detects a foot swipe via Doppler shift.
- **Valet Parking (SAE Level 4):** Combined with surround-view cameras, ultrasonic sensors enable fully automated garage parking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Distance Equation from Time of Flight

We derive the fundamental relationship $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ from first principles.

**Assumption:** The wave travels at constant speed $c$ in a homogeneous, still medium (air at uniform temperature, no wind).

**Setup:** Let a sensor be located at position $x = 0$. At time $t = 0$, the sensor emits an ultrasonic pulse. The pulse travels outward, strikes a target located at distance $d$, and is reflected. The echo returns to the sensor at time $t = \Delta t$.

**Step 1 — One-way travel time:**
The pulse travels from the sensor to the target, covering a one-way distance $d$ at constant speed $c$. By the definition of constant velocity:

$$v = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{d}{t_{one-way}}$$

Solving for the one-way travel time:

$$t_{one-way} = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip travel time:**
The echo travels the same distance $d$ back to the sensor at the same speed $c$. The total round-trip time $\Delta t$ is therefore:

$$\Delta t = t_{one-way,tx} + t_{one-way,rx} = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for distance $d$:**
Multiply both sides of the equation by $c$:

$$c \cdot \Delta t = 2d$$

Divide both sides by 2:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style):**
A rear parking sensor measures a round-trip ToF of **$\Delta t = 12.0$ ms** at an air temperature of **$T = 30°\text{C}$**. Find the distance to the obstacle.

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 349 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$d = \frac{349 \cdot 0.012}{2} = \frac{4.188}{2} = 2.094 \;\text{m}$$

The obstacle is **2.09 m** behind the bumper.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's note:** Always state the temperature compensation step explicitly. If the question gives $c = 340$ m/s as a default (commonly used for quick problems), you may skip the temperature calculation. Otherwise, show the full $c = 331 + 0.6T$ substitution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Beam Cone Angle vs. Transducer Aperture

The approximate half-angle $\theta$ of the ultrasonic beam is governed by diffraction physics:

**Starting point — Fraunhofer diffraction for a circular aperture:**

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$$

where $D$ is the **diameter of the transducer aperture** and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

**Step 1 — Small-angle approximation:**
For typical automotive apertures ($D \approx 16$ mm) and wavelengths ($\lambda \approx 8.6$ mm), $\theta$ is small, so $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ in radians:

$$\theta \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda = c / f$:**

$$\theta = \frac{1.22 \cdot c}{f \cdot D}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**
For $f = 40$ kHz, $c = 343$ m/s, $D = 16$ mm = 0.016 m:

$$\theta = \frac{1.22 \cdot 343}{40000 \cdot 0.016} = \frac{418.46}{640} \approx 0.654 \;\text{rad} \approx 37.5°$$

So the full beam cone (full angle, not half-angle) is approximately **75°**, consistent with typical automotive ultrasonic specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design insight:** A larger aperture $D$ produces a narrower beam (better directionality), while a smaller $D$ produces a wider beam (better coverage). Automotive OEMs trade these off depending on use case — narrow beams for precise gap measurement, wide beams for general object detection.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Error Sources & Compensation

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix is a KTU-favourite 14-mark sub-question: "Discuss the sources of error in ultrasonic distance measurement and the compensation techniques used in modern ADAS ECUs."**

| # | Error Source | Physical Origin | Magnitude | Compensation Technique |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Temperature drift of $c$** | $c$ varies as $331 + 0.6T$ m/s | **~0.6 m/s per °C** (≈ 0.18%/°C) | Onboard NTC thermistor in sensor housing; ECU applies $c(T)$ lookup table |
| 2 | **Humidity variation** | Water vapour reduces air density | **~0.1 m/s per 1% RH** | Optional humidity sensor or fixed-compensation table |
| 3 | **Cross-talk (inter-sensor interference)** | Adjacent bumper sensors pick up each other's echoes | Echo at wrong ToF | **Time-Division Multiple Access (TDMA)**: each sensor fires in a dedicated time slot |
| 4 | **Acoustic ringing / dead zone** | Crystal continues vibrating after Tx burst | Cannot detect objects **< 15–25 cm** | Use **separate Tx and Rx crystals** in premium systems |
| 5 | **Specular reflection** | Smooth surfaces (glass, polished metal) reflect beam away from sensor | Echo never returns | Use **multiple sensors with overlapping cones**; combine with camera data |
| 6 | **Absorption by soft materials** | Clothing, foliage absorb ultrasound | Very weak echo | Use **higher transmit power**; raise receiver gain dynamically |
| 7 | **Wind / air turbulence** | Bulk air motion alters effective $c$ | **< 1 m/s** (negligible for parking) | Generally ignored in low-speed ADAS |
| 8 | **Beam edge clipping** | Object at beam edge gives weak echo | Erroneous ToF | **Multi-sample averaging** across consecutive bursts |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: The Round-Trip Time for a Given Maximum Range

The maximum range of an ultrasonic sensor is determined by how long the ECU waits for an echo before declaring "no object detected".

**Given:** Maximum measurable distance $D_{max} = 4.0$ m, air temperature $T = 25°C$.

**Step 1 — Compute $c$:**

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 25 = 346 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the round-trip relation:**

$$\Delta t_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot D_{max}}{c} = \frac{2 \cdot 4.0}{346} = 0.02312 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to milliseconds:**

$$\Delta t_{max} \approx 23.1 \;\text{ms}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design implication:** The ECU must allocate at least ~24 ms between successive transmit events to allow the longest possible echo to return. This sets the **refresh rate** of the sensor at approximately $\dfrac{1}{0.024} \approx 42$ Hz, which is sufficient for smooth parking assistance display updates.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Resolution and Minimum Detectable Distance Change

The **range resolution** of an ultrasonic sensor depends on the **timing resolution** of the ECU counter.

**Given:** The ECU timer has a clock period of $\Delta t_{clock} = 1 \;\mu\text{s}$. Air temperature is 20°C, so $c = 343$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Distance resolution from timing resolution:**

$$\delta d = \frac{c \cdot \delta t}{2} = \frac{343 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 1.715 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Express in millimetres:**

$$\delta d \approx 0.17 \;\text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A high-end ultrasonic ECU with 1 µs timer resolution can resolve distance changes of **less than 0.2 mm** — more than sufficient for precision parking applications where the typical requirement is **±1 cm** at ranges up to 2 m.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Sensor Subsystem

The diagram below shows the **end-to-end signal and data flow** inside a single automotive ultrasonic sensor module and its connection to the vehicle ADAS ECU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    oscA[Oscillator 40kHz Burst Generator] --> pztA[Piezoelectric Crystal Tx Mode]
    pztA --> airA[Air Medium Pressure Wave]
    airA --> tgtA[Target Object Wall Vehicle]
    tgtA --> airB[Reflected Echo Pressure Wave]
    airB --> pztB[Piezoelectric Crystal Rx Mode]
    pztB --> ampA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    ampA --> filtA[Bandpass Filter 40kHz plus minus 2kHz]
    filtA --> compA[Threshold Comparator]
    compA --> tmrA[ToF Timer Counter]
    oscA --> tmrA
    tmrA --> mcuA[Local Microcontroller Distance Conversion]
    tempA[NTC Thermistor Temperature Sensor] --> mcuA
    mcuA --> canA[CAN Transceiver 500kbps]
    canA --> ecanet[Vehicle CAN BUS]
    ecanet --> ecuA[ADAS ECU Park Assist Controller]
    ecuA --> dispA[Driver Display HMI Buzzer]
    ecuA --> actA[Steering and Throttle Actuator Auto Park]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow splits into two parallel paths — the **signal path** (left/top, from oscillator to CAN) and the **compensation path** (NTC thermistor feeding the local MCU). The MCU combines both to publish a temperature-compensated distance value on the CAN bus.

---

## 4.2 Operational State Machine — Tx/Rx Multiplexing

A single-transducer ultrasonic sensor must switch between transmit and receive modes. The state machine below captures this timing-critical process.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> TxBurst: ECU triggers measurement
    TxBurst: Transmit Burst 16 cycles 40kHz
    TxBurst --> RingDown: Burst ends crystal rings
    RingDown: Ring Down Settling approx 1 to 2 ms
    RingDown --> Listen: Crystal damped
    Listen: Listen for Echo via threshold detector
    Listen --> EchoValid: Echo above threshold
    Listen --> Timeout: No echo within max range window
    EchoValid --> Compute: ToF measured
    Timeout --> NoTarget: Report max range
    Compute --> Publish: Compute d equals c times dt over 2
    NoTarget --> Publish
    Publish: Publish distance to CAN bus
    Publish --> Idle: Cycle complete approx 24 ms
    Idle --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU focus area:** Notice the **Ring Down** state. The student must explain that during ring-down, the crystal's residual mechanical vibration would otherwise be misinterpreted by the receiver as a "near-target echo", which is why the ECU must wait 1–2 ms before enabling the receiver. This is the **physical origin of the dead zone**.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bumper-Mounted ADAS Ultrasonic Array

The diagram below illustrates how **multiple ultrasonic sensors** coordinate on a vehicle bumper to provide 360° low-speed perception.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FrontBumper
        SF1[Sensor Front Left 40kHz]
        SF2[Sensor Front Centre Left]
        SF3[Sensor Front Centre Right]
        SF4[Sensor Front Right]
    end
    subgraph RearBumper
        SR1[Sensor Rear Left]
        SR2[Sensor Rear Centre Left]
        SR3[Sensor Rear Centre Right]
        SR4[Sensor Rear Right]
    end
    SF1 --> TDMA[TDMA Time Slot Scheduler]
    SF2 --> TDMA
    SF3 --> TDMA
    SF4 --> TDMA
    SR1 --> TDMA
    SR2 --> TDMA
    SR3 --> TDMA
    SR4 --> TDMA
    TDMA --> ECU[Central ADAS ECU]
    ECU --> HMI[HMI Display and Audible Warnings]
    ECU --> APA[Automated Parking Assist Module]
    ECU --> RCTA[Rear Cross Traffic Alert Module]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TDMA is critical:** Without time-slot scheduling, the rear-left sensor's transmit burst would be received by the rear-centre-left sensor, producing a **false echo at the wrong range** — a phenomenon called **cross-talk**. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Beam-Pattern Diagram (Textual Schematic)

A simple side-by-side comparison of the ultrasonic sensor's beam pattern at two common operating frequencies:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transducer Aperture D equals 16mm] --> B{Operating Frequency}
    B -- 40 kHz --> C[Lambda approx 8.6 mm Beam Cone approx 75 degrees wide coverage lower resolution]
    B -- 58 kHz --> D[Lambda approx 5.9 mm Beam Cone approx 50 degrees narrower beam higher resolution shorter range]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off summary:**
> - **40 kHz:** wider beam, longer range (~5 m), preferred for general parking.
> - **58 kHz:** narrower beam, shorter range (~2.5 m), preferred for precision gap measurement in auto-park systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Time of Flight (ToF)" as applied to ultrasonic distance measurement. State the governing equation and identify the two key physical parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Time of Flight (ToF) is the **total elapsed time** between the instant an ultrasonic pulse is transmitted by the sensor and the instant its reflected echo is received back at the same sensor.

**Governing equation (1 mark):**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Two key physical parameters (1 mark):**
1. **$c$** — the speed of sound in the propagation medium (air), approximately **343 m/s at 20°C**, given by $c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ where $T$ is in °C.
2. **$\Delta t$** — the measured round-trip time interval, captured by a high-resolution hardware timer in the ECU.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: What is the "dead zone" of an ultrasonic sensor? State its typical value for an automotive park-assist sensor and explain the physical reason for its existence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **dead zone** is the minimum distance from the sensor face within which the ECU cannot reliably detect a target.

**Typical value (1 mark):** For a standard automotive park-assist ultrasonic sensor, the dead zone is typically **15 cm to 25 cm**.

**Physical reason (1 mark):** Immediately after the transmit burst ends, the piezoelectric crystal continues to mechanically vibrate (a phenomenon called **ring-down**) for **1 to 2 ms**. If an echo were to return during this period, the receiver circuit cannot distinguish the residual ring-down vibration from a genuine echo. Therefore, the ECU deliberately disables the receiver for ~1–2 ms after transmission, creating a "blind spot" near the sensor.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a functional block diagram, explain the operating principle of an automotive ultrasonic sensor used in a Rear Park-Assist System. Describe the role of the piezoelectric transducer, the transmit/receive multiplexing scheme, and the time-of-flight measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic parking sensor measures a round-trip time of flight of **9.5 ms** at an ambient air temperature of **35°C**. Calculate:
  - (i) the speed of sound in air at this temperature,
  - (ii) the distance to the obstacle,
  - (iii) the wavelength of the 40 kHz ultrasonic wave. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Principle & Block Diagram (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** An automotive ultrasonic sensor is an **active, non-contact distance sensor** based on the **echolocation principle**. It emits a short burst of high-frequency acoustic energy (typically 10–16 cycles of a **40 kHz** wave) and measures the **Time of Flight (ToF)** of the echo reflected from a nearby obstacle.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The **piezoelectric crystal** is the heart of the sensor. In **transmit mode**, an applied AC voltage causes the crystal to physically deform via the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, launching a pressure wave into the air. In **receive mode**, an incoming pressure wave deforms the crystal, generating a small AC voltage via the **direct piezoelectric effect**. A single crystal is typically used in automotive sensors, with a **multiplexer** switching it between Tx and Rx modes.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The **timing sequence** is as follows:
1. ECU triggers a **transmit burst** of ~16 cycles of 40 kHz.
2. Burst ends; crystal undergoes **ring-down** for ~1–2 ms (defines the **dead zone**).
3. Receiver is enabled; ECU starts a **high-resolution counter**.
4. Echo is detected by a **threshold comparator**; counter is latched.
5. Local MCU computes $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ using a **temperature-compensated** value of $c$.
6. Distance is published on the **CAN bus** to the ADAS ECU.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The ADAS ECU aggregates distances from 4–8 bumper-mounted sensors and triggers **audible beeps** and/or **visual graphics** on the driver HMI, with beep frequency increasing as the obstacle distance decreases.

**Block Diagram (reference, draw in exam):**

```
[Oscillator] -> [PZT Crystal Tx] -> [Air] -> [Target] -> [Air] -> 
[PZT Crystal Rx] -> [Amplifier] -> [Bandpass Filter] -> [Comparator] -> 
[ToF Timer] -> [Local MCU] -> [CAN Bus] -> [ADAS ECU] -> [HMI/Audio]
        ^                                         ^
        |                                         |
   [NTC Temp Sensor] ---> Temperature Comp -----+
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\Delta t = 9.5$ ms $= 9.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $T = 35°\text{C}$
- $f = 40$ kHz $= 40 \times 10^{3}$ Hz

**Sub-part (i): Speed of sound at 35°C** (2 marks)

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 35 = 331 + 21 = 352 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Sub-part (ii): Distance to obstacle** (3 marks)

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{352 \cdot 9.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{3.344}{2} = 1.672 \;\text{m}$$

The obstacle is **1.67 m** behind the rear bumper.

**Sub-part (iii): Wavelength of 40 kHz wave** (2 marks)

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{352}{40 \times 10^{3}} = 8.8 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} = 8.8 \;\text{mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently **forget to divide by 2** in the distance equation, reporting the round-trip path length (3.34 m) instead of the one-way distance (1.67 m). This is the **single most common marking-deduction error** in ultrasonic ToF problems. Always write the equation $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ first, substitute, then solve.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the phenomenon of **piezoelectricity** and describe how it is exploited in an ultrasonic transducer for both transmission and reception of acoustic waves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's reverse park-assist system uses **6 ultrasonic sensors** on the rear bumper. Each sensor has a maximum range of **4.5 m** at an air temperature of **20°C**. The ECU uses a **Time-Division Multiple Access (TDMA)** scheme, scheduling each sensor to transmit in a dedicated time slot.

Calculate:
  - (i) the maximum round-trip time of flight for the maximum range,
  - (ii) the minimum total cycle time required to poll all 6 sensors sequentially,
  - (iii) the resulting refresh rate of the system in Hz. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Piezoelectricity (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** **Piezoelectricity** is the property of certain crystalline materials (notably quartz, lead zirconate titanate — PZT, and certain ceramics) to generate an electric charge across their faces when subjected to mechanical stress (**direct piezoelectric effect**), and conversely, to physically deform when an electric field is applied across them (**inverse or converse piezoelectric effect**).

**Transmission mode (2.5 marks):** When the ECU applies a **high-frequency AC voltage** (typically 40 kHz, 10–16 V peak-to-peak) across electrodes plated onto the piezoelectric crystal, the crystal undergoes rapid mechanical deformation at the same frequency. This vibrating surface pushes against the surrounding air, creating **alternating compressions and rarefactions** — i.e., a **longitudinal pressure wave** that propagates outward as ultrasonic energy.

**Reception mode (2.5 marks):** When an incoming echo (a pressure wave) strikes the crystal face, the mechanical stress induces a small alternating electric charge across the crystal electrodes, in accordance with the **direct piezoelectric effect**. This weak AC signal (typically millivolts) is then amplified, bandpass-filtered, and compared against a threshold to confirm echo detection and latch the ToF timer.

#### Part (b) — TDMA Cycle Time (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D_{max} = 4.5$ m
- $T = 20°\text{C}$ $\Rightarrow$ $c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 20 = 343$ m/s
- Number of sensors $N = 6$

**Sub-part (i): Maximum round-trip ToF** (2 marks)

$$\Delta t_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot D_{max}}{c} = \frac{2 \cdot 4.5}{343} = \frac{9.0}{343} = 0.02624 \;\text{s} \approx 26.24 \;\text{ms}$$

**Sub-part (ii): Minimum total TDMA cycle time** (2 marks)

In the worst case, all 6 sensors are polled sequentially, and each sensor must wait for its own echo before the next sensor fires. Therefore:

$$T_{cycle,min} = N \cdot \Delta t_{max} = 6 \cdot 26.24 = 157.4 \;\text{ms}$$

**Sub-part (iii): Refresh rate** (3 marks)

$$f_{refresh} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle,min}} = \frac{1}{0.1574} = 6.35 \;\text{Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** A refresh rate of ~6 Hz is **adequate for parking manoeuvres** at low speeds (<10 km/h) but is **far too slow** for highway-speed ADAS (where 20–50 Hz is required). This is why ultrasonic sensors are **exclusively used for low-speed applications**, and high-speed perception relies on radar and cameras instead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A common student error is to use **only the one-way travel time** (i.e., $\Delta t = D/c$) instead of the round-trip $\Delta t = 2D/c$. Always re-derive the relationship from the round-trip geometry before substituting numerical values. Also, students sometimes forget to multiply by $N$ for the TDMA cycle, calculating only the per-sensor cycle time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — print this and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Ultrasonic sensor = active, non-contact distance sensor operating on the **echolocation / Time of Flight principle** above 20 kHz.
- [x] **Standard automotive frequency:** **40 kHz** (most common) or **58 kHz** (precision applications).
- [x] **Core equation:** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ — **always divide by 2** for round-trip.
- [x] **Speed of sound:** $c = 331 + 0.6T$ m/s, with $T$ in °C. $c \approx 343$ m/s at 20°C.
- [x] **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c / f \approx 8.6$ mm at 40 kHz.
- [x] **Piezoelectric effect:** Inverse (Tx — voltage → deformation) and Direct (Rx — pressure → voltage).
- [x] **Dead zone:** 15–25 cm; caused by crystal **ring-down** after Tx burst.
- [x] **Maximum range:** Typically **4–5 m** for park-assist; refresh rate ~6 Hz for 6-sensor TDMA system.
- [x] **Error sources:** Temperature, humidity, cross-talk, specular reflection, soft-material absorption.
- [x] **Cross-talk solution:** **TDMA (Time-Division Multiple Access)** scheduling across bumper sensors.
- [x] **Beam pattern:** Cone of **~75°** at 40 kHz, ~50° at 58 kHz; narrower beam = better directionality.
- [x] **Reflection:** Depends on acoustic impedance mismatch; $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$.
- [x] **ADAS applications:** Park-assist, automated parking, rear cross-traffic alert, kick-to-open trunk, low-speed AEB.
- [x] **Limitation:** Ultrasonic sensors are **only for low-speed (<10 km/h) ADAS**; radar/LiDAR/cameras handle highway speeds.
- [x] **Refresh rate math:** $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot \Delta t_{max}}$ for an $N$-sensor TDMA system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Parking Assist and Obstacle Detection
Infrared Sensors and Their Role in ADAS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Infrared Sensors and Their Role in ADAS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Infrared (IR) Sensors** are electro-optical transducers that operate within the electromagnetic spectrum between visible light and microwave bands, covering wavelengths from approximately **0.7 µm to 1000 µm**. In Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), IR sensors function as non-contact, environment-sensing devices that detect, measure, and interpret thermal radiation emitted or reflected by objects, enabling perception tasks such as night vision, pedestrian identification, occupant classification, and driver monitoring. The infrared region of the electromagnetic spectrum is conventionally subdivided (per ISO 20473 scheme and CIE 17.27 classification) as follows: | Sub-band | Wavelength Range | Engineering Nomenclature | Primary ADAS Use | |---|---|---|---| | **NIR** | 0.75 – 1.4 µm | Near Infrared | Active illumination, traffic sign recognition | | **SWIR** | 1.4 – 3 µm | Short-Wave Infrared | Fog penetration, moisture sensing | | **MWIR** | 3 – 8 µm | Mid-Wave Infrared | Hot-object detection (exhaust, tires) | | **LWIR** | 8 – 15 µm | Long-Wave Infrared | Thermal imaging, pedestrian detection | | **FIR** | 15 – 1000 µm | Far Infrared | Specialized spectroscopy | > [!NOTE] > **Why LWIR dominates automotive thermal imaging:** Human skin emits peak thermal radiation at approximately 9.5 µm (per Wien's Displacement Law at 305 K), and this conveniently falls within the **8–14 µm atmospheric transmission window**, making LWIR the most relevant sub-band for ADAS pedestrian/cyclist detection. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat Camera" for Cars Imagine a car equipped with a pair of **"night-vision eyes"** that can see what regular headlights cannot. A standard camera works like your daytime eyes — it needs reflected visible light to form an image. But on a pitch-dark rural road, where a pedestrian in dark clothing is invisible, those regular eyes fail. An **infrared sensor is the car's superpower goggles**. Instead of waiting for photons to bounce back from the headlights, the IR sensor directly "feels" the **body heat** (thermal radiation) that every object naturally radiates. A human at 37°C glows brilliantly against a cold 5°C roadside. A deer crossing the road appears as a bright spot against a cool background. This is exactly how a snake's pit organ (detecting warm-blooded prey in total darkness) operates — IR is a wavelength we cannot see, but the sensor reads it as a **temperature map**. In ADAS, this "heat vision" is fused with regular RGB cameras and radar to: - Warn the driver of pedestrians hidden beyond headlight reach. - Track the driver's eyelid droop and head tilt (driver monitoring). - Detect the warm signature of a vehicle ahead cutting into the lane. - Identify the thermal footprint of a child left inside a parked car. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.670374419 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-4}$ - **Wien's displacement constant:** $b = 2.897771955 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.62607015 \times 10^{-34} \; \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$ - **Speed of light:** $c = 2.99792458 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$ - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$ - **First radiation constant:** $c_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 1.19104297 \times 10^{-16} \; \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^2$ - **Second radiation constant:** $c_2 = hc/k_B = 1.438776877 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ - **Standard reference temperature for emissivity:** $T_0 = 273.15 \; \text{K}$ (often 298 K in lab calibration) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 expects students to (i) classify IR sensors into thermal and quantum types, (ii) describe active vs passive IR topologies, (iii) explain the role of IR in night vision, DMS, and BSD, and (iv) compare IR with Lidar/Radar/Camera modalities. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Infrared Radiation ### 2.1.1 Blackbody Radiation (Planck's Law) Every object with a temperature above absolute zero (0 K) emits electromagnetic radiation. A **blackbody** is a hypothetical perfect emitter/absorber whose spectral radiance $B_\lambda$ is given by: $$ B_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1} \quad \text{[W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{sr}^{-1}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\text{]} $$ - Why this matters: It sets the **theoretical upper bound** of IR emission. Real objects (cars, pedestrians, asphalt) emit *less*, characterized by **emissivity** $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$. - For a real surface: $E_\lambda^{\text{real}} = \varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot B_\lambda(\lambda, T)$. ### 2.1.2 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Emitted Power) $$ P = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4 $$ where $A$ is the radiating surface area. The fourth-power dependence explains why a small temperature difference produces a large radiometric signal — perfect for **thermal contrast-based object detection**. ### 2.1.3 Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength) $$ \lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T} $$ - Why this matters: Tells you *which* IR band to look at for a given target temperature. A pedestrian (T ≈ 305 K) → $\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 9.5 \, \mu\text{m}$ (LWIR). A vehicle exhaust (T ≈ 800 K) → $\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 3.6 \, \mu\text{m}$ (MWIR). ### 2.1.4 Passive vs. Active IR Systems | Aspect | **Passive IR (PIR)** | **Active IR (AIR)** | |---|---|---| | Source | Uses natural thermal emission from target | Uses onboard IR emitter (LED/VCSEL) | | Wavelength | 8–14 µm (LWIR) | 0.85–0.94 µm (NIR, near-IR LEDs) | | ADAS use | Night vision, DMS, occupant detection | Gesture recognition, rain sensing, lane detection | | Cost | Higher (microbolometer arrays) | Lower (photodiodes) | | Range | 100–300 m typical | 5–50 m typical | ## 2.2 Classification of IR Sensors ### A. Thermal (Non-quantum) Detectors Convert incident IR energy into heat, then into an electrical signal. **Wavelength-independent** response. Slow but broadband. 1. **Thermopile** — Series of thermocouples; output voltage ∆V ≈ N·α·∆T. 2. **Bolometer** — Resistance changes with temperature; used in microbolometer FPAs (FLIR Boson, Seek Thermal). 3. **Pyroelectric** — Spontaneous polarization ∆P with ∆T; needs AC modulation (chopper). 4. **Microbolometer Array** — VOx or a-Si pixels; uncooled, dominates automotive LWIR cameras. ### B. Quantum (Photonic) Detectors Convert incident photons directly into electron-hole pairs. **Wavelength-selective**, faster, but typically need cryogenic cooling (or thermo-electric cooling for automotive). 1. **Photoconductive (PC)** — e.g., PbS, HgCdTe (MCT). 2. **Photovoltaic (PV)** — e.g., InGaAs (SWIR), InSb (MWIR). 3. **Quantum Well Infrared Photodetector (QWIP)** — Used in some premium automotive night vision. ## 2.3 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Radiation Collection:** IR optics (typically Germanium or Chalcogenide glass lenses) focus incoming IR onto the detector array. Germanium is preferred for LWIR because of its high refractive index (~4.0) and low dispersion at 8–14 µm. **Step 2 — Conversion:** - *Thermal path:* IR → heat ∆T → resistance change (bolometer) / charge change (pyroelectric) / EMF (thermopile). - *Quantum path:* IR photon (hν) → electron excitation → photocurrent $I_{\text{ph}} = \eta q \Phi$ where $\eta$ is quantum efficiency, $\Phi$ is photon flux. **Step 3 — Signal Conditioning:** Low-noise pre-amplifier → ADC → image processing (e.g., contrast enhancement, edge detection). **Step 4 — Object Detection & Classification:** Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on thermal imagery (e.g., YOLOv8-Thermal, FLIR ADAS dataset) identify and localize pedestrians, vehicles, animals. **Step 5 — Decision & Actuation:** Output is fused with radar/camera data → ECU issues AEB, LKA, or HMI alert. ## 2.4 ADAS Applications of IR Sensors ### 2.4.1 Night Vision Systems (NVS) — BMW Night Vision, Mercedes Night View Assist - Uses **LWIR microbolometer** (e.g., FLIR Tau 2 / Bosch IRLAS). - Detects pedestrians beyond headlight range (~150–250 m). - Warns driver via HUD or instrument cluster. - Reduces pedestrian fatalities at night by up to 50% in fleet studies. ### 2.4.2 Driver Monitoring System (DMS) — Euro NCAP 2026 mandate - **NIR camera (940 nm)** illuminates driver's face. - Detects drowsiness, distraction, seat-belt usage, gaze direction. - Uses eye-aspect-ratio (EAR) and head-pose estimation CNNs. ### 2.4.3 Occupant Classification & Child-Presence Detection (CPD) - **MWIR/LWIR sensor** in headliner detects thermal signature of occupants. - Required by Euro NCAP and proposed US HOT CARS Act. - Distinguishes between adult, child, and infant seat by temperature-area map. ### 2.4.4 Blind Spot Detection (BSD) and Cross-Traffic Alert - **NIR + CMOS sensor** or active IR emitter-receiver pair. - Complements radar (24/77 GHz) with optical signature (pedestrian recognition). ### 2.4.5 Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Augmentation - IR + Lidar fusion for stationary-object detection in congestion. ### 2.4.6 Lane-Keep Assist (LKA) Backup - NIR cameras perform better than RGB in low-light and tunnel conditions. ## 2.5 Sensor Fusion Hierarchy in ADAS $$ \text{Final Decision} = f(\text{Camera}, \text{Radar}, \text{Lidar}, \text{IR}) \in \{\text{brake}, \text{steer}, \text{warn}, \text{no-op}\} $$ - IR's unique strength: **Robust to lighting** (no headlights needed) and **detects living targets via thermal contrast**. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Unit | Application in ADAS | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann total power [W] | Radiative heat emitted by car/pedestrian | | 2 | $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = b / T$ | Wien's peak wavelength [m] | Choosing correct IR band for target | | 3 | $B_\lambda = \dfrac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k_BT} - 1)}$ | Planck's spectral radiance [W/m²/sr/m] | Detector spectral response design | | 4 | $R = \dfrac{V_\text{out}}{P_\text{in}}$ | Responsivity [V/W] | Detector sensitivity metric | | 5 | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}}$ | Specific detectivity [cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W] | Detector performance benchmarking | | 6 | $\text{NEP} = \dfrac{\text{NESD}}{R}$ | Noise Equivalent Power [W] | Minimum detectable power | | 7 | $I_\text{ph} = \eta q \Phi$ | Photocurrent from quantum detector [A] | Signal chain design | | 8 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{I_\text{signal}}{I_\text{noise}}$ | Signal-to-Noise ratio [dB] | Detection reliability | | 9 | $\text{NETD} = \dfrac{4 F^2 k_B T_\text{scene}^2}{\varepsilon \tau_o D^*}$ | Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference [mK] | Thermal camera sensitivity | | 10 | $T_\text{atm} = e^{-\alpha \cdot R}$ | Atmospheric transmittance (Beer's law) | Range estimation | | 11 | $f = c / \lambda$ | Frequency-wavelength relation [Hz] | Photon energy $E = hf$ | | 12 | $\text{EAR} = \dfrac{\vert y_{36} - y_{18} \vert}{\vert x_{36} - x_{18} \vert}$ | Eye Aspect Ratio (drowsiness) | DMS algorithm | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Insight:** The vertical bar symbol `|` is strictly avoided in tables. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX, e.g., $\vert y_{36} - y_{18} \vert$. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mercedes-Benz Night View Assist Plus**: Uses **near-IR camera + pedestrian spotlight** in headlamp to actively highlight detected pedestrians. - **BMW Night Vision with Dynamic Light Spot**: FLIR PathfindIR II sensor + headlamp beam that pivots toward detected pedestrians. - **Tesla Cabin Camera (HW3/HW4)**: NIR camera (940 nm) inside cabin for DMS, glare-free in any ambient light. - **FLIR ADK (Automotive Development Kit)**: Reference platform for OEMs to integrate thermal cameras. - **Valeo & Veoneer** lead the automotive thermal sensor supply chain (Tier-1). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Emission Wavelength for a Pedestrian **Goal:** Show that a human body at skin temperature $T = 305 \; \text{K}$ emits peak radiation in the LWIR band. **Step 1 — Start with Wien's Displacement Law.** $$ \lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Wien constant and skin temperature.** $$ \lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.897771955 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}\cdot\text{K}}{305 \; \text{K}} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the division.** $$ \lambda_{\text{peak}} = 9.500564 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to micrometres for engineering readability.** $$ \lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 9.50 \; \mu\text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion (KTU Examinable Statement).** > The peak lies squarely within the **8–14 µm atmospheric transmission window**, which is exactly why **LWIR microbolometer cameras** are chosen for automotive pedestrian detection. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Radiative Power from a Pedestrian vs. Asphalt **Scenario:** Compare emitted IR power from a pedestrian's exposed skin (face, hands) versus the asphalt road surface on a winter night. **Step 1 — State the Stefan-Boltzmann law with emissivity.** $$ P = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4 $$ **Step 2 — Set up the parameters.** | Object | Temperature $T$ | Emissivity $\varepsilon$ | Exposed Area $A$ | |---|---|---|---| | Human face (skin) | 305 K | 0.98 | 0.05 m² | | Asphalt (cold night) | 275 K | 0.93 | 1.00 m² | **Step 3 — Calculate power emitted per unit area from each.** For human skin: $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{skin} = \varepsilon_\text{skin} \cdot \sigma \cdot T_\text{skin}^4 $$ $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{skin} = 0.98 \times (5.6704 \times 10^{-8}) \times (305)^4 $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate $305^4$ (intermediate step).** $$ 305^2 = 93025, \quad 305^4 = (93025)^2 = 8.65366 \times 10^9 $$ **Step 5 — Multiply coefficients.** $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{skin} = 0.98 \times 5.6704 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.65366 \times 10^9 $$ $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{skin} = 0.98 \times 490.6 \; \text{W/m}^2 $$ $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{skin} \approx 480.8 \; \text{W/m}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Repeat for asphalt.** For asphalt: $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{asph} = \varepsilon_\text{asph} \cdot \sigma \cdot T_\text{asph}^4 $$ $$ 275^2 = 75625, \quad 275^4 = (75625)^2 = 5.71914 \times 10^9 $$ $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{asph} = 0.93 \times 5.6704 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.71914 \times 10^9 $$ $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{asph} = 0.93 \times 324.2 \; \text{W/m}^2 $$ $$ P/A \; \big\vert_\text{asph} \approx 301.5 \; \text{W/m}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Compute total radiated power for each object.** For skin: $$ P_\text{skin} = 480.8 \; \text{W/m}^2 \times 0.05 \; \text{m}^2 = 24.04 \; \text{W} $$ For asphalt (per square metre — since the asphalt patch is normalised to 1 m²): $$ P_\text{asph} = 301.5 \; \text{W/m}^2 \times 1 \; \text{m}^2 = 301.5 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 8 — Important Engineering Caveat.** Although asphalt emits **more total power per m²** (301.5 W vs. 480.8 W/m² × 0.05 m² = 24 W), the **angular resolution of a thermal imager** treats each pixel as a small field-of-view (FOV).
