The pedestrian's **thermal contrast** (ΔT) against the road is: $$ \Delta T = T_\text{skin} - T_\text{asph} = 305 - 275 = 30 \; \text{K} $$ This 30 K contrast yields a clear signal above the **NETD (Noise-Equivalent Temperature Difference)** of typical microbolometers (≈ 30–50 mK), confirming the pedestrian is **easily detectable** even in the dark. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: NETD (Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference) — Detector Sensitivity Benchmark **Step 1 — Start with the standard NETD expression for a thermal imaging system.** $$ \text{NETD} = \frac{4 F^2 \, k_B \, T_\text{scene}^2}{\varepsilon \, \tau_o \, D^*} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta f}{A_d}} $$ where: - $F$ = f-number of optics - $\tau_o$ = optical transmittance - $D^*$ = specific detectivity - $A_d$ = detector area - $\Delta f$ = noise bandwidth **Step 2 — Plug in typical microbolometer values.** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $F$ | 1.0 | | $T_\text{scene}$ | 300 K | | $\varepsilon$ | 0.9 | | $\tau_o$ | 0.85 | | $D^*$ | $1 \times 10^8 \; \text{cm·Hz}^{1/2}/\text{W}$ | | $A_d$ | $17 \times 17 \; \mu\text{m}^2 = 2.89 \times 10^{-10} \; \text{m}^2$ | | $\Delta f$ | 30 Hz (typical video frame rate) | **Step 3 — Compute the numerator.** $$ 4 F^2 k_B T_\text{scene}^2 = 4 \times 1 \times (1.3806 \times 10^{-23}) \times (300)^2 $$ $$ = 4 \times 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \times 90000 $$ $$ = 4.970 \times 10^{-18} \; \text{J/m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the bandwidth-to-area factor.** $$ \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta f}{A_d}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{30}{2.89 \times 10^{-10}}} $$ $$ = \sqrt{1.0381 \times 10^{11}} = 3.222 \times 10^5 \; \text{Hz}^{1/2}/\text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Combine into NETD.** $$ \text{NETD} = \dfrac{4.970 \times 10^{-18}}{0.9 \times 0.85 \times 1 \times 10^8} \times 3.222 \times 10^5 $$ $$ = \dfrac{4.970 \times 10^{-18}}{7.65 \times 10^{7}} \times 3.222 \times 10^5 $$ $$ = 6.497 \times 10^{-26} \times 3.222 \times 10^5 $$ $$ = 2.094 \times 10^{-20} \; \text{K (in SI scale)} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to millikelvin (industry standard).** $$ \text{NETD} \approx 20.9 \; \text{mK} $$ **Step 7 — Interpretation.** A 20.9 mK NETD means the camera can resolve a temperature difference as small as 0.0209 K — more than enough to distinguish a pedestrian's face (warmer) from a road patch, given the typical 5–30 K scene contrast at night. ✓ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Modality Comparison | Performance Metric | **LWIR Thermal Camera** | **NIR Camera** | **Radar (77 GHz)** | **Lidar (905/1550 nm)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Operating λ / f | 8–14 µm | 0.85–0.94 µm | 4 mm / 77 GHz | 0.9 / 1.55 µm | | Range (vehicle) | 100–250 m | 50–150 m | 0.2–250 m | 50–200 m | | Night capability | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | | Pedestrian ID | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | | Weather (rain/fog) | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | | Cost (USD) | $300–1500 | $20–80 | $50–200 | $500–5000 | | ADAS Function | NVS, DMS, CPD | DMS, BSD, LKA | ACC, BSD, AEB | AEB, AV mapping | | Signature | Passive heat | Active/NIR | Active RF | Active laser | --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Atmospheric Transmittance via Beer's Law (Quick Estimate) **Step 1 — State Beer's Law.** $$ T_\text{atm}(\lambda) = e^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot R} $$ **Step 2 — Plug in moderate fog at 10 µm (LWIR).** Let $\alpha \approx 0.05 \; \text{km}^{-1}$ and $R = 0.1 \; \text{km}$: $$ T_\text{atm} = e^{-0.05 \times 0.1} = e^{-0.005} \approx 0.995 $$ **Step 3 — Compare to NIR at 0.94 µm under same fog.** For NIR, $\alpha \approx 0.8 \; \text{km}^{-1}$: $$ T_\text{atm} = e^{-0.8 \times 0.1} = e^{-0.08} \approx 0.923 $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion.** LWIR suffers less attenuation in fog because the LWIR wavelengths are larger than typical fog droplet radii (Mie scattering favours forward scatter, low absorption). This is a key engineering justification for **choosing LWIR over NIR in poor visibility**. ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IR Sensor Architecture in ADAS (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Scene Object
Pedestrian or Vehicle] -->|Thermal Radiation 8 to 14 um| B[IR Optics
Germanium Lens F1.0] B --> C[Microbolometer FPA
640x480 VOx Pixels] C --> D[Analog Front End
Low Noise Preamplifier] D --> E[ADC 14 bit 60 Hz] E --> F[Image Signal Processor
Non Uniformity Correction] F --> G[Perception CNN
YOLOv8 Thermal] G --> H[Sensor Fusion ECU
Radar plus Camera plus Lidar] H --> I[Decision Layer
AEB or FCW or LKA] I --> J[Actuators
Brake or Steer or HUD Alert] K[IR Emitter LED 940 nm DMS] -->|NIR Flood| L[Driver Face] L -->|Reflected NIR| M[Driver Facing Camera CMOS] M --> N[DMS Algorithm EAR plus Gaze] N --> H ``` ## 4.2 IR Sensor Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD IR[Infrared Sensors] --> A[Thermal Detectors] IR --> B[Quantum Detectors] IR --> C[Active NIR 850 to 940 nm] A --> A1[Thermopile] A --> A2[Bolometer and Microbolometer] A --> A3[Pyroelectric] B --> B1[Photoconductive PbS MCT] B --> B2[Photovoltaic InGaAs InSb] B --> B3[QWIP] C --> C1[IR LED 850 nm] C --> C2[VCSEL 940 nm] C --> C3[Active DMS Flood] A2 --> ADAS1[LWIR Night Vision 8 to 14 um] A2 --> ADAS2[Occupant and CPD Detection] B2 --> ADAS3[SWIR Fog Penetration] C2 --> ADAS4[Driver Monitoring DMS] C2 --> ADAS5[Gesture and Cabin Sensing] ``` ## 4.3 ADAS Functional Mapping with IR ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Perceive[Perception Layer] P1[Front LWIR Camera] P2[In Cabin NIR Camera] P3[Side NIR Camera BSD] end subgraph Process[Processing Layer] Q1[Pedestrian Detection CNN] Q2[Drowsiness EAR Algorithm] Q3[Cross Traffic Tracker] end subgraph Decide[Decision Layer] R1[AEB Trigger] R2[DMS Warning Sound and Haptic] R3[Side Alert] end subgraph Act[Actuation Layer] S1[Hydraulic Brake Booster] S2[Steering Rack EPS] S3[Infotainment HUD] end P1 --> Q1 P2 --> Q2 P3 --> Q3 Q1 --> R1 Q2 --> R2 Q3 --> R3 R1 --> S1 R1 --> S2 R2 --> S3 R3 --> S3 ``` ## 4.4 Wavelength Allocation Diagram (ASCII-Equivalent via Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Visible V[0.4 to 0.7 um RGB] end subgraph NIR N1[0.75 to 1.4 um Active DMS] end subgraph SWIR S[1.4 to 3 um InGaAs] end subgraph MWIR M[3 to 8 um Hot Object Exhaust] end subgraph LWIR L[8 to 14 um Microbolometer Night Vision] end Visible --> NIR NIR --> SWIR SWIR --> MWIR MWIR --> LWIR ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper Set A] **Define the term "Emissivity" in the context of infrared radiation. Why is a pedestrian's skin a near-perfect IR emitter, while polished metal is a poor emitter?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Emissivity $\varepsilon$ is the ratio of the thermal radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature, given by $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. **Reason — Pedestrian's skin (1 mark):** Human skin has $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$ because it is an organic, opaque, and rough surface that approximates blackbody behaviour (high absorption → high emission per Kirchhoff's law). **Reason — Polished metal (1 mark):** Polished metal has $\varepsilon \approx 0.02$–$0.10$ because its high electrical conductivity produces strong reflection of incident IR radiation; per Kirchhoff's law of thermal equilibrium, low absorption implies low emission. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester] **List the two main classifications of IR detectors and give one example of each. State one advantage of each type.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Classification 1 (1 mark):** **Thermal detectors** — e.g., microbolometer (FLIR Boson). *Advantage:* Wavelength-independent response; uncooled operation enables low-cost automotive use. **Classification 2 (1 mark):** **Quantum (photonic) detectors** — e.g., HgCdTe (MCT) photodiode. *Advantage:* Higher sensitivity and faster response time, suitable for high-frame-rate imaging. **Bonus point (1 mark):** A third class — active NIR — uses GaAs LEDs (850 nm) and CMOS sensors for DMS. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — ESE Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU End Semester — July 2024 (Expected), Module 2]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of an **infrared-based night vision system (NVS)** used in ADAS for pedestrian detection. Explain the role of the microbolometer detector. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using **Wien's displacement law** and **Stefan-Boltzmann law**, compute the peak emission wavelength and the radiated power per unit area from: 1. A pedestrian's skin at $T = 305 \; \text{K}$ with $\varepsilon = 0.98$. 2. A vehicle tyre at $T = 350 \; \text{K}$ with $\varepsilon = 0.95$. Comment on which IR band (NIR, MWIR, LWIR) is best suited to detect each. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (Question A) **(a) Working Principle of IR NVS — 7 marks** 1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Scene (target) → IR-transparent Germanium lens → Microbolometer FPA (640×480 VOx) → Analog readout circuit → ADC → Image signal processor (with non-uniformity correction) → Object detection CNN → ECU warning / HUD display. 2. **Working in stages (3 marks):** - The thermal energy radiated by the pedestrian (8–14 µm) is focused by the Germanium lens onto the focal plane array. - Each microbolometer pixel absorbs IR, causing a small temperature rise (a few mK). - The bolometer's electrical resistance changes proportionally ($\Delta R / R = \alpha \Delta T$). - The change in resistance is read out as a voltage variation per pixel. - The image processor converts this into a thermal image, which a CNN interprets to identify pedestrians. 3. **Role of the microbolometer (2 marks):** - **Wavelength-independent** (senses total heating, not photon count), enabling broadband LWIR sensitivity. - **Uncooled operation** (vanadium-oxide film) simplifies packaging and reduces cost vs. cooled MCT sensors. - Typical NETD ≈ 30–50 mK is sufficient to resolve a pedestrian in ambient night conditions. - High thermal contrast between human body (~305 K) and surroundings (~275 K night road) makes the target stand out. --- **(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks** **Part (1): Pedestrian's skin at 305 K** *Step 1 — Peak wavelength (Wien's Law):* $$ \lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.8978 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}}{305 \; \text{K}} = 9.50 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m} = 9.50 \; \mu\text{m} $$ This lies in the **LWIR band (8–14 µm)**. **(2 marks)** *Step 2 — Radiated power per unit area (Stefan–Boltzmann):* $$ P/A = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4 = 0.98 \times (5.6704 \times 10^{-8}) \times (305)^4 $$ $$ (305)^4 = 8.654 \times 10^9 $$ $$ P/A = 0.98 \times 5.6704 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.654 \times 10^9 $$ $$ P/A = 0.98 \times 490.6 = 480.8 \; \text{W/m}^2 $$ **(2 marks)** — $P/A \approx 480.8 \; \text{W/m}^2$. **Part (2): Tyre at 350 K** *Step 1 — Peak wavelength:* $$ \lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.8978 \times 10^{-3}}{350} = 8.28 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m} = 8.28 \; \mu\text{m} $$ This is at the **boundary between MWIR and LWIR**, best captured by LWIR with a slight MWIR overlap. **(1 mark)** *Step 2 — Radiated power per unit area:* $$ (350)^2 = 122500, \quad (350)^4 = 1.5006 \times 10^{10} $$ $$ P/A = 0.95 \times 5.6704 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.5006 \times 10^{10} $$ $$ P/A = 0.95 \times 850.8 = 808.3 \; \text{W/m}^2 $$ **(1 mark)** — $P/A \approx 808.3 \; \text{W/m}^2$. **Part (3) — Band Selection Comment (1 mark):** - **Pedestrian** → peak at 9.5 µm → **LWIR** microbolometer camera is best suited (also most practical since LWIR falls in the 8–14 µm atmospheric window). - **Tyre** → peak at 8.28 µm → still **LWIR** works, though a cooled MWIR camera (3–5 µm) could also be used for higher signal-to-noise. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the difference between **active and passive infrared systems** in ADAS. Give two examples of ADAS functions for each type, with their operating wavelength. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A forward-facing **LWIR microbolometer** has the following specifications: - $D^* = 1 \times 10^{8} \; \text{cm·Hz}^{1/2}/\text{W}$ - Detector area $A_d = 25 \times 25 \; \mu\text{m}^2$ - Optical $F$-number = 1.2 - $T_\text{scene} = 300 \; \text{K}$, $\varepsilon = 0.95$, $\tau_o = 0.80$ - Bandwidth $\Delta f = 30 \; \text{Hz}$ **Calculate the NETD** in millikelvin. Comment on whether the sensor is suitable for pedestrian detection at night. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (Question B) **(a) Active vs. Passive IR — 7 marks** 1. **Definition difference (2 marks):** - **Passive IR (PIR):** Detects naturally emitted thermal radiation from the target (no external illumination). Operates in the LWIR/MWIR band. - **Active IR (AIR):** Onboard IR source (LED/VCSEL) emits IR toward the scene; detector captures the reflected radiation. Operates in the NIR band (0.85–0.94 µm). 2. **Passive IR examples (2.5 marks):** - **Night Vision System (NVS):** LWIR (8–14 µm) microbolometer detects pedestrians at 100–250 m beyond headlight range. - **Occupant / Child Presence Detection (CPD):** LWIR sensor in headliner monitors cabin temperature distribution; required by Euro NCAP. 3. **Active IR examples (2.5 marks):** - **Driver Monitoring System (DMS):** NIR LED (940 nm) floods the driver's face; CMOS sensor tracks eye closure and gaze. - **Gesture Recognition:** NIR camera + Time-of-Flight pattern projector for cabin HMI. --- **(b) NETD Calculation — 7 marks** *Step 1 — State formula:* $$ \text{NETD} = \frac{4 F^2 k_B T^2}{\varepsilon \, \tau_o D^*} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta f}{A_d}} $$ *Step 2 — Numerator:* $$ 4 F^2 k_B T^2 = 4 \times (1.2)^2 \times 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \times (300)^2 $$ $$ = 4 \times 1.44 \times 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \times 90000 $$ $$ = 4 \times 1.44 \times 1.2425 \times 10^{-18} $$ $$ = 7.157 \times 10^{-18} \; \text{J/m}^2 $$ **(2 marks)** *Step 3 — Denominator:* $$ \varepsilon \, \tau_o D^* = 0.95 \times 0.80 \times 1 \times 10^{8} = 7.6 \times 10^{7} \; \text{cm·Hz}^{1/2}/\text{W·m}^2 $$ *Step 4 — Geometric factor:* $$ \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta f}{A_d}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{30}{625 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{4.8 \times 10^{10}} = 2.191 \times 10^5 \; \text{Hz}^{1/2}/\text{m} $$ **(1 mark)** *Step 5 — Final NETD:* $$ \text{NETD} = \frac{7.157 \times 10^{-18}}{7.6 \times 10^{7}} \times 2.191 \times 10^5 $$ $$ = 9.417 \times 10^{-26} \times 2.191 \times 10^5 $$ $$ = 2.064 \times 10^{-20} \; \text{K (SI)} $$ **(2 marks)** *Step 6 — Convert to mK:* $$ \text{NETD} \approx 20.6 \; \text{mK} $$ **(1 mark)** *Step 7 — Comment (1 mark):* Since the human–road thermal contrast at night is typically 5–30 K (i.e., 5000–30000 mK), and NETD ≈ 20.6 mK, the signal-to-thermal-noise ratio is **> 240×**, making the sensor **highly suitable** for pedestrian detection in night-time ADAS applications. ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting units in $(T)^4$ computation** — always show the squared-then-squared expansion. > 2. **Confusing $\varepsilon$ with reflectance $\rho$** — Kirchhoff's law applies at thermal equilibrium; a polished bumper has low $\varepsilon$ but high $\rho$. > 3. **Mis-stating wavelength bands** — students often write "infrared" generically. KTU expects explicit sub-bands (NIR/SWIR/MWIR/LWIR). > 4. **Skipping the conversion to mK** — examiners look for unit awareness; raw SI values lose partial credit. > 5. **In the long-answer, omitting the block diagram** — a 1-mark-losing omission if not drawn. > 6. **Confusing "emissivity" with "transmissivity"** — three distinct terms exist ($\varepsilon$, $\rho$, $\tau$) with $\varepsilon + \rho + \tau = 1$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **IR spectrum spans 0.7 µm to 1000 µm**, divided into NIR / SWIR / MWIR / LWIR / FIR. - [ ] **Pedestrians (305 K) peak at ~9.5 µm** → LWIR is the automotive sweet spot. - [ ] **Stefan-Boltzmann:** $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ (fourth-power sensitivity). - [ ] **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = b / T$ (key to band selection). - [ ] **Planck's Law** gives the spectral radiance $B_\lambda$ of a blackbody. - [ ] **Two main detector families:** Thermal (slow, broadband, uncooled) vs Quantum (fast, narrow-band, often cooled). - [ ] **Microbolometer** dominates automotive LWIR (e.g., FLIR Boson, Seek Thermal). - [ ] **Active NIR @ 850/940 nm** is the standard for Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS). - [ ] **NETD** is the key sensitivity metric; values < 50 mK are excellent for ADAS. - [ ] **IR applications in ADAS:** Night Vision, DMS, CPD, BSD, ACC augmentation, LKA back-up. - [ ] **Sensor fusion:** IR complements Radar (range), Lidar (precision), and Camera (classification). - [ ] **Fog penetration:** LWIR > NIR due to Mie scattering physics — choose LWIR in poor visibility. - [ ] **Euro NCAP mandates DMS and CPD** from 2025–2026 — IR sensors are *required*, not optional. - [ ] **Key Tier-1 suppliers:** FLIR/Teledyne, Valeo, Veoneer, Bosch, Continental, Aptiv. - [ ] **Pitfall #1:** Confusing $b$ (Wien's constant) with $k_B$ (Boltzmann's constant) — they are not interchangeable. - [ ] **Pitfall #2:** Always include the emissivity correction when computing real radiated power. - [ ] **Pitfall #3:** Horizontal pipeline (camera → ECU → actuator) is a 2-mark block-diagram question — practice drawing it.

topics.title: Sensor Data Fusion - Introduction to Sensor Data Fusion
Techniques for Combining Data from Different Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Combining Data from Different Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sensor Data Fusion** is the formal process of integrating data from multiple heterogeneous sensors (Cameras, RADAR, LIDAR, Ultrasonic, IMU, GPS) to produce a unified, accurate, consistent, and complete representation of the vehicle's operational environment that is superior to what could be achieved using any individual sensor acting alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (JDL Model Aligned):** Sensor fusion is a multi-level, multi-faceted process dealing with the automatic detection, association, correlation, estimation, and combination of data and information from multiple single-source sensors for the purpose of achieving refined position, identity, and threat assessment, along with comprehensive and timely situation and threat assessment.

The Joint Directors of Laboratories (JDL) Data Fusion Model classifies fusion into five levels:
- **Level 0** – Source Pre-Processing (Signal/Feature Assessment)
- **Level 1** – Object Refinement (Tracking & Identity)
- **Level 2** – Situation Refinement
- **Level 3** – Threat/Impact Refinement
- **Level 4** – Process Refinement (Resource Management)

For an Autonomous Vehicle, the practical focus lies primarily on **Level 1 Object Refinement** and **Level 2 Situation Refinement**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Simple Analogy: "The Cocktail Party Problem"**
> Imagine you are at a noisy party trying to hear a friend. You use:
> - 👁️ **Your Eyes** to see their lips move (Camera/Visual sensor)
> - 👂 **Your Ears** to hear the pitch of their voice (Audio/RADAR sensor)
> - 🧠 **Your Brain** to merge these noisy inputs and decide what they said
>
> Neither eyes nor ears alone is enough. By **fusing** them, you understand the message. An Autonomous Vehicle does exactly this — the *perception stack* acts as the "brain" that fuses the noisy, sometimes contradictory, sensor streams into a coherent understanding of the road.

### 1.3 Why Sensor Fusion is Non-Negotiable in ADAS

| Sensor Modality | Strength | Weakness | Fusion Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Camera** | High semantic resolution, lane/sign classification | Poor depth, weather sensitive | Receives depth from RADAR |
| **RADAR** | Accurate radial velocity (Doppler), all-weather | Low angular resolution | Receives object class from Camera |
| **LIDAR** | Precise 3D geometry (cm accuracy) | Expensive, sparse at long range | Confirms object existence with Camera |
| **Ultrasonic** | Cheap, short-range (< 5 m) | Only proximity, no speed at distance | Backs up parking fusion |
| **IMU/GPS** | Localization, ego-motion | Drift (IMU), tunnels (GPS) | Tightly coupled for SLAM |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in Fusion

- **Precision:** Repeatability of measurement (σ in **meters** or **radians**)
- **Accuracy:** Closeness to ground truth (Bias in **m**)
- **Confidence / Certainty:** Probability output (0 to 1)
- **Latency:** End-to-end delay in **milliseconds (ms)** — KTU typical threshold: **< 100 ms** for L2 ADAS, **< 50 ms** for L4
- **Robustness:** Graceful degradation when a sensor fails (fault tolerance)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Sensor Fusion Architectures

#### A. By Data Combination Stage

**1) Data-Level (Low-Level / Pixel-Level) Fusion**
- Combines raw data *before* any feature extraction.
- Requires sensors of the **same modality** (e.g., two cameras in stereo).
- Preserves maximum information but is bandwidth-intensive and sensitive to noise.

**2) Feature-Level (Mid-Level) Fusion**
- Each sensor extracts features independently (edges from camera, point clusters from LIDAR), then these feature vectors are concatenated/merged.
- Most commonly used in modern deep-learning perception (e.g., Frustum PointNets combining 2D image features with 3D point clouds).

**3) Decision-Level (High-Level / Late Fusion) Fusion**
- Each sensor makes an independent decision (e.g., "object at 30 m"). The fusion module arbitrates between these decisions.
- Robust to single-sensor failure but loses cross-modal correlation.

#### B. By System Topology

**1) Centralized Fusion** — All raw data sent to a central processor. High bandwidth, high accuracy, low fault tolerance.

**2) Distributed Fusion** — Each sensor has a local processor producing local estimates. Only estimates are sent to a central node. Lower bandwidth, higher fault tolerance, slightly suboptimal accuracy.

**3) Hierarchical (Hybrid) Fusion** — A combination of centralized and distributed. Industry standard for ADAS.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundations of Fusion

#### 2.2.1 The Bayesian Fusion Framework

Bayes' theorem is the bedrock of probabilistic sensor fusion.

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

Where:
- $P(H \vert E)$ = Posterior probability of Hypothesis $H$ given Evidence $E$
- $P(E \vert H)$ = Likelihood — how well evidence $E$ supports hypothesis $H$
- $P(H)$ = Prior probability of $H$ before seeing $E$
- $P(E)$ = Marginal probability of evidence

> [!NOTE]
> **In an ADAS context:** Hypothesis $H$ might be "Pedestrian is at (x, y)". Each sensor provides Evidence $E_i$. Multi-sensor fusion applies Bayes sequentially to update the posterior with each new sensor reading.

#### 2.2.2 Weighted Average (Simplest Fusion)

When two redundant sensors measure the same quantity (e.g., two wheel-odometry units):

$$\hat{x} = w_1 \cdot x_1 + w_2 \cdot x_2 \quad \text{where} \quad w_1 + w_2 = 1$$

Optimal weights are inversely proportional to the **measurement noise covariance**:

$$w_i = \frac{1/\sigma_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1/\sigma_j^2}$$

#### 2.2.3 Kalman Filter (KF) — The Workhorse

The Kalman Filter is a **recursive linear Bayesian estimator** that optimally fuses a **prediction** (from a motion model) with a **measurement update** (from a sensor).

It has two steps:

**Prediction Step:**
$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B_k u_k$$
$$P_{k\vert k-1} = F_k P_{k-1\vert k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$

**Update Step:**
$$K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$$
$$P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\vert k-1}$$

Where:
- $\hat{x}$ = State estimate
- $P$ = State covariance (uncertainty)
- $F$ = State transition matrix
- $H$ = Measurement matrix
- $Q$ = Process noise covariance
- $R$ = Measurement noise covariance
- $K$ = Kalman Gain (weight given to measurement vs prediction)
- $z$ = Measurement vector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $K_k$ matters:** When $R_k \to 0$ (sensor very accurate), $K_k \to 1$ → trust measurement fully. When $R_k \to \infty$ (sensor unreliable), $K_k \to 0$ → trust prediction only. This **automatic gain weighting** is the magic of fusion.

#### 2.2.4 Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for Nonlinear Systems

EKF linearizes the nonlinear system around the current estimate using the Jacobian matrix:

$$F_k = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right\vert_{\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_k}, \quad H_k = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right\vert_{\hat{x}_{k}}$$

Where $f$ is the nonlinear state-transition and $h$ is the nonlinear measurement function.

#### 2.2.5 Particle Filter (PF) — Non-parametric, Highly Nonlinear

Represents the posterior probability density by a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles):

$$p(x_k \vert z_{1:k}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_k^{(i)} \delta(x_k - x_k^{(i)})$$

The Sequential Importance Resampling (SIR) algorithm:
1. **Predict:** Sample particles from proposal distribution $x_k^{(i)} \sim q(x_k \vert x_{k-1}^{(i)}, z_k)$
2. **Weight:** $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_k \vert x_k^{(i)})$
3. **Resample:** Discard low-weight particles, duplicate high-weight ones

#### 2.2.6 Dempster-Shafer Evidence Theory

Used when sensor confidence is uncertain. It assigns a *belief mass* $m(A)$ to each subset $A$ of the frame of discernment $\Theta$, satisfying:

$$\sum_{A \subseteq \Theta} m(A) = 1$$

Combination rule (Dempster's rule of combination):
$$m_{1 \oplus 2}(C) = \frac{1}{1-K} \sum_{A \cap B = C} m_1(A) \cdot m_2(B)$$

Where $K = \sum_{A \cap B = \emptyset} m_1(A) \cdot m_2(B)$ is the conflict coefficient.

#### 2.2.7 Complementary Filter

A lightweight, computationally cheap alternative to KF, used primarily for **IMU + orientation sensor fusion**:

$$\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot (\hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{acc,k}$$

Where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is the filter coefficient (typically 0.95–0.98).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Name | Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Bayes' Theorem | Probabilistic fusion | dimensionless |
| $w_i = \frac{1/\sigma_i^2}{\sum_j 1/\sigma_j^2}$ | Optimal Weight | Redundant sensor fusion | dimensionless |
| $K = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | Measurement vs prediction | dimensionless |
| $x_{k\vert k} = x_{k\vert k-1} + K(z_k - H x_{k\vert k-1})$ | State Update | KF state estimation | state units (m, m/s) |
| $P_{k\vert k} = (I - K H) P_{k\vert k-1}$ | Covariance Update | Uncertainty reduction | variance units |
| $F_k = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ | Jacobian (State) | EKF linearization | dimensionless |
| $N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w^{(i)})^2}$ | Effective Sample Size | Particle Filter quality | count |
| $\alpha$ | Complementary Coefficient | IMU fusion | dimensionless |
| $K = \sum_{A \cap B = \emptyset} m_1(A) m_2(B)$ | Conflict Coefficient | Dempster-Shafer | $[0,1]$ |

### 2.4 Why Engineers Choose a Given Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Fusion Method |
|---|---|
| Two redundant odometers | Weighted Average |
| GPS + IMU (Linear) | **Kalman Filter** |
| GPS + IMU + Vehicle dynamics (Nonlinear) | **EKF / UKF** |
| LIDAR localization (Highly nonlinear, non-Gaussian) | **Particle Filter** |
| Camera + RADAR object confidence | **Dempster-Shafer** or Bayesian |
| IMU Gyro + Accelerometer orientation | **Complementary Filter** |
| Multi-modal DL detection | **Feature-level fusion (late neural fusion)** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Kalman Filter Update Equations

We start with the linear dynamic system model:

$$x_k = F_k x_{k-1} + B_k u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$$
$$z_k = H_k x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$$

**Goal:** Derive the optimal linear estimator $\hat{x}_{k \vert k}$ that minimizes the a posteriori error covariance $P_{k \vert k} = E[(x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k})(x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k})^T]$.

#### Step 1: Define the Innovation (Measurement Residual)

The measurement residual is the difference between the actual measurement and the predicted measurement:

$$\nu_k = z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$$

This term quantifies how much "new information" the sensor provides beyond the prediction.

#### Step 2: Express the A Posteriori State Estimate as a Linear Combination

We postulate the optimal estimator is a linear blend of the prior estimate and the innovation:

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \nu_k = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$$

Where $K_k$ is the **Kalman Gain** to be determined.

#### Step 3: Compute the A Posteriori Error

The estimation error is:

$$e_k = x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k} = x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} - K_k (H_k x_k + v_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$$

$$e_k = (I - K_k H_k)(x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) - K_k v_k$$

#### Step 4: Substitute and Compute the A Posteriori Error Covariance

$$P_{k \vert k} = E[e_k e_k^T]$$

Assuming $w_k, v_k$ are uncorrelated and zero-mean:

$$P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1} (I - K_k H_k)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

#### Step 5: Minimize the Trace of $P_{k \vert k}$

To find the optimal $K_k$, take the derivative of $\text{tr}(P_{k \vert k})$ with respect to $K_k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(P_{k \vert k})}{\partial K_k} = -2 (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + 2 K_k R_k = 0$$

Solving:

$$P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T - K_k H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + K_k R_k = 0$$

$$K_k (H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k) = P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T$$

$$\boxed{K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}}$$

#### Step 6: Substitute Back to Get Joseph Form

Plugging $K_k$ back into $P_{k \vert k}$ yields the simplified update:

$$\boxed{P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The Kalman Filter is provably **optimal** (in the minimum mean-square-error sense) only for **linear Gaussian** systems. For nonlinear or non-Gaussian scenarios, EKF, UKF, or Particle Filters are used.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: KF Variants Comparison

| Property | Kalman Filter (KF) | Extended KF (EKF) | Unscented KF (UKF) | Particle Filter (PF) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **System Type** | Linear | Mildly Nonlinear | Nonlinear | Highly Nonlinear, Non-Gaussian |
| **Distribution** | Gaussian | Gaussian (approx.) | Gaussian (approx.) | Arbitrary |
| **Linearization** | None (exact) | 1st-order Taylor | Deterministic sigma points | Monte Carlo sampling |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^3)$ | $O(N \cdot n)$ |
| **Accuracy** | Optimal | Can diverge | High (2nd–3rd order) | Asymptotically exact |
| **AD/AV Use Case** | INS/GPS | Vehicle tracking | GPS/INS tight coupling | LIDAR SLAM |
| **Jacobian Needed?** | No | Yes | No | No |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fusion Level Comparison

| Property | Data-Level | Feature-Level | Decision-Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information Preserved** | Maximum | Moderate | Minimum |
| **Bandwidth** | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Heterogeneous Sensors** | Hard (needs alignment) | Easy | Very Easy |
| **Typical Use in AV** | Stereo Camera | Camera + LIDAR (PointPainting) | RADAR + Camera (AEB) |
| **Example Algorithm** | Image stitching | PointPainting, Frustum PointNet | Independent classifiers + voting |

### 3.4 Sensor Noise Covariance Values (Typical Industry Benchmarks)

| Sensor | Measurement Type | Typical $\sigma$ (Std. Dev.) | Typical $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Automotive RADAR | Range | **0.2 m** | $0.04 \, m^2$ |
| Automotive RADAR | Radial Velocity | **0.1 m/s** | $0.01 \, (m/s)^2$ |
| Camera (Mono) | Lateral Position | **0.5 px → 0.3 m at 50 m** | $0.09 \, m^2$ |
| LIDAR | Range | **0.02 m (2 cm)** | $4 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$ |
| GPS (Civilian) | Position | **2.5 m horizontal** | $6.25 \, m^2$ |
| IMU (MEMS) | Acceleration Bias | **0.01 m/s²** | $10^{-4} \, (m/s^2)^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule:** Always set $R$ higher than the manufacturer's nominal $\sigma$ by 2–5× to account for unmodeled effects (multipath, vibration, temperature).

### 3.5 Worked Example: Two-Sensor Weighted Fusion

A vehicle is localized at $x$ m by two redundant GPS receivers.
- Sensor 1: $x_1 = 50.2$ m, $\sigma_1 = 1.0$ m
- Sensor 2: $x_2 = 49.5$ m, $\sigma_2 = 0.5$ m

**Step 1:** Compute inverse variances.

$$1/\sigma_1^2 = 1/1.0^2 = 1.0, \quad 1/\sigma_2^2 = 1/0.5^2 = 4.0$$

**Step 2:** Sum of inverse variances.

$$S = 1.0 + 4.0 = 5.0$$

**Step 3:** Compute optimal weights.

$$w_1 = 1.0 / 5.0 = 0.20, \quad w_2 = 4.0 / 5.0 = 0.80$$

**Step 4:** Compute fused estimate.

$$\hat{x} = 0.20 \cdot 50.2 + 0.80 \cdot 49.5 = 10.04 + 39.60 = 49.64 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5:** Compute fused standard deviation.

$$\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{1/S} = \sqrt{1/5.0} \approx 0.447 \, \text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice the fused estimate (49.64 m) is much closer to Sensor 2 (more accurate), and the fused uncertainty (0.447 m) is *less* than either individual sensor's uncertainty. This is the **super-resolution** property of fusion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Centralized Sensor Fusion Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Camera Array] --> RAW[Raw Data Buffer]
    A2[Long Range RADAR] --> RAW
    A3[Short Range RADAR] --> RAW
    A4[LIDAR Unit] --> RAW
    A5[Ultrasonic Sensors] --> RAW
    A6[IMU and GPS] --> RAW

    RAW --> PRE[Synchronization and Time Stamping]
    PRE --> CAL[Coordinate Frame Calibration]

    CAL --> FUS[Centralized Fusion Engine]
    FUS --> KF1[Kalman Filter Tracker]
    FUS --> EKF[Extended Kalman Filter]
    FUS --> PF[Particle Filter Module]
    FUS --> BAY[Bayesian Probability Combiner]

    KF1 --> OBJ[Object List with Covariance]
    EKF --> OBJ
    PF --> OBJ
    BAY --> OBJ

    OBJ --> OUT[Environment Model for Planning]

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A3 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A4 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A5 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A6 fill:#e1f5ff
    style FUS fill:#ffe1e1
    style OUT fill:#d4f5d4
```

### 4.2 Distributed Fusion Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SensorNode1
        B1[Camera] --> P1[Local Processor 1]
        P1 --> EST1[Local Estimate 1]
    end

    subgraph SensorNode2
        B2[RADAR] --> P2[Local Processor 2]
        P2 --> EST2[Local Estimate 2]
    end

    subgraph SensorNode3
        B3[LIDAR] --> P3[Local Processor 3]
        P3 --> EST3[Local Estimate 3]
    end

    EST1 --> NET[Vehicle Network BUS or Ethernet]
    EST2 --> NET
    EST3 --> NET

    NET --> FUSM[Central Fusion Module]
    FUSM --> GLOBAL[Global Track and State Estimate]

    style EST1 fill:#fff4cc
    style EST2 fill:#fff4cc
    style EST3 fill:#fff4cc
    style FUSM fill:#ffe1e1
    style GLOBAL fill:#d4f5d4
```

### 4.3 Kalman Filter Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Start Cycle k] --> PR1[Predict State xhat k given k minus 1]
    PR2[Predict Covariance P k given k minus 1] --> PR1
    PR1 --> MEAS{New Sensor Measurement zk Available?}
    MEAS -->|No| OUT1[Output xhat and P and Wait]
    MEAS -->|Yes| KG[Compute Kalman Gain Kk]
    KG --> UP[Update State xhat k given k]
    UP --> UPC[Update Covariance P k given k]
    UPC --> OUT2[Output Fused Estimate]
    OUT1 --> NEXT[Increment k and Loop]
    OUT2 --> NEXT

    style PR1 fill:#cce5ff
    style KG fill:#ffe1e1
    style UP fill:#d4f5d4
    style UPC fill:#d4f5d4
```

### 4.4 Fusion Level Hierarchy (Data → Feature → Decision)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Camera Raw Pixels] --> DL[Data Level Fusion]
    S2[Stereo Camera Raw Pixels] --> DL
    DL --> F1[Combined Image with Depth]

    F1 --> FL[Feature Level Fusion]
    S3[LIDAR Point Cloud] --> FL
    FL --> F2[Object Features in Unified Space]

    F2 --> DCL[Decision Level Fusion]
    S4[RADAR Track List] --> DCL
    S5[Classifier Outputs] --> DCL
    DCL --> F3[Final Object Hypothesis with Confidence]

    style FL fill:#fff4cc
    style DCL fill:#ffe1e1
    style F3 fill:#d4f5d4
```

### 4.5 Sensor Fusion Decision Tree (Which Algorithm to Choose?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the system linear and Gaussian?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the state space small 4-6 dimensions?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is the nonlinearity mild?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| EKF[Use Extended Kalman Filter]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Can you afford 1000 to 5000 particles?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| PF[Use Particle Filter]
    Q4 -->|No| UKF[Use Unscented Kalman Filter]
    Q2 -->|Yes| KF[Use Standard Kalman Filter]
    Q2 -->|No| UKF2[Use Unscented Kalman Filter]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

Define sensor data fusion. List any **four advantages** of combining data from multiple sensors in an autonomous vehicle.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Sensor data fusion is the process of integrating data from multiple heterogeneous sensors to produce a unified, accurate, and complete representation of the environment that is more informative than any individual sensor.

**Four Advantages (½ mark each):**

1. **Improved Accuracy** — Combining sensors reduces random noise and bias; precision exceeds that of the best single sensor.
2. **Increased Robustness / Fault Tolerance** — Failure of one sensor does not collapse the perception; redundancy enables graceful degradation.
3. **Extended Spatial and Temporal Coverage** — Cameras provide lateral resolution, RADAR provides long-range all-weather coverage, LIDAR provides 3D geometry.
4. **Reduced Uncertainty** — Mathematical combination (e.g., Kalman Filter) provably reduces the covariance of the state estimate below any single-sensor level.
5. **Enhanced Object Classification** — Cross-modal evidence (e.g., camera shape + RADAR Doppler) yields higher classification confidence.

> **Marking Tip:** Definition = 1M, listing any 4 advantages with **one-line explanation** = 0.5M each. Bare lists without explanation fetch only 0.25M per point.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

Differentiate between **data-level, feature-level, and decision-level sensor fusion** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark per row):**

| Level | When fusion occurs | Information preserved | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data-Level** | Before any feature extraction | Maximum (raw data) | Stereo camera image + depth map fusion |
| **Feature-Level** | After individual feature extraction | Moderate | Camera 2D bounding boxes concatenated with LIDAR point cloud features (Frustum PointNets) |
| **Decision-Level** | After each sensor makes an independent decision | Minimum | Independent RADAR + Camera classifiers; final vote via Bayesian combination |

> **Marking Tip:** A clean table scores full marks. Sketches/diagrams earn bonus but are not mandatory at 3-Mark depth.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Aligned to Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **centralized and distributed sensor fusion architectures** for an autonomous vehicle. Compare them on **six key parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Kalman Filter update equations** starting from the linear state-space model. Clearly state the role of the **Kalman Gain** $K_k$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Architectures (7 Marks)

**Centralized Fusion (3 Marks):**

In a centralized architecture, all sensors (Camera, RADAR, LIDAR, IMU, GPS) feed their **raw measurements** into a single central processing unit. The central unit performs time synchronization, coordinate frame calibration, and runs the fusion algorithm.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
Sensors → Raw Data Bus → Central Processor → Fused Output
   (Cam, RADAR, LIDAR, IMU, GPS)  (CPU/GPU)      (Tracks, Pose)
```

**Distributed Fusion (2 Marks):**

In a distributed architecture, each sensor has a **local processor** that produces a local state estimate (e.g., local Kalman Filter output). These estimates, along with their covariances, are transmitted to a central fusion node via a vehicle network (CAN, Ethernet, FlexRay). The central node fuses the estimates.

**Comparison (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | Centralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth required | Very High | Low (only estimates) |
| Fault tolerance | Low (single point of failure) | High |
| Accuracy | Highest (no info loss) | Slightly lower |
| Computational load on central CPU | Very High | Low |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent |
| Latency | Lowest | Higher (network delay) |

---

#### Part (b) — Kalman Filter Derivation (7 Marks)

**State-Space Model (1 Mark):**

$$x_k = F_k x_{k-1} + B_k u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$$

$$z_k = H_k x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$$

**Define the Innovation (1 Mark):**

$$\nu_k = z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$$

**Posteriori State Estimate (1 Mark):**

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \nu_k$$

**Posteriori Error (1 Mark):**

$$e_k = (I - K_k H_k)(x_k - \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) - K_k v_k$$

**Minimize Trace of Covariance (2 Marks):**

Setting $\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(P_{k \vert k})}{\partial K_k} = 0$ yields:

$$K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$

$$P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1}$$

**Role of Kalman Gain $K_k$ (1 Mark):**
- $K_k$ weighs the **measurement** $z_k$ against the **prediction** $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}$.
- When sensor noise $R_k$ is small, $K_k \to I$ → trust the sensor.
- When $R_k$ is large (unreliable sensor), $K_k \to 0$ → trust the prediction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Aligned to May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bayesian approach to sensor fusion**. How is it applied to fuse RADAR and Camera detections for pedestrian recognition? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**? Derive the EKF prediction and update equations. State **two limitations** of the EKF. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Bayesian Fusion (7 Marks)

**Bayes' Theorem (2 Marks):**

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

- $P(H)$: Prior probability of the hypothesis (e.g., pedestrian at a location) before observing new evidence.
- $P(E \vert H)$: Likelihood — the probability of observing the sensor evidence $E$ assuming the hypothesis is true.
- $P(H \vert E)$: Posterior — updated belief after seeing the evidence.

**Recursive Multi-Sensor Update (2 Marks):**

For $n$ independent sensors providing evidence $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$:

$$P(H \vert E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n) \propto P(H) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i \vert H)$$

The posterior after sensor $i$ becomes the prior for sensor $i+1$ — a **recursive Bayesian update**.

**Application: RADAR + Camera Pedestrian Fusion (3 Marks):**
1. **Camera CNN** outputs $P(\text{Pedestrian} \vert \text{Image Patch})$ — high likelihood for human-shape silhouette, low for inanimate objects.
2. **RADAR** reports $P(\text{Pedestrian} \vert \text{RCS, Doppler})$ — humans have small Radar Cross-Section and slow Doppler return; vehicles have larger RCS and distinct Doppler signature.
3. **Bayesian fusion** combines both: a weak camera probability (0.4) and weak RADAR probability (0.5) can combine to a strong joint probability (≈ 0.8) **if the two detections spatially coincide** (overlapping bounding box).
4. **Odds-form (Log-Odds) Update** is computationally efficient:
$$\log \frac{P(H \vert E)}{1 - P(H \vert E)} = \log \frac{P(H)}{1 - P(H)} + \log \frac{P(E \vert H)}{1 - P(E \vert H)}$$

This additive form allows fast incremental updates as new sensor data arrives each cycle.

---

#### Part (b) — Extended Kalman Filter (7 Marks)

**Why EKF is Needed (1 Mark):**

The standard Kalman Filter assumes **linear** state transition and measurement models. Most real-world vehicle systems are nonlinear (e.g., polar-to-Cartesian RADAR, rotating ego-vehicle). The EKF handles mild nonlinearities via **first-order Taylor series linearization**.

**Nonlinear Model (1 Mark):**

$$x_k = f(x_{k-1}, u_k) + w_k$$
$$z_k = h(x_k) + v_k$$

**Jacobians (2 Marks):**

$$F_k = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right\vert_{\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_k} \quad H_k = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right\vert_{\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}}$$

**EKF Prediction (1 Mark):**

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1}, u_k)$$
$$P_{k \vert k-1} = F_k P_{k-1 \vert k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$

**EKF Update (1 Mark):**

$$K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}))$$
$$P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1}$$

**Two Limitations of EKF (1 Mark):**

1. **First-Order Approximation Error:** Linearization via Jacobian introduces error proportional to the square of the operating distance from the linearization point; highly nonlinear systems can cause **filter divergence**.
2. **Jacobian Computation:** Requires analytically differentiable models, which is impossible for black-box deep-learning perception models. Also, the computation of Jacobians is expensive for high-dimensional state spaces.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $Q$ and $R$:** $Q$ is **process** noise (model uncertainty), $R$ is **measurement** noise (sensor uncertainty). Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Skipping the "Why" of Kalman Gain:** Simply writing the formula for $K_k$ without explaining that it *adapts* to sensor reliability is a **2-mark loss**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Independence Assumption:** Multi-sensor Bayesian fusion assumes conditionally independent evidence. Failing to state this is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing Up EKF with KF:** EKF uses Jacobians $F_k, H_k$ — students often forget to differentiate the nonlinear functions.
> 5. **Wrong Notation:** Use $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$ for *prediction* and $\hat{x}_{k \vert k}$ for *updated* estimate. Reversing these loses marks.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-Mark questions:** Part (a) of Part B questions *expect* a block diagram. Without it, expect a **1–2 mark penalty**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Yield Rapid Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam**

- **Sensor Fusion Definition:** Multi-sensor integration to produce a more accurate, robust environment model than any single sensor.
- **Why fuse?** Accuracy ↑, Robustness ↑, Uncertainty ↓, Coverage ↑, Classification ↑.
- **Three Levels:** Data-level (raw), Feature-level (after features), Decision-level (after decisions).
- **Three Architectures:** Centralized (raw data, high accuracy, low fault tolerance), Distributed (estimates only, low bandwidth, high fault tolerance), Hybrid (industry standard).
- **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(H \vert E) \propto P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)$ — foundation of probabilistic fusion.
- **Weighted Average:** Weights $\propto 1/\sigma^2$ — for redundant sensors.
- **Kalman Filter:** Two steps — Predict (use model) and Update (use sensor). Optimal for **linear Gaussian** systems.
- **Kalman Gain $K_k$:** Automatic weighting between prediction and measurement; high $R$ → low $K$ → trust model more.
- **$Q$ vs $R$:** $Q$ = process noise covariance; $R$ = measurement noise covariance. Tuning these is the practical art of KF.
- **EKF:** Linearizes nonlinear $f(\cdot), h(\cdot)$ via Jacobians. **Limitations:** first-order error, divergence, needs differentiable model.
- **UKF:** Uses deterministic *sigma points* to capture mean and covariance through nonlinear transforms. No Jacobians needed; more accurate than EKF for the same cost.
- **Particle Filter:** Monte-Carlo sampling; works for **highly nonlinear, non-Gaussian** systems. **Limitation:** computationally expensive; $N$ particles needed.
- **Dempster-Shafer:** Belief-mass based; handles *uncertainty* about sensor confidence via $m(A)$ and conflict $K$.
- **Complementary Filter:** Cheap high-pass + low-pass combination; perfect for **IMU orientation** estimation.
- **Industry Standards:** RADAR noise $\sigma \approx 0.2$ m, LIDAR $\sigma \approx 0.02$ m, GPS civilian $\sigma \approx 2.5$ m.
- **Latency Targets:** < 100 ms for L2 ADAS, < 50 ms for L4 autonomy.
- **JDL Fusion Levels:** 0 (pre-process) → 1 (object) → 2 (situation) → 3 (threat) → 4 (process refinement).
- **Modern Deep Learning:** PointPainting, Frustum PointNets, TransFusion — all are feature-level fusion techniques.
- **Common Exam Trick:** "Why is KF optimal?" — because it minimizes the trace of the a posteriori error covariance.

> **Best of luck for your KTU examinations! 🎓 Drive safely — and let your Kalman Filter converge.** 🚗💨

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ROS (Robot Operating System)

# ROS (Robot Operating System) — KTU 2024 Premier Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Operating System (ROS)** is an open-source, meta-operating system for robots that provides hardware abstraction, low-level device control, implementation of commonly-used functionality, message-passing between processes, and package management. It is **not a true operating system** but a flexible distributed framework (middleware) that runs on top of a conventional OS (typically Ubuntu Linux) to facilitate modular robot software development.

For the **PEMUT865 (ADAS & Autonomous Vehicles)** course, ROS is the de-facto middleware used to integrate perception, planning, control, and actuation modules in self-driving stacks such as **Autoware** and **Apollo**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office of Robots"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of ROS as the *postal service of a self-driving car*.
> - The **Master (roscore)** acts as the *post office registry* — it knows every citizen's address.
> - **Nodes** are the *citizens* (camera, LiDAR, planner, brake controller).
> - **Topics** are *mailing channels* (e.g., a TV channel broadcast).
> - **Messages** are the *letters* being delivered.
> - **Services** are *registered post* — a direct request-reply between two specific people.
> - **Actions** are *long-duration courier tasks* with feedback and the ability to cancel.
> - **Launch files** are the *morning shift schedule* — they tell who starts when.

This middleware layer decouples hardware drivers from high-level algorithms, allowing a perception algorithm written for one LiDAR to seamlessly replace another.

### 1.3 Key Architectural Metrics & Standards

- **Communication Protocol:** TCP/IP + UDP (default XMLRPC + TCPROS)
- **Default Master Port:** **11311**
- **Master URI:** `http://<hostname>:11311`
- **Coordinate Standard:** REP-103 (Standard Units of Measure and Coordinate Conventions) — SI units, right-handed axes
- **Supported OS:** Ubuntu (primary), macOS, Windows 10 (experimental)
- **Latest Stable Series:** ROS 2 Humble / Iron / Jazzy (LTS), built on **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** middleware

### 1.4 Why ROS is Critical for ADAS & Autonomous Vehicles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern autonomous stacks (e.g., **Autoware Universe**, **Apollo**, **NVIDIA DriveWorks**) use ROS 2 because of:
> 1. **Deterministic real-time communication** via DDS-QoS policies.
> 2. **Modular node architecture** matching the sensor-fusion pipeline.
> 3. **Heterogeneous hardware support** (GPU, FPGA, ECU, NPU).
> 4. **Open-source ecosystem** with reusable perception/control packages.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Levels of ROS Architecture

ROS is organized in **three hierarchical levels** as mandated by REP (ROS Enhancement Proposal) standards:

| Level | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Filesystem Level** | Packages, Manifests (`package.xml`), Message types (`.msg`), Service types (`.srv`), Launch files (`.launch`) | How the code is **organized on disk** |
| **Computation Graph Level** | Nodes, Master, Parameter Server, Topics, Services, Messages, Bags | How the processes **communicate at runtime** |
| **Community Level** | Distributions, Repositories (e.g., `github.com/ros/`, ROS-Industrial, Autoware) | How **knowledge and code** are shared globally |

### 2.2 Core Computational Graph Entities

> [!NOTE]
> **Nodes:** A node is an executable process that performs computation. In an autonomous vehicle, each subsystem (perception, localization, planning) typically runs as one or more nodes.
>
> **Master (`roscore`):** The name service that enables nodes to find and talk to each other. It runs the **Parameter Server** and the **ROS Master** daemon.
>
> **Parameter Server:** A shared, multi-variate dictionary (key-value store) accessible via network APIs. Used to store configuration (e.g., camera intrinsics: `fx=525.0, fy=525.0, cx=320.0, cy=240.0`).
>
> **Messages (`.msg`):** Strictly-typed data structures sent over topics. Defined in `msg/` directory using field types: `int8`, `float32`, `string`, `Header`, nested messages.
>
> **Topics:** Named buses over which nodes exchange messages via **publish/subscribe** semantics — anonymous, many-to-many, asynchronous.
>
> **Services (`.srv`):** Synchronous request/reply RPCs — defined by `request` and `response` parts in a `.srv` file.
>
> **Bags:** Recorded message streams for offline analysis and replay (essential for ADAS dataset annotation).

### 2.3 Communication Paradigms — The Three Pillars

| Paradigm | Pattern | Use-Case in ADAS/AV | Latency Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topic (Pub/Sub)** | Asynchronous, anonymous, many-to-many | Sensor streams (LiDAR point cloud, camera image, GNSS) | Low (streaming) |
| **Service (Client/Server)** | Synchronous, named, 1-to-1 RPC | Map-server request, calibration trigger, mode-switch | Higher (transactional) |
| **Action (Goal/Feedback/Result)** | Asynchronous with feedback, cancellable, long-duration | Lane-change execution, path-following, parking maneuver | Preemptable |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Commands & APIs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 ESE, you must memorize the following command set:**

| Command | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `roscore` | Start Master + Parameter Server + `rosout` logging | `roscore` |
| `rosrun <pkg> <node>` | Run a single node | `rosrun rviz rviz` |
| `roslaunch <pkg> <file>.launch` | Start multiple nodes from XML | `roslaunch my_pkg bringup.launch` |
| `rosnode list` / `rosnode info <n>` | List / inspect nodes | `rosnode info /camera_driver` |
| `rostopic list` / `rostopic echo <t>` | Inspect topics live | `rostopic echo /lidar/points` |
| `rostopic pub <t> <type> <args>` | Publish a message from CLI | `rostopic pub /cmd_vel geometry_msgs/Twist "linear: {x: 1.0}"` |
| `rosservice call <srv> <args>` | Invoke a service | `rosservice call /reset` |
| `rosparam set/get/load/dump` | Manipulate Parameter Server | `rosparam set /camera/fx 525.0` |
| `rosbag record <topics>` / `rosbag play <bag>` | Record/replay for offline analysis | `rosbag record -a` |
| `rviz` | 3D visualization tool | Visualize point clouds, paths, markers |
| `rqt_graph` | Visualize the node-topic graph | Debug topic wiring |
| `rosmsg show <type>` | Show message definition | `rosmsg show sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` |
| `catkin_make` / `catkin build` | Build the workspace | Compile C++/Python packages |
| `rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y` | Install dependencies | Setup phase |

### 2.5 Quality of Service (QoS) Policies — ROS 2 (Critical for AVs)

> [!NOTE]
> **DDS QoS** lets you tune communication for safety-critical AV use-cases:

| Policy | Values | AV Application |
|---|---|---|
| `reliability` | `RELIABLE` / `BEST_EFFORT` | `BEST_EFFORT` for raw camera stream; `RELIABLE` for `/cmd_brake` |
| `history` | `KEEP_LAST(n)` / `KEEP_ALL` | `KEEP_LAST(10)` for radar tracks |
| `durability` | `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` / `VOLATILE` | `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` for static map topic |
| `deadline` | Duration | Ensure `/emergency_brake` arrives within **100 ms** |
| `lifespan` | Duration | Drop stale path-planning goals |
| `liveliness` | `AUTOMATIC` / `MANUAL_BY_TOPIC` | Heartbeat for safety controller |

### 2.6 TF (Transform) System — The Spatial Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every sensor in an AV publishes its 6-DoF pose via `/tf` and `/tf_static`. The **tf2** library maintains a time-synchronized tree of coordinate frames:
>
> `map → odom → base_link → lidar_top → camera_front`
>
> Standard frame conventions (REP-105): **map** (global), **odom** (locally accurate, continuous), **base_link** (robot body), with `odom → base_link` drift-corrected by localization (e.g., EKF, NDT).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autoware Universe**: ~100+ ROS 2 nodes for L4 autonomy.
- **Apollo**: Uses Cyber RT (custom), but bridges to ROS for dev tools.
- **NVIDIA Isaac / DriveWorks**: Provides ROS 2 wrappers for GPU-accelerated perception.
- **ROS-Industrial**: Brings ROS to manufacturing robots (used by BMW, Siemens).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Message-Transport Latency Model

For ADAS perception pipelines, the **end-to-end latency** $L_{e2e}$ from sensor capture to actuator command is a safety-critical parameter. We derive the components contributed by the ROS middleware.

Let:
- $T_{s}$ = Sensor exposure/integration time (e.g., **camera shutter**)
- $T_{d}$ = Driver read-out time (e.g., LiDAR sweep time)
- $T_{p}$ = Publishing serialization time (msg pack + network frame)
- $T_{n}$ = Network transport time (DDS discovery + UDP/TCP transmission)
- $T_{sub}$ = Subscriber deserialization + callback queueing
- $T_{alg}$ = Algorithm processing time
- $T_{cmd}$ = Command publish latency (control → actuator bus)

The end-to-end latency is the sum of these serial stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e} &= T_{s} + T_{d} + T_{p} + T_{n} + T_{sub} + T_{alg} + T_{cmd}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Sensor stage.** A typical automotive camera has $T_{s} \approx 5\text{ ms}$ (shutter) and $T_{d} \approx 30\text{ ms}$ (sensor → buffer). A 10 Hz LiDAR has $T_{d} = 100\text{ ms}$ (full rotation).

**Step 2 — Publishing stage.** ROS 2 serializes a `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` of $N$ points into CDR (Common Data Representation). For $N = 130{,}000$ points, payload $\approx 130{,}000 \times 32\text{ bytes} = 4.16\text{ MB}$. On 1 Gbps Ethernet:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{p} &= \frac{\text{payload}}{\text{bandwidth}} = \frac{4.16 \times 10^{6}\text{ bytes}}{1.25 \times 10^{8}\text{ bytes/s}} \approx 33.3\text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Network stage.** DDS with `BEST_EFFORT` typically gives $T_{n} \approx 1\text{–}5\text{ ms}$ on a switched 1 GbE automotive Ethernet backbone.

**Step 4 — Subscriber stage.** Deserialization mirrors $T_{p}$, but with multi-threaded executor $T_{sub} \approx 5\text{ ms}$ for 4 parallel cores.

**Step 5 — Algorithm stage.** For a YOLOv8 inference on an Orin (30 TOPS): $T_{alg} \approx 33\text{ ms}$. For a LiDAR-based NDT localization: $T_{alg} \approx 50\text{ ms}$.

**Step 6 — Command stage.** Control command on CAN-FD: $T_{cmd} \approx 2\text{ ms}$.

**Step 7 — Total.** For a perception-then-control chain (e.g., AEB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e}^{AEB} &= \underbrace{5}_{\text{camera}} + \underbrace{33}_{T_{alg}} + \underbrace{2}_{T_{cmd}} \approx 40\text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e}^{LiDAR\text{-}plan} &= \underbrace{100}_{T_{d}} + \underbrace{33.3}_{T_{p}} + \underbrace{3}_{T_{n}} + \underbrace{50}_{T_{alg}} + \underbrace{2}_{T_{cmd}} \approx 188\text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** AEB functions typically require $L_{e2e} \le 100\text{ ms}$. The LiDAR-only pipeline at 188 ms **violates** the budget — hence multi-sensor fusion and faster compute (GPU) are mandatory.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — ROS Communication Selection

| Requirement | Use Topic | Use Service | Use Action |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Continuous stream (sensor data) | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Discrete trigger, immediate reply | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Long task with progress feedback | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Many publishers, many subscribers | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Preemption required (e.g., cancel maneuver) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Persistent state recording | ✓ (bags) | ✗ | ✗ |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Launching a Simple AV Sensor Stack

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** Write a ROS 2 launch file that starts a LiDAR driver, a camera driver, and a perception node, all publishing to a common topic namespace `/av/sensors`.

**Step 1 — Package layout** (per REP-127 / REP-140):

```
my_av_stack/
├── package.xml
├── setup.py
├── launch/
│   └── perception_bringup.launch.py
├── config/
│   ├── lidar_params.yaml
│   └── camera_params.yaml
└── my_av_stack/
    └── __init__.py
```

**Step 2 — Python launch file:**

```python
from launch import LaunchDescription
from launch_ros.actions import Node
import os

def generate_launch_description():
    config_dir = os.path.join(get_package_share_directory('my_av_stack'), 'config')

    lidar_node = Node(
        package='velodyne_driver',
        executable='velodyne_node',
        name='lidar_top',
        namespace='av/sensors',
        parameters=[os.path.join(config_dir, 'lidar_params.yaml')],
        output='screen'
    )

    camera_node = Node(
        package='usb_cam',
        executable='usb_cam_node_exe',
        name='front_camera',
        namespace='av/sensors',
        parameters=[os.path.join(config_dir, 'camera_params.yaml')],
        output='screen'
    )

    perception_node = Node(
        package='my_av_stack',
        executable='object_detector',
        name='yolov8_detector',
        namespace='av/perception',
        parameters=[{'model': 'yolov8n.engine', 'conf': 0.5}],
        output='screen'
    )

    return LaunchDescription([lidar_node, camera_node, perception_node])
```

**Step 3 — Launch & inspect:**

```bash
ros2 launch my_av_stack perception_bringup.launch.py
ros2 node list              # /av/sensors/lidar_top, /av/sensors/front_camera, /av/perception/yolov8_detector
ros2 topic list             # /av/sensors/lidar_top/points, /av/sensors/front_camera/image_raw, /av/perception/objects
ros2 run rqt_graph rqt_graph  # visualize the graph
```

This demonstrates **namespace isolation**, **parameter loading**, and **multi-node orchestration** — a frequent 7–14 mark KTU question.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROS Computation Graph for a Typical ADAS Perception Stack

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safeguards applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, labels are plain uppercase text, no reserved keywords used.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SENSOR_LAYER
        LIDAR[LiDAR Driver Node]
        CAM[Camera Driver Node]
        RADAR[Radar Driver Node]
        GNSS[GNSS Receiver Node]
    end

    subgraph PERCEPTION_LAYER
        PCL[Point Cloud Preprocessor]
        DET[Object Detector YOLO]
        FUS[Sensor Fusion EKF]
    end

    subgraph PLANNING_LAYER
        LOC[Localization NDT]
        PRED[Trajectory Predictor]
        PLAN[Behavior Planner]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_LAYER
        CTRL[Vehicle Controller]
        ACT[Actuator Driver CAN]
    end

    MASTER[ROS 2 DDS Domain Participant]

    LIDAR -- /av/lidar/points --> PCL
    CAM -- /av/camera/image_raw --> DET
    RADAR -- /av/radar/tracks --> FUS
    GNSS -- /av/gnss/fix --> LOC

    PCL -- /av/lidar/objects --> FUS
    DET -- /av/camera/detections --> FUS
    FUS -- /av/fused/objects --> PLAN
    LOC -- /av/odometry --> PLAN
    PRED -- /av/predicted_paths --> PLAN

    PLAN -- /av/planned_trajectory --> CTRL
    CTRL -- /av/cmd/control --> ACT

    LIDAR -.register.-> MASTER
    CAM -.register.-> MASTER
    FUS -.register.-> MASTER
    PLAN -.register.-> MASTER
    CTRL -.register.-> MASTER
```

### 4.2 ROS File-System Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    WORKSPACE[Catkin Workspace]
    WORKSPACE --> SRC[src]
    WORKSPACE --> BUILD[build]
    WORKSPACE --> DEVEL[devel]
    WORKSPACE --> INSTALL[install]
    WORKSPACE --> LOG[log]

    SRC --> PKG1[pkg perception lidar]
    SRC --> PKG2[pkg perception camera]
    SRC --> PKG3[pkg planning]
    SRC --> PKG4[pkg control]
    SRC --> PKG5[pkg description urdf]

    PKG1 --> MSG1[msg]
    PKG1 --> SRV1[srv]
    PKG1 --> CFG1[config]
    PKG1 --> LCH1[launch]
    PKG1 --> SRC1[src]

    PKG5 --> URDF[urdf xacro]
    PKG5 --> MESH[meshes]
    PKG5 --> LAUNCH[launch]
```

### 4.3 Communication Pattern Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Need to send data between ROS nodes]
    Q1{Is the data continuous streaming?}
    Q2{Is there exactly one request and one reply?}
    Q3{Does the task take a long time and need feedback or preemption?}

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> TOPIC[Use TOPIC pub sub]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> SVC[Use SERVICE req res]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> ACT[Use ACTION goal feedback result]
    Q3 -- No --> PARAM[Use PARAMETER SERVER]
```

### 4.4 ROS 1 vs ROS 2 — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ROS1
        R1APP[Node A] --> R1XMLRPC[XMLRPC Handshake]
        R1XMLRPC --> R1TCPROS[TCPROS Stream]
        R1TCPROS --> R1APP2[Node B]
        R1MASTER[roscore Master] --- R1APP
        R1MASTER --- R1APP2
    end

    subgraph ROS2
        R2APP[Node A] --> R2DDS[DDS Layer]
        R2DDS --> R2RTPS[RTPS Discovery]
        R2RTPS --> R2APP2[Node B]
        R2APP2 --> R2DDS
        R2DDS -.no master needed.-> R2APP
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exam:** ROS 1 = centralized master (single point of failure). ROS 2 = peer-to-peer DDS (fault-tolerant, real-time, suitable for safety-critical AV stacks).
