topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Path Planning and Control Systems - Fundamentals of Path Planning Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Path Planning and Control Systems

## Topic: Fundamentals of Path Planning Algorithms

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of autonomous vehicles, **Path Planning** is defined as the computational process of determining an optimal, collision-free, and kinematically feasible trajectory or sequence of states between an initial configuration and a goal configuration, while satisfying a set of constraints (obstacle avoidance, vehicle dynamics, road rules, time, energy, comfort).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT865 — Module 3) taxonomy, path planning algorithms are broadly classified into the following hierarchical families:

1. **Graph Search / Discrete Planners** — Discretize the configuration space (C-space) into a graph and search using informed/uninformed heuristics (e.g., Dijkstra, A*).
2. **Sampling-Based Planners** — Randomly sample the C-space to build a probabilistic roadmap or tree (e.g., PRM, RRT, RRT*).
3. **Optimization-Based Planners** — Formulate path generation as a constrained optimization problem (e.g., Polynomial Trajectory, MPC, Elastic Band).
4. **Reactive / Local Planners** — Generate control actions in real time based on local sensor feedback (e.g., Dynamic Window Approach, Artificial Potential Fields, VFH).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board-Favorite):**
> - **Path** = A geometric curve in space (sequence of waypoints).
> - **Trajectory** = A path parameterized by **time** (includes velocity and acceleration profiles).
> The planner produces a path; the controller turns it into a trajectory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration Space (C-Space)**: The space of all possible positions/orientations a robot/vehicle can attain. For a car, this is typically SE(2) = ℝ² × S¹ (x, y, θ). Obstacles in the workspace map to C-obstacles.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blindfolded Driver in a Maze"

Imagine you are blindfolded inside a giant maze and given a map of the maze. You cannot see walls, but you can tap a stick to detect them. Your task is to find the exit **as quickly as possible** without bumping into walls.

- **Dijkstra's algorithm** = you tap your stick in *every direction equally* until you find the exit. Works, but slow.
- **A* algorithm** = you tap your stick, but you also *use a compass* pointing toward the exit (the **heuristic**). You go in the most promising direction first. Much faster.
- **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** = you shoot your stick in *random directions*; wherever it doesn't hit a wall, you plant a flag. Soon flags connect, forming a path. Great for high-dimensional spaces.
- **Potential Fields** = imagine the exit *attracts* you like a magnet, and walls *repel* you like opposing magnets. You simply follow the resultant force vector.

This maze metaphor is the conceptual backbone of nearly every path planning algorithm in autonomous driving.

### 1.3 Classification of Path Planning Algorithms

| Family | Representative Algorithms | Best Use Case in AVs |
|---|---|---|
| Graph Search | Dijkstra, A*, D*, Hybrid A* | Highway lane change, urban grid |
| Sampling-Based | PRM, RRT, RRT*, Informed RRT* | Parking, unstructured off-road |
| Optimization-Based | Polynomial, MPC, Frenet planner | Smooth highway trajectories |
| Reactive / Local | DWA, APF, VFH | Real-time obstacle avoidance |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Pillars of Any Path Planner

Regardless of the family, every planner must address three core engineering questions:

**Pillar 1 — Discretization (How do we represent the world?)**
- The continuous configuration space C is converted into a finite structure.
- Methods include: uniform grids (occupancy grids), quadtrees, road graphs (lane-centerline), lattices, or random samples.

**Pillar 2 — Search / Sampling (How do we explore?)**
- Once the structure exists, the algorithm must explore it to find a connecting path.
- This is the *algorithmic heart* of the planner.

**Pillar 3 — Smoothing / Optimization (How do we make it drivable?)**
- Raw paths from search algorithms are typically jerky, non-holonomic, and kinematically infeasible.
- A post-processing step (spline fitting, gradient descent, quadratic programming) produces a smooth, executable trajectory.

---

### 2.2 Graph Search Algorithms — The Deterministic Backbone

#### 2.2.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm (1956)

A **uniform-cost search** that finds the shortest path in a graph with non-negative edge weights by expanding nodes in order of cumulative cost $g(n)$ from the start.

**Cost function:**
$$f(n) = g(n)$$

where $g(n)$ is the actual cost from start to node $n$.

- **Optimal**: Yes (given non-negative weights).
- **Complete**: Yes (will find a path if one exists).
- **Drawback**: Blindly explores in all directions; computationally expensive for large maps.

#### 2.2.2 A* Algorithm (Hart, Nilsson, Raphael — 1968)

A* is Dijkstra augmented with a **heuristic function** $h(n)$ that estimates the cost-to-go from node $n$ to the goal.

**Cost function:**
$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

- $g(n)$ = cost-so-far (actual)
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost-to-go (heuristic)
- The algorithm always expands the node with the **lowest $f(n)$ first**.

**Admissibility Condition for $h(n)$:**

> A heuristic is *admissible* if it never overestimates the true cost.

$$0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)$$

If $h$ is admissible, A* is guaranteed to return an optimal path. The tighter (closer to $h^*$) the heuristic, the fewer nodes expanded.

**Common heuristics for grid-based AV planners:**

$$h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) = \vert x_n - x_{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_n - y_{\text{goal}} \vert$$

$$h_{\text{Euclidean}}(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{\text{goal}})^2 + (y_n - y_{\text{goal}})^2}$$

$$h_{\text{Chebyshev}}(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_{\text{goal}} \vert, \vert y_n - y_{\text{goal}} \vert)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For 4-connected grids use **Manhattan**; for 8-connected grids (diagonal moves) use **Chebyshev** or **Euclidean**.

#### 2.2.3 Variants Used in Autonomous Vehicles

- **Hybrid A*** — Combines A* search with continuous-state validity checks for non-holonomic vehicles (uses Reeds-Shepp or Dubins curves).
- **D* / D* Lite** — Dynamic A*; efficient for replanning when the environment changes (sensor updates).
- **Anytime Repairing A\* (ARA\*)** — Returns a feasible solution quickly, then refines it as time permits.

---

### 2.3 Sampling-Based Planners

#### 2.3.1 Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM) — Kavraki et al. (1996)

**Two-phase algorithm:**

1. **Learning phase**: Sample $N$ collision-free configurations; connect nearby samples with edges (a "roadmap").
2. **Query phase**: Connect start and goal to the roadmap; run a graph search (A*) on the roadmap.

Pros: Multi-query friendly. Cons: Narrow passages problem.

#### 2.3.2 Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) — LaValle (1998)

A single-query, incremental algorithm that grows a tree rooted at $q_{\text{start}}$ by iteratively:

1. Sampling a random configuration $q_{\text{rand}}$.
2. Finding the nearest node $q_{\text{near}}$ in the tree.
3. Extending from $q_{\text{near}}$ toward $q_{\text{rand}}$ by a step $\epsilon$.
4. If the extension is collision-free, adding $q_{\text{new}}$ to the tree.

**Probability of reaching goal** improves with sample count, and RRT is **Probabilistically Complete** (as $N \to \infty$, the probability of finding a solution, if one exists, approaches 1).

#### 2.3.3 RRT* — Optimal Asymptotic Variant (Karaman & Frazzoli, 2011)

RRT* improves RRT by adding a **rewiring** step: after adding $q_{\text{new}}$, it searches within a radius $r_N$ for nearby nodes and rewires the tree if a shorter path is found.

**Radius update rule:**

$$r_N = \gamma \left( \frac{\log N}{N} \right)^{1/d}$$

where $d$ is the dimension of the configuration space and $\gamma > \gamma^* = 2 \left(1 + \frac{1}{d}\right)^{1/d} \left(\frac{\mu(\mathcal{X}_{\text{free}})}{\zeta_d}\right)^{1/d}$ is a tuning constant.

- **Asymptotically Optimal**: The cost of the returned path converges to the optimum as $N \to \infty$.

---

### 2.4 Reactive / Local Planners

#### 2.4.1 Artificial Potential Field (APF) — Khatib (1986)

The vehicle is treated as a point in a potential field:

$$U(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + U_{\text{rep}}(q)$$

**Attractive potential** (pulls toward goal):
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \, d^2(q, q_{\text{goal}})$$

**Repulsive potential** (pushes away from obstacles):
$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} \left( \dfrac{1}{d(q, q_{\text{obs}})} - \dfrac{1}{d_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } d(q, q_{\text{obs}}) \leq d_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The control force is the negative gradient:
$$F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} - \nabla U_{\text{rep}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Local Minima Problem**: APF can trap the vehicle at saddle points where $F = 0$ but the goal is not reached. Solutions include virtual obstacle placement or randomized escape maneuvers.

#### 2.4.2 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — Fox, Burgard, Thrun (1997)

Samples the **velocity space** $(v, \omega)$ reachable in one time step (the "dynamic window") and scores each candidate trajectory by:

$$\text{score}(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{vel}(v, \omega)$$

The highest-scoring velocity is sent to the controller.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Symbols | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $f$: total cost, $g$: cost-so-far, $h$: heuristic | cost-units | A* evaluation |
| 2 | $0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | admissibility | — | A* optimality |
| 3 | $h_{\text{Manhattan}} = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | grid step | grid-units | 4-connected A* |
| 4 | $h_{\text{Euclidean}} = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Cartesian | length-units | 8-connected A* |
| 5 | $r_N = \gamma (\log N / N)^{1/d}$ | RRT* rewiring radius | length-units | Sampling planner |
| 6 | $U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} d^2(q, q_g)$ | attractive potential | energy-units | APF planner |
| 7 | $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} (1/d - 1/d_0)^2$ | repulsive potential | energy-units | APF planner |
| 8 | $F = -\nabla U(q)$ | control force | Newtons | APF control law |
| 9 | $q_{k+1} = q_k + \epsilon \cdot \frac{q_{\text{rand}} - q_{\text{near}}}{\vert\vert q_{\text{rand}} - q_{\text{near}} \vert\vert}$ | RRT extension | configuration-units | Sampling |
| 10 | $\text{score} = \alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{vel}$ | weighted sum | dimensionless | DWA |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — A* Optimality from Admissible Heuristic

**Statement (to prove):** If $h(n)$ is admissible, then A* expands every node $n$ on an optimal path with $f(n) \leq C^*$, where $C^*$ is the cost of the optimal path.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Setup.** Let $n$ be any node on an optimal path $P^*$ from start $s$ to goal $g$, with optimal cost $C^* = g^*(g)$.

**Step 2 — Decompose the cost.** The cost of reaching $n$ along $P^*$ is $g^*(n) = g(n)$ along the optimal prefix. By the optimal-substructure property, the remaining cost to $g$ along $P^*$ is $h^*(n) = C^* - g^*(n)$.

**Step 3 — Apply admissibility.**
$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) = C^* - g^*(n)$$

**Step 4 — Compute $f(n)$ at node $n$ on $P^*$.**
$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

Since $g(n) = g^*(n)$ (because $n$ lies on $P^*$ and edge costs are non-negative), we substitute:

$$f(n) = g^*(n) + h(n) \leq g^*(n) + h^*(n) = g^*(n) + (C^* - g^*(n)) = C^*$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
$$\boxed{f(n) \leq C^*}$$

Thus A* will pop every such $n$ from the open list **before** it ever pops a node with $f > C^*$. This guarantees the first goal reached is along the optimal path. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RRT* Asymptotic Optimality Radius

**Statement:** To achieve asymptotic optimality, the rewiring radius $r_N$ must shrink no faster than $\gamma (\log N / N)^{1/d}$.

**Step 1 — Volume of a $d$-dimensional ball.**
$$V_d(r) = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2 + 1)} \, r^d$$

**Step 2 — Coverage requirement.** For RRT* to be optimal, every ball of radius $r_N$ around a new node must contain at least one sample (in expectation). The expected number of samples in such a ball after $N$ samples is:
$$\mathbb{E}[\text{samples in ball}] = N \cdot \frac{V_d(r_N)}{\mu(\mathcal{X}_{\text{free}})}$$

**Step 3 — Set the expectation to a constant (e.g., $e$).**
$$N \cdot \frac{V_d(r_N)}{\mu(\mathcal{X}_{\text{free}})} = e \quad \Longrightarrow \quad r_N^d = \frac{e \cdot \Gamma(d/2+1)}{\pi^{d/2}} \cdot \frac{\mu(\mathcal{X}_{\text{free}})}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce logarithmic correction for boundary effects** (standard in the literature):
$$r_N = \gamma \left( \frac{\log N}{N} \right)^{1/d}$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**
$$\boxed{r_N = \gamma \left( \frac{\log N}{N} \right)^{1/d}, \quad \gamma > \gamma^* = 2 \left(1+\frac{1}{d}\right)^{1/d} \left( \frac{\mu(\mathcal{X}_{\text{free}})}{\zeta_d} \right)^{1/d}}$$

where $\zeta_d$ is the volume of the unit ball in ℝ$^d$. This radius decreases fast enough to keep computation tractable, but slowly enough to maintain optimality. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Artificial Potential Field Control Law

**Step 1 — Define attractive potential** (parabolic, gradient is constant attractive force far away):
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \, \rho^2(q), \quad \rho(q) = \vert\vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \vert\vert$$

**Step 2 — Take the gradient (control force) of the attractive part:**
$$F_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -k_{\text{att}} (q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

This produces a force proportional to distance — strong when far, gentle when near.

**Step 3 — Define repulsive potential** (active only within distance $d_0$ of an obstacle):
$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right)^2, \quad \text{for } \rho(q) \leq d_0$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\rho$ (assume single obstacle, $\partial \rho / \partial q = (q - q_{\text{obs}}) / \rho$):**
$$F_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = -k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{q - q_{\text{obs}}}{\vert\vert q - q_{\text{obs}} \vert\vert}}$$

Direction: from obstacle toward vehicle. Magnitude: diverges as $\rho \to 0$ (safety guarantee).

**Step 5 — Net control force:**
$$F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{att}} + \sum_{i=1}^{M} F_{\text{rep},i}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check.** Let $k_{\text{att}} = 1.0$, $k_{\text{rep}} = 2.0$, $d_0 = 2.0$ m, $q = (1, 1)$, $q_g = (10, 10)$, $q_{\text{obs}} = (2, 2)$.

Attractive magnitude: $\vert\vert F_{\text{att}} \vert\vert = 1.0 \cdot \sqrt{81 + 81} = 12.73$ N.

Distance to obstacle: $\rho = \sqrt{1+1} = 1.414$ m. Since $\rho < d_0$:
$$\vert\vert F_{\text{rep}} \vert\vert = 2.0 \left( \frac{1}{1.414} - \frac{1}{2.0} \right) \frac{1}{(1.414)^2} = 2.0 \cdot (0.707 - 0.5) \cdot 0.5 = 0.207 \text{ N}$$

Net force balances goal attraction with mild obstacle repulsion. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Dijkstra | A* | PRM | RRT | RRT* | APF | DWA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Completeness | ✔ | ✔ | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Asymp. optimal | ✘ (local minima) | ✔ |
| Optimality | ✔ | ✔ (if $h$ admissible) | ✘ | ✘ | Asymptotic | Local | Local |
| Time complexity | $O(V^2)$ | $O(b^d)$ worst | Sample-dep. | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(M)$ | $O(V^2)$ |
| Handles dynamics | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ |
| Narrow passages | Good | Good | Poor | Poor | Moderate | N/A | N/A |
| Real-time replan | ✘ | Moderate | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| AV typical use | Grid nav | Lane planning | Static map | Quick feas. | Optimal parking | Obstacle avoid | Local ctrl |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Path Planning Pipeline in an AV Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HD Map and Perception Input] --> B[Global Path Planner]
    B --> B1[Graph Search: A Star, Dijkstra]
    B --> B2[Sampling Based: RRT, PRM]
    B --> B3[Optimization Based: Frenet, MPC]
    B1 --> C[Reference Path and Waypoints]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Trajectory Optimizer]
    D --> E[Local Path Planner]
    E --> E1[Dynamic Window Approach]
    E --> E2[Artificial Potential Field]
    E --> E3[Model Predictive Control]
    E1 --> F[Control Commands: Steering, Throttle, Brake]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Vehicle Actuators]
    G --> H[Feedback: IMU, GPS, Odometry]
    H --> D
    H --> E
```

### 4.2 A* Search Expansion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize Open List with Start Node] --> B[Pop Node n with Lowest f of n]
    B --> C{Is n the Goal}
    C -- Yes --> D[Reconstruct Path and Return]
    C -- No --> E[Generate Successors of n]
    E --> F[For Each Successor Compute g prime and h prime]
    F --> G{g prime less than existing g of successor}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update Parent and Push to Open List]
    G -- No --> I[Discard]
    H --> B
    I --> B
```

### 4.3 RRT Tree Growth Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Configuration q start] --> B[Sample Random q rand]
    B --> C[Find Nearest q near in Tree]
    C --> D[Extend by Step Epsilon Toward q rand]
    D --> E{Is Edge Collision Free}
    E -- Yes --> F[Add q new to Tree]
    E -- No --> G[Discard Sample]
    F --> H{Is q new Near Goal}
    H -- Yes --> I[Return Path]
    H -- No --> B
    G --> B
```

### 4.4 Artificial Potential Field Force Vector Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle Position q] --> B[Compute Distance to Goal]
    A --> C[Compute Distance to Each Obstacle]
    B --> D[Attractive Force F att toward Goal]
    C --> E[Repulsive Force F rep away from Obstacle]
    D --> F[Vector Sum F net]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Steering Command from F net Direction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four diagrams above collectively describe the **block-level functional architecture** of an autonomous vehicle's path planning stack, aligned with the KTU 2024 PEMUT865 Module 3 syllabus. The planner hierarchy is *Global → Trajectory → Local → Control*, and the loops between blocks indicate the **sense–plan–act** feedback cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
*Differentiate between a path and a trajectory in the context of autonomous vehicle motion planning. Why is this distinction critical for an AV control stack?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Path | Trajectory |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A geometric curve; sequence of waypoints | A path parameterized by time |
| Includes | Only spatial information | Spatial + velocity + acceleration profile |
| Output of | Global path planner | Trajectory optimizer / local planner |
| Used by | High-level mission planner | Low-level controller (throttle, brake, steering) |

**Why critical (1 mark):** A geometric path may be kinematically infeasible (e.g., a sharp 90° turn at 60 km/h) or violate acceleration/jerk limits. The trajectory adds the **time dimension**, enabling the controller to track it respecting vehicle dynamics, comfort, and safety. Without this distinction, an AV could not be smoothly controlled.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 3]**
*State and explain the admissibility condition for the heuristic function used in the A* algorithm. What happens if the heuristic is non-admissible?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Admissibility condition (1 mark):**
$$0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n$$

The heuristic $h(n)$ must never overestimate the true minimum cost $h^*(n)$ from $n$ to the goal.

**Explanation (1 mark):** If $h$ underestimates (or equals) the true cost, A* is guaranteed to return an optimal path because the priority queue always pops the most promising node.

**If non-admissible (1 mark):** A* loses its optimality guarantee. It may still find a valid path (because $g$ is always exact), but the path cost may be suboptimal. A common practice is to use an **inadmissible but consistent** heuristic with a weight $\epsilon > 1$ (weighted A*) for faster but suboptimal solutions — acceptable in real-time AV applications.

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 3 questions carry an internal choice. Both alternatives are given below.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the hierarchical path planning architecture used in autonomous vehicles. Compare global planning and local planning in terms of objective, time horizon, and computational constraints.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the A* algorithm in detail. Derive the admissibility condition and show, with a worked grid example (4×4 grid with one obstacle), how A* finds the optimal path using the Manhattan heuristic.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

A typical AV uses a **three-tier hierarchy**:

**Tier 1 — Global Path Planner** (offline / low-rate, ~1 Hz)
- Uses HD map + coarse static obstacles.
- Algorithms: A*, Dijkstra on road graph.
- Time horizon: full route (km scale).

**Tier 2 — Trajectory / Behavior Planner** (mid-rate, ~10 Hz)
- Generates time-parameterized trajectory in Frenet frame.
- Algorithms: polynomial fitting, optimization, lattice planner.

**Tier 3 — Local / Reactive Planner** (high-rate, ~50–100 Hz)
- Handles dynamic obstacles, pedestrians, sensor feedback.
- Algorithms: DWA, APF, MPC.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Global | Local |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | Minutes to hours | 1–5 seconds |
| Update rate | ~1 Hz | 50–100 Hz |
| Map scale | HD map, km | Local occupancy, <50 m |
| Computation | Heavy, offline-OK | Must be real-time |
| Goal | Road-level routing | Collision-free motion |

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**A* algorithm** (3 marks): Maintains two lists — `Open` (priority queue sorted by $f = g + h$) and `Closed`. Repeatedly pops node with lowest $f$, expands neighbors, updates costs.

**Admissibility derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1: if $h \leq h^*$, then for any node $n$ on optimal path, $f(n) \leq C^*$, ensuring the goal is reached with optimal cost.

**Worked grid example (2 marks):** 4×4 grid, start (0,0), goal (3,3), obstacle at (1,1). With Manhattan heuristic:
- $h(0,0) = 6$, $g = 0$, $f = 6$
- Expand (0,0) → successors (1,0) and (0,1)
- $f(1,0) = 1+5 = 6$, $f(0,1) = 1+5 = 6$
- Tie-break: expand (1,0) → successor (2,0) with $f = 2+4 = 6$, and (1,1) is obstacle, skip
- Continue: (2,0) → (3,0) $f = 3+3 = 6$ → (3,1) $f = 4+2 = 6$ → (3,2) $f = 5+1 = 6$ → (3,3) GOAL $f = 6+0 = 6$
- Optimal path cost = **6**, optimal path = $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3)$ (L-shaped, cost 6 since Manhattan = optimal here).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) algorithm. How does RRT* improve upon RRT? Derive the expression for the rewiring radius $r_N$ in RRT* and justify the logarithmic term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An autonomous vehicle encounters a static obstacle at distance $\rho = 1.0$ m, with $d_0 = 2.0$ m. The attractive constant $k_{\text{att}} = 1.5$ N/m and repulsive constant $k_{\text{rep}} = 3.0$ N. The vehicle is 5.0 m from the goal in a straight line. Compute the net APF control force and the steering direction.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**RRT algorithm (3 marks):** As described in Section 2.3.2 — iteratively sample, find nearest, extend by $\epsilon$, collision check, add to tree. Probabilistically complete, but **not optimal**.

**RRT* improvement (2 marks):** Adds two operations:
1. **Near-neighbor search** within $r_N$ ball around $q_{\text{new}}$.
2. **Rewiring**: re-route parent of each near neighbor through $q_{\text{new}}$ if cheaper.

**Radius derivation (2 marks):** As in Section 3.2:
$$r_N = \gamma \left( \frac{\log N}{N} \right)^{1/d}$$

The $\log N$ term arises because in high dimensions, the expected nearest-neighbor distance decreases only logarithmically with $N$ in a bounded C-space; without $\log N$, the rewiring radius would shrink too fast in narrow passages, breaking optimality.

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

Given: $k_{\text{att}} = 1.5$ N/m, $k_{\text{rep}} = 3.0$ N, $\rho_{\text{goal}} = 5.0$ m, $\rho_{\text{obs}} = 1.0$ m, $d_0 = 2.0$ m.

**Step 1 — Attractive force magnitude (1 mark):**
$$\vert\vert F_{\text{att}} \vert\vert = k_{\text{att}} \cdot \rho_{\text{goal}} = 1.5 \times 5.0 = 7.5 \text{ N}$$

Direction: along $(q_{\text{goal}} - q)/\vert q_{\text{goal}} - q \vert$.

**Step 2 — Repulsive force magnitude (2 marks):**
$$\vert\vert F_{\text{rep}} \vert\vert = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs}}} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs}}^2}$$
$$= 3.0 \times \left( \frac{1}{1.0} - \frac{1}{2.0} \right) \times \frac{1}{1.0^2} = 3.0 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 1.5 \text{ N}$$

Direction: along $(q - q_{\text{obs}})/\vert q - q_{\text{obs}} \vert$ (away from obstacle).

**Step 3 — Net force vector (2 marks):** Assuming the obstacle lies *perpendicular* to the goal direction (worst case for a right-angle turn), the net force magnitude is:
$$\vert\vert F_{\text{net}} \vert\vert = \sqrt{7.5^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{56.25 + 2.25} = \sqrt{58.5} \approx 7.65 \text{ N}$$

Steering angle from goal axis:
$$\theta = \arctan\left( \frac{1.5}{7.5} \right) = \arctan(0.2) \approx 11.31°$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The vehicle steers ~11.3° away from the obstacle, with a net control force of 7.65 N, successfully avoiding collision while continuing toward the goal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Path and Trajectory** (–1 mark): Always distinguish geometric path vs time-parameterized trajectory. Examiners explicitly look for this.
> 2. **Forgetting admissibility proof** (–2 marks): When A* is asked, state the admissibility condition $h \leq h^*$ explicitly. A bare formula is insufficient.
> 3. **RRT* derivation skipped** (–3 marks): If the question says "derive," you must derive the $r_N$ expression — do not just quote it.
> 4. **APF sign errors** (–2 marks): Repulsive force points *away* from obstacle, not toward it. The gradient is negative; force is $-\nabla U$.
> 5. **Units mismatch** (–1 mark): Always attach N (force), m (distance), s (time) units to numerical answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Path** = geometry in space; **Trajectory** = path + time (velocity, acceleration).
- ✅ **Configuration Space (C-space)** is the universe of the planner; obstacles in workspace map to C-obstacles.
- ✅ **Dijkstra** = $f(n) = g(n)$, blind search.
- ✅ **A\*** = $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$; admissible $h$ ⇒ optimal path.
- ✅ **Manhattan heuristic** for 4-connected grids; **Euclidean** for 8-connected.
- ✅ **RRT** = probabilistically complete, NOT optimal; **RRT*** = asymptotically optimal via rewiring.
- ✅ **RRT* rewiring radius** = $r_N = \gamma (\log N / N)^{1/d}$.
- ✅ **APF** has the **local minima** problem — biggest pitfall in board answers.
- ✅ **DWA** operates in the **velocity space** and scores by heading + clearance + speed.
- ✅ **Frenet frame** is the standard for highway trajectory planning (separate $s$–$d$ coordinates).
- ✅ **Replanning** for dynamic environments uses **D\***, **ARA\***, or **MPC** with rolling horizon.
- ✅ **Three-tier AV planning hierarchy** = Global (A*/road graph) → Trajectory (optimization) → Local (DWA/APF/MPC).
- ✅ For 14-mark derivations, always include the **admissibility proof** or **$r_N$ derivation** explicitly.
- ✅ Numerical APF problems: always compute $F_{\text{att}}$ and $F_{\text{rep}}$ **separately**, then **vector-add** them.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation** refers to the integrated suite of feedback and feedforward control algorithms that regulate a vehicle's longitudinal motion (speed/distance), lateral motion (steering/yaw stability), and vertical motion (roll/pitch), while simultaneously tracking a desired path or trajectory in a global reference frame using multi-sensor fusion. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT865)**, this encompasses three interlocking engineering domains: 1. **Vehicle Dynamics Control (VDC)** – maintaining the vehicle within its stable handling envelope. 2. **Path Tracking Control (PTC)** – executing a geometric trajectory with minimal cross-track error. 3. **Navigation & Localization** – estimating the vehicle's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ relative to a global frame using sensor fusion. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: The "Balanced Bicycle with a Blindfolded Helmsman"** > Imagine riding a bicycle while blindfolded. You have three problems: (1) **not falling over** (stability), (2) **staying on a curved road** (path tracking), and (3) **knowing where you are** (navigation). Your **inner ear** acts like an **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)**, your **eyes** act like a **camera/LiDAR**, and your **brain** acts like a **controller**. The brain takes inputs from these "sensors" and decides how hard to push the handlebars (steering) and how hard to pedal/brake (throttle/brake). A modern ADAS controller does exactly the same — only it executes the decisions in **milliseconds** with mathematical precision. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Standard Metrics (Memorize):** > - Standard gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ > - Nominal tire-road friction coefficient (dry asphalt): $\mu \approx 0.9$ > - Maximum stable lateral acceleration (passenger car): $a_{y,\text{max}} \approx 0.4g$ (without ESC), $0.6g$ (with ESC) > - Standard steering ratio (passenger car): $\approx 16:1$ > - Yaw rate sample rate: $\geq 100 \ \text{Hz}$ > - GPS update rate: $1$–$10 \ \text{Hz}$ > - IMU update rate: $100$–$1000 \ \text{Hz}$ --- ### 1.4 The Stability Triangle Vehicle stability is governed by three rotational degrees of freedom around the **center of gravity (CoG)**: **Yaw (Z-axis)**, **Pitch (Y-axis)**, and **Roll (X-axis)**. | Degree of Freedom | Physical Meaning | Controlling Actuator | Primary Sensor | |---|---|---|---| | Yaw rotation | Vehicle spinning around vertical axis | Differential braking, steering | Yaw rate gyroscope | | Pitch rotation | Nose-dive/squat under braking/acceleration | Active suspension, throttle | Longitudinal accelerometer | | Roll rotation | Body lean during cornering | Active anti-roll bars, ESC | Lateral accelerometer | --- ### 1.5 Why This Topic is Critical in ADAS > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern **SAE Level 3+ autonomous vehicles** cannot decouple stability from navigation. A perfect path planner is useless if the vehicle cannot physically execute the trajectory without skidding, rolling, or losing localization. Hence, KTU explicitly groups these as a single Module to emphasize the **co-design philosophy** of modern vehicle control. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two-Axes Vehicle Dynamics Model (Bicycle Model) The most widely used model in KTU-relevant literature is the **kinematic bicycle model** for path tracking and the **dynamic bicycle model** for stability. #### 2.1.1 Kinematic Bicycle Model (Low Speed / Path Tracking) Assumptions: no slip, no load transfer, planar motion. $$\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$$ $$\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$$ $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L} \tan(\delta)$$ Where: - $(x, y)$ = CoG position in global frame - $\theta$ = heading angle - $v$ = longitudinal velocity at CoG - $L$ = wheelbase length - $\delta$ = front-wheel steering angle #### 2.1.2 Dynamic Bicycle Model (Stability Analysis) Incorporates tire forces and vehicle inertia: $$m(\dot{v}_x - v_y \dot{\psi}) = F_{xr} + F_{xf} \cos(\delta) - F_{yf} \sin(\delta)$$ $$m(\dot{v}_y + v_x \dot{\psi}) = F_{yr} + F_{yf} \cos(\delta) + F_{xf} \sin(\delta)$$ $$I_z \ddot{\psi} = l_f F_{yf} \cos(\delta) - l_r F_{yr} + l_f F_{xf} \sin(\delta)$$ Where: - $m$ = vehicle mass - $I_z$ = yaw moment of inertia - $\dot{\psi}$ = yaw rate - $F_{xf}, F_{xr}$ = front/rear longitudinal tire forces - $F_{yf}, F_{yr}$ = front/rear lateral tire forces - $l_f, l_r$ = CoG-to-front-axle / CoG-to-rear-axle distances ### 2.2 The Linear Tire Model (Linear Range) For small slip angles, lateral tire force is proportional to slip angle: $$F_{yi} = C_{\alpha i} \cdot \alpha_i$$ Where: - $C_{\alpha i}$ = cornering stiffness (N/rad) - $\alpha_i$ = tire slip angle Slip angles for bicycle model: $$\alpha_f = \delta - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v_y + l_f \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right)$$ $$\alpha_r = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v_y - l_r \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > The **cornering stiffness** $C_{\alpha}$ is the single most important tire parameter. For a typical passenger car tire: $C_{\alpha} \approx 80{,}000 \ \text{N/rad}$ per tire. ### 2.3 Yaw Stability – The Understeer Gradient The **understeer gradient** $K$ determines whether a vehicle is **understeering**, **neutral steer**, or **oversteering**: $$K = \frac{m}{L}\left(\frac{l_r}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{l_f}{C_{\alpha r}}\right)$$ > [!TIP] > **Steering Behavior Classification:** > - $K > 0$ → **Understeer** (stable, self-correcting) — desirable for safety > - $K = 0$ → **Neutral steer** > - $K < 0$ → **Oversteer** (unstable, requires driver skill or ESC) ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Kinematic yaw rate | $\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan(\delta)}{L}$ | rad/s | Path tracking at low speed | | 2 | Dynamic yaw rate (steady-state) | $\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{v_x / L}{1 + K v_x^2} \cdot \delta$ | rad/s | Stability envelope check | | 3 | Linear tire force | $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ | N | Tire model in linear region | | 4 | Slip ratio (longitudinal) | $\kappa = \frac{R\omega - v_x}{v_x}$ | dimensionless | TCS / ABS | | 5 | Slip angle (lateral) | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v_y}{v_x}\right)$ | rad | ESC / VDC | | 6 | Understeer gradient | $K = \frac{m}{L}\left(\frac{l_r}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{l_f}{C_{\alpha r}}\right)$ | rad/(m/s²) | Vehicle classification | | 7 | Critical speed (oversteer) error | $e_{lat} = d \sin(\alpha)$ | m | Geometric path tracker | | 10 | Stanley steering law | $\delta(t) = \theta_e(t) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{k \cdot e_{fa}}{v(t)}\right)$ | rad | Autonomous steering | | 11 | GPS/INS EKF state | $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, v_x, v_y, \theta]^T$ | mixed | Sensor fusion | | 12 | Wheel slip optimum | $\kappa_{opt} \approx 0.08$–$0.15$ | – | ABS braking efficiency | > [!WARNING] > **PITFALL**: Do not write absolute values using the `|` symbol inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table rendering. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | System | Stability Function | Navigation Function | |---|---|---| | **ESC (Electronic Stability Control)** | Corrects yaw deviation via differential braking | — | | **TCS (Traction Control)** | Limits wheel slip during acceleration | — | | **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)** | Maintains safe longitudinal spacing | Uses GPS/map for curvature preview | | **LKA (Lane Keeping Aid)** | Reduces lateral error to lane center | Uses camera-based lane detection | | **Autonomous Highway Pilot** | Combined longitudinal + lateral control | HD-map + GNSS + LiDAR localization | --- ### 2.6 Hierarchical Control Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Emphasis:** The standard ADAS/AV architecture is **two-layer**: > 1. **Upper Layer (Path Planner / Behavior Layer):** Generates reference trajectory $(x_{ref}, y_{ref}, v_{ref}, \psi_{ref})$. > 2. **Lower Layer (Vehicle Control / Stabilization Layer):** Tracks the reference via steering, throttle, and brake actuators while enforcing stability constraints. --- ### 2.7 Path Tracking Controllers — Three Pillars #### 2.7.1 Pure Pursuit Controller A **geometric** algorithm: the vehicle chases a "look-ahead point" on the path located at distance $L_d$ ahead. $$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2 L \sin(\alpha)}{L_d}\right)$$ Where $\alpha$ is the angle between vehicle heading and the look-ahead vector. #### 2.7.2 Stanley Controller Used in the **Stanford DARPA Grand Challenge** vehicle. Minimizes both heading error and front-axle cross-track error: $$\delta(t) = \theta_e(t) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{k \cdot e_{fa}(t)}{v(t)}\right)$$ > [!TIP] > The term $\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{k e_{fa}}{v}\right)$ is the **nonlinear gain** — at low speed $v$, the steering correction becomes large; at high speed, it becomes small. This is a **natural stability feature**. #### 2.7.3 Model Predictive Control (MPC) A **receding-horizon optimization** approach. At each timestep, it solves: $$\min_{u_{0:T-1}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ \Vert x_k - x_{ref,k} \Vert_Q^2 + \Vert u_k \Vert_R^2 \right] + \Vert x_N - x_{ref,N} \Vert_P^2$$ Subject to: - Vehicle dynamics: $x_{k+1} = f(x_k, u_k)$ - Stability constraints: $\vert \alpha_f \vert \leq \alpha_{f,\max}$ - Actuator limits: $\vert \delta \vert \leq \delta_{\max}$, $\vert a \vert \leq a_{\max}$ > [!NOTE] > MPC is the **gold standard** in modern AVs (Tesla, Waymo) because it explicitly handles constraints — but it requires significant onboard compute. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Yaw Rate Response > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **KTU-favorite derivation** because it unifies tire, vehicle, and steering into one equation. We derive the closed-form steady-state yaw rate as a function of steering angle. **Step 1: Assume steady-state cornering** $\Rightarrow \dot{v}_y = 0$, $\ddot{\psi} = 0$. The lateral and yaw moment equations simplify to: $$m v_x \dot{\psi} = F_{yr} + F_{yf} \cos(\delta)$$ $$0 = l_f F_{yf} \cos(\delta) - l_r F_{yr} + l_f F_{xf} \sin(\delta)$$ **Step 2: For small steering angles** $\delta \ll 1$, we use the small-angle approximations: $$\cos(\delta) \approx 1, \quad \sin(\delta) \approx \delta$$ **Step 3: Linearize the slip angles** (assuming $v_y \ll v_x$): $$\alpha_f \approx \delta - \frac{v_y + l_f \dot{\psi}}{v_x}$$ $$\alpha_r \approx -\frac{v_y - l_r \dot{\psi}}{v_x}$$ **Step 4: Substitute the linear tire model** $F_{yi} = C_{\alpha i} \alpha_i$: $$m v_x \dot{\psi} = C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r + C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f$$ $$0 = l_f C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f - l_r C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$$ **Step 5: Solve the moment equation** (Eq. 2) for $\alpha_r$: $$\alpha_r = \frac{l_f C_{\alpha f}}{l_r C_{\alpha r}} \alpha_f$$ **Step 6: Substitute into the force equation** (Eq. 1): $$m v_x \dot{\psi} = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f + C_{\alpha r} \cdot \frac{l_f C_{\alpha f}}{l_r C_{\alpha r}} \alpha_f$$ $$m v_x \dot{\psi} = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f \left( 1 + \frac{l_f}{l_r} \right)$$ **Step 7: Express $\alpha_f$** using the velocity sum: $v_y = \frac{l_r \dot{\psi}}{1 + K v_x^2}$ (this is the **Ackermann-like steady-state side slip**). After algebra (substituting $K = \frac{m}{L}\left(\frac{l_r}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{l_f}{C_{\alpha r}}\right)$): $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{v_x / L}{1 + K v_x^2} \cdot \delta$$ This is the **fundamental yaw-rate response equation** required for ESC tuning and stability envelope design. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Pure Pursuit Steering Law > [!NOTE] > The Pure Pursuit algorithm is derived from **circular arc geometry** between rear axle and look-ahead point. **Step 1: Geometry setup.** The vehicle is at position $(x, y)$ with heading $\theta$. The look-ahead point is at distance $L_d$ along the path. **Step 2: The inscribed angle theorem** states that for a circle passing through the rear axle, the front axle, and the look-ahead point, the steering curvature is: $$\gamma = \frac{2 x}{L_d^2}$$ Where $x$ is the lateral offset (cross-track error) of the look-ahead point relative to the vehicle's heading. **Step 3: Relate curvature to steering angle** via the Ackermann steering relation: $$\gamma = \frac{\tan(\delta)}{L}$$ **Step 4: Equate and solve** for $\delta$: $$\frac{\tan(\delta)}{L} = \frac{2 x}{L_d^2}$$ $$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left( \frac{2 L x}{L_d^2} \right)$$ > [!TIP] > **Design insight:** Larger $L_d$ gives smoother but less accurate tracking; smaller $L_d$ gives tighter tracking but oscillates. A common rule of thumb: $L_d \approx 1$–$3$ seconds of forward travel, i.e., $L_d = v \cdot t_{look}$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Extended Kalman Filter for GPS/INS Fusion > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently tests the **linearized Kalman Filter update equations** in exams. **State vector:** $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & v_x & v_y & \theta \end{bmatrix}^T$ **Prediction Step:** $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$ $$\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1|k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$$ **Update Step:** $$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T \left( \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k \right)^{-1}$$ $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \left( \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} \right)$$ $$\mathbf{P}_{k|k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1}$$ **Where:** - $\mathbf{F}_k$ = state transition matrix (from bicycle model Jacobians) - $\mathbf{H}_k$ = observation matrix (GPS provides $x, y$; IMU provides $\dot{\theta}$) - $\mathbf{Q}_k$ = process noise covariance - $\mathbf{R}_k$ = measurement noise covariance - $\mathbf{K}_k$ = **Kalman Gain** (weights between prediction and measurement) > [!TIP] > **Why EKF and not KF?** The bicycle model is **nonlinear** in $\theta$ (it contains $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$). The EKF linearizes about the current estimate using Jacobians: > $$\mathbf{F} = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right\rvert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Steady-State Yaw Rate > [!NOTE] > This problem is a **standard KTU-style numerical** that tests stability understanding. **Given:** - $m = 1500 \ \text{kg}$ - $L = 2.5 \ \text{m}$ - $l_f = 1.2 \ \text{m}$, $l_r = 1.3 \ \text{m}$ - $C_{\alpha f} = C_{\alpha r} = 80{,}000 \ \text{N/rad}$ (per axle, combined tires) - $v_x = 20 \ \text{m/s}$ ($\approx 72 \ \text{km/h}$) - $\delta = 2^\circ = 0.0349 \ \text{rad}$ **Find:** Steady-state yaw rate and the vehicle's steering behavior. **Step 1: Compute the understeer gradient:** $$K = \frac{m}{L}\left(\frac{l_r}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{l_f}{C_{\alpha r}}\right)$$ $$K = \frac{1500}{2.5}\left(\frac{1.3}{80{,}000} - \frac{1.2}{80{,}000}\right)$$ $$K = 600 \times \frac{0.1}{80{,}000} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{rad/(m/s)}^2$$ **Step 2: Since $K > 0$, the vehicle is UNDERSTEERING** (stable). **Step 3: Compute steady-state yaw rate:** $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{v_x / L}{1 + K v_x^2} \cdot \delta$$ $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{20 / 2.5}{1 + (7.5 \times 10^{-4})(400)} \cdot 0.0349$$ $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{8}{1.3} \cdot 0.0349 = 6.154 \times 0.0349$$ $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} \approx 0.215 \ \text{rad/s} \approx 12.3 \ \deg/\text{s}$$ **Step 4: Compute the steady-state lateral acceleration:** $$a_y = v_x \cdot \dot{\psi}_{ss} = 20 \times 0.215 = 4.3 \ \text{m/s}^2 \approx 0.44g$$ > [!WARNING] > Since $a_y = 0.44g$ is **close to the friction limit** ($\mu g \approx 0.9g$), the linear tire model is still valid but approaching saturation. In reality, a safety margin (e.g., 30%) should be enforced by the ESC. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Controller Selection vs. Scenario | Scenario | Speed Range | Recommended Controller | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Parking (low speed) | $< 5 \ \text{m/s}$ | Pure Pursuit | Simple, low-cost, geometric | | Highway lane keeping | $20$–$30 \ \text{m/s}$ | Stanley | Smooth convergence, low oscillation | | Urban autonomous | $5$–$20 \ \text{m/s}$ | MPC | Constraint handling (obstacles, friction) | | Emergency avoidance | Any | MPC with hard constraints | Stability-critical | | Off-road / rough terrain | $< 10 \ \text{m/s}$ | MPC + slip-aware model | Tire slip must be modeled | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Two-Layer ADAS Control Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A[Perception Layer
CAMERA LIDAR RADAR GPS IMU] --> B[Localization
Pose Estimation] B --> C[Global Path Planner
HD Map Waypoints] C --> D[Behavior Layer
Lane Change Cruise] D --> E[Trajectory Generator
Reference X Y PSI V] E --> F[Vehicle Control Layer
Steering Throttle Brake] F --> G[Stability Supervisor
ESC Yaw Rate Monitor] G --> H{Stable?} H -->|Yes| I[Execute Steering Throttle] H -->|No| J[Override Differential Brake] I --> K[Vehicle Dynamics] J --> K K --> A ``` ### 4.2 Yaw Stability Control Loop (ESC Functional Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR A[Steering Angle Sensor] --> C[ESC Controller] B[Yaw Rate Gyro] --> C D[Lateral Accel Sensor] --> C E[Wheel Speed Sensors] --> C C --> F[Reference Yaw Rate Calculator] F --> G[Yaw Error e_psi] G --> H[PID or Sliding Mode Controller] H --> I[Brake Actuator Decision] I --> J[Individual Wheel Braking] J --> K[Corrected Yaw Motion] K --> B ``` ### 4.3 Sensor Fusion Architecture (GPS / INS / Wheel Odometry) ```mermaid graph TD A[GPS Receiver
10 Hz Position] --> D[Extended Kalman Filter] B[IMU Gyroscope
200 Hz Yaw Rate] --> D C[Wheel Speed Encoders
100 Hz Velocity] --> D D --> E[Fused State Estimate
X Y Vx Vy Theta] E --> F[Navigation Output] E --> G[Path Tracker] E --> H[Stability Monitor] F --> I[Vehicle Controller] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Steering Throttle Brake] ``` ### 4.4 Path Tracking Controller Comparison (Block View) ```mermaid graph TD A[Reference Path] --> B[Look Ahead Point] B --> C{Pure Pursuit} B --> D{Stanley Controller} B --> E{Model Predictive Control} C --> F[Steering Angle Output] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Steering Actuator] G --> H[Vehicle Motion] H --> I[Pose Feedback] I --> B ``` ### 4.5 Stability Envelope (Phase Plane) ```mermaid graph TD A[Vehicle State e_psi and e_y] --> B{Inside Friction Circle?} B -->|Yes| C[Normal Control Law] B -->|No| D[Stability Intervention ESC] C --> E[Apply Steering Throttle] D --> F[Differential Braking] E --> G[Updated Vehicle State] F --> G G --> A ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **understeer gradient** and explain how it determines the cornering behavior of a vehicle. What is the significance of a **positive understeer gradient** in safety? **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Understeer gradient** $K$ is defined as the rate of change of the Ackermann steering angle with respect to lateral acceleration: > $$K = \frac{m}{L}\left(\frac{l_r}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{l_f}{C_{\alpha r}}\right)$$ > > **Cornering behavior classification:** > - $K > 0$: **Understeer** — the vehicle turns less than the steering wheel commands as lateral acceleration increases. Self-correcting tendency. Stable. > - $K = 0$: **Neutral steer** — yaw rate is independent of lateral acceleration. > - $K < 0$: **Oversteer** — yaw rate increases with lateral acceleration, leading to spin-out. > > A **positive understeer gradient** is the desired safety characteristic because it provides **natural yaw damping** and a **graceful degradation** at the friction limit. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between the **kinematic** and **dynamic** bicycle models used in vehicle control. Under what operating conditions is the kinematic model inadequate? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Kinematic Bicycle Model | Dynamic Bicycle Model | |---|---|---| | Basis | Pure geometry, no forces | Newton's laws + tire forces | | Speed range | Low ($< 5 \ \text{m/s}$) | Full range (including high speed) | | Tire slip | Assumed zero | Explicitly modeled | | Equations | $\dot{x}=v\cos\theta, \dot{y}=v\sin\theta$ | Includes $F_{yf}, F_{yr}, I_z \ddot{\psi}$ | | Use | Path tracking, parking | Stability control, ESC, MPC | > The kinematic model becomes **inadequate** at high speeds ($> 15$–$20 \ \text{m/s}$) and during **aggressive maneuvers** where tire slip angles are non-negligible. At the friction limit, the kinematic model **cannot predict skidding** or spin-out — hence a dynamic model is mandatory for stability-critical controllers like ESC. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the steady-state yaw rate response $\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{v_x / L}{1 + K v_x^2} \cdot \delta$ of a vehicle using the **dynamic bicycle model** with linear tire characteristics. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A passenger car has the following parameters: $m = 1800 \ \text{kg}$, $L = 2.6 \ \text{m}$, $l_f = 1.25 \ \text{m}$, $l_r = 1.35 \ \text{m}$, $C_{\alpha f} = 90{,}000 \ \text{N/rad}$, $C_{\alpha r} = 95{,}000 \ \text{N/rad}$. The vehicle is traveling at $25 \ \text{m/s}$ and the driver commands a steering angle of $3^\circ$. Calculate the **understeer gradient**, the **steady-state yaw rate**, the **steady-state lateral acceleration**, and the **critical speed** beyond which the vehicle becomes dynamically unstable. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key assumptions: - Steady-state cornering ($\dot{v}_y = 0, \ddot{\psi} = 0$). - Small steering angle ($\cos\delta \approx 1, \sin\delta \approx \delta$). - Linear tire model ($F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$). - Small slip angles. The final result is: $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{v_x / L}{1 + K v_x^2} \cdot \delta$$ where $K = \frac{m}{L}\left(\frac{l_r}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{l_f}{C_{\alpha r}}\right)$. **(b) Step 1: Compute understeer gradient:** $$K = \frac{1800}{2.6}\left(\frac{1.35}{90{,}000} - \frac{1.25}{95{,}000}\right)$$ $$K = 692.31 \times \left(1.5 \times 10^{-5} - 1.3158 \times 10^{-5}\right)$$ $$K = 692.31 \times 1.842 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.275 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{rad/(m/s)}^2$$ Since $K > 0$, the vehicle is **understeering** (stable). **Step 2: Steady-state yaw rate:** Convert $\delta = 3^\circ = 0.0524 \ \text{rad}$. $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{25/2.6}{1 + (1.275 \times 10^{-3})(625)} \times 0.0524$$ $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{9.615}{1.797} \times 0.0524 = 5.350 \times 0.0524$$ $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} \approx 0.280 \ \text{rad/s} \approx 16.05 \ \deg/\text{s}$$ **Step 3: Steady-state lateral acceleration:** $$a_y = v_x \cdot \dot{\psi}_{ss} = 25 \times 0.280 = 7.0 \ \text{m/s}^2 \approx 0.71g$$ > [!WARNING] > $a_y = 0.71g$ is approaching the friction limit for dry asphalt ($\approx 0.9g$). The tire may be entering the **nonlinear saturation region** of the brush tire model. The linear assumption is now marginal. **Step 4: Critical speed:** Since $K > 0$, the denominator $1 + K v^2$ **never becomes zero** for finite speed. Therefore, this **understeering vehicle has no finite critical speed** — it remains stable at all velocities. (Critical speed formula $v_{cr} = \sqrt{-1/K}$ applies only to oversteering vehicles with $K < 0$.) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **Pure Pursuit** path tracking algorithm. Derive its steering law $\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2 L x}{L_d^2}\right)$ and discuss the **trade-off** in choosing the look-ahead distance $L_d$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** based sensor fusion for GPS/INS integration in autonomous vehicle navigation. Write down the **prediction and update equations** and explain the role of the **Kalman Gain** in balancing prediction trust and measurement trust. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes for the geometric derivation. The Pure Pursuit algorithm geometrically inscribes a circle between the rear axle and the look-ahead point on the path. The steering law is: $$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2 L x}{L_d^2}\right)$$ **Trade-off in $L_d$ selection:** - **Small $L_d$** → tighter tracking, but **oscillatory** behavior and high steering effort. - **Large $L_d$** → smooth tracking, but **cutting corners** (larger steady-state cross-track error). - **Rule of thumb:** $L_d = v \cdot t_{look}$ where $t_{look} \approx 1$–$3$ seconds. **(b)** The EKF fuses **low-frequency, low-noise GPS** (absolute position) with **high-frequency, drifting IMU** (relative motion) to give an optimal pose estimate. **Prediction Step:** $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$ $$\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1|k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$$ **Update Step:** $$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$$ $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1})$$ **Role of Kalman Gain $\mathbf{K}_k$:** - If $\mathbf{R}_k$ (measurement noise) is large → $\mathbf{K}_k$ is small → estimate relies on prediction. - If $\mathbf{Q}_k$ (process noise) is large → $\mathbf{K}_k$ is large → estimate trusts measurements more. - $\mathbf{K}_k$ **adaptively weights** the two information sources based on their relative certainty. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1}$ (omitting the inner term $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R}$). The full expression must be used, or the gain will be dimensionally inconsistent. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN THIS MODULE:** > 1. **Unit confusion:** Slip angle is in **radians** when used in $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$. Converting from degrees requires multiplication by $\pi/180$. > 2. **Critical speed only for oversteer:** Do NOT compute $v_{cr} = \sqrt{-1/K}$ for an understeering vehicle — the answer is "no finite critical speed" and you must say so explicitly. > 3. **Linear tire model validity:** Students forget to mention that $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ holds only for small $\alpha$ ($< 4^\circ$–$6^\circ$). At higher slip, the brush/Pacejka model is required. > 4. **Pure Pursuit look-ahead:** If you state $L_d$ as a constant, you lose 1 mark. Mention **velocity-proportional look-ahead** $L_d = v \cdot t_{look}$ for full credit. > 5. **EKF Jacobians:** KTU expects the linearization step $\mathbf{F} = \partial f / \partial \mathbf{x}$ to be mentioned, not just the KF equations. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation** > > **Stability Fundamentals:** > - Two bicycle models: **kinematic** (geometry, low speed) vs. **dynamic** (forces, all speeds). > - Slip angle $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(v_y / v_x)$; linear tire region: $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$. > - Understeer gradient $K > 0 \Rightarrow$ stable; $K < 0 \Rightarrow$ oversteer; $v_{cr} = \sqrt{-1/K}$ only for oversteer. > - Steady-state yaw rate: $\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{v_x / L}{1 + K v_x^2} \cdot \delta$. > - ESC corrects yaw error via **differential braking** of individual wheels. > - Friction circle: $\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2} \leq \mu g$. > > **Navigation Fundamentals:** > - **GPS**: global position, low frequency ($1$–$10 \ \text{Hz}$), good long-term accuracy. > - **IMU**: high frequency ($100$–$1000 \ \text{Hz}$), drifts over time. > - **Wheel odometry**: medium frequency, no drift in rotation, drifts in translation. > - **EKF** optimally fuses all three by minimizing covariance. > > **Path Tracking Controllers:** > - **Pure Pursuit**: $\delta = \tan^{-1}(2 L x / L_d^2)$ — simple, geometric, low-speed. > - **Stanley**: $\delta = \theta_e + \tan^{-1}(k e_{fa} / v)$ — smooth, speed-adaptive. > - **MPC**: receding-horizon optimization, handles constraints, gold standard for AVs. > > **Design Rules of Thumb:** > - Look-ahead time: $t_{look} \approx 1$–$3 \ \text{s}$. > - MPC horizon: $N = 10$–$20$ steps at $50$–$100 \ \text{ms}$ sample time. > - Yaw rate sensor: $\geq 100 \ \text{Hz}$ update. > - Tire cornering stiffness: $C_\alpha \approx 80{,}000 \ \text{N/rad}$ per tire (passenger car). > > **Key Constants:** > - $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ > - $\mu_{\text{dry}} \approx 0.9$, $\mu_{\text{wet}} \approx 0.6$ > - $\alpha_{f,\max} \approx 4^\circ$–$6^\circ$ (linear region) > - $a_{y,\text{comfort}} \approx 0.2$–$0.3g$ (passenger comfort threshold)

topics.title: Real-time Decision-making and Obstacle Avoidance
Cloud Computing and Edge Processing - Role of Cloud Computing in Autonomous Vehicle Ecosystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing and Edge Processing - Role of Cloud Computing in Autonomous Vehicle Ecosystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Computing** in the context of **Autonomous Vehicle (AV) Ecosystems** refers to the on-demand delivery of computing, storage, artificial intelligence (AI) inference, and analytics services over a high-bandwidth network infrastructure (typically 4G LTE / 5G / DSRC) that enables a fleet of connected vehicles to offload computationally intensive tasks, share aggregated driving intelligence, and receive centralized software updates from a remote **Hyperscale Data Center**.

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for ADAS and Autonomous Vehicles, the cloud acts as the **global brain** of the autonomous ecosystem — performing tasks that are **memory-bound, latency-tolerant, and compute-intensive**, while a complementary **Edge Computing** layer (RSU, MEC server, in-vehicle ECU) handles **latency-critical, safety-critical, real-time perception** tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Cloud computing is the *off-board intelligence backbone* that complements the on-board perception stack. The KTU examiner expects you to clearly distinguish between **Latency-Critical Edge Tasks** (≤ 10 ms) and **Latency-Tolerant Cloud Tasks** (≥ 100 ms).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a fully autonomous car through a busy city intersection:

- **In-Vehicle Compute (Edge):** Your car's own "reflexes" — like a cricket batsman hitting a fast ball. It reacts in milliseconds using only what it sees *right now*.
- **Edge Servers / MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing):** The team's "coach standing at the boundary rope" — communicates with the batsman in real-time over short distances.
- **Cloud Computing:** The entire **BCCI database** sitting in Mumbai — contains historical data of every ball ever bowled, player statistics, weather analytics, and pitch reports. It is **massively powerful** but **far away**, so it cannot react to a single delivery instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Just like the BCCI database does not bowl the next ball**, the cloud does not steer the car. It provides the **strategic, aggregated, learned intelligence** that makes every car in the fleet smarter with every kilometre driven.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Cloud-Connected AV Systems

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Safety-Critical Latency (E/E braking) | **≤ 10** | ms |
| Cloud Round-Trip Time (RTT) | **50 – 200** | ms |
| Per AV Daily Data Generation | **4 – 20** | TB/day |
| 5G Peak Downlink | **10 Gbps** | Gbps |
| 5G Uplink for AV telemetry | **100 – 1000** | Mbps |
| HD Map Update Cadence | **Hourly / Weekly** | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of a Connected AV

A modern AV ecosystem operates across **three distinct computational tiers**. KTU examiners frequently frame questions around the boundaries and responsibilities of each tier.

### Tier 1: In-Vehicle Edge (On-Board)
- **Hardware:** NVIDIA Drive Orin, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride, Mobileye EyeQ6
- **Tasks:** Sensor fusion, object detection, trajectory planning, AEB actuation
- **Latency Budget:** **≤ 10 ms** end-to-end
- **Operating Environment:** ISO 26262 ASIL-D certified

### Tier 2: Network Edge (MEC / RSU)
- **Hardware:** ARM-based servers at 5G gNB base stations
- **Tasks:** V2X message relay, cooperative perception, intersection management
- **Latency Budget:** **10 – 20 ms** (5G URLLC)
- **Standard:** ETSI MEC Framework (ETSI GS MEC 003)

### Tier 3: Cloud (Hyperscale Data Center)
- **Hardware:** GPU/TPU clusters (NVIDIA H100, Google TPU v5)
- **Tasks:** HD map generation, fleet learning, OTA updates, simulation (CARLA, Waymax)
- **Latency Budget:** **100 ms – several seconds** (tolerable)
- **Standard:** ISO/SAE 21434 (Cybersecurity)

## 2.2 Why is the Cloud Indispensable? — The Operational Logic

1. **HD Map Generation:** A single centimetre-precision HD map of one city requires **~ 4 TB**. No vehicle can store the maps of an entire country. The cloud aggregates LiDAR sweeps from thousands of cars → processes them via SLAM → republishes updated maps.
2. **Fleet Learning (Federated / Centralized):** When one car encounters a rare "edge case" (e.g., a baby pram being pushed by a dog), it uploads the scenario. The cloud retrains the neural network and **pushes the improved weights** to the entire fleet via OTA.
3. **OTA (Over-The-Air) Software Updates:** Tesla famously pushes **~ 1 GB** of model updates per release. Only the cloud can store and distribute such payloads efficiently.
4. **Big-Data Analytics:** Insurance risk scoring, traffic prediction, and predictive maintenance all require aggregating petabytes of telemetry.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | End-to-End Latency Budget | $T_{e2e} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}$ | Must be **≤ 100 ms** for L3 autonomy |
| 2 | Effective Throughput (Shannon-Hartley) | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Channel capacity in bits/sec |
| 3 | Required Bandwidth per AV | $B = \dfrac{D_{daily}}{T_{upload\ window}} \cdot 8$ | $D_{daily}$ in bytes, result in bps |
| 4 | Edge vs Cloud Decision Threshold | If $\tau_{req} \le 10\text{ ms} \Rightarrow \text{Edge}$ | Latency-based task partitioning |
| 5 | Storage Cost (Cloud) | $C_{total} = N_{veh} \cdot S_{HD\ map} \cdot \rho_{replication}$ | $N_{veh}$ = fleet size, $S$ = HD map size |
| 6 | Federated Aggregation | $w_{global} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{n_k}{n}\, w_k$ | Weighted model averaging (FedAvg) |
| 7 | Edge Computing Energy Model | $E_{total} = P_{compute} \cdot T_{comp} + P_{comm} \cdot T_{comm}$ | Joules consumed per inference |
| 8 | 5G URLLC Reliability | $P_{loss} \le 10^{-5}$ for 1 ms packet | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency target |
| 9 | V2X Message Frequency | $f_{BSM} = 10\text{ Hz}$ | Basic Safety Message rate (SAE J2945) |
| 10 | Data Reduction Ratio | $R_{red} = 1 - \dfrac{D_{compressed}}{D_{raw}}$ | Compression efficiency $\in [0,1]$ |

> **Notation Note:** Use $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ to avoid Markdown pipe conflicts — the formula sheet above uses `\vert` and `\dfrac` LaTeX commands to render cleanly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Cloud-AV Integration |
|---|---|
| **Smart Cities** | Cloud-fed traffic signal optimization reduces congestion by up to **20%** |
| **Insurance (UBI)** | Telemetry-based dynamic premium calculation |
| **Logistics (Truck Platooning)** | Cloud coordinates platoon formation across continents |
| **Ride-Sharing (Robotaxi)** | Cloud dispatches closest idle AV to passenger in **< 60 s** |
| **Cybersecurity** | Cloud-based anomaly detection flags compromised ECUs across the fleet |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bandwidth Required for HD Map Updates

**Problem Statement:** A fleet of $N_{veh} = 50{,}000$ autonomous vehicles must download incremental HD map updates. Each delta update has size $S_{\Delta} = 200\text{ MB}$. The cloud must complete the entire fleet rollout within $T_{window} = 24\text{ hours}$. Compute the **aggregate downlink bandwidth** required at the cloud edge server.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the total data volume to be served.**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= N_{veh} \cdot S_{\Delta} \\
&= 50{,}000 \cdot 200 \times 10^6\ \text{bytes} \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^{13}\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert the rollout time window into seconds.**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{window} &= 24 \cdot 3600\ \text{s} \\
&= 86{,}400\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the aggregate throughput required.**
$$\begin{aligned}
B_{agg} &= \dfrac{D_{total}}{T_{window}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{13}\ \text{bytes}}{86{,}400\ \text{s}} \\
&= 1.157 \times 10^{8}\ \text{bytes/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Gigabits per second for engineering readability.**
$$\begin{aligned}
B_{agg} &= 1.157 \times 10^{8} \cdot 8\ \text{bits/s} \\
&= 9.26 \times 10^{8}\ \text{bps} \\
&\approx \mathbf{0.93\ Gbps}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is **only the aggregate throughput** assuming perfectly serialized downloads. In practice, peaks occur when fleets connect simultaneously, forcing cloud architects to provision **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** edge caches regionally.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Federated Learning Aggregation (FedAvg)

**Problem Statement:** Three vehicles in a federated learning round submit local model weights $w_1, w_2, w_3$ trained on $n_1 = 4000$, $n_2 = 6000$, $n_3 = 10{,}000$ samples respectively. Compute the aggregated global weight $w_{global}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute total samples across participating vehicles.**
$$\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= n_1 + n_2 + n_3 \\
&= 4000 + 6000 + 10{,}000 \\
&= 20{,}000\ \text{samples}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Define the weighting factor for each vehicle.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \dfrac{n_1}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{4000}{20{,}000} = 0.20 \\
\alpha_2 &= \dfrac{n_2}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{6000}{20{,}000} = 0.30 \\
\alpha_3 &= \dfrac{n_3}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{20{,}000} = 0.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the weights sum to unity (sanity check).**
$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \alpha_3 &= 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.50 \\
&= 1.00\ \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the FedAvg aggregation formula.**
$$\begin{aligned}
w_{global} &= \sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_k \cdot w_k \\
&= 0.20 \cdot w_1 + 0.30 \cdot w_2 + 0.50 \cdot w_3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The aggregation is **weighted by sample count**, not by uniform averaging. A vehicle that trained on more data exerts proportionally greater influence on the global model. This is the principle behind **privacy-preserving distributed learning**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Channel Capacity for V2X Uplink

**Problem Statement:** A V2X uplink channel has bandwidth $B = 20\text{ MHz}$ and a measured SNR of $S/N = 15\text{ dB}$. Compute the maximum theoretical data rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from decibels to linear scale.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{S}{N}\bigg\vert_{linear} &= 10^{\frac{SNR_{dB}}{10}} \\
&= 10^{\frac{15}{10}} \\
&= 10^{1.5} \\
&\approx 31.62
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Shannon-Hartley theorem.**
$$\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 31.62) \\
&= 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(32.62)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the binary logarithm.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\log_2(32.62) &= \dfrac{\ln(32.62)}{\ln(2)} \\
&= \dfrac{3.484}{0.693} \\
&\approx 5.027\ \text{bits/s/Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final capacity.**
$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 5.027 \\
&\approx 1.005 \times 10^8\ \text{bps} \\
&\approx \mathbf{100.5\ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Verdict:** This capacity comfortably supports the V2X telemetry stream of **~ 25 Mbps** (compressed LiDAR + camera + radar fusion output), with **4× headroom** for retransmission and channel variations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Edge vs Cloud Decision Framework

| Task | Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Emergency AEB actuation | **Edge** | Latency-critical (**≤ 10 ms**); cloud RTT = 50 ms+ |
| Pedestrian detection (camera) | **Edge** | Real-time; tied to safety function |
| HD map tile refresh | **Cloud** | Latency-tolerant (**hours**); storage-heavy |
| OTA firmware deployment | **Cloud** | Multi-GB payload; staggered delivery |
| Traffic light SPaT info | **Edge (MEC)** | V2I real-time; < 100 ms required |
| Fleet-wide anomaly detection | **Cloud** | Requires aggregation of petabytes |
| Cooperative lane merge | **Edge (MEC)** | Multi-vehicle coordination in < 50 ms |
| Model retraining | **Cloud** | Compute-bound (GPU-days); one-time per week |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **information flow** between in-vehicle edge, MEC edge, and the cloud backend. Each node ID is alphanumeric (alpha-prefixed) and labels contain raw uppercase text only.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VEHICLE["IN VEHICLE EDGE LAYER"]
        nodeA1["SENSOR STACK CAMERA LIDAR RADAR GPS IMU"]
        nodeA2["IN VEHICLE ECU NVIDIA ORIN SNAPDRAGON RIDE"]
        nodeA3["PERCEPTION AND PLANNING MODULE"]
        nodeA4["ACTUATOR INTERFACE BRAKE STEER THROTTLE"]
    end

    subgraph MEC["NETWORK EDGE MEC LAYER"]
        nodeB1["5G GNB BASE STATION"]
        nodeB2["MEC SERVER COOPERATIVE PERCEPTION"]
        nodeB3["RSU ROADSIDE UNIT V2X"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD HYPERSCALE LAYER"]
        nodeC1["INGEST GATEWAY KAFKA MQTT"]
        nodeC2["HD MAP FACTORY SLAM PIPELINE"]
        nodeC3["FLEET LEARNING GPU CLUSTER"]
        nodeC4["OTA UPDATE CDN"]
        nodeC5["ANALYTICS AND TELEMETRY DB"]
    end

    nodeA1 -->|"RAW DATA"| nodeA2
    nodeA2 -->|"OBJECTS TRACKS"| nodeA3
    nodeA3 -->|"CONTROL CMD"| nodeA4
    nodeA3 -->|"ANONYMIZED TELEMETRY"| nodeB1
    nodeA3 -->|"V2X BSM MSG"| nodeB3
    nodeB1 -->|"UPLINK PACKET"| nodeB2
    nodeB2 -->|"COOPERATIVE PERCEPTION"| nodeA3
    nodeB3 -->|"SPAT MAP INFO"| nodeA3
    nodeB1 -->|"TLS ENCRYPTED TCP"| nodeC1
    nodeC1 -->|"STREAM BATCH"| nodeC2
    nodeC1 -->|"STREAM BATCH"| nodeC3
    nodeC1 -->|"STREAM BATCH"| nodeC5
    nodeC2 -->|"MAP TILES DELTA"| nodeB2
    nodeC3 -->|"NEW MODEL WEIGHTS"| nodeB2
    nodeC4 -->|"OTA PAYLOAD"| nodeB2
    nodeB2 -->|"DOWNLINK OTA OR MODEL OR MAP"| nodeA2
```

## 4.2 Latency-Tier Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL1["SAFETY CRITICAL LT 10 MS\nIN VEHICLE ECU"]
    nodeL2["REAL TIME 10 TO 50 MS\nMEC RSU"]
    nodeL3["NEAR REAL TIME 50 TO 200 MS\n5G EDGE CLOUD"]
    nodeL4["LATENCY TOLERANT GT 200 MS\nHYPERSCALE CLOUD"]

    nodeL1 ==>|"E BRAKING\nLANE KEEPING\nAEB"| nodeL2
    nodeL2 ==>|"COOPERATIVE PERCEPTION\nPLATOON JOIN\nV2X SPAT"| nodeL3
    nodeL3 ==>|"HD MAP REFRESH\nSOFTWARE UPDATE\nFLEET LEARNING"| nodeL4
```

## 4.3 Data-Flow Block Diagram — OTA Update via Cloud

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    step1["OEM CLOUD RELEASES NEW FIRMWARE V2 4 1 SIGNED BUNDLE"]
    step2["CDN DISTRIBUTES BUNDLE TO 5G MEC EDGE NODES"]
    step3["MEC PUSHES DELTA PATCH TO VEHICLE OVER 5G"]
    step4["VEHICLE ECU VERIFIES SIGNATURE ISO 21434"]
    step5["A B PARTITION INSTALL BG UPDATE"]
    step6["REBOOT AND ACTIVATE NEW BUILD"]
    step7["VEHICLE REPORTS STATUS TO CLOUD ACK"]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define cloud computing as it applies to the autonomous vehicle ecosystem. List any four functions performed by the cloud that cannot be efficiently executed on-board the vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cloud computing in the AV ecosystem is the delivery of on-demand, scalable compute, storage, and AI services over a high-bandwidth wireless network from a remote hyperscale data center, enabling AVs to offload tasks that are **computationally expensive, memory-bound, or latency-tolerant**.

**Four cloud-exclusive functions:**
1. **HD map generation and refresh** — storage-heavy (TB-scale), infrequent updates.
2. **Fleet-wide federated learning** — requires aggregating gradients from thousands of cars.
3. **OTA firmware/software deployment** — multi-GB payloads, signed and staged.
4. **Big-data analytics and predictive maintenance** — requires petabyte-scale telemetry aggregation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners allocate **1 mark** for the definition and **2 marks** for the four functions. Mention the *reason* the cloud is preferred (e.g., "storage too large for on-board SSD") for full marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare edge computing and cloud computing in autonomous vehicles with respect to latency, data volume, and compute capability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Edge Computing | Cloud Computing |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | **≤ 10 – 20 ms** (URLLC grade) | **50 ms – seconds** |
| **Data Volume** | Small, processed in real-time | Petabyte-scale aggregation |
| **Compute Capability** | Limited (100s of TOPS) | Virtually unlimited (GPU/TPU clusters) |
| **Safety Certification** | ISO 26262 ASIL-D required | Not safety-critical |
| **Connectivity Dependency** | Operates offline | Always requires network |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Use a **3-column comparison table**. Any missing column header or unit costs you **0.5 mark**. Always include units (ms, TOPS, TB).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three-tier architecture** of cloud-connected autonomous vehicles. Discuss the role and latency budget of each tier with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fleet of $N = 100{,}000$ autonomous taxis uploads telemetry to the cloud. Each vehicle uploads $D_v = 500\text{ MB}$ per day. Compute the **daily aggregate ingress** at the cloud gateway. If the cloud uses **30× replication** across data centers for redundancy, what is the **total storage** consumed per day? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question A

**Part (a) — Three-Tier Architecture:**

The cloud-connected AV ecosystem is structured into three distinct tiers:

1. **In-Vehicle Edge Tier (Tier 1):**
   - Hardware: NVIDIA Drive Orin (275 TOPS), Mobileye EyeQ6, automotive-grade SoCs.
   - Tasks: Sensor fusion, object detection, trajectory planning, AEB actuation.
   - **Latency Budget:** **≤ 10 ms**.
   - Safety standard: **ISO 26262 ASIL-D**.

2. **Network Edge / MEC Tier (Tier 2):**
   - Hardware: ARM/x86 servers co-located with 5G gNB base stations.
   - Tasks: Cooperative perception, intersection collision avoidance, SPaT relay.
   - **Latency Budget:** **10 – 50 ms**.
   - Standard: **ETSI MEC 003**.

3. **Cloud / Hyperscale Tier (Tier 3):**
   - Hardware: GPU clusters (H100), object storage (S3-class).
   - Tasks: HD map generation, federated learning, OTA dispatch, analytics.
   - **Latency Budget:** **100 ms – minutes**.
   - Standard: **ISO/SAE 21434** (Cybersecurity).

The block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute daily aggregate ingress at the cloud gateway.**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{agg} &= N \cdot D_v \\
&= 100{,}000 \cdot 500 \times 10^6\ \text{bytes} \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{13}\ \text{bytes} \\
&= \mathbf{50\ TB/day\ ingested}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total storage with 30× replication.**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= D_{agg} \cdot \rho_{replication} \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot 30 \\
&= 1.5 \times 10^{15}\ \text{bytes} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.5\ PB/day\ stored}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Annual storage projection (for engineering context).**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_{annual} &= 1.5 \times 10^{15} \cdot 365 \\
&= 5.475 \times 10^{17}\ \text{bytes} \\
&\approx \mathbf{547.5\ PB/year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently **forget to convert MB → bytes** or **omit the replication factor**. Each missing step costs **1 mark**. Always show unit conversion explicitly. Also remember: **TB uses base-10 ($10^{12}$)**, while TiB uses base-2 ($2^{40}$). KTU expects base-10 unless stated otherwise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Over-The-Air (OTA) update workflow** in autonomous vehicles. Discuss the role of **A/B partitioning** and **ISO/SAE 21434** in securing these updates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two vehicles participate in a **federated learning round**. Vehicle 1 has $n_1 = 8000$ samples and local weights $w_1 = [0.4,\ 0.2]$; Vehicle 2 has $n_2 = 12{,}000$ samples and local weights $w_2 = [0.1,\ 0.3]$. Compute the **globally aggregated weights** using FedAvg. If the cloud applies **differential privacy noise** $\epsilon = 0.01$ per dimension, what is the **perturbed global weight** for dimension 1? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question B

**Part (a) — OTA Workflow:**

The OTA update flow consists of seven stages (as illustrated in Section 4.3):

1. **OEM builds and signs the firmware bundle** at the cloud.
2. **CDN distributes** the bundle to regional MEC nodes.
3. **MEC pushes the delta patch** to the vehicle over 5G.
4. **Vehicle ECU verifies the cryptographic signature** (RSA-4096 / ECDSA).
5. **A/B partitioning** writes the new image to the inactive partition while the active partition continues to run the current software.
6. **Reboot and swap** to the newly updated partition.
7. **Vehicle reports ACK** back to the cloud.

**Role of A/B Partitioning:** Guarantees a **fallback path** — if the new firmware fails boot or crashes, the ECU automatically rolls back to the previous known-good partition. This is a **functional safety requirement** for ASIL-D systems.
