**Role of ISO/SAE 21434:** Provides the **cybersecurity engineering framework** for the entire OTA pipeline — covering threat modelling, cryptographic signing, secure boot, intrusion detection, and incident response.

**Part (b) — Federated Aggregation with Differential Privacy:**

**Step 1 — Compute sample weights.**
$$\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= 8000 + 12{,}000 = 20{,}000 \\
\alpha_1 &= \dfrac{8000}{20{,}000} = 0.40 \\
\alpha_2 &= \dfrac{12{,}000}{20{,}000} = 0.60
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate each dimension using FedAvg.**

For dimension 1:
$$\begin{aligned}
w_{global}^{(1)} &= \alpha_1 \cdot w_1^{(1)} + \alpha_2 \cdot w_2^{(1)} \\
&= 0.40 \cdot 0.4 + 0.60 \cdot 0.1 \\
&= 0.16 + 0.06 \\
&= \mathbf{0.22}
\end{aligned}$$

For dimension 2:
$$\begin{aligned}
w_{global}^{(2)} &= \alpha_1 \cdot w_1^{(2)} + \alpha_2 \cdot w_2^{(2)} \\
&= 0.40 \cdot 0.2 + 0.60 \cdot 0.3 \\
&= 0.08 + 0.18 \\
&= \mathbf{0.26}
\end{aligned}$$

Thus, $w_{global} = [0.22,\ 0.26]$.

**Step 3 — Apply differential privacy noise $\epsilon = 0.01$ to dimension 1.**

The Laplace mechanism perturbs by noise drawn from $\text{Laplace}(0, 1/\epsilon)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Scale parameter}\ b &= \dfrac{1}{\epsilon} = \dfrac{1}{0.01} = 100
\end{aligned}$$

For this analytical problem (assuming one standard perturbation sample $= b$ for illustration):
$$\begin{aligned}
w_{global}^{(1),\ perturbed} &= 0.22 + b \\
&= 0.22 + 100 \\
&= \mathbf{100.22}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note for KTU candidates:** The Laplace noise above is **mathematically correct but numerically extreme** because $\epsilon = 0.01$ is an extremely tight privacy budget. In practice, real-world deployments use $\epsilon \in [0.1, 10]$. The question asks for the *mechanism application*, not for a realistic deployment value. Examiners award **2 marks** for correctly identifying $b = 1/\epsilon$ and **2 marks** for the perturbed value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> 1. **Forgetting the weighted average** (treating both vehicles as equal) — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Misidentifying the Laplace scale** as $\epsilon$ instead of $1/\epsilon$ — costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing FedAvg with FedProx or FedNova** — KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests only **FedAvg**.
> 4. **Skipping the OTA diagram** — the diagram itself carries **2 of the 7 marks** in part (a).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Cloud Computing in AV Ecosystems**

- ☐ **Three Tiers:** In-Vehicle Edge, MEC Edge, Cloud Hyperscale — know the latency budget of each.
- ☐ **Latency Rule of Thumb:** ≤ 10 ms = Edge | 10–50 ms = MEC | ≥ 100 ms = Cloud.
- ☐ **HD Map Storage:** A single city HD map ≈ **4 TB** — impossible to store fully on-board.
- ☐ **Per-AV Data:** Each autonomous car generates **4 – 20 TB/day** raw sensor data.
- ☐ **5G URLLC Specs:** Latency **≤ 1 ms**, reliability **99.999%** ($P_{loss} \le 10^{-5}$).
- ☐ **OTA Security:** ISO/SAE 21434 mandates cryptographic signing + secure boot + rollback.
- ☐ **A/B Partitioning:** Mandatory for fail-safe OTA in ASIL-D systems.
- ☐ **FedAvg Aggregation:** Weights are **proportional to local sample count**, not uniform.
- ☐ **Federated Learning Privacy:** Raw data never leaves the vehicle; only weights are uploaded.
- ☐ **Shannon Capacity Formula:** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — convert SNR from dB to linear via $10^{SNR_{dB}/10}$.
- ☐ **Cloud Standards:** ISO/SAE 21434 (Cybersecurity), ETSI MEC 003 (Edge), ISO 26262 (Safety).
- ☐ **CDN Role:** Reduces cloud egress load by caching OTA payloads at regional MEC nodes.
- ☐ **Telemetry Compression:** Compression ratio $R_{red} = 1 - D_{compressed}/D_{raw}$ directly affects uplink cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question says *"Discuss the role of cloud in autonomous vehicles,"* always structure your answer as **(1) What it does, (2) Why it must be cloud (not edge), (3) Real-world example, (4) Limitation/Challenge**. Examiners reward structured, layered answers over descriptive prose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Edge Processing for Real-time Data Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Edge Processing for Real-time Data Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Edge Processing** in the context of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) refers to the paradigm of performing computational tasks — including sensor data acquisition, signal conditioning, feature extraction, deep-learning inference, sensor fusion, and control-loop actuation — *locally on or near the data source* (i.e., inside the vehicle, on the Electronic Control Units, SoCs, FPGAs, or dedicated AI accelerators) rather than transmitting raw data to a remote cloud or central server for processing.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, Edge Processing is formally positioned as the **on-board, time-deterministic compute layer** of the ADAS/AV stack, sitting between the *raw sensor layer* (cameras, radar, LiDAR, IMU) and the *actuation/decision layer* (brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire, driver-warning HMI). The system must satisfy **real-time, hard-deadline constraints** of the order of **10 ms to 100 ms** depending on the safety-criticality of the function (ISO 26262 ASIL-D functions typically require deterministic latency ≤ **10 ms**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Module 3):** Edge processing is treated as the *enabling pillar* of Level-3 and above autonomous driving, because round-trip-latency to the cloud (typically **50–200 ms** even on 5G) is unacceptable for emergency braking, lane-keeping, and obstacle avoidance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a self-driving car as a **human reflex action**. When a child suddenly runs in front of a moving car, the driver does **not** stop to upload the visual scene to a remote brain to decide whether to brake. The brain, the eyes, and the feet — all part of one *body* — act together in a fraction of a second. This **"thinking right where the action happens"** is exactly *edge processing*.

The opposite extreme — **cloud processing** — would be like the driver closing his eyes, broadcasting the road scene to a far-away surgeon who has to call back and shout "BRAKE!" — by which time the accident has already happened.

In engineering terms:
- **Cloud = Brain in another city (slow, but unlimited brainpower)**
- **Edge = Spinal-cord reflex (local, fast, bounded brainpower)**
- **ADAS/AV = A vehicle that must overwhelmingly rely on its spinal-cord reflexes**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Edge Processing

The following **key performance metrics** govern edge-processing design in ADAS:

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| End-to-end perception latency | **10 – 100** | ms |
| Throughput (frames per second) | **30 – 60** | FPS |
| Power budget per SoC | **5 – 30** | Watts |
| Operating temperature (Automotive Grade 2/3) | **−40 to +85** | °C |
| Functional safety level | **ASIL-B to ASIL-D** | — |
| Compute throughput (TOPS) | **10 – 1000** | TOPS |
| Data-rate per camera (4K @ 30 fps RAW) | **~ 1.5** | Gbps |
| LiDAR point-cloud rate (128-layer) | **~ 10** | Million pts/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **TOPS** = Tera (10¹²) Operations Per Second. Modern automotive SoCs like **NVIDIA Orin** deliver **~ 254 TOPS** while **Qualcomm Ride** delivers up to **~ 700 TOPS** of integer-8 inference throughput.

## 1.4 Why Edge Processing is Non-Negotiable for ADAS/AV

1. **Latency Determinism:** Hard real-time deadlines cannot tolerate network jitter or radio handovers.
2. **Bandwidth Economics:** A single autonomous vehicle can generate **~ 4 TB/hour** of raw sensor data — economically and physically impossible to upload continuously.
3. **Connectivity Independence:** Tunnels, rural highways, parking garages — cloud is unavailable. Safety must not vanish with the cellular signal.
4. **Privacy & Regulatory Compliance:** GDPR, ISO 21434 (cybersecurity), and national transport laws restrict transmission of license-plate and face data.
5. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262):** Distributed cloud paths violate the *freedom from interference* requirement for ASIL-D functions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Edge-Processing Computational Stack

The on-board edge stack in an ADAS/AV is organised as a layered pipeline:

1. **Sensor I/O Layer** — MIPI CSI-2 (cameras), CAN-FD / Automotive Ethernet (radar), SPI/PCIe (LiDAR).
2. **Pre-Processing Layer** — Image Signal Processor (ISP), radar FFT, LiDAR point-cloud decoding.
3. **Perception Layer** — CNN-based object detection (YOLO, EfficientDet), semantic segmentation, lane detection, multi-object tracking.
4. **Fusion Layer** — Kalman / Extended Kalman / Particle filters fusing camera + radar + LiDAR + IMU.
5. **Planning Layer** — Behavioural planner, trajectory planner, motion controller (MPC / PID).
6. **Actuation Layer** — Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) issuing brake, steering, throttle commands over **CAN, LIN, FlexRay, or Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1)**.

## 2.2 Real-Time Task Model

Each compute task τᵢ in the edge pipeline is characterised by the **Liu & Layland real-time model**:

- **Period (Tᵢ):** How often the task is invoked (e.g., perception loop at 33 ms ≈ 30 Hz).
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET, Cᵢ):** The upper bound on how long the task may run.
- **Deadline (Dᵢ):** The maximum time the result is allowed to take.
- **Phase (φᵢ):** The first invocation time.

A task set is **schedulable** under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** if and only if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

As $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches **$\ln(2) \approx 0.693$**, meaning RMS can theoretically use at most ~69.3% of the CPU without missing a deadline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** In ADAS, deadlines are often *equal* to periods (Dᵢ = Tᵢ). In safety-critical *braking* tasks, the deadline is *harder* (Dᵢ < Tᵢ) — students must be able to state this distinction.

## 2.3 Latency Budget Decomposition

The end-to-end latency from sensor photon → actuation command is decomposed as:

$$L_{e2e} = L_{sensor} + L_{readout} + L_{pre} + L_{perception} + L_{fusion} + L_{plan} + L_{actuate}$$

A representative Level-3 ADAS budget:

| Stage | Typical Latency (ms) |
|---|---|
| Sensor exposure + readout (camera) | **5** |
| ISP pre-processing | **3** |
| CNN inference (object detection) | **15** |
| Sensor fusion (EKF update) | **2** |
| Trajectory planning (QP solver) | **10** |
| Actuation (brake-by-wire) | **5** |
| **Total** | **~ 40 ms** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU utilisation (RMS schedulability) | $U = \sum C_i / T_i$ | Dimensionless; must be $\le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ |
| 2 | RMS asymptotic utilisation bound | $U_{\infty} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | As $n \to \infty$ |
| 3 | EDF schedulability (exact) | $\sum C_i / T_i \le 1$ | For preemptive uniprocessor |
| 4 | End-to-end latency budget | $L_{e2e} = \sum L_k$ | $k$ = pipeline stages |
| 5 | LiDAR point-cloud data rate | $R = N_{pts} \cdot f_{scan} \cdot B$ | B = bytes/point, default **4** |
| 6 | Camera data rate (raw RGB) | $R = W \cdot H \cdot f_{fps} \cdot 3$ | Bytes/sec; **W=3840, H=2160, f=30 → 0.75 GB/s** |
| 7 | Radar ADC data rate | $R = N_{chirps} \cdot N_{samples} \cdot 2$ | I+Q complex samples |
| 8 | CNN inference time (Amdahl-style) | $T_{inf} = T_{conv} + T_{mem} \cdot \alpha$ | $\alpha$ = memory-bound factor |
| 9 | TOPS for INT8 MAC | $\text{TOPS} = 2 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot N_{MAC}$ | Factor 2 accounts for MAC = 2 ops |
| 10 | Compute-bound threshold | $\frac{\text{OPS}}{\text{BW}} \ge \frac{N_{param}}{8}$ | When above, *compute-bound*; below, *memory-bound* |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NVIDIA Drive Orin / Thor** — central AV compute; runs DriveOS (RTOS) on top of Linux.
- **Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride** — heterogenous SoC with dedicated VPU, GPU, and safety island.
- **Mobileye EyeQ5/EyeQ6** — ultra-low-power (~10 W) ASIC specialised for CNN perception.
- **Xilinx Versal AI Edge (FPGA+AI Engine)** — used in radar preprocessing and sensor pre-fusion.
- **NXP S32G** — vehicle networking + service-oriented gateway processor.

These platforms are the **physical instantiation of edge processing** in production vehicles (BMW, Mercedes, Tesla, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — RMS Utilisation Bound for n = 3 Tasks

**Problem statement:** Three periodic real-time tasks run on a single edge-compute SoC. Determine the maximum theoretical utilisation bound using Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

**Step 1 — State the Liu & Layland bound:**

$$U_{bound}(n) = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute n = 3:**

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the cube root numerically:**

$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

**Step 4 — Subtract 1:**

$$2^{1/3} - 1 \approx 0.2599$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by n = 3:**

$$U_{bound}(3) \approx 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7797$$

**Interpretation:** When only 3 perception tasks share the SoC, RMS guarantees schedulability up to ~**77.97%** CPU utilisation. Beyond this, deadlines may be missed even though the average load looks safe.

> [!NOTE]
> As more tasks are added, the bound shrinks toward **0.693**, leaving ~**30% CPU slack** to absorb jitter, context switches, and interrupts.

## 3.2 Derivation — Latency Budget for a Level-3 Highway Pilot

**Given data** (representative industry values):

- Camera: 4K @ 30 FPS, 3-byte raw RGB
- Front radar: 16 chirps × 1024 samples, complex I+Q
- LiDAR: 128 layers, 200 000 points/scan, 10 Hz rotation
- CNN: ResNet-50 back-bone, INT8 quantised, ~2 GMACs per frame
- SoC: 30 TOPS available

**Step 1 — Camera data rate:**

$$R_{cam} = 3840 \times 2160 \times 30 \times 3 = 746{,}496{,}000 \text{ Bytes/s} \approx 0.746 \text{ GB/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Gbps:**

$$R_{cam} = 0.746 \times 8 = 5.97 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 3 — LiDAR data rate:**

$$R_{lidar} = 200{,}000 \times 10 \times 4 = 8{,}000{,}000 \text{ Bytes/s} = 8 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 4 — Radar data rate:**

$$R_{radar} = 16 \times 1024 \times 2 = 32{,}768 \text{ Bytes/chirp} \approx 32.8 \text{ KB/chirp}$$

For a 20 Hz radar:

$$R_{radar} = 32.8 \times 1024 \times 20 \approx 656 \text{ KB/s}$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate sensor bandwidth:**

$$R_{total} = R_{cam} + R_{lidar} + R_{radar} \approx 754 \text{ MB/s} \approx 6.0 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 6 — CNN compute load:**

$$T_{cnn} = \frac{2 \times 10^9 \text{ MACs}}{30 \times 10^{12} \text{ OPS}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 66.7 \text{ μs}$$

(Pre-processing, post-processing, and NMS typically push this closer to **15–25 ms** in practice.)

**Step 7 — Total end-to-end budget (summing the practical table from §2.3):**

$$L_{e2e} = 5 + 3 + 20 + 2 + 10 + 5 = 45 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 8 — Verify against the 100 ms functional-safety ceiling (ISO 26262):**

$$L_{e2e} = 45 \text{ ms} \le 100 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \text{ (safe)}$$

**Margin remaining for safety:**

$$\Delta L = 100 - 45 = 55 \text{ ms (55% budget slack)}$$

This slack is what engineers rely on to absorb OS jitter, cache misses, and thermal throttling.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Edge vs. Fog vs. Cloud

| Parameter | Edge (in-vehicle) | Fog (RSU / MEC) | Cloud (data centre) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | **1 – 50 ms** | **20 – 100 ms** | **100 – 1000 ms** |
| Compute | **10 – 1000 TOPS** | **10 – 100 TOPS/server** | **> 1 ETOPS** |
| Power budget | **5 – 500 W** | **1 – 10 kW** | **MW scale** |
| Connectivity dependence | **None** | **Local wired/wireless** | **Always connected** |
| Data retention | **Seconds to minutes** | **Minutes to hours** | **Days to years** |
| Failure impact | **Localised, recoverable** | **Regional** | **Fleet-wide** |
| Suitable for | Emergency braking, perception | HD-map crowdsourcing | Model training, OTA analytics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** Question like *"Why is edge processing mandatory for emergency braking?"* — expected answer must cite (i) latency, (ii) connectivity independence, (iii) functional-safety, (iv) bandwidth economics — *not just* "it is faster."

## 3.4 Workload Scheduling — Earliest Deadline First (EDF) vs. RMS

**EDF bound** (exact, for preemptive uniprocessor):

$$U_{EDF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1$$

**Comparison:**

$$\frac{U_{RMS}}{U_{EDF}} = \frac{0.693}{1} = 69.3\%$$

Therefore, EDF can use **100%** of the CPU; RMS only **69.3%** (asymptotically). However, RMS is **simpler to implement** and is the **default in AUTOSAR Classic** — so it dominates automotive deployment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Edge-Processing Pipeline in an Autonomous Vehicle

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the in-vehicle edge pipeline, mapping the data flow from raw sensors through perception, fusion, planning, and finally to actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    camA[Front Camera 4K RAW] --> isp1[Image Signal Processor]
    camB[Surround Camera L/R] --> isp1
    rad1[Front Radar 77GHz] --> fft1[FFT Doppler Processing]
    lid1[LiDAR 128 Layer] --> pc1[Point Cloud Decoder]
    imu1[IMU 6 Axis 200Hz] --> pre1[Pre Filter]
    can1[Vehicle CAN Bus] --> gw1[Central Gateway SoC]
    
    isp1 --> cnn1[CNN Perception Engine]
    fft1 --> fk1[Kalman Filter Tracker]
    pc1 --> fk1
    cnn1 --> fk1
    pre1 --> fk1
    
    fk1 --> fsn1[Sensor Fusion Object List]
    fsn1 --> pln1[Behavioural Planner]
    pln1 --> pln2[Trajectory Planner MPC]
    pln2 --> ctl1[Vehicle Control Unit]
    
    ctl1 --> act1[Brake By Wire]
    ctl1 --> act2[Steer By Wire]
    ctl1 --> act3[Throttle By Wire]
    
    gw1 --> ctl1
```

## 4.2 Edge-Compute SoC Architecture (Heterogeneous Multi-Core)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpu1[ARM Cortex A78 Cluster x8 - Application Linux]
    cpu2[ARM Cortex R52 Lockstep x3 - AUTOSAR Safety Island]
    gpu1[NVIDIA Ampere GPU 2048 CUDA Cores]
    dsp1[Programmable DSP Array for Radar FFT]
    vpu1[Dedicated VPU for INT8 CNN Inference]
    isp2[Hardware ISP Block for Camera]
    mem1[LPDDR5 32 GB Shared Memory]
    eth1[Automotive Ethernet 1000BASE T1 x8]
    pcie1[PCIe Gen4 to External Sensors]
    can2[CAN FD x12 Channels]
    
    cpu1 --- mem1
    cpu2 --- mem1
    gpu1 --- mem1
    dsp1 --- mem1
    vpu1 --- mem1
    isp2 --- mem1
    
    mem1 --- eth1
    mem1 --- pcie1
    mem1 --- can2
```

## 4.3 Real-Time Scheduling — RMS vs. EDF Visual

```mermaid
gantt
    title Real-Time Scheduling on Edge SoC
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    
    section RMS Schedule
    Task 1 Period 30 ms WCET 8 :a1, 0, 8
    Task 2 Period 50 ms WCET 12 :a2, 8, 12
    Task 3 Period 100 ms WCET 18 :a3, 20, 18
    Idle :a4, 38, 12
    
    section EDF Schedule
    Task A Period 30 ms WCET 8 :b1, 0, 8
    Task B Period 50 ms WCET 12 :b2, 8, 12
    Task C Period 100 ms WCET 18 :b3, 20, 18
    Idle :b4, 38, 12
```

## 4.4 Edge vs. Cloud Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Sensor Data Arrives] --> q1{Latency Requirement}
    q1 -- Less than 50 ms --> edge[Process On-Board Edge SoC]
    q1 -- Greater than 500 ms --> cloud[Send to Cloud]
    q1 -- Between 50 and 500 ms --> fog[Offload to MEC Fog Node]
    edge --> act[Actuate Vehicle Control]
    fog --> q2{Result Needed Before}
    q2 -- Deadline --> edge2[Wait for MEC or Fall Back to Edge]
    q2 -- No Deadline --> cloud
    cloud --> ml[Model Training / HD Map Update]
    ml --> ota[OTA Update to Edge]
    ota --> edge
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]** *Define "edge processing" in the context of ADAS. State any two advantages over cloud processing.*

**Model Answer:**
Edge processing is the on-board, in-vehicle execution of compute tasks (perception, fusion, planning, control) on local ECUs/SoCs, *without* offloading raw sensor data to a remote server.  
**Advantages:** (i) deterministic low latency (≤ 100 ms) enabling safety functions such as AEB, (ii) operation independent of cellular connectivity, (iii) reduced uplink bandwidth (~4 TB/h raw data would saturate 5G), (iv) improved data privacy and ISO 21434 compliance.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** *List any three automotive-grade edge-AI SoCs and their typical TOPS ratings.*

**Model Answer:**
(a) **NVIDIA Orin** — ~254 TOPS (INT8), 12 Cortex-A78 + Ampere GPU.  
(b) **Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride** — up to ~700 TOPS (heterogeneous).  
(c) **Mobileye EyeQ6** — ~128 TOPS, ultra-low ~12 W power.  
(d) **NXP S32V** — ~4 TOPS, ASIL-C, vision-only.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-3 Internal Choice Questions

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) —  *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Set A]*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered edge-computing pipeline of a Level-3 autonomous vehicle. Identify the typical latency contributed by each layer.  
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A perception SoC must schedule three tasks using RMS:
> - Task P1: T₁ = 30 ms, C₁ = 6 ms
> - Task P2: T₂ = 50 ms, C₂ = 10 ms
> - Task P3: T₃ = 100 ms, C₃ = 18 ms
> 
> Determine whether the task set is schedulable under RMS. If yes, suggest the maximum additional load that can be added.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Layered Pipeline:** A block diagram is drawn (refer to §4.1) showing:
- Sensor I/O layer → ISP/FFT/LiDAR decoder.
- Pre-processing layer (denoising, rectification).
- Perception layer (CNN detection + segmentation).
- Sensor-fusion layer (EKF/UKF tracker).
- Planning layer (behavioural + trajectory).
- Actuation layer (VCU → brake/steer/throttle).

**Latency table (typical Level-3 values):**

| Layer | Latency (ms) |
|---|---|
| Sensor exposure + readout | 5 |
| ISP / pre-processing | 3 |
| CNN inference | 15–20 |
| Sensor fusion (EKF) | 2 |
| Trajectory planning | 10 |
| Actuation | 5 |
| **Total** | **40–45 ms** |

**Discussion:** This 40–45 ms is comfortably within the **100 ms ISO-26262 ASIL-D budget** and the **150 ms UN ECE R157** Automated Lane Keeping System (ALKS) limit, leaving ~55–60 ms of slack for OS jitter and thermal throttling.

**(b) RMS Schedulability Check:**

**Step 1 — Compute per-task utilisations:**

$$u_1 = \frac{6}{30} = 0.200$$

$$u_2 = \frac{10}{50} = 0.200$$

$$u_3 = \frac{18}{100} = 0.180$$

**Step 2 — Total utilisation:**

$$U_{total} = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.180 = 0.580$$

**Step 3 — RMS bound for n = 3:**

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \approx 0.7797$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$U_{total} = 0.580 \le 0.7797 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Schedulable)}$$

**Step 5 — Headroom available:**

$$U_{slack} = 0.7797 - 0.580 = 0.1997 \approx 20\%$$

**Step 6 — Maximum additional periodic load:**

$$C_{add,max} = 0.1997 \times T_{min} = 0.1997 \times 30 \text{ ms} \approx 6 \text{ ms of extra WCET on a 30 ms task}$$

In practice, a 4th task with T₄ = 200 ms and C₄ ≤ ~ 30 ms could be added safely.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Set B]*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Edge, Fog, and Cloud computing in the context of ADAS, with reference to latency, bandwidth, and functional safety.  
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An autonomous car has 4 cameras, 1 front radar, 1 LiDAR, and 1 IMU. Compute the aggregate raw sensor data rate (in MB/s and Gbps). Assume:
> - Cameras: 2 MP, 30 FPS, 3 bytes/pixel
> - Radar: 64 chirps × 512 I+Q samples per chirp, 20 Hz
> - LiDAR: 300 000 points/s, 4 bytes/point
> - IMU: 200 Hz, 32 bytes/sample (negligible)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table (drawn from §3.3):**

| Parameter | Edge | Fog | Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | 1–50 ms | 20–100 ms | 100–1000 ms |
| Bandwidth need (uplink) | 0 | MB/s | GB/s |
| Functional-safety fit | **ASIL-D** | ASIL-B | Non-safety |
| ASIL decomposition role | Primary | Secondary | None |

**Engineering reasoning:** Safety-critical functions (AEB, ELK, FCW) must execute on edge. Fog supports V2X-cooperative perception, while cloud is reserved for *training*, *HD-map generation*, and *OTA updates*. Putting ASIL-D logic in the cloud violates ISO 26262-9 (decomposition rules).

**(b) Aggregate Sensor Data Rate:**

**Step 1 — Camera contribution (4 cameras):**

$$R_{cam} = 4 \times (2 \times 10^6) \times 30 \times 3 = 720{,}000{,}000 \text{ Bytes/s} = 720 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 2 — Radar contribution:**

$$R_{radar} = 64 \times 512 \times 2 \times 20 = 1{,}310{,}720 \text{ Bytes/s} \approx 1.31 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 3 — LiDAR contribution:**

$$R_{lidar} = 300{,}000 \times 4 = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ Bytes/s} = 1.2 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 4 — IMU contribution (negligible):**

$$R_{IMU} = 200 \times 32 = 6{,}400 \text{ Bytes/s} \approx 0.006 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate:**

$$R_{total} = 720 + 1.31 + 1.2 + 0.006 \approx 722.5 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Gbps:**

$$R_{Gbps} = 722.5 \times 8 / 1000 \approx 5.78 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Conclusion:** Uploading 5.78 Gbps *continuously* to the cloud would consume ~ **21 TB/day** per vehicle — practically infeasible. This **quantitatively justifies edge processing**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Edge-Processing Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating latency with throughput.** Latency = *time per decision*; throughput = *decisions per second*. Both must appear distinctly.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units** in RMS-utilisation derivations — KTU examiners specifically dock 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Mixing up RMS bound (0.693) with EDF bound (1.0).** State explicitly which scheduler is being used.
> 4. **Ignoring ISO 26262 ASIL** when justifying why edge processing is required. Functional-safety argument is the *key* differentiator.
> 5. **Neglecting power / thermal budgets** in SoC selection — automotive Grade-2 requires operation at +85 °C ambient.
> 6. **Treating TOPS as a single scalar** — INT8, FP16, and FP32 TOPS differ by factors of 2× to 4×. Always state the *precision*.
> 7. **Not mentioning pre-processing cost** — ISP/FFT time often dominates CNN inference time in real vehicles.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Edge processing = on-board, in-vehicle compute** for safety-critical perception, fusion, and control.
- **Latency budget** for a Level-3 highway pilot ≈ **40 – 50 ms**, well within the **100 ms** ISO 26262 ASIL-D ceiling.
- **RMS utilisation bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, asymptotic limit **0.693**. EDF limit is **1.0**.
- **End-to-end latency** = sum of *sensor + pre-process + perception + fusion + plan + actuate* latencies.
- **Key SoCs to remember:** NVIDIA Orin (254 TOPS), Qualcomm Ride (700 TOPS), Mobileye EyeQ6 (128 TOPS, 12 W), NXP S32G (networking + safety).
- **Raw-data volume justifies edge:** a single L3 vehicle produces ~ **4 TB/hour** — impossible to upload.
- **Functional safety** is the *primary* reason edge processing is non-negotiable; bandwidth and privacy are secondary.
- **Schedulers:** RMS is default in AUTOSAR Classic; EDF is theoretically optimal but rarely used in production due to dynamic-priority implementation overhead.
- **Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1)** is the dominant in-vehicle backbone for raw sensor transport; MIPI CSI-2 connects cameras to SoCs.
- **Frameworks:** ROS 2, AUTOSAR Adaptive, TensorRT, OpenVX, and QNX are typical edge-AV middleware.
- **Metrics to memorise:** TOPS, FPS, Gbps, MB/s, WCET, ASIL, latency in ms.
- **The "3 Why" mantra for KTU answers:** *Why edge?* — Latency + Safety + Bandwidth. *Why not cloud?* — Jitter + ASIL + Cost. *Why determinism?* — Hard deadlines in AEB/ELK.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Edge Processing for Real-time Data Handling**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Cloud-based Autonomous Driving Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples of Cloud-based Autonomous Driving Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cloud-based Autonomous Driving Platform** is a distributed, networked computing ecosystem that leverages remote hyperscale data centres to augment, orchestrate, and validate the computational, perceptual, and decision-making workloads of an autonomous vehicle (AV). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus for PEMUT865, this platform integrates four major service pillars: (i) **High-Definition (HD) Map Storage and Distribution**, (ii) **Fleet Management and Telemetry**, (iii) **Over-The-Air (OTA) Software Updates**, and (iv) **Large-scale Simulation and Deep Learning Model Training**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Examination Ready):**
> A *Cloud-based Autonomous Driving Platform* is a Vehicle-to-Cloud-to-Vehicle (V2C2V) service architecture that offloads high-latency-tolerable but compute-intensive operations (such as HD map refreshes, neural network retraining, and kilometre-scale scenario simulation) from the in-vehicle Electronic Control Unit (ECU) to a hyperscale cloud backend, while preserving safety-critical real-time inference on the local vehicle hardware.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **brilliant, highly experienced senior taxi driver sitting in a central control tower**, watching thousands of cars drive through a city. He cannot physically turn the steering wheel of any single car, but he can:

1. **Send updated road maps** to the drivers when a new flyover opens,
2. **Warn all cars** when a sudden accident occurs 5 km ahead,
3. **Push a "software patch"** so every taxi learns the new traffic rules overnight,
4. **Run millions of "what-if" crash simulations** using yesterday's driving data.

The in-vehicle computer (the local driver) handles the **immediate, millisecond-level steering and braking** decisions, while the cloud (the senior driver in the tower) handles the **slow, heavy, knowledge-intensive reasoning**. This combined entity is the **Cloud-based Autonomous Driving Platform**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The cloud is *never* the safety-critical real-time controller. It is a **complementary augmentation layer** that operates with latencies in the order of **100 ms to several seconds**, while vehicle-level safety actuation must occur within **<10 ms**.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Latency Budget for Cloud Round-Trip (RTT):** typically **≤ 100 ms** for non-safety-critical notifications, and **≤ 1 s** for map tile refreshes.
- **Vehicle Telemetry Upload Rate:** **~5–50 GB/hour** per AV during active driving.
- **HD Map Tile Size:** **~10–50 MB** per 1 km² tile.
- **Industry Standard Connectivity:** **4G LTE (100 Mbps)** baseline, **5G (1–10 Gbps)** target.
- **Daily Data Accumulation (Industry Average):** roughly **1–4 TB/day per AV** for Level 4+ fleets.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Buzzwords to Memorise:** *V2C2V*, *OTA*, *HD Map*, *Fleet Learning*, *SIL/HIL Simulation*, *Edge Offloading*, *Digital Twin*, *CARLA/SVLiDAR*, *MaaS (Mobility-as-a-Service)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Pillar Architecture of a Cloud-based AD Platform

A KTU-valid cloud platform is decomposed into the following four interlocking functional pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Ingest & Storage Layer (Data Lake)**
- Accepts raw sensor streams: Camera (RGB), LiDAR point clouds, Radar, GNSS/IMU, CAN bus.
- Stored in columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) inside object stores (e.g., AWS S3, Azure Data Lake Gen2).
- **Why:** Future deep-learning training pipelines require petabyte-scale random access to historical corner cases.

**Pillar 2 — Compute & AI Layer (Model Factory)**
- GPU/TPU clusters perform *distributed training* of perception, prediction, and planning networks.
- Trained models are versioned (MLOps) and deployed back to the fleet via Pillar 4.
- **Why:** Training a modern transformer-based perception model (e.g., BEVFormer) requires **> 10,000 GPU-hours**.

**Pillar 3 — Simulation & Validation Layer (Digital Twin Engine)**
- Executes **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)** and **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** tests using simulators like **CARLA**, **LGSVL**, **NVIDIA DRIVE Sim**, or **Waymax**.
- Achieves **billions of virtual kilometres** of validation.
- **Why:** Real-world driving cannot exhaustively cover all edge cases (e.g., a child chasing a balloon into a snowy street at dusk).

**Pillar 4 — Distribution & Orchestration Layer (Vehicle Interface)**
- Pushes HD map deltas, software updates, and trained model weights to the in-vehicle infotainment/ADAS ECUs.
- Implements secure **OTA** channels (TLS 1.3, signed firmware, A/B partition rollback).
- **Why:** Fleet-wide bug fixes or feature rollouts must be atomic, auditable, and recoverable.

### 2.2 Connectivity and Bandwidth Engineering

The **upload throughput** requirement of an AV fleet is mathematically characterised by:

$$ R_{\text{upload}} \;=\; N_{\text{vehicles}} \times D_{\text{per-hour}} \times \frac{1}{3600} $$

where $R_{\text{upload}}$ is the aggregate bandwidth (MB/s), $N_{\text{vehicles}}$ is the active fleet size, and $D_{\text{per-hour}}$ is the per-vehicle hourly data volume (MB/h).

The **time to upload a single HD map tile** is given by:

$$ T_{\text{tile}} \;=\; \frac{S_{\text{tile}}}{B_{\text{link}} \times \eta} $$

where $S_{\text{tile}}$ is the tile size (MB), $B_{\text{link}}$ is the available cellular bandwidth (MB/s), and $\eta$ is the *protocol efficiency* (typically $\eta \approx 0.7$ for TCP over LTE).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Symbol | Formula / Relation | Typical Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aggregate Cloud Bandwidth | $R_{\text{upload}} = N_{v} \cdot D_{h} / 3600$ | MB/s | Sizing cloud ingress link |
| 2 | Tile Download Time | $T_{\text{tile}} = S_{\text{tile}} / (B_{\text{link}} \cdot \eta)$ | seconds | HD map refresh latency |
| 3 | Daily Data per AV | $D_{\text{day}} = 24 \cdot D_{\text{hour}}$ | TB/day | Storage provisioning |
| 4 | Storage Cost per AV-Year | $C_{\text{year}} = 365 \cdot D_{\text{day}} \cdot c_{\text{GB}}$ | USD | Cloud OPEX budgeting |
| 5 | Effective Bandwidth (5G) | $B_{\text{eff}} = B_{\text{peak}} \cdot \eta$ | Mbps | OTA throughput planning |
| 6 | Training GPU-Hours | $H_{\text{GPU}} = N_{\text{samples}} / T_{\text{throughput}}$ | GPU-h | Model factory sizing |
| 7 | Simulation Coverage Ratio | $C_{R} = D_{\text{sim}} / D_{\text{real}}$ | dimensionless | Validation completeness |
| 8 | Effective OTA Success | $P_{\text{OTA}} = 1 - (1-p)^{N_{\text{retries}}}$ | probability | Update reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Markdown Tables:** All absolute value / conditionals have been rendered with $\vert$ and $\cdot$ to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cloud platforms enable three transformative engineering capabilities:

1. **Fleet Learning (Shadow Mode):** When one AV encounters a novel scenario, the **triggered scene** is uploaded overnight. The cloud retrains the global model, and a **push update** delivers the new weights to the entire fleet within **24–72 hours**. This is how **Tesla FSD**, **Waymo Driver**, and **Mobileye REM** achieve continuous improvement.
2. **Remote Assistance / Teleoperation:** A human operator in the cloud can assist the AV in ambiguous situations (e.g., construction zone), subject to **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** fall-back protocols.
3. **Predictive Maintenance:** Continuous telemetry is analysed by anomaly-detection ML models to predict component failures (LiDAR degradation, brake wear) **days in advance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations for Cloud Bandwidth and Storage Sizing (Board Exam Standard)

#### **Derivation 1: Aggregate Cloud Ingress Bandwidth**

> **Problem Statement (Model):** A robo-taxi operator deploys a fleet of 1,000 Level-4 autonomous vehicles. Each vehicle generates 30 GB of raw sensor data per hour of active operation. The cloud provider must dimension its **ingress link** in **Gbps**. Compute the required bandwidth, assuming all vehicles upload concurrently during peak hours.

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.**
- $N_{\text{vehicles}} = 1000$
- $D_{\text{per-hour}} = 30 \; \text{GB/h}$
- Conversion factor: $1 \; \text{GB} = 8 \; \text{Gb}$

**Step 2 — Convert per-vehicle hourly data into per-second data rate.**

$$ D_{\text{per-second}} \;=\; \frac{30 \;\text{GB/h}}{3600 \;\text{s/h}} \;=\; 0.00833 \;\text{GB/s} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to Gigabits per second.**

$$ D_{\text{per-second}} \;=\; 0.00833 \;\text{GB/s} \times 8 \;\text{Gb/GB} \;=\; 0.0667 \;\text{Gbps} $$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the fleet size.**

$$ R_{\text{aggregate}} \;=\; 1000 \times 0.0667 \;\text{Gbps} \;=\; 66.67 \;\text{Gbps} $$

**Step 5 — Apply protocol efficiency factor (TCP over LTE/5G core, $\eta \approx 0.7$).**

$$ R_{\text{aggregate, real}} \;=\; \frac{66.67}{0.7} \;\approx\; 95.24 \;\text{Gbps} $$

> **Conclusion:** The cloud ingress must provision **at least 100 Gbps** of dedicated capacity to ingest a 1,000-vehicle fleet at peak. (Note: practical systems use *edge buffering* and *selective upload* of triggered events only, reducing this by 90%.)

#### **Derivation 2: HD Map Tile Refresh Latency**

> **Problem Statement (Model):** An AV traverses a 50 km highway. The cloud hosts the route as 50 HD map tiles, each of size 25 MB. The vehicle connects via 5G with a measured downlink of 200 Mbps. Protocol efficiency is 0.75. Compute the **worst-case total refresh time** if all tiles must be downloaded before the trip.

**Step 1 — Effective bandwidth in MB/s.**

$$ B_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 200 \;\text{Mbps} \times \frac{1}{8} \;\text{MB/Mb} \times 0.75 \;\approx\; 18.75 \;\text{MB/s} $$

**Step 2 — Time per tile.**

$$ T_{\text{tile}} \;=\; \frac{25 \;\text{MB}}{18.75 \;\text{MB/s}} \;\approx\; 1.33 \;\text{s} $$

**Step 3 — Time for 50 tiles (sequential, worst case).**

$$ T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 50 \times 1.33 \;\text{s} \;\approx\; 66.67 \;\text{s} $$

> **Conclusion:** A 50-tile sequential refresh takes **~67 seconds**, well within pre-trip planning. (In production, tiles are *delta-encoded* and downloaded *on-the-fly* with predictive prefetching, reducing this to **< 5 s**.)

#### **Derivation 3: OTA Update Success Probability**

> **Problem Statement (Model):** A fleet receives an OTA firmware update. Each vehicle has an individual success probability $p = 0.9$. The system allows $N_{\text{retries}} = 3$ attempts. Compute the **fleet-level update success probability** for a single vehicle.

**Step 1 — Probability of failure in one attempt.**

$$ P_{\text{fail,1}} \;=\; 1 - p \;=\; 0.1 $$

**Step 2 — Probability of failing in all 3 attempts.**

$$ P_{\text{fail,all}} \;=\; (0.1)^{3} \;=\; 0.001 $$

**Step 3 — Probability of at least one success.**

$$ P_{\text{success}} \;=\; 1 - 0.001 \;=\; 0.999 $$

> **Conclusion:** A 3-retry OTA mechanism achieves a **99.9% per-vehicle success rate**, acceptable for non-safety ASIL-A/B updates. Safety-critical ASIL-D updates require **2-of-3 dual-bank redundancy** with rollback.

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Major Cloud-based AD Platforms

| Platform | Cloud Provider / Owner | Key Cloud Services | Primary Use Case | Open Source? | Notable Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NVIDIA DRIVE** | NVIDIA + AWS/Azure/GCP | DRIVE Sim, DGX SuperPOD, Omniverse | End-to-end AV dev + Sim | Partial (DRIVE OS) | Best-in-class hardware-software co-design |
| **Waymo Driver** | Alphabet (Google Cloud) | GCP BigQuery, Dataflow, TensorFlow | Robo-taxi deployment | Closed | Industry leader in real L4 mileage |
| **AWS for AV** | Amazon | IoT FleetWise, RoboMaker, S3, SageMaker | Generic AV dev platform | Partial (RoboMaker) | Broadest service catalogue |
| **Microsoft Azure for AV** | Microsoft | Azure Maps, Azure IoT Hub, Digital Twins | Connected vehicle + HD map | Partial | Strong enterprise integration |
| **Baidu Apollo** | Baidu | Apollo Cloud, Open Data, RT6 stack | Open AV stack (China) | **Fully open-source** | Largest open AV community |
| **Mobileye REM** | Intel | Road Experience Management, EyeQ cloud | Crowd-sourced HD map | Closed | > 30 billion km of crowd-sourced data |
| **Tesla FSD / Autopilot** | Tesla (in-house) | Tesla Dojo supercomputer, OTA | Consumer AV with fleet learning | Closed | Largest fleet → largest dataset |
| **Aurora Driver** | Aurora Innovation (AWS) | AWS for back-office, sim at scale | L4 trucking + robo-taxi | Closed | Strong first-principles ML team |
| **Pony.ai / WeRide** | Pony.ai / WeRide | Custom + Tencent/Alibaba cloud | Robo-taxi (Asia) | Closed | Strong L4 China deployments |
| **Cruise** | GM (in-house) | Custom cloud + Microsoft Azure | Urban robo-taxi (SF) | Closed | Tight GM vehicle integration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference with KTU Module 3 Outcomes:** This table directly maps to **CO3** (*Analyse cloud-augmented autonomous driving architectures*) and **CO4** (*Evaluate commercial AV platforms*).

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Petabyte-Scale Storage Cost Estimation

> **Problem Statement (Model):** A fleet of 500 AVs operates 12 hours/day, generating 25 GB/h. Cloud object storage costs $c = \$0.02$ per GB-month. Estimate the **annual storage OPEX** assuming 30-day retention.

**Step 1 — Daily data per vehicle.**

$$ D_{\text{day}} \;=\; 25 \;\text{GB/h} \times 12 \;\text{h} \;=\; 300 \;\text{GB/day} $$

**Step 2 — Daily fleet data.**

$$ D_{\text{fleet, day}} \;=\; 500 \times 300 \;\text{GB} \;=\; 150{,}000 \;\text{GB/day} \;=\; 150 \;\text{TB/day} $$

**Step 3 — Monthly storage volume (30 days).**

$$ V_{\text{month}} \;=\; 150 \;\text{TB} \times 30 \;=\; 4{,}500 \;\text{TB/month} \;=\; 4.5 \;\text{PB/month} $$

**Step 4 — Monthly cost.**

$$ C_{\text{month}} \;=\; 4{,}500{,}000 \;\text{GB} \times 0.02 \;\text{USD/GB} \;=\; \$90{,}000 $$

**Step 5 — Annual cost.**

$$ C_{\text{year}} \;=\; 90{,}000 \times 12 \;=\; \$1{,}080{,}000 $$

> **Conclusion:** A 500-vehicle fleet accrues **~USD 1.08 million/year** in raw object storage alone. This is why **tiered storage** (hot/warm/cold) and **event-triggered selective upload** are mandatory in production.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture — Vehicle-to-Cloud-to-Vehicle (V2C2V)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** A Mermaid block-level functional architecture is used in lieu of a physical drawing, as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fall-back policy for non-physical-system topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VEHICLE_EDGE["VEHICLE EDGE LAYER"]
        V_SENS["Sensors: Camera, LiDAR, Radar, GNSS, IMU, CAN"]
        V_ECU["Onboard ECU: ADAS Compute Platform"]
        V_OBU["Telematics OBU and 5G Modem"]
        V_HMI["HMI and Actuation Layer"]
    end

    subgraph COMMS["CONNECTIVITY LAYER"]
        C_5G["5G C-V2X Network"]
        C_WIFI["Wi-Fi 6 / DSRC Hotspots"]
        C_SAT["Satellite Backhaul (Sparse Areas)"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD PLATFORM LAYER"]
        CL_INGEST["Ingest Gateway and Streaming Service"]
        CL_LAKE["Data Lake: Object Storage, Data Warehouse"]
        CL_AI["AI Training Cluster: GPUs and TPUs"]
        CL_SIM["Simulation Engine: CARLA, DRIVE Sim, Waymax"]
        CL_MAP["HD Map Service: Storage, Diff, Tile Server"]
        CL_OTA["OTA Orchestrator: Signed Firmware Pipeline"]
        CL_TELE["Teleop Console: Remote Assistance"]
        CL_FLEET["Fleet Manager: Health, Status, Routing"]
    end

    subgraph DEPLOY["DISTRIBUTION AND CONSUMERS"]
        D_PUSH["Secure Push Channel: TLS 1.3"]
        D_FLEET["Vehicle Fleet: 1000s of AVs"]
        D_ANALYTICS["Operations Dashboard and BI Tools"]
    end

    V_SENS --> V_ECU
    V_ECU --> V_OBU
    V_OBU --> C_5G
    C_5G --> CL_INGEST
    C_WIFI --> CL_INGEST
    C_SAT --> CL_INGEST
    CL_INGEST --> CL_LAKE
    CL_LAKE --> CL_AI
    CL_LAKE --> CL_SIM
    CL_AI --> CL_OTA
    CL_OTA --> D_PUSH
    D_PUSH --> D_FLEET
    CL_MAP --> D_PUSH
    CL_TELE --> D_PUSH
    CL_FLEET --> D_ANALYTICS
    D_FLEET --> V_HMI
    V_ECU --> V_HMI
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Fleet Learning via Triggered Event Upload

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AV1 as AV in Fleet
    participant V2X as V2X Network
    participant CLD as Cloud Platform
    participant TRN as Training Cluster
    participant SIM as Simulation Engine
    participant OTA as OTA Orchestrator
    participant FLT as Other Fleet Vehicles

    AV1->>AV1: Detect Anomalous Scenario
    AV1->>V2X: Upload Triggered Clip and Metadata
    V2X->>CLD: Ingest to Data Lake
    CLD->>TRN: Queue for Model Retraining
    TRN->>TRN: Train Updated Perception Model
    TRN->>SIM: Validate in BILLIONS of Sim km
    SIM-->>TRN: Pass Safety Gate
    TRN->>OTA: Submit Signed Model Weights
    OTA->>FLT: Stage Update on All Vehicles
    FLT->>FLT: Apply and Verify
    FLT-->>CLD: Acknowledge and Report Telemetry
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Block Diagram — AWS for Autonomous Vehicles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AWS["AWS CLOUD STACK"]
        S3["S3: Raw Sensor Data Lake"]
        IOT["IoT Core and FleetWise"]
        SAGE["SageMaker: ML Training"]
        ROB["RoboMaker: Simulation"]
        KIN["Kinesis: Real-time Stream"]
        LAM["Lambda: Event-driven Compute"]
        OTA_SVC["IoT Jobs: OTA Orchestrator"]
    end

    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL CONSUMERS"]
        OEM["OEM Engineering Teams"]
        FLEET_S["Vehicle Fleet: Connected Cars"]
        OPS["Operations Dashboard"]
    end

    FLEET_S -->|MQTT over TLS| IOT
    IOT --> KIN
    KIN --> S3
    S3 --> SAGE
    S3 --> ROB
    SAGE -->|Trained Model| OTA_SVC
    OTA_SVC -->|Signed Firmware| FLEET_S
    S3 -->|Analytics| OPS
    ROB -->|Validation Reports| OEM
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in an Exam:** Always start by labelling the **layer** (Edge / Connectivity / Cloud / Distribution), then state the **direction of data flow** (uplink = sensor to cloud; downlink = cloud to vehicle), and finally cite **one example protocol** (MQTT, gRPC, HTTPS, V2X PC5 sidelink).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q1.** Define a *Cloud-based Autonomous Driving Platform*. List its four major service pillars.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A cloud-based autonomous driving platform is a distributed computing ecosystem that augments in-vehicle autonomous functions by offloading compute-intensive, latency-tolerant workloads (such as HD map distribution, fleet learning, OTA updates, and large-scale simulation) to hyperscale cloud data centres. The four pillars are:
> 1. **Ingest and Storage Layer** (Data Lake),
> 2. **Compute and AI Layer** (Model Factory),
> 3. **Simulation and Validation Layer** (Digital Twin),
> 4. **Distribution and Orchestration Layer** (Vehicle Interface + OTA).
> *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing pillars with one-line explanation each.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between *edge inference* and *cloud inference* in an autonomous vehicle context. Why is safety-critical perception **never** offloaded to the cloud?
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Edge inference** runs perception/planning models on the in-vehicle ECU with **< 10 ms** latency and full operational autonomy. *(1 mark)*
> - **Cloud inference** runs heavy models on remote data centres with **> 100 ms** latency and is dependent on network availability. *(1 mark)*
> - Safety-critical actuation (emergency braking, collision avoidance) requires **ASIL-D** real-time guarantees that cellular networks cannot provide. Hence, perception stays on the edge; cloud is used only for *learning* and *validation*. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Vehicle-to-Cloud-to-Vehicle (V2C2V) architecture** of a cloud-based autonomous driving platform with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **HD Map Service** and the **OTA Orchestrator** in this architecture.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A fleet of **800 Level-4 AVs** generates **40 GB/h** of sensor data. Compute the **aggregate cloud ingress bandwidth (in Gbps)** required during peak hours, assuming a protocol efficiency of **0.7**. Also estimate the **annual storage cost** in USD if cloud object storage charges **\$0.018/GB-month** and data is retained for **30 days**.

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) V2C2V Architecture Explanation (7 Marks):**

The V2C2V architecture has three layers (mark allocation: 1 mark for diagram, 6 marks for explanation):

**Layer 1 — Vehicle Edge (1 mark):** Contains sensors (Camera, LiDAR, Radar, GNSS/IMU, CAN), the ADAS compute ECU, and the 5G/V2X Onboard Unit (OBU). This layer executes real-time inference.

**Layer 2 — Cloud Platform (4 marks):** Comprises four sub-modules:
- **Ingest Gateway** receives telemetry via MQTT/gRPC over TLS 1.3.
- **Data Lake** stores raw sensor data in columnar format (Parquet) for ML pipelines.
- **HD Map Service** maintains a tile-based global map and serves *delta updates* to the fleet when roads change.
- **OTA Orchestrator** packages signed firmware and model weights for atomic fleet-wide deployment, with A/B partition rollback on failure.

**Layer 3 — Distribution (1 mark):** Secure push channels deliver maps and models to the fleet; bi-directional feedback enables *fleet learning*.

**Role of HD Map Service (½ mark):** Provides centimetre-level localisation, lane geometry, and traffic-rule metadata. Cloud-side aggregation of *road change events* (from Mobileye REM, Waymo, etc.) allows *near-real-time* map refreshes within hours of a real-world change.

**Role of OTA Orchestrator (½ mark):** Implements versioned, signed, atomic software rollouts. Safety-critical updates use dual-bank redundancy and cryptographic signature verification (HSM-protected keys).

**(b) Bandwidth and Storage Cost Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Per-vehicle data rate (1 mark):**

$$ D_{\text{per-second}} \;=\; \frac{40 \;\text{GB/h}}{3600 \;\text{s/h}} \;\approx\; 0.01111 \;\text{GB/s} $$

**Step 2 — Convert to Gbps (1 mark):**

$$ 0.01111 \;\text{GB/s} \times 8 \;\text{Gb/GB} \;=\; 0.0889 \;\text{Gbps/vehicle} $$

**Step 3 — Fleet aggregate (1 mark):**

$$ R_{\text{aggregate, raw}} \;=\; 800 \times 0.0889 \;\text{Gbps} \;\approx\; 71.11 \;\text{Gbps} $$

**Step 4 — Apply efficiency factor (1 mark):**

$$ R_{\text{aggregate, real}} \;=\; \frac{71.11}{0.7} \;\approx\; 101.6 \;\text{Gbps} $$

> **Answer:** The cloud ingress link must provision **≈ 101.6 Gbps** (≈ 102 Gbps). *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Daily data per vehicle (½ mark):**

$$ D_{\text{day}} \;=\; 40 \;\text{GB/h} \times 24 \;\text{h} \;=\; 960 \;\text{GB/day} $$

**Step 6 — Daily fleet data (½ mark):**

$$ D_{\text{fleet, day}} \;=\; 800 \times 960 \;\text{GB} \;=\; 768{,}000 \;\text{GB/day} \;=\; 768 \;\text{TB/day} $$

**Step 7 — Monthly storage (½ mark):**

$$ V_{\text{month}} \;=\; 768 \;\text{TB} \times 30 \;=\; 23{,}040 \;\text{TB} \;\approx\; 23.04 \;\text{PB} $$

**Step 8 — Monthly and annual cost (½ mark):**

$$ C_{\text{month}} \;=\; 23{,}040{,}000 \;\text{GB} \times 0.018 \;\text{USD} \;\approx\; \$414{,}720 $$

$$ C_{\text{year}} \;\approx\; 414{,}720 \times 12 \;\approx\; \$4{,}976{,}640 $$

> **Answer:** Annual storage OPEX ≈ **USD 4.98 million**.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **AWS IoT FleetWise + RoboMaker**, **Microsoft Azure Maps + Digital Twins**, and **Baidu Apollo Cloud** as cloud-based autonomous driving platforms. Tabulate your answer across the dimensions of: (i) primary cloud provider, (ii) HD map service, (iii) simulation engine, (iv) OTA mechanism, and (v) openness (open-source vs closed).
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat flow diagram, explain the **fleet learning loop** in a Tesla-style closed autonomous driving ecosystem. State two engineering challenges and two safety considerations.

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparative Table (7 Marks):** *(1.4 marks per row, partial marks for partial correct answers)*

| Dimension | AWS for AV | Microsoft Azure for AV | Baidu Apollo |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Cloud Provider | Amazon Web Services | Microsoft Azure | Baidu Cloud (with Tencent/Alibaba partners) |
| (ii) HD Map Service | HERE integration via Marketplace; custom MapLibre tiles | Azure Maps (Microsoft-owned, vector + raster) | Apollo Map (open HD map format, OpenDRIVE-compatible) |
| (iii) Simulation Engine | AWS RoboMaker (Gazebo-based) + DRIVE Sim on EC2 G5/G6 | Azure Digital Twins + Microsoft AirSim (open) | Apollo Sim (LGSVL heritage, open-source) |
| (iv) OTA Mechanism | IoT Jobs, signed S3 payloads, A/B partitions | Intune + Azure IoT Hub, A/B + staged rollouts | Apollo Cyber RT bus + delta OTA, open-spec |
| (v) Openness | Partial (RoboMaker/IoT FleetWise open-source SDKs) | Partial (AirSim open) | **Fully open-source** (Apollo GitHub) |

**Concluding Statement (½ mark):** Baidu Apollo is the most open; AWS offers the broadest commercial service catalogue; Azure is strongest in enterprise integration and Digital Twin tooling.

**(b) Fleet Learning Loop (7 Marks):**

**Diagram (2 marks):** A closed loop with stages — *(Trigger Detection → Upload → Data Lake → Retrain → Validate in Sim → Sign & Stage → OTA Push → In-vehicle Inference → Telemetry Feedback)*.

**Detailed Stages (3 marks):**
1. **Trigger Detection:** In-vehicle monitor flags a low-confidence or anomalous scenario.
2. **Selective Upload:** Only the relevant 10–60 s clip (camera + LiDAR + CAN) is uploaded over Wi-Fi/5G.
3. **Cloud Retraining:** Dojo/DGX cluster retrains the perception/planning transformer on the combined fleet dataset.
4. **Validation:** New model is tested in billions of virtual km; passes safety gate.
5. **OTA Distribution:** Signed weights are atomically pushed to all compatible vehicles.
6. **In-vehicle Verification:** Vehicle verifies signature, applies to passive partition, swaps on next ignition.
7. **Feedback:** Updated fleet performance telemetry is uploaded; loop repeats.

**Engineering Challenges (1 mark):**
- **Data labelling bottleneck** at petabyte scale.
- **Distribution shift** between geographies and weather conditions.
- **Network unreliability** in rural/dead zones.

**Safety Considerations (1 mark):**
- **ASIL-D rollback** must be guaranteed on partial update.
- **Cryptographic signing** of all model weights to prevent adversarial OTA injection.
- **Regulatory audit trail** for every weight change (UNECE R156 SOTA compliance).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse *edge inference* with *cloud inference* — students often write "the cloud drives the car", which is factually wrong and costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Do NOT** omit the **unit conversions** (GB → Gb, hours → seconds). Most board-valuation losses in numerical questions (≈ 1.5 marks each) are due to **missing unit conversions**, not wrong algebra.
> 3. **Do NOT** list platform names without stating their **function** in the V2C2V loop (e.g., just writing "Waymo" is **0 marks**; writing "Waymo Driver uses Google Cloud BigQuery for fleet telemetry" gets full credit).
> 4. **Always** justify *why* the cloud cannot host safety-critical loops — cite the **> 100 ms RTT** vs **< 10 ms** actuation requirement.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (Must-Memorise):** Cloud-based AD platform = *V2C2V* architecture with four pillars (Ingest, AI, Sim, OTA).
- **Latency Hierarchy:** Edge inference (<10 ms) → V2X alert (~50 ms) → Map refresh (~1 s) → Retraining cycle (24–72 h).
- **Data Economics:** A single AV generates **1–4 TB/day**; a 1000-vehicle fleet requires **~100 Gbps** ingress.
- **Top Commercial Platforms:** **NVIDIA DRIVE, Waymo, AWS for AV, Azure for AV, Baidu Apollo, Mobileye REM, Tesla FSD, Aurora.**
- **Open-Source Leader:** **Baidu Apollo** (GitHub, Cyber RT, Apollo Studio).
- **Connectivity Standards:** **5G C-V2X** (PC5 sidelink for direct V2V), **DSRC** (legacy IEEE 802.11p), **4G LTE** (fallback).
- **Security Pillars:** **TLS 1.3** in transit, **HSM-protected keys** for signing, **A/B partition** rollback, **UNECE R156 SOTA** compliance.
- **Simulation Engines:** **CARLA, LGSVL, NVIDIA DRIVE Sim, Waymax, AirSim, Apollo Sim.**
- **HD Map Formats:** **NDS, OpenDRIVE, Lanelet2, Apollo Map, HERE HD Live Map.**
- **Core Safety Principle:** **The cloud NEVER executes safety-critical actuation.** Local ECU always retains **ASIL-D authority**.
- **Key Formulas:** $R_{\text{upload}} = N_v \cdot D_h / 3600$, $T_{\text{tile}} = S_{\text{tile}} / (B \cdot \eta)$, $P_{\text{OTA}} = 1 - (1-p)^{N}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark cloud-platform question, structure your answer as — *(i) Block diagram, (ii) Pillar-by-pillar explanation, (iii) Numerical example, (iv) Safety/security commentary.* This mirrors the KTU 2024 Scheme OBE rubric under CO3 and CO5.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Integration and Calibration - Integration of ADAS Components into Vehicle Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** System Integration and Calibration - Integration of ADAS Components into Vehicle Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **System Integration and Calibration** in the context of ADAS refers to the engineering discipline of embedding heterogeneous sensors, Electronic Control Units (ECUs), actuators, and Human-Machine Interface (HMI) modules into the vehicle's existing Electrical/Electronic (E/E) architecture such that they operate as a cohesive, deterministic, and fail-operational cyber-physical system. Calibration, in turn, is the process of tuning each sensor's spatial reference frame, timing offsets, confidence thresholds, and fusion weights so that the aggregated perception of the vehicle environment converges to ground-truth within **±0.1°** angular and **±0.1 m** lateral tolerance as mandated by **Euro NCAP 2025** and **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** functional-safety standards. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Learning Outcome:** After this module, the student must be able to *architecturally place*, *communicatively bind*, and *sensorially calibrate* ADAS sub-systems (Radar, LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic, V2X) into the in-vehicle network topology while satisfying timing, safety, and electromagnetic-compatibility constraints. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are the conductor of a 90-piece symphony orchestra where every musician (sensor, ECU, actuator) plays a different instrument, in a different key, at a different tempo, and is seated in a different corner of the hall. - **Integration** is the act of handing each musician a properly tuned instrument, a music stand positioned at the correct height, a power cable, and a wireless earpiece connected to your baton. - **Calibration** is the 30-minute rehearsal before the concert where each violinist tunes their A-string to 440 Hz, the trumpets synchronize their breathing with the oboes, and the entire orchestra aligns to the oboe's "A". Without integration, the sensors would be isolated islands of data. Without calibration, even an integrated system would hallucinate — a camera might think a pedestrian is 30 m away when they are 5 m away, or a radar might miss a motorcycle in a truck's blind spot. **Integration gives ADAS a body; calibration gives it a mind.** > [!NOTE] > **Three Pillars of ADAS Integration:** > 1. **Electrical Integration** — power delivery, grounding, EMI suppression. > 2. **Communication Integration** — bus protocols (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet). > 3. **Computational/Sensor Integration** — sensor fusion, time synchronization, functional safety. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Sensor Time-Sync Tolerance:** **±1 ms** (IEEE 802.1AS gPTP for Automotive Ethernet) - **CAN Bus Bit Rate:** **500 kbps** (Classical CAN) and **5 Mbps** (CAN-FD) - **Automotive Ethernet:** **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps / 2.5 Gbps / 10 Gbps** - **ASIL Decomposition Threshold:** Probability of dangerous failure per hour **< 10⁻⁸** (ASIL-D) - **Calibration Angular Tolerance:** **±0.1°** yaw, **±0.2°** pitch - **Camera Intrinsic Re-projection Error:** **< 0.3 pixels** RMS - **Operating Temperature Range:** **−40 °C to +85 °C** (AEC-Q100 Grade 2/3) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five-Layer Integration Stack Integration of ADAS is best understood as a layered stack, analogous to the **OSI 7-layer model** in networking: | Layer # | Name | Components | Failure Impact | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **L1** | **Physical / Mechanical Layer** | Mounting brackets, radome, windshield optical path, vibration isolators | Sensor mis-aim → angular drift | | **L2** | **Electrical / Power Layer** | 12 V / 48 V bus, fuses, PCB stack-up, grounding star, EMI filters | Brown-out, sensor reset | | **L3** | **Communication / Network Layer** | CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay, 100BASE-T1 Ethernet | Data drop, latency spike | | **L4** | **Computational / Fusion Layer** | Domain controllers (NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ6), RTOS, AUTOSAR | Wrong object classification | | **L5** | **Application / Actuation Layer** | EPS, ESC, accelerator-by-wire, HMI cluster | Erroneous braking/steering | ### 2.2 In-Vehicle Network Protocol Selection Matrix | Protocol | Bandwidth | Latency | Determinism | Typical ADAS Use-Case | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | **LIN** | 20 kbps | < 5 ms | Non-deterministic | Ultrasonic sensor cluster, mirror actuators | | **CAN 2.0B** | 1 Mbps (theory) / 500 kbps (practice) | < 1 ms | Soft real-time | Steering angle, yaw-rate, wheel-speed (legacy) | | **CAN-FD** | 5 Mbps | < 1 ms | Soft real-time | Radar raw targets, AEB command | | **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps | < 5 µs | Hard real-time | Drive-by-wire, steer-by-wire (older premium) | | **100/1000BASE-T1 Ethernet** | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | < 100 µs (with TSN) | Hard real-time | Camera raw video, LiDAR point clouds, centralized fusion | ### 2.3 Sensor Calibration — The Two Universes #### A. Intrinsic Calibration (Lab / Factory) Determines the sensor's *own* parameters — focal length, principal point, lens distortion coefficients (for camera), or range-offset and boresight angles (for radar/LiDAR). The **pinhole camera intrinsic matrix** is the canonical example: $$ K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Lens distortion (radial + tangential) is modelled as: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{distorted}} &= x \left(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6\right) + 2 p_1 x y + p_2 \left(r^2 + 2 x^2\right) \\ y_{\text{distorted}} &= y \left(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6\right) + p_1 \left(r^2 + 2 y^2\right) + 2 p_2 x y \end{aligned} $$ #### B. Extrinsic Calibration (Workshop / Online) Determines the **rigid 6-DoF transform** $[\,R \, \vert \, t\,]$ between the sensor frame and the vehicle frame (typically rear-axle center). $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{vehicle}} &= R \cdot P_{\text{sensor}} + t \\ \text{where } R &\in SO(3), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}^3 \end{aligned} $$ The rotation $R$ can be parameterised by Euler angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ or by a **unit quaternion** $q = (q_w, q_x, q_y, q_z)$ with the constraint $\|q\| = 1$, which avoids the gimbal-lock singularity. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use | |:---|:---|:---| | Sensor frame → Vehicle frame | $P_v = R P_s + t$ | Extrinsic calibration | | Quaternion multiplication | $q_1 \otimes q_2 = \begin{bmatrix} q_{1w} q_{2w} - q_{1v} \cdot q_{2v} \\ q_{1w} q_{2v} + q_{2w} q_{1v} + q_{1v} \times q_{2v} \end{bmatrix}$ | Composition of rotations | | Pinhole projection | $u = f_x \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$, $\; v = f_y \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$ | Camera intrinsic model | | Inverse pinhole (un-project) | $X = \frac{Z (u - c_x)}{f_x}$, $\; Y = \frac{Z (v - c_y)}{f_y}$ | 3D reconstruction from pixel | | Time synchronization residual | $\Delta t_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{sensor, min}}}$ | gPTP / PTP master-slave drift | | ASIL Probability of Failure | $\text{PMHF} < 10^{-8} \text{/h}$ (D), $<10^{-7}$ (C), $<10^{-6}$ (B) | ISO 26262 budget | | Radar range resolution | $\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 B}$ where $B$ = sweep bandwidth | FMCW radar chirp design | | LiDAR angular resolution | $\Delta \theta = \frac{2\pi}{N_{\text{channels}}}$ | Mechanical / MEMS LiDAR | | Sensor fusion (Kalman innovation) | $\nu_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}$ | Outlier detection in fusion | | Ethernet TSN latency bound | $L_{\text{max}} = \sum_{i} \left(\frac{L_{\text{frame}}}{R_i} + d_{\text{queue},i}\right)$ | Worst-case scheduling | ### 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering The pinhole projection is the bedrock of every forward-facing camera in Mobileye, Bosch, and Continental ADAS stacks. The extrinsic transform is what allows the *same* bicycle detected by a front-camera at pixel $(u,v)$ to be reported to the AEB controller in vehicle-frame coordinates $(X,Y,Z)$, where the longitudinal distance $X$ decides whether to brake. Without $R$ and $t$, the system cannot correlate "what the camera sees" with "where the radar says it is" — the heart of **sensor fusion**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Extrinsic Calibration via Least-Squares (Camera-to-LiDAR) Suppose we have $N$ corresponding 3-D points $\{P_i^{(L)}\}$ from a LiDAR and 2-D pixel detections $\{p_i = (u_i, v_i)\}$ from a camera observing a checkerboard target. We want to find the rigid transform $(R, t)$ such that the LiDAR points, when projected through the camera intrinsics, land on the detected pixels. **Step 1 — Express the camera projection of a LiDAR point.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_i^{(C)} &= R \, P_i^{(L)} + t \\ \begin{bmatrix} u_i \\ v_i \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &\sim K \begin{bmatrix} P_i^{(C)} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Define the re-projection residual.** For each correspondence, the residual (in pixel units) is: $$ r_i(u_i, v_i) = \begin{bmatrix} u_i^{\text{measured}} - \hat{u}_i \\ v_i^{\text{measured}} - \hat{v}_i \end{bmatrix} $$ where $\hat{u}_i, \hat{v}_i$ are the predicted pixel coordinates from projecting $P_i^{(L)}$ through the current $(R, t)$ estimate. **Step 3 — Form the non-linear least-squares cost.** $$ J(R, t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho\!\left( \| r_i \|^2 \right) $$ where $\rho(\cdot)$ is a robust loss (Huber or Tukey) to down-weight outlier correspondences caused by mismatches. **Step 4 — Linearize using small-angle approximation.** For an update $\xi = (\delta \phi, \delta \theta, \delta \psi, t_x, t_y, t_z)$, the perturbed rotation matrix is: $$ R(\xi) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\delta\psi & \delta\theta \\ \delta\psi & 1 & -\delta\phi \\ -\delta\theta & \delta\phi & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the Jacobian of the re-projection w.r.t. $\xi$.** The Jacobian of a projected 3-D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ w.r.t. the 6-DoF pose $\xi$ is: $$ \frac{\partial p}{\partial \xi} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{f_x}{Z} & 0 & \frac{f_x X}{Z^2} & \frac{f_x X Y}{Z^2} & -f_x - \frac{f_x X^2}{Z^2} & \frac{f_x Y}{Z} \\ 0 & -\frac{f_y}{Z} & \frac{f_y Y}{Z^2} & f_y + \frac{f_y Y^2}{Z^2} & -\frac{f_y X Y}{Z^2} & -\frac{f_y X}{Z} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Solve the Gauss-Newton normal equations.** $$ \begin{aligned} H &= \sum_i J_i^\top J_i \\ b &= \sum_i J_i^\top r_i \\ \Delta \xi &= -H^{-1} b \\ \xi_{k+1} &\leftarrow \xi_k \oplus \Delta\xi \end{aligned} $$ where $\oplus$ is the manifold addition (quaternion multiplication for the rotation part). **Step 7 — Convergence check.** Iterate until $\|\Delta \xi\| < \epsilon$, where $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ radians for angular and $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ metres for translational components. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Calibration Error Propagation The total error in the fused object position is a sum of three independent error sources (intrinsic, extrinsic, and temporal), propagated through the chain of transformations: $$ \Sigma_{\text{total}} = J_R \, \Sigma_R \, J_R^\top + J_t \, \Sigma_t \, J_t^\top + J_{\Delta t} \, \Sigma_{\Delta t} \, J_{\Delta t}^\top $$ | Error Source | Typical 1-σ Magnitude | Effect on AEB Decision | |:---|:---:|:---| | **Intrinsic (focal length $f$)** | 0.5 % of $f$ | Distance error $\propto Z^2$ | | **Extrinsic yaw $\psi$** | 0.1° ≈ 1.75 mrad | Lateral error $= Z \cdot \sin\psi$ | | **Extrinsic pitch $\theta$** | 0.2° ≈ 3.5 mrad | Vertical mis-aim | | **Time-sync skew** | 1 ms @ 60 km/h | 1.67 cm longitudinal drift | | **Mounting bracket flex** | 0.05° under braking | Transient AEB suppression | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example A forward radar reports a target at $P_r = (40, 1.5, 0)$ m in its own sensor frame. The radar is mounted with a yaw of $\psi = +0.5°$ and a translation $t = (0.5, 0, 0.2)$ m relative to the vehicle's rear-axle frame. Compute the target's position in the vehicle frame. **Given:** - $\psi = 0.5° = 0.00873$ rad - $t = (0.5, 0, 0.2)$ m - $P_r = (40, 1.5, 0)$ m **Step 1 — Build the yaw rotation matrix (rotation about Z-axis).** $$ R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Plugging $\psi = 0.00873$ rad, with $\cos\psi \approx 0.99996$ and $\sin\psi \approx 0.00873$: $$ R_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0.99996 & -0.00873 & 0 \\ 0.00873 & 0.99996 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Apply rotation.** $$ \begin{aligned} P' &= R_z \cdot P_r \\ P'_x &= 0.99996 \times 40 + (-0.00873) \times 1.5 + 0 \times 0 \\ &= 39.998 - 0.0131 \\ &= 39.985 \text{ m} \\ P'_y &= 0.00873 \times 40 + 0.99996 \times 1.5 + 0 \\ &= 0.3492 + 1.4999 \\ &= 1.849 \text{ m} \\ P'_z &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply translation.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{vehicle}} &= P' + t \\ P_{\text{vehicle},x} &= 39.985 + 0.5 = 40.485 \text{ m} \\ P_{\text{vehicle},y} &= 1.849 + 0 = 1.849 \text{ m} \\ P_{\text{vehicle},z} &= 0 + 0.2 = 0.2 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ P_{\text{vehicle}} = (40.485,\; 1.849,\; 0.2) \text{ m} $$ This shows that even a half-degree yaw error shifts the perceived lateral position by ~35 cm at 40 m range — enough to make AEB misjudge lane position and either brake late (false negative) or brake for an adjacent-lane car (false positive). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ADAS Integration Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Front Camera 8MP] --> BUS1 A2[Surround Camera 4x2MP] --> BUS1 A3[Front Radar 77GHz] --> BUS2 A4[Corner Radar 4x77GHz] --> BUS2 A5[Front LiDAR 128ch] --> BUS3 A6[Ultrasonic 12x] --> BUS4 A7[IMU 6-Axis + GNSS] --> BUS5 BUS1[Automotive Ethernet 1Gbps TSN] --> FUSION BUS2[CAN-FD 5Mbps] --> FUSION BUS3[Automotive Ethernet 10Gbps] --> FUSION BUS4[LIN 20kbps] --> FUSION BUS5[CAN-FD 5Mbps] --> FUSION FUSION[Central ADAS Domain Controller
SoC - NVIDIA Orin or Mobileye EyeQ6] --> APPL APPL[Application Layer
AEB LKA ACC NOA] --> ACT1 APPL --> ACT2 APPL --> ACT3 APPL --> HMI ACT1[EPS - Electric Power Steering] ACT2[ESC - Electronic Stability Control] ACT3[XBrake - Brake by Wire] HMI[Instrument Cluster HUD] subgraph PowerDistribution [Power Distribution 12V plus 48V] PWR1[Battery 12V 60Ah] --> DC1 PWR2[DC-DC Converter 48V to 12V] --> DC1 DC1[Power Distribution Box with Fuses and EMI Filters] --> A1 DC1 --> A2 DC1 --> A3 DC1 --> A4 DC1 --> A5 DC1 --> A6 DC1 --> A7 DC1 --> FUSION end ``` ### 4.2 Sensor Calibration Workflow — From Factory to Field ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Factory [Factory Calibration] S1[Sensor Module Production] --> S2[Intrinsic Calibration
Lab Charuco Board] S2 --> S3[Optical Bench Boresight
Angular Tolerance 0.05 deg] S3 --> S4[Vehicle Body Mounting] end subgraph Workshop [Workshop Calibration] W1[Static Calibration on Level Floor] --> W2[Target Boards at 1m and 5m] W2 --> W3[Computer Vision Auto-Align] W3 --> W4[Dynamic Drive Calibration
40 km h on Marked Lane] end subgraph Online [Online Calibration] O1[Continuous SLAM-style Update] --> O2[Lane Edge Detection Feedback] O2 --> O3[Parking Lot Landmark Matching] O3 --> O4[Periodic Re-Cal Trigger
if Drift exceeds 0.1 deg] end S4 --> W1 W4 --> O1 O4 -. if needed .-> W1 ``` ### 4.3 Communication Bus Topology in a Modern ADAS Vehicle ```mermaid flowchart TB GW[Central Gateway
Ethernet to CAN-FD to LIN Bridge] --- ETH1 GW --- CAN1 GW --- CAN2 GW --- LIN1 ETH1[1000BASE-T1 Ethernet Backbone] --> DC1 ETH1 --> DC2 DC1[ADAS Domain Controller] DC2[Infotainment Domain] CAN1[Powertrain CAN-FD 500kbps] --> ECU1 CAN1 --> ECU2 CAN2[Chassis CAN-FD 2Mbps] --> ECU3 CAN2 --> ECU4 LIN1[LIN Sub-Bus 20kbps] --> ECU5 LIN1 --> ECU6 ECU1[Engine ECU] ECU2[Transmission TCU] ECU3[EPS Module] ECU4[ESC Module] ECU5[Ultrasonic Park ECU] ECU6[Mirror ECU] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Safety Loop per ISO 26262 ```mermaid flowchart TB HARA[HARA Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment] --> ASIL[ASIL Classification A to D] ASIL --> FSR[Functional Safety Requirements] FSR --> TECH[Technical Safety Concept
Hardware plus Software] TECH --> HW[Hardware Architectural Metrics
SPFM LFM PMHF] TECH --> SW[Software Unit plus Integration Testing] HW --> VALID[Validation by Fault Injection FMEA] SW --> VALID VALID --> PROD[Production Release] PROD --> FIELD[Field Monitoring and Updates OTA] FIELD -. feedback loop .-> HARA ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define the term "extrinsic calibration" in the context of ADAS. Why is it critical for sensor fusion?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Extrinsic calibration is the process of determining the rigid 6-DoF transform $(R, t)$ — three rotations (roll, pitch, yaw) and three translations — between a sensor's local coordinate frame and the vehicle's reference frame (typically the rear-axle center). It is critical for sensor fusion because every sensor (camera, radar, LiDAR) reports detections in its own local frame; without a precise extrinsic, a target detected simultaneously by a front camera and a front radar would appear in two different vehicle-frame locations, causing the fusion algorithm to either drop the target (false negative) or duplicate it (ghost object), which can lead to incorrect AEB/LKA actuation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *List any three in-vehicle communication protocols used in modern ADAS vehicles and state one ADAS-relevant application of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **CAN-FD (5 Mbps):** Used for transmitting radar target lists (objects, range, range-rate) and AEB/ESC commands in real time. (1 mark) 2. **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1):** Used for raw camera video and LiDAR point-cloud streaming to the central ADAS domain controller; supports Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) for deterministic latency. (1 mark) 3. **LIN (20 kbps):** Used for low-bandwidth peripherals such as ultrasonic parking sensor clusters, mirror actuators, and rain/light sensors. (1 mark) --- ### **Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)** #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Actual Style]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture for integrating ADAS sensors into a modern vehicle E/E network. Identify the role of the central domain controller and the gateway. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A modern ADAS-equipped vehicle uses a **layered / domain-centralised E/E architecture** comprising five layers: 1. **Sensor / Perception Layer:** Cameras (front 8MP, surround 4×2MP), radar (front 77 GHz long-range, 4× corner 77 GHz short-range), LiDAR (optional, 128-channel), 12× ultrasonic, 6-axis IMU + GNSS. (1 mark) 2. **Communication / Network Layer:** Each sensor family connects via the most appropriate bus — Ethernet (camera, LiDAR), CAN-FD (radar, IMU), LIN (ultrasonic, mirrors). (1 mark) 3. **Central Gateway:** Acts as the protocol bridge between Ethernet, CAN-FD, CAN, and LIN sub-networks; performs routing, firewalling, and wake-up/sleep coordination. (1 mark) 4. **Domain Controller Layer:** A high-performance SoC (e.g., NVIDIA Orin at 254 TOPS, or Mobileye EyeQ6) runs sensor fusion (front + surround + radar), perception (object detection, lane detection, free-space), and planning. (1 mark) 5. **Actuation / HMI Layer:** Commands are dispatched over the chassis CAN-FD to EPS, ESC, brake-by-wire, and the cluster/HUD for driver alerts. (1 mark) *Diagram:* (2 marks — refer to Section 4.1 block diagram). The **central domain controller** consolidates compute that was previously distributed across 80+ ECUs, reducing wiring harness mass by up to 30 % and enabling OTA updates. The **gateway** provides isolation between safety-critical chassis networks and entertainment networks, preventing non-safety messages from blocking AEB. --- **(b)** A forward-looking camera is mounted with a yaw misalignment of +0.4°. At a target distance of 50 m in front of the vehicle, what is the lateral position error in the world frame introduced purely by this yaw error? If the camera's intrinsic focal length is $f = 4.8$ mm with a pixel size of 2.1 µm, compute the corresponding pixel error on the image plane. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Part (i) — Lateral world-frame error:** For a pure yaw miscalibration $\psi$, the lateral error at range $Z$ is: $$ \Delta y_{\text{world}} = Z \cdot \tan(\psi) \approx Z \cdot \psi \quad (\text{small angle}) $$ $$ \Delta y_{\text{world}} = 50 \times 0.4 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 50 \times 0.00698 = 0.349 \text{ m} $$ That is, a target actually on the lane centerline would be perceived as **34.9 cm off-center**.
