(2 marks) **Part (ii) — Pixel error on the image plane:** Convert world error to pixels using the focal length and pixel pitch: $$ \Delta y_{\text{pixel}} = \frac{\Delta y_{\text{world}} \cdot f}{Z \cdot p} $$ where $p = 2.1 \times 10^{-6}$ m is the pixel size, $f = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $Z = 50$ m. $$ \Delta y_{\text{pixel}} = \frac{0.349 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.675 \times 10^{-3}}{1.05 \times 10^{-4}} = 15.95 \text{ pixels} $$ (3 marks for substitution; 2 marks for final computation) **Final Answer:** $\Delta y_{\text{world}} = 0.349$ m and $\Delta y_{\text{pixel}} \approx 16$ pixels. --- #### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **static and dynamic calibration procedures** for a forward-facing ADAS camera after windshield replacement. Mention the typical tolerances and the standards governing the procedure. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** - **Static Calibration (Workshop, level floor):** A level concrete floor with controlled lighting; calibrated target boards (e.g., Bosch, Hella Gutmann, or OEM-specific patterns) placed at prescribed distances (1 m, 5 m, and 10 m) and heights in front of the vehicle. The vehicle's wheel alignment, ride height, and fuel level must be at "ready-to-drive" condition. The ADAS calibration tool runs a computer-vision routine that adjusts the camera's extrinsic $(R, t)$ until the detected pattern corners align with the expected image coordinates to within **±0.1°** angular and **±0.05 m** lateral tolerance. (3 marks) - **Dynamic Calibration (Road):** After static calibration, a 5–20 minute drive at 30–60 km/h on a road with clear lane markings allows the system to fine-tune longitudinal pitch and validates that lane-detection accuracy is within **±5 cm at 30 m**. Some OEMs (Tesla, Toyota) rely entirely on dynamic, eliminating the workshop step. (2 marks) - **Governing Standards:** ISO 26262 (functional safety), ISO 11452 (EMC), SAE J3061 (cybersecurity), and OEM-specific service-information standards (e.g., Volkswagen ELSA, Toyota TIS). (1 mark) - **Pitfalls:** Windshield replacement must use OEM-spec glass with correct wedge angle; otherwise, optical path distortion invalidates calibration. (1 mark) --- **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **AUTOSAR layered software architecture** for an ADAS ECU. How does it aid portability and functional safety? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** - **Application Layer (ASW):** Contains ADAS algorithms (object fusion, AEB logic, LKA control) as software components (SWCs) that communicate via virtual function bus (VFB). (1 mark) - **Runtime Environment (RTE):** Acts as the middleware that maps virtual SWC communication to actual ECU hardware; abstracts OS, communication drivers, and memory. (1 mark) - **Basic Software (BSW) Layer:** Contains services — OS, memory, COM stack (CAN, Ethernet, FlexRay drivers), diagnostic (UDS/DOIP), watchdog, and complex drivers for sensor I/O. (1 mark) - **Microcontroller Abstraction Layer (MCAL):** Lowest layer — direct register-level drivers for ADC, PWM, SPI, FlexIO, etc. (1 mark) - **Diagram:** (2 marks) **Portability benefit:** The same ASW SWC code can be re-compiled and deployed on a different ECU hardware by swapping only the MCAL and BSW — proven by BMW, Daimler, and Bosch reusing perception stacks across 10+ vehicle programs. **Functional-safety benefit:** The BSW enforces memory partitioning, end-to-end protection (E2E), and watchdog supervision, satisfying ISO 26262 ASIL-D requirements. (1 mark) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation** in extrinsic yaw error: writing $\tan(\psi)$ as just $\psi$ without stating the small-angle justification loses ½ mark. > 2. **Confusing intrinsic vs extrinsic calibration:** students often state "calibration finds the focal length" — that is intrinsic; extrinsic is the sensor-to-vehicle rigid transform. > 3. **Not naming the governing standard:** any ADAS integration answer without mention of **ISO 26262** is considered incomplete by KTU examiners. > 4. **Omitting the gateway** while drawing the E/E architecture — KTU consistently awards marks for the gateway's role in network isolation. > 5. **Using the wrong bus for raw camera data:** students often write "camera data goes over CAN" — that is incorrect; raw camera requires Automotive Ethernet. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** - ⭐ **Integration = Electrical + Communication + Computational fusion** — never treat any layer in isolation. - ⭐ **Extrinsic calibration** is the 6-DoF rigid transform $(R, t)$ between sensor frame and vehicle frame — governed by **±0.1°** yaw tolerance. - ⭐ **Intrinsic calibration** finds sensor-internal parameters (focal length, distortion, principal point) using a Charuco or checkerboard pattern in the lab. - ⭐ **In-vehicle bus selection rule of thumb:** Ultrasonic → LIN; Radar/IMU → CAN-FD; Camera/LiDAR → Automotive Ethernet with TSN. - ⭐ **Time synchronisation** within **±1 ms** across all sensors is mandatory for any fusion algorithm — implement via IEEE 802.1AS gPTP. - ⭐ **Functional safety** per ISO 26262 mandates ASIL-D for AEB/LKA, requiring PMHF **< 10⁻⁸** failures/hour. - ⭐ **AUTOSAR Classic** (CP) is for real-time ECUs; **AUTOSAR Adaptive (AP)** for high-performance domain controllers running POSIX OS. - ⭐ **Static workshop calibration** is being progressively replaced by **online dynamic self-calibration** (Tesla, Mobileye) using SLAM-style updates. - ⭐ **Windshield wedge angle** must be OEM-spec — a 1° deviation can shift the camera's optical axis by **0.5°**, invalidating the calibration. - ⭐ **OTA updates** require a fail-safe rollback architecture (A/B partitioning) to comply with ISO 26262 after integration. - ⭐ **Domain consolidation** trend: from ~80 distributed ECUs to 3–5 high-performance domain controllers (ADAS, Cockpit, Powertrain, Body, Chassis). - ⭐ **Sensor mounting tolerance** under dynamic loads: use vibration isolators and validate with 0.05° bracket flex budget under full braking.

topics.title: Calibration Techniques for Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calibration Techniques for Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Calibration in the ADAS / AV Context?

**Calibration** is the process of quantitatively defining the response of a measurement or actuation system to known, traceable inputs, and using that definition to correct the system's output so that it represents the true physical quantity with acceptable accuracy.

In the **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** and **Autonomous Vehicle (AV)** domain, every sensor (camera, LiDAR, radar, IMU, GNSS) and every actuator (steering motor, electronic throttle, brake-by-wire modulator) must be geometrically, temporally, and dynamically calibrated. Without calibration, a vehicle cannot reliably know *where* obstacles are, *how fast* it is moving, or *exactly how much* steering angle has been commanded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> *Calibration* establishes a mathematical relationship between the **raw signal** produced by a sensor/actuator and the corresponding **true physical quantity** (length, angle, time, force, etc.), thereby minimizing systematic errors and aligning the coordinate frames of heterogeneous subsystems.

## 1.2 Sensor Calibration vs. Actuator Calibration

| Aspect | Sensor Calibration | Actuator Calibration |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Map raw measurement → true world quantity | Map command input → true physical action |
| **Output** | Correction parameters, offset, gain, intrinsic matrix | PWM-to-angle, voltage-to-force, dead-zone compensation |
| **KTU Example** | Camera intrinsic matrix **K**, LiDAR-camera extrinsic **T** | Steer-by-wire angle sensor, throttle position curve |
| **Frequency** | Mostly offline + periodic online | Real-time closed-loop |
| **Standards** | ISO 8855, ISO 23150 | ISO 26262, ISO 11446 |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of calibration as the **"first-day eye-test + prescription glasses"** process:

- Your **eyes** (sensors) can see, but they may be near-sighted — calibration writes the "prescription" (intrinsic parameters, distortion coefficients).
- Your **two eyes** must look at the same point from a known geometry — this is **extrinsic calibration** (the relative position/orientation between the two cameras, or between camera and LiDAR).
- Your **hands** (actuators) must turn the steering wheel *exactly* the amount you command — this is **actuator calibration** (mapping command voltage → wheel angle, including dead-band and hysteresis compensation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (must remember):**
> - **Accuracy** = closeness to the *true* value (systematic error).
> - **Precision / Repeatability** = closeness of *repeated* measurements to each other (random error).
> - **Resolution** = smallest detectable change.
> - **Latency** = time delay between physical event and digital output.
> Units: Accuracy and precision in **m**, **m/s**, **deg**, or **pixels** depending on modality.

## 1.4 Why Calibration is Mission-Critical for ADAS / AV

A Level 3+ autonomous vehicle operating at **13.9 m/s** (≈ 50 km/h) travels roughly **3.86 mm every millisecond**. A miscalibrated camera extrinsics of just **0.5°** in pitch translates into a lateral position error of:

$$\Delta y \;\approx\; d\,\tan(0.5^\circ)$$

For an object at distance $d = 50\,\text{m}$:

$$\Delta y \;\approx\; 50 \times \tan(0.5^\circ) \;\approx\; 0.436\,\text{m}$$

That is enough to misclassify the lane and trigger a false AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) event or, worse, miss a real one. Hence calibration is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Calibration Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes calibration along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Modality**
   - **Camera Calibration** — intrinsic (focal length, principal point, skew, distortion) + extrinsic (rotation, translation).
   - **LiDAR Calibration** — mechanical (bore-sight angle, range offset) and temporal (time-stamping).
   - **Radar Calibration** — range, Doppler, angle bias.
   - **IMU Calibration** — bias, scale-factor, cross-axis sensitivity, Allan-variance.
   - **GNSS Calibration** — lever-arm, antenna phase centre, multi-path.

2. **By Target / Stimulus**
   - **Target-based** (chessboard, AprilTag, calibration board, corner reflector).
   - **Targetless / Self-calibration** (motion-based, SLAM-based, online bundle adjustment).
   - **Stimulus-based** (known acceleration on shaker table, known turn rate on rate table).

3. **By Timing**
   - **Offline (Factory / Service)** — performed in a controlled environment.
   - **Online (In-field)** — continuous, real-time, using redundancy and motion.
   - **Periodic / Event-triggered** — after collision, suspension change, windshield replacement.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Generic Calibration Pipeline

The reasoning behind *every* calibration method, irrespective of sensor, follows six universal steps. Memorize them:

- **Step 1 — Define the measurand.** What physical quantity are we trying to recover?
- **Step 2 — Choose a reference (ground truth).** A standard of known value must be available.
- **Step 3 — Acquire raw data.** The sensor observes the reference under varied conditions (poses, distances, temperatures).
- **Step 4 — Model the system.** A parametric model links raw data → measurand (e.g., pinhole model, polynomial).
- **Step 5 — Estimate parameters.** Optimization (least squares, Levenberg–Marquardt, RANSAC) yields parameters.
- **Step 6 — Validate.** Compute residuals, reprojection error, or cross-validation error against held-out data.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked "explain calibration", always list these six steps. It instantly shows structured thinking and earns full marks.

## 2.3 Why and How of Each Major Sub-Module

### 2.3.1 Pinhole Camera — Why and How
- **Why:** A real camera lens distorts and projects 3-D world points onto a 2-D sensor. We need a *mathematical inverse* so that a 2-D pixel can be back-projected into a 3-D ray for triangulation, object detection, and SLAM.
- **How:** Define intrinsic matrix **K**, extrinsic **[R ∣ t]**, and distortion coefficients, then solve by observing a planar calibration target of known geometry from many poses.

### 2.3.2 LiDAR–Camera Fusion — Why and How
- **Why:** LiDAR gives accurate depth but sparse points; cameras give dense texture but no absolute scale. Fusing them requires a *rigid 6-DoF transform* between their coordinate frames.
- **How:** Detect a common target (e.g., a checkerboard with retro-reflective corners) in both modalities, solve **AX = XB** (hand-eye) or PnP-style optimization, and refine with non-linear least squares.

### 2.3.3 Steer-by-Wire Actuator — Why and How
- **Why:** A commanded PWM of 1500 µs may not produce exactly 0° steering angle; dead-band, friction, and hysteresis corrupt the command. Safety-critical lane-keeping (LKAS) demands < 0.1° accuracy.
- **How:** Sweep the steering motor across full range, record command vs. measured angle from a high-resolution encoder, fit a polynomial or lookup table, and verify with a centre-finding routine.

### 2.3.4 IMU — Why and How
- **Why:** MEMS gyros/accelerometers have bias (turn-on drift), scale-factor errors, and cross-axis coupling. A 1°/h gyro error becomes 1.7 m position drift after 1 hour of dead reckoning.
- **How:** Use the **six-position tumble test** (gravity reversal) for accelerometer, and **rate-table** for gyroscope; estimate bias $b$ and scale $s$ via least squares.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically canonical** and appear in KTU 2024 expected answer sheets. Master the symbols and units.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s\,\mathbf{m} = K\,[\,R \mid t\,]\,\mathbf{M}$ | Pinhole projection: world $\mathbf{M}$ → pixel $\mathbf{m}$ | Camera calibration | $s$: scale (px) |
| 2 | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic matrix | Camera calibration | $f_x, f_y$ in **px**, $c_x, c_y$ in **px** |
| 3 | $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ | Radial distance for distortion | Lens distortion | dimensionless |
| 4 | $x_d = x(1+k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + 2p_1 xy + p_2(r^2+2x^2)$ | Brown–Conrady distortion | Lens correction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $y_d = y(1+k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + p_1(r^2+2y^2) + 2p_2 xy$ | Brown–Conrady distortion | Lens correction | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{m}_i - \hat{\mathbf{m}}_i \Vert_2$ | Reprojection error (RMS) | Calibration validation | **pixels** |
| 7 | $A\,X = X\,B$ | Hand-eye / sensor-fusion constraint | LiDAR–camera extrinsics | homogeneous 4×4 |
| 8 | $\mathbf{q} = [q_w, q_x, q_y, q_z]^\top$ with $q_w^2+q_x^2+q_y^2+q_z^2 = 1$ | Unit quaternion | Rotation representation | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R}\right)$ | Ackermann steering angle | Actuator geometry | **rad** or **deg** |
| 10 | $\theta_{\text{cmd}} = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3$ | Polynomial command map | Steer-by-wire calibration | **deg** or **rad** |
| 11 | $\epsilon_{\text{static}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{meas}}}{\mu_{\text{true}}}\times 100\%$ | Static accuracy (relative) | Validation | **%** |
| 12 | $b = \dfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{a}_{+g} + \mathbf{a}_{-g})$ | Accelerometer bias from 6-position test | IMU calibration | **m/s²** |
| 13 | $\sigma_{\text{Allan}}(T) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2(N-1)}\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}(\bar{\Omega}_{k+1} - \bar{\Omega}_k)^2}$ | Allan deviation | IMU noise characterization | varies |

> [!WARNING]
> Notice that the relative-accuracy formula above uses division and a percentage. Do **not** confuse it with the absolute accuracy formula $\epsilon = \mu_{\text{meas}} - \mu_{\text{true}}$. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Camera intrinsic calibration** is the foundation of structure-from-motion (SfM), visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM3, VINS-Mono), and visual odometry.
- **LiDAR–camera extrinsics** are essential for **3-D object detection** (PointPainting, BEVFusion, TransFusion).
- **IMU calibration** is mandatory for **INS/GNSS** integrated navigation in GNSS-denied tunnels.
- **Actuator calibration** is at the heart of **functional safety** (ISO 26262) — without it, the controller is operating on a *wrong* plant model.
- **Online self-calibration** keeps the AV safe across the entire vehicle life-cycle: thermal expansion, vibration, aging, accident repair.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pinhole Projection Equation

The pinhole model is the bedrock of camera calibration. The full derivation is given below; every algebraic step is explicit.

### 3.1.1 World Point to Camera Coordinates

Let a 3-D world point be $\mathbf{M}_w = [X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^\top$ in homogeneous form. The camera coordinate system is obtained via a rigid-body transformation composed of a rotation $\mathbf{R}$ (3×3) and a translation $\mathbf{t}$ (3×1):

$$
\mathbf{M}_c = \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Compose rigid transform.** This places the world point into the camera's own frame where the optical axis is $Z_c$.

**Step 2 — Drop the homogeneous row** for the next step (we only need $[X_c, Y_c, Z_c]^\top$).

### 3.1.2 Perspective Division

The ideal pinhole projects the 3-D point onto the image plane at $Z = f$ (focal length), using similar triangles:

$$
x_i = f\,\dfrac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_i = f\,\dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply perspective division** so that depth $Z_c$ becomes the scaling denominator.

### 3.1.3 Inclusion of Intrinsics (Finite Image Sensor)

The image plane is at a distance $f$ from the optical centre, but pixels are sampled in a digital array. Each pixel has a physical size $(s_x, s_y)$ and a principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ which is the pixel-coordinate image of the optical centre:

$$
u = f\,s_x\,\dfrac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \qquad v = f\,s_y\,\dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to pixel units** by multiplying with sensor pitch $s_x, s_y$ and translating by principal point $(c_x, c_y)$.

Define $f_x = f\,s_x$ and $f_y = f\,s_y$ for compactness. Allowing for a possible skew $\gamma$ (non-rectangular pixels) we get:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\sim\; \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the intrinsic matrix K.** The symbol $\sim$ means "equal up to a non-zero scale factor" because of homogeneous coordinates.

**Step 6 — Combine with extrinsics.** The full projection is therefore:

$$
s\,\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K\,\bigl[\,\mathbf{R} \;\;\mathbf{t}\,\bigr]\,\begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $s = Z_c$ is the projective scale.

> [!NOTE]
> This single equation is the **most-tested formula** in KTU PEMUT865 for the calibration topic. Be ready to write it from memory and explain each of the 9 entries of $K$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Lens-Distortion Correction (Brown–Conrady)

Real lenses deviate from the ideal pinhole. The dominant error is **radial** (image points move radially inward or outward), and a smaller **tangential** component arises from de-centring of lens elements.

### 3.2.1 Ideal (Distortion-Free) Projection

Let $(x, y)$ be the **normalized** image coordinates (i.e., after dividing by $Z_c$). So:

$$
x = \dfrac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y = \dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c}
$$

**Step 7 — Define normalized coordinates.** These are dimensionless, lying on the $Z=1$ plane.

### 3.2.2 Radial Distortion

Empirically, the radial offset $\Delta r$ depends on the square of the radius $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$:

$$
x_d^{\text{rad}} = x\,(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)
$$

$$
y_d^{\text{rad}} = y\,(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)
$$

**Step 8 — Model radial displacement.** $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are the **radial distortion coefficients** to be calibrated; $k_1$ dominates for moderate fields of view, $k_3$ is needed for wide-angle / fisheye lenses.

### 3.2.3 Tangential Distortion

Tangential distortion is described by two further coefficients $p_1, p_2$:

$$
x_d^{\text{tan}} = 2p_1 xy + p_2 (r^2 + 2x^2)
$$

$$
y_d^{\text{tan}} = p_1 (r^2 + 2y^2) + 2p_2 xy
$$

**Step 9 — Model tangential displacement.** This arises when lens groups are not perfectly aligned with the optical axis.

### 3.2.4 Total Distorted Coordinates

Adding both contributions gives the **Brown–Conrady model** used in OpenCV's `calibrateCamera()`:

$$
x_d = x\,(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + 2p_1 xy + p_2 (r^2 + 2x^2)
$$

$$
y_d = y\,(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + p_1 (r^2 + 2y^2) + 2p_2 xy
$$

**Step 10 — Form the full distorted point** and convert to pixels via $K$:

$$
u_d = f_x\,x_d + c_x, \qquad v_d = f_y\,y_d + c_y
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Order of execution in software:** KTU examiners often ask the *order* of distortion correction. The answer is: **(a)** apply intrinsics, **(b)** normalize, **(c)** add distortion in the *forward* direction during projection, and **(d)** use *undistort* (iterative inverse) during measurement correction.

## 3.3 Derivation of Reprojection Error

After calibration, we must measure how well the estimated parameters fit the data.

### 3.3.1 Reprojection

For every observed 3-D point $\mathbf{M}_w^{(i)}$, project it using the estimated $K, \mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i$ to obtain a predicted pixel $\hat{\mathbf{m}}_i = [\hat{u}_i, \hat{v}_i]^\top$.

**Step 11 — Re-project all N points** under the calibrated parameters.

### 3.3.2 Per-Point Error

The Euclidean pixel distance between the predicted and observed pixel is:

$$
e_i = \Vert \mathbf{m}_i - \hat{\mathbf{m}}_i \Vert_2 = \sqrt{(u_i - \hat{u}_i)^2 + (v_i - \hat{v}_i)^2}
$$

**Step 12 — Compute the L2 norm** of each residual.

### 3.3.3 RMS Aggregate

$$
E = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i^{\,2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{m}_i - \hat{\mathbf{m}}_i \Vert_2^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 13 — Take the RMS** over all points. For a well-calibrated ADAS front camera, $E$ should be **< 0.3 px** on a checkerboard dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU pitfall: students sometimes use mean absolute error (MAE) instead of RMS. The KTU 2024 expected metric is the **RMS reprojection error** in pixels, computed as above.

## 3.4 Derivation of Hand-Eye Calibration (AX = XB)

This is the mathematical heart of **LiDAR–Camera fusion calibration** and **multi-sensor extrinsic** estimation.

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

Let:
- $\mathbf{X}$ = unknown 4×4 rigid transform between two sensors (e.g., camera and LiDAR), constant.
- $\mathbf{A}_k$ = known motion of sensor 1 (camera) between pose $k$ and pose $k+1$ — obtained from fiducials.
- $\mathbf{B}_k$ = known motion of sensor 2 (LiDAR) between the same two poses — obtained from matching.

The hand-eye equation then reads:

$$
\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{B}_k
$$

**Step 14 — Derive the constraint.** This is the classical result of **Tsai & Lenz (1989)** and **Park & Martin (1994)**. Intuitively, if you move the *robot/sensor rig* by $\mathbf{A}_k$ in world, and the *tool* on the robot moves by $\mathbf{B}_k$ relative to the same world, then the hand-eye transform $\mathbf{X}$ must commute with both motions.

### 3.4.2 Two-Stage Solution (Tsai)

- **Stage 1 — Solve for rotation.** Rewrite the rotation part of the equation. Let $\mathbf{A}_k = \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{R}_{A,k} & \mathbf{t}_{A,k}\\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1\end{bmatrix}$ and similarly for $\mathbf{B}_k$. Then the rotation block yields:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{A,k}\,\mathbf{R}_X = \mathbf{R}_X\,\mathbf{R}_{B,k}
$$

Taking the log of both sides converts this to a linear system in the **Rodrigues parameters** of $\mathbf{R}_X$, solvable by SVD.

**Step 15 — Linearize rotation** using axis-angle or quaternion log.

- **Stage 2 — Solve for translation.** Once $\mathbf{R}_X$ is known, the translation block becomes linear:

$$
(\mathbf{R}_{A,k} - \mathbf{I})\,\mathbf{t}_X = \mathbf{R}_X\,\mathbf{t}_{B,k} - \mathbf{t}_{A,k}
$$

Solve this linear system in the least-squares sense over all motion pairs $k = 1, \dots, M$.

**Step 16 — Solve translation by least squares** with $M \geq 2$ independent motions.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 expected minimum for hand-eye calibration is **two non-parallel motions** of the sensor rig. Fewer motions yield an under-determined system.

## 3.5 Derivation of Steer-by-Wire Actuator Calibration Map

### 3.5.1 Ackermann Steering Geometry

For a vehicle with wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\delta$ on the inner wheel, the instantaneous centre of rotation lies on the rear-axle perpendicular at a radius:

$$
R = \dfrac{L}{\tan \delta}
$$

**Step 17 — Geometric derivation.** This is the **Ackermann condition** that ensures both front wheels turn about a common centre, eliminating tire scrub.

### 3.5.2 Command-to-Angle Polynomial

The relationship between the controller command $u$ (e.g., PWM duty cycle in µs, or torque request in Nm) and the measured front-wheel angle $\theta$ is non-linear due to friction, linkage compliance, and dead-zone. We model it as a cubic polynomial:

$$
\theta(u) = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3
$$

**Step 18 — Empirical polynomial model.** The four coefficients $\{a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3\}$ are calibrated by recording $N$ samples $(u_i, \theta_i)$ and solving:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & u_1 & u_1^2 & u_1^3 \\ 1 & u_2 & u_2^2 & u_2^3 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 1 & u_N & u_N^2 & u_N^3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \\ \vdots \\ \theta_N \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 19 — Solve the linear system** by pseudoinverse $\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{U}^\top\mathbf{U})^{-1}\mathbf{U}^\top\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

### 3.5.3 Centre-Finding Validation

To validate the calibrated map, the wheel must return to within $\pm 0.1^\circ$ of mechanical centre when commanded. The residual error after polynomial compensation is:

$$
\epsilon_{\text{centre}} = \theta_{\text{meas}}(u_{\text{ctr}}) - \theta_{\text{true,ctr}}
$$

**Step 20 — Validate at the centre command** as a final acceptance test.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Calibration Methods

| Method | Type | Target / Stimulus | Accuracy | Computation | Use in ADAS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zhang's checkerboard | Offline, target | Planar grid | ~ 0.1 px RMS | Medium (LM) | Front/rear/surround cameras |
| OpenCV `calibrateCamera` | Offline, target | Planar grid | ~ 0.2 px RMS | Medium | Mono / stereo cameras |
| Kalibr | Offline, target | AprilGrid / checkerboard | ~ 0.1–0.3 px | High | IMU–camera fusion |
| Targetless online (SLAM) | Online, targetless | Scene structure | ~ 0.5–1.0 px | High | Continuous AV self-cal |
| Bore-sight (LiDAR) | Offline, target | Corner cube on board | ~ 0.05° | Low | LiDAR mounting |
| Hand-eye (AX=XB) | Offline, target | Calibration rig | ~ 0.1° / 1 cm | Medium | LiDAR–camera fusion |
| Six-position tumble | Offline, stimulus | Gravity reversals | < 0.5 mGal | Low | MEMS IMU |
| Rate-table gyro cal. | Offline, stimulus | Known rotation rate | < 0.01°/s | Low | Gyroscope |
| Polynomial actuator map | Online / offline | Sweep command | < 0.1° | Low | Steer-by-wire |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Multi-Sensor Calibration Pipeline

The following block diagram shows the **complete calibration pipeline** of a typical Level-3 autonomous vehicle, mapping how raw sensor and actuator data flow through the calibration stages to produce a unified self-consistent vehicle model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef sensor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    camFront[Front Camera Raw Frames]:::sensor
    camRear[Rear Camera Raw Frames]:::sensor
    camSide[Side Camera Raw Frames]:::sensor
    lidarTop[Roof LiDAR Point Cloud]:::sensor
    radarFrnt[Front Radar Track List]:::sensor
    imuBody[Body IMU 6 Axis Data]:::sensor
    gnssAntenna[GNSS Receiver Raw]:::sensor

    camIntr[Camera Intrinsic Calibration<br>Zhang Method 9 Parameter]:::process
    camExtr[Camera Extrinsic Calibration<br>Pose Graph Optimisation]:::process
    lidarIntr[LiDAR Intrinsic Calibration<br>Range and Angular Bias]:::process
    lidarCam[LiDAR Camera Fusion<br>Hand Eye AX equals XB]:::process
    radarCal[Radar Calibration<br>Range Doppler Bias]:::process
    imuCal[IMU Calibration<br>Bias and Scale Factor]:::process
    gnssCal[GNSS Lever Arm and<br>Antenna Phase Centre]:::process

    tempSync[Temporal Synchronisation<br>PTP and Hardware Trigger]:::process
    spatAlign[Spatial Alignment<br>Vehicle Body Frame]:::process
    fusionModel[Unified Vehicle Sensor Model]:::output
    selfCal[Online Self Calibration<br>Motion Based and SLAM]:::process

    steer[Steer by Wire Motor]:::actuator
    steerCal[Steering Actuator Calibration<br>Polynomial Map and Centre]:::process
    throttle[Electronic Throttle Body]:::actuator
    thrCal[Throttle Calibration<br>PWM to Pedal Map]:::process
    brake[Brake by Wire Modulator]:::actuator
    brkCal[Brake Calibration<br>Pressure Sensor Linearisation]:::process
    ctrlUnit[Vehicle Control Unit<br>Validated Plant Model]:::output

    camFront --> camIntr --> camExtr
    camRear --> camIntr
    camSide --> camIntr
    lidarTop --> lidarIntr --> lidarCam
    camExtr --> lidarCam
    radarFrnt --> radarCal
    imuBody --> imuCal
    gnssAntenna --> gnssCal

    camExtr --> tempSync
    lidarCam --> tempSync
    radarCal --> tempSync
    imuCal --> tempSync
    gnssCal --> tempSync

    tempSync --> spatAlign
    spatAlign --> fusionModel
    fusionModel --> selfCal
    selfCal --> spatAlign

    steer --> steerCal --> ctrlUnit
    throttle --> thrCal --> ctrlUnit
    brake --> brkCal --> ctrlUnit
    fusionModel --> ctrlUnit
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above represents a **block-level functional architecture**, not a physical wiring diagram. This is the recommended KTU drawing style for viva and written examinations.

## 4.2 Camera Calibration Coordinate-Frame Hierarchy

The relationship among the four coordinate frames involved in camera calibration is fundamental and frequently asked in KTU viva.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef frame fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef transf fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000

    W[World Frame<br>Origin at Target Centre]:::frame
    C[Camera Frame<br>Origin at Optical Centre]:::frame
    I[Image Frame<br>Origin at Top Left Pixel]:::frame
    D[Distorted Image<br>Lens Effects Included]:::frame

    Extr[Extrinsic Transform<br>R 3x3 and t 3x1]:::transf
    Intr[Intrinsic Matrix<br>K 3x3]:::transf
    Dist[Brown Conrady Distortion<br>k1 k2 p1 p2 k3]:::transf

    W -- Extr --> C
    C -- Intr --> I
    I -- Dist --> D
```

## 4.3 Sensor-Fusion Hand-Eye Calibration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    pose1[Move Rig to Pose 1]:::step

    X[Recovered Transform X<br>4x4 Homogeneous]:::result

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define sensor calibration and actuator calibration in the context of ADAS. List any two standard calibration targets used for camera calibration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Sensor calibration* is the process of determining the mathematical relationship between a sensor's raw output and the true physical quantity being measured, in order to minimize systematic errors such as bias, scale-factor error, distortion, and frame misalignment. In ADAS, this includes camera intrinsic/extrinsic calibration, LiDAR bore-sight, radar range/Doppler bias, and IMU bias estimation.

*Actuator calibration* is the process of determining the mathematical mapping from a controller command to the actual physical response of the actuator — for example, from steering PWM duty cycle to actual wheel angle, or from brake-pressure request to wheel-cylinder pressure.

**Two standard calibration targets for cameras:**
1. **Planar checkerboard pattern** (used in Zhang's method) — easy to fabricate and detect corner features.
2. **AprilGrid / ChArUco board** — combines checkerboard with ArUco markers for robust detection even under partial occlusion, widely used in Kalibr.

*(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for the two named targets.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic calibration? State the typical number of parameters in each for a single pinhole camera with radial–tangential distortion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Intrinsic calibration** determines the *internal* optical parameters of the camera — focal length $(f_x, f_y)$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, skew $\gamma$, and the lens distortion coefficients $k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$.
- **Extrinsic calibration** determines the *external* pose of the camera in a chosen world frame — rotation $\mathbf{R}$ (3 parameters, e.g., Euler angles or 4 quaternion components) and translation $\mathbf{t}$ (3 parameters).

**Parameter counts for a single pinhole camera with Brown–Conrady distortion:**

| Group | Parameters | Count |
|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic $(f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma)$ | 5 |
| Distortion $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$ | 5 |
| **Total intrinsic** | **10** |
| Extrinsic rotation | 3 |
| Extrinsic translation | 3 |
| **Total extrinsic per view** | **6** |

*(1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark for intrinsic count = 10, 1 mark for extrinsic = 6 per view.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the pinhole camera projection equation starting from a 3-D world point and explain each term of the intrinsic matrix $K$. State the typical order of execution when applying and removing lens distortion.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **Zhang's planar calibration method**. Describe the role of the homography, the constraints it imposes on the intrinsic matrix, and the closed-form plus non-linear refinement procedure used to recover all parameters.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

We begin with a 3-D world point $\mathbf{M}_w = [X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^\top$ in homogeneous form.

**Step 1 — World to camera transform:**

$$
\mathbf{M}_c = \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Perspective projection** with focal length $f$:

$$
x = f\,\dfrac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y = f\,\dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Pixel sampling** with sensor pitch $s_x, s_y$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, including skew $\gamma$:

$$
s\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $f_x = f\,s_x$ and $f_y = f\,s_y$.

**Meaning of each entry of $K$:**

- $f_x, f_y$ — focal length in **pixel units along $x$ and $y$** axes.
- $c_x, c_y$ — coordinates of the **principal point** (image of the optical centre).
- $\gamma$ — **skew** between image axes; usually 0 for modern CMOS sensors.

**Order of execution:**

1. Apply extrinsics to map world → camera frame.
2. Apply intrinsics to project onto image plane.
3. Apply Brown–Conrady distortion *forward* during projection.
4. To **remove** distortion (image rectification), use an iterative inverse of the distortion function because it is a non-linear polynomial without a closed-form inverse for $k_i \neq 0$.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for explanation of $K$, 2 for distortion order.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Zhang's Method (1999)** is the de-facto standard for offline camera intrinsic calibration. It uses a planar target (checkerboard) viewed from ≥ 3 different orientations.

**Step 1 — Acquire images.** Capture $N \geq 3$ images of the planar target at different poses; assume the target plane is $Z_w = 0$.

**Step 2 — Estimate homography per view.** The 3×3 homography $\mathbf{H}_i$ mapping target plane to image satisfies:

$$
s\,\mathbf{m}_i = \mathbf{H}_i\,\begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{H}_i = \lambda_i\,[\,\mathbf{r}_1 \;\; \mathbf{r}_2 \;\; \mathbf{t}\,]
$$

where $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2$ are the first two columns of $\mathbf{R}_i$ and $\lambda_i$ is a non-zero scale.

**Step 3 — Intrinsic constraints.** Using $\mathbf{r}_1 \perp \mathbf{r}_2$ and $\Vert \mathbf{r}_1 \Vert = \Vert \mathbf{r}_2 \Vert$, two independent constraints on the intrinsic matrix are obtained from each view:

$$
\mathbf{h}_1^\top\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{h}_2 = 0
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_1^\top\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{h}_1 = \mathbf{h}_2^\top\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{h}_2
$$

where $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{K}^{-\top}\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ is the **image of the absolute conic**.

**Step 4 — Closed-form solution.** With $N \geq 3$ views, the six unknowns of $\mathbf{B}$ are solved linearly; $\mathbf{K}$ is then obtained by Cholesky factorisation of $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$.

**Step 5 — Recover extrinsics per view:**

$$
\mathbf{r}_1 = \lambda\,\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_1, \quad \mathbf{r}_2 = \lambda\,\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_2, \quad \mathbf{r}_3 = \mathbf{r}_1 \times \mathbf{r}_2, \quad \mathbf{t} = \lambda\,\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_3
$$

**Step 6 — Non-linear refinement.** Minimise the **reprojection error** (RMS in pixels) over all $N$ views and all detected corners using Levenberg–Marquardt.

$$
\min_{K, \mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i, k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2, k_3} \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{M} \Vert \mathbf{m}_{ij} - \hat{\mathbf{m}}_{ij}(K, \mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i, k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2, k_3) \Vert_2^{\,2}
$$

**Validation:** Accept the calibration only if $E_{\text{RMS}} < 0.3\,\text{px}$ and $\det(\mathbf{R}_i) > 0$ for all views.

*(7 marks: 1 for setup, 2 for homography & constraints, 2 for closed-form, 1 for refinement objective, 1 for validation.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **hand-eye calibration problem** ($A\,X = X\,B$) used for LiDAR–camera fusion. Derive the two-stage Tsai solution and state the minimum number of motion pairs required.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the calibration of a **steer-by-wire actuator** for an autonomous vehicle, including the Ackermann relationship, polynomial command map, dead-zone compensation, and centre-finding acceptance test.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The hand-eye calibration problem arises when we mount two sensors (e.g., a camera and a LiDAR) on the *same moving rig* and wish to recover the rigid 6-DoF transform $\mathbf{X}$ between them. Let:

- $\mathbf{A}_k$ = motion of the rig (measured by sensor 1, e.g., camera) from pose $k$ to pose $k+1$.
- $\mathbf{B}_k$ = motion of the same physical event measured by sensor 2 (e.g., LiDAR).
- $\mathbf{X}$ = the unknown sensor-to-sensor transform, constant.

Then, because moving the rig moves both sensors rigidly:

$$
\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{B}_k
$$

This is the **AX = XB** constraint introduced by Tsai & Lenz (1989).

**Stage 1 — Rotation recovery (closed-form).** Write $\mathbf{A}_k = \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{R}_A & \mathbf{t}_A\\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1\end{bmatrix}$ and similarly for $\mathbf{B}_k$. The rotation block gives:

$$
\mathbf{R}_A\,\mathbf{R}_X = \mathbf{R}_X\,\mathbf{R}_B
$$

Apply logarithm to convert to a linear system in the angle-axis vector of $\mathbf{R}_X$. With $M \geq 2$ non-parallel motions, solve by **SVD**:

$$
\min_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_X} \sum_{k=1}^{M} \Vert \log(\mathbf{R}_A \mathbf{R}_X \mathbf{R}_B^{-1}) - \boldsymbol{0} \Vert^2
$$

**Stage 2 — Translation recovery.** Once $\mathbf{R}_X$ is known, the translation block becomes:

$$
(\mathbf{R}_A - \mathbf{I})\,\mathbf{t}_X = \mathbf{R}_X\,\mathbf{t}_B - \mathbf{t}_A
$$

Stack over $M$ motions and solve by least squares.

**Minimum number of motions:** $M \geq 2$ non-collinear, non-parallel rotations are mathematically sufficient; in practice **$M \geq 5$** is used for noise robustness.

*(7 marks: 2 for problem formulation, 2 for rotation, 2 for translation, 1 for minimum motions.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Ackermann geometry derivation.** For a vehicle of wheelbase $L$ turning about an instantaneous centre at radius $R$:

$$
\delta_{\text{inner}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R}\right), \qquad \delta_{\text{outer}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R + T}\right)
$$

where $T$ is the track width. The condition $\delta_{\text{inner}} > \delta_{\text{outer}}$ is the **Ackermann condition**, ensuring both front wheels are perpendicular to the same radius — minimising tire scrub.

**Step 2 — Static calibration of the command map.** Sweep the steering command $u$ (PWM in µs) across the full range, e.g., from $u_{\min} = 1000$ to $u_{\max} = 2000$ µs in 21 steps, holding each step for 2 s. Record the measured angle $\theta_i$ from the high-resolution encoder. Fit a cubic polynomial:

$$
\theta(u) = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3
$$

Solve the linear least-squares system for the four coefficients.

**Step 3 — Dead-zone compensation.** Identify the smallest $\vert u - u_{\text{ctr}} \vert$ that produces a measurable change in $\theta$. Subtract this half-width from the command before sending it to the motor. This compensates for static friction in the steering column.

**Step 4 — Hysteresis correction (optional).** Capture *increasing* and *decreasing* sweeps separately. Fit two polynomials $\theta_{\uparrow}(u)$ and $\theta_{\downarrow}(u)$; during operation, blend based on the sign of $\dot{u}$.

**Step 5 — Centre-finding acceptance test.** Command $u = u_{\text{ctr}}$ (the value that *should* map to $\theta = 0^\circ$). The acceptance criterion is:

$$
\vert \theta_{\text{meas}}(u_{\text{ctr}}) \vert \;\le\; 0.1^\circ
$$

If exceeded, recenter by adjusting $a_0$ and repeat.

**Step 6 — In-vehicle validation.** Drive at 30 km/h in a straight line, log the lateral offset from lane centre over 1 minute; the standard deviation should be $< 5$ cm for an LKAS-grade calibration.

*(7 marks: 1 for Ackermann, 2 for polynomial map, 2 for dead-zone/hysteresis, 1 for centre test, 1 for in-vehicle validation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing world frame with camera frame** in the projection equation — examiners will deduct 1 mark if you write the matrix form but label the columns wrongly.
> 2. **Forgetting the homogeneous scale $s$** in $s\,\mathbf{m} = K[R \mid t]\mathbf{M}$. Always state "$s$ is the projective scale (depth)".
> 3. **Mixing forward and inverse distortion** — forward distortion is used to *generate* synthetic distorted images; inverse (iterative) is used to *rectify* captured images.
> 4. **Hand-eye minimum motions** — many students answer "two motions" but do not specify *non-parallel*; full credit requires the word "non-parallel".
> 5. **Steering calibration** — students often forget the **dead-zone** and the **in-vehicle lane-keeping validation**; both are required by KTU 2024 marking scheme.
> 6. **Units** — angles must be in **rad** for internal math, but steering metrics in KTU are expected in **deg** for the final answer; convert explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it in your pocket the night before the exam.**

- **Pinhole model:** $s\,\mathbf{m} = K\,[\,\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}\,]\,\mathbf{M}$.
- **Intrinsic matrix** has 5 unknowns: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$.
- **Distortion model** is Brown–Conrady: 3 radial ($k_1, k_2, k_3$) + 2 tangential ($p_1, p_2$).
- **Zhang's method** needs $\geq 3$ planar views; closed-form from homography, then LM refinement.
- **Reprojection error** is the **RMS pixel distance** between observed and re-projected corners — must be $< 0.3$ px for ADAS cameras.
- **Hand-eye equation:** $\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{B}_k$; need $\geq 2$ non-parallel motions (Tsai) or $\geq 5$ for robustness.
- **IMU calibration** uses the **six-position tumble test** to find accelerometer bias $b = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{a}_{+g} + \mathbf{a}_{-g})$; gyros are calibrated on a **rate table**; noise is characterized by the **Allan deviation** $\sigma_{\text{Allan}}(T)$.
- **Steer-by-wire map** is a cubic polynomial $\theta(u) = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3$; acceptance is $\vert \theta(u_{\text{ctr}}) \vert \le 0.1^\circ$.
- **Ackermann geometry:** $\delta = \arctan(L/R)$.
- **Online self-calibration** is mandatory for AVs and must include **temporal synchronisation** (PTP / hardware trigger) before spatial alignment.
- **Order of operations in any sensor calibration:** define measurand → choose reference → acquire data → model → estimate → validate.
- **Units to remember:** focal length in **pixels**, steering angle in **deg** (or **rad**), LiDAR range in **m**, IMU bias in **m/s²** (accel) or **rad/s** (gyro), reprojection error in **pixels**, accuracy in **%** of true value.
- **KTU 2024 hot keywords:** *intrinsic, extrinsic, distortion, hand-eye, reprojection, bore-sight, lever-arm, Allan deviation, Ackermann, dead-zone, polynomial command map, online self-calibration, PTP synchronisation.*
- **Always state the assumption** "$Z_w = 0$ for a planar target" when deriving Zhang's method — examiners mark this as a vital step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges in System Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Challenges in System Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**System Integration** in the context of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) is defined as the systematic process of composing heterogeneous subsystems — including perception, planning, actuation, communication, and energy management — into a unified, functionally cohesive cyber-physical system that satisfies ISO 26262 functional safety, ISO/SAE 21434 cybersecurity, real-time deterministic latency budgets, and ASAM/ AUTOSAR standardization constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module 4):** *System Integration challenges refer to the technical, architectural, functional, safety, and validation-related obstacles encountered when combining disparate sensors (cameras, radar, LiDAR, ultrasonic, IMU/GNSS), ECUs, communication buses (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet), middleware (ROS 2, AUTOSAR Adaptive), and AI/ML inference pipelines into a single, certifiable, production-grade autonomous driving stack.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Metaphor

Imagine trying to build a single human being by **transplanting organs from different donors** that were never originally designed to work together. You have:
- A heart (engine ECU) designed for one type of blood pressure
- Eyes (cameras) that see in daylight but are blind at night
- Ears (radar) that hear Doppler shifts but cannot see shapes
- A spinal cord (CAN bus) that only carries **1000 messages per second**
- A brain (SoC compute platform) that processes in **watts**, not megawatts

Every organ speaks a different language, has a different latency, and was built by a different manufacturer. **The challenge of system integration is the engineering discipline of making all these organs cooperate as if they were designed from birth as one body** — within milliseconds, at 130 km/h, in rain, fog, snow, with zero tolerance for software crashes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world context (2024–2026):** Waymo, Tesla FSD, Cruise, and Mercedes Drive Pilot all reported system-integration-related recalls or delays. Tesla's 2023 recall of 362,000 FSD vehicles and Cruise's October 2023 suspension were both rooted in **system integration failures** — not in any single sensor being defective.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **End-to-End Latency Budget (L4 Autonomy)** | **< 100 ms** | Perception-to-actuation loop |
| **Sensor Data Rate (8MP Camera @ 30 fps)** | **≈ 720 MB/s** | Raw data ingestion |
| **Automotive Ethernet Bandwidth** | **1 Gbps / 2.5 Gbps / 10 Gbps** | 100BASE-T1 to 1000BASE-T1 |
| **CAN 2.0B Bus Bandwidth** | **1 Mbps max** | Legacy ECU communication |
| **ISO 26262 ASIL Level** | **A, B, C, D** | Automotive Safety Integrity Level |
| **SOTIF (ISO 21448) Residual Risk** | **< 10⁻⁸ per hour** | Acceptable failure rate |
| **AUTOSAR Release Version** | **R20-11 / R22-11 / R24-11** | Standard middleware |
| **Operating Temperature (Automotive Grade 2)** | **−40 °C to +105 °C** | AEC-Q100 qualification |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of System Integration Challenges

### Pillar 1: Sensor Heterogeneity & Time Synchronization

Different sensors operate on **different physical principles** and produce data at **incompatible clock domains**:

- **Camera:** 30–60 fps, RGB frame exposure, rolling/global shutter, microsecond-stamped
- **LiDAR:** 10–20 Hz rotation, point cloud generation in milliseconds
- **Radar:** 20 Hz chirp sequence, range-Doppler map, microsecond Doppler FFT
- **Ultrasonic:** 50 ms cycle, burst-echo, very low bandwidth
- **IMU:** 200–1000 Hz sampling, but drift accumulates over minutes
- **GNSS:** 1–10 Hz position fixes, but suffers multipath in urban canyons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Time-Synchronization Problem:** A pedestrian crossing the street may be captured by the camera at t = 30.000 ms and by the radar at t = 30.017 ms. Without precise PTP (Precision Time Protocol, IEEE 1588) synchronization to **sub-millisecond accuracy**, the perception stack will fuse *spatially misaligned* objects, leading to ghost tracks and unsafe trajectory predictions.

### Pillar 2: Bandwidth Saturation on In-Vehicle Networks

Modern AVs generate **terabytes of sensor data per hour**. A single 8 MP front camera running at 30 fps produces:

$$
D_{\text{camera}} = W \times H \times C \times f_{\text{fps}} \times t
$$

where $W$ is width in pixels, $H$ is height, $C$ is channel count, $f_{\text{fps}}$ is frame rate, and $t$ is duration in seconds.

For a typical 8MP (3840×2160) RGB camera at 30 fps over 1 hour:
$$
D_{\text{camera}} = 3840 \times 2160 \times 3 \times 30 \times 3600 \approx 2.69 \text{ TB/hour}
$$

This far exceeds the **1 Gbps Automotive Ethernet** capacity (≈ 450 GB/hour usable after protocol overhead), forcing **on-sensor pre-processing** (e.g., ISP compression, region-of-interest extraction, neural-network-based feature extraction on the sensor itself).

### Pillar 3: Real-Time Determinism vs. AI Non-Determinism

Classical control loops (steering, braking) require **bounded worst-case execution time (WCET)** determinism. AI/ML inference, particularly deep neural networks, is **inherently data-dependent**:

- A convolutional neural network may take **8 ms** on a "simple" highway scene
- But **23 ms** on a "complex" urban scene with many small objects
- This **non-deterministic execution time** violates ASIL-D timing guarantees

The integration challenge is bridging **deterministic safety-critical tasks** (brake-by-wire) with **best-effort perception tasks** (object classification) on the **same shared SoC**.

### Pillar 4: Functional Safety (ISO 26262) & SOTIF (ISO 21448)

ISO 26262 defines **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** from **A** (lowest) to **D** (highest). System integration must ensure:

- **Freedom from Interference (FFI)** between software elements of different ASIL levels
- **Diagnostic Coverage ≥ 99 %** for ASIL-D systems
- **Hardware Architectural Metrics:** SPFM (Single-Point Fault Metric), LFM (Latent Fault Metric)

> [!NOTE]
> **SOTIF (ISO 21448)** addresses hazards from **intended functionality** — e.g., a perception algorithm that correctly recognizes 99.99 % of objects but fails on rare edge cases (white truck against bright sky, which was the root cause of the Tesla 2016 fatal crash in Florida).

### Pillar 5: Cybersecurity (ISO/SAE 21434)

Connected vehicles expose **70+ ECUs** and **100+ MB of code** to attack surfaces. System integration must include:

- **Secure Boot** with HSM (Hardware Security Module)
- **SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication)** for CAN/Ethernet message authentication
- **OTA (Over-The-Air) update** integrity verification
- **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** on every bus

### Pillar 6: Validation, Verification & Certification

Testing an L4 autonomous system requires **billions of kilometers** of driving. The integration challenge is demonstrating safety across:
- **Scenario-based testing** (Euro NCAP, GBT 39901-2021 China, UN-R157 ALKS)
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** simulation
- **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)** simulation
- **Driver-in-the-Loop** validation
- **X-in-the-Loop continuous integration**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — System Integration Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **End-to-End Latency Budget** | $t_{\text{e2e}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{compute}} + t_{\text{plan}} + t_{\text{actuate}}$ | $< 100$ ms for L3+ |
| 2 | **Sensor Data Rate** | $D = W \times H \times C \times f_{\text{fps}}$ | MB/s |
| 3 | **Bus Utilization (CAN)** | $U = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i / T_i}{B_{\text{max}}}$ | $\leq 0.65$ for headroom |
| 4 | **Reliability (Series System)** | $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Used for redundant ECUs |
| 5 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{sys}}}$ | $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum \lambda_i$ |
| 6 | **ASIL Decomposition (ASIL-D split)** | $\text{ASIL-D}(C) = \text{ASIL-D}(C_1) \oplus \text{ASIL-C}(C_2)$ | Redundancy-based |
| 7 | **Gantt / Timing Margin** | $M = t_{\text{deadline}} - t_{\text{WCET}}$ | $M > 0$ always |
| 8 | **PTP Synchronization Accuracy** | $\sigma_t < 1$ μs | IEEE 1588v2 |
| 9 | **Packet Loss Probability** | $P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ | $p$ = bit error |
| 10 | **Coverage Probability (Communication)** | $P_{\text{cov}} = P(\text{SINR} > \gamma_{\text{th}})$ | V2X, 5G NR-V2X |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** The pipe symbol `|` in absolute-value expressions such as $|x|$ is rendered as `\vert` in markdown tables above to prevent table-rendering corruption.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The system integration discipline is what separates a **research prototype** (a Stanford-trained CNN running on a gaming GPU in a lab) from a **production autonomous vehicle** (a certifiable, ASIL-D, ISO 21434-compliant, 100,000-USD system that operates at −40 °C in Finnish Lapland and +55 °C in Dubai). Every KTU examination question on Module 4 ultimately tests whether the student understands that **integration challenges are the dominant reason L5 full autonomy remains unsolved in 2025**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Latency Budget Allocation

**Problem Statement:** An L3 autonomous vehicle's perception-to-actuation chain must complete within **100 ms**. The chain consists of:
- Camera + Radar + LiDAR sensing: $t_{\text{sense}}$
- Sensor fusion + object detection (CNN inference): $t_{\text{compute}}$
- Behavior planning + trajectory generation: $t_{\text{plan}}$
- Steering + braking actuation: $t_{\text{actuate}}$

Given the system-integration targets listed below, derive the **maximum allowable compute budget** $t_{\text{compute}}^{\max}$ and verify if a 15-frame CNN at 30 fps violates it.

### Given Values

$$
t_{\text{total}} = 100 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{\text{sense}} = 15 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{\text{plan}} = 10 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{\text{actuate}} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the end-to-end latency equation (KTU Module 4 core relation):

$$
t_{\text{e2e}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{compute}} + t_{\text{plan}} + t_{\text{actuate}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known totals:

$$
100 = 15 + t_{\text{compute}} + 10 + 5
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the right-hand side constants:

$$
100 = 30 + t_{\text{compute}}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t_{\text{compute}}$:

$$
t_{\text{compute}} = 100 - 30 = 70 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify the CNN frame budget. A 30 fps camera produces a frame every:

$$
t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{fps}}} = \dfrac{1}{30} \approx 33.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6:** The CNN has 33.33 ms per frame, but the integration budget allows only 70 ms for **two cascaded frames** (since radar + camera are desynchronized by ~33 ms in a typical TDM architecture):

$$
t_{\text{compute, available per frame}} = \dfrac{70}{2} = 35 \text{ ms/frame}
$$

**Step 7:** Comparison:

$$
t_{\text{compute}} = 35 \text{ ms} > t_{\text{frame}} = 33.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Result:** ✅ The CNN **does NOT violate** the budget, with a **safety margin** of:

$$
M = 35 - 33.33 = 1.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 8:** Express the margin as a percentage:

$$
M_{\%} = \dfrac{1.67}{35} \times 100\% \approx 4.77\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 4.77 % margin is **dangerously thin** for ASIL-D. Production systems reserve ≥ 30 % margin to absorb jitter from cache misses, OS scheduling, thermal throttling, and worst-case garbage collection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CAN Bus Utilization Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle's ADAS domain has **5 ECUs** sharing a CAN 2.0B bus. Calculate the total bus utilization and verify headroom compliance.

### Given

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ECU}_1: & \quad L_1 = 8 \text{ bytes}, \quad T_1 = 10 \text{ ms} \\
\text{ECU}_2: & \quad L_2 = 8 \text{ bytes}, \quad T_2 = 20 \text{ ms} \\
\text{ECU}_3: & \quad L_3 = 4 \text{ bytes}, \quad T_3 = 50 \text{ ms} \\
\text{ECU}_4: & \quad L_4 = 8 \text{ bytes}, \quad T_4 = 100 \text{ ms} \\
\text{ECU}_5: & \quad L_5 = 2 \text{ bytes}, \quad T_5 = 100 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

CAN bit stuffing adds **≈ 23 % overhead**, and the arbitration field + inter-frame space adds **≈ 47 bits per frame**. CAN 2.0B max bandwidth $B_{\max} = 1 \text{ Mbps}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Effective frame length in bits per ECU:

$$
L_{\text{eff},i} = (8 L_i + 47) \times 1.23
$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{eff},1} &= (8 \times 8 + 47) \times 1.23 = 111 \times 1.23 = 136.5 \text{ bits} \\
L_{\text{eff},2} &= (8 \times 8 + 47) \times 1.23 = 136.5 \text{ bits} \\
L_{\text{eff},3} &= (8 \times 4 + 47) \times 1.23 = 79 \times 1.23 = 97.2 \text{ bits} \\
L_{\text{eff},4} &= (8 \times 8 + 47) \times 1.23 = 136.5 \text{ bits} \\
L_{\text{eff},5} &= (8 \times 2 + 47) \times 1.23 = 63 \times 1.23 = 77.5 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Bus utilization is:

$$
U = \dfrac{1}{B_{\max}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_{\text{eff},i}}{T_i}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute each contribution (bits per second):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \dfrac{136.5}{0.010} = 13{,}650 \text{ bps} \\
R_2 &= \dfrac{136.5}{0.020} = 6{,}825 \text{ bps} \\
R_3 &= \dfrac{97.2}{0.050} = 1{,}944 \text{ bps} \\
R_4 &= \dfrac{136.5}{0.100} = 1{,}365 \text{ bps} \\
R_5 &= \dfrac{77.5}{0.100} = 775 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum and normalize:

$$
U = \dfrac{13{,}650 + 6{,}825 + 1{,}944 + 1{,}365 + 775}{1{,}000{,}000} = \dfrac{24{,}559}{1{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
U = 0.02456 = 2.456\%
$$

**Step 6:** Verification against the 65 % rule:

$$
U = 2.456\% \ll 65\% \quad \therefore \text{ System has } \mathbf{62.5\%} \text{ headroom}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Integration Pitfall:** Adding the L4 perception camera stream (≈ 6 Mbps raw via Ethernet) does **NOT** show up in this CAN calculation because it uses a different bus. Students often confuse **per-bus utilization** with **system-wide bandwidth**, leading to incorrect conclusions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ASIL Decomposition for Redundant Braking

**Problem Statement:** A brake-by-wire system originally designed as a **single ASIL-D channel** must be decomposed across **two ECUs**. Show the decomposition and verify fault tolerance.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Initial integrity level:

$$
\text{ASIL}(C) = \text{ASIL-D}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the ISO 26262-9:2018 decomposition rule (Rule 8):

$$
\text{ASIL-D}(C) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASIL-D}(C_1) + \text{ASIL-C}(C_2)
$$

**Step 3:** Both channels independently implement the braking torque calculation.

**Step 4:** Define the channel reliability requirement. For an ASIL-D element, the target failure rate is:

$$
\text{PMHF}_{\text{ASIL-D}} \leq 10^{-8} \text{/h}
$$

**Step 5:** After decomposition, each sub-element inherits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PMHF}_{\text{ASIL-D}}(C_1) &\leq 10^{-8} \text{/h} \\
\text{PMHF}_{\text{ASIL-C}}(C_2) &\leq 10^{-7} \text{/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** System-level combined failure rate (assuming independence and a voter):

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \approx \lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{\text{voter}}
$$

**Step 7:** Numerical example:

$$
\lambda_1 = 10^{-8} \text{/h}, \quad \lambda_2 = 10^{-7} \text{/h}
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} = 10^{-8} \times 10^{-7} = 10^{-15} \text{/h}
$$

This is **seven orders of magnitude safer** than the ASIL-D target, demonstrating the **massively redundant safety margin** achievable through proper decomposition.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Challenges to Solutions

| Integration Challenge | Root Cause | KTU 2024 Solution | Standard / Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor time desync | Independent clocks | PTP (IEEE 1588) grandmaster | IEEE 1588v2 |
| Bandwidth saturation | Raw sensor data | On-sensor ISP + compression | MIPI CSI-2, H.265 |
| Mixed-criticality scheduling | AI + control on same SoC | Hypervisor + multi-core isolation | ACRN, Jailhouse |
| ASIL-D non-compliance | Software bug | Static analysis + MISRA-C | ISO 26262, MISRA-C:2012 |
| SOTIF edge cases | ML blind spots | Scenario coverage testing | ISO 21448, PEGASUS |
| CAN bus saturation | Many ECUs | Migration to Automotive Ethernet | 100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1 |
| Cybersecurity breach | Network exposure | HSM + SecOC | ISO/SAE 21434 |
| OTA failure | Bad firmware | A/B partition + signed images | Uptane, RAUC |
| Validation cost | Real-world testing | HIL + scenario simulation | CARLA, IPG CarMaker |
| Thermal runaway | High compute density | Liquid cooling + DVFS | AEC-Q100 Grade 2 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
