<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q:** List and briefly explain **three major system integration challenges** in L3+ autonomous vehicles.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ≈ 90–110 words):**

1. **Sensor Heterogeneity & Time Synchronization:** Cameras, radar, LiDAR, and IMU operate at different clock domains and sample rates. Without PTP (IEEE 1588) sub-millisecond synchronization, the fusion layer produces spatially misaligned object tracks, leading to unsafe trajectories. **(1 mark)**

2. **Bandwidth Saturation on In-Vehicle Networks:** An 8 MP camera at 30 fps produces ≈ 720 MB/s raw, overwhelming 1 Gbps Automotive Ethernet. This forces on-sensor pre-processing, region-of-interest extraction, and migration from CAN to Ethernet TSN. **(1 mark)**

3. **Mixed-Criticality Scheduling:** AI inference (non-deterministic) and safety-critical control (deterministic ASIL-D) must share the same SoC. A hypervisor with spatial and temporal isolation (e.g., Jailhouse) is required. **(1 mark)**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** What is **ASIL decomposition**? Give an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ≈ 90–110 words):**

**Definition:** ASIL decomposition is an **ISO 26262-9:2018 technique** that splits a safety requirement originally assigned to a single element across multiple redundant elements, each carrying a lower (or equal) ASIL burden. This is enabled by **Rule 8** of the standard.

**Example:** A brake-by-wire function originally at **ASIL-D** can be decomposed into:
- Channel 1: ASIL-D (primary brake ECU)
- Channel 2: ASIL-C (secondary fallback ECU)
- Voter logic: QM (Quality Management)

The combined system retains the original ASIL-D safety target while relaxing the development burden on each individual ECU, enabling more efficient fault-tolerant design. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — *Latency Budget Analysis*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 Internal Choice]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **end-to-end latency budget** for an L3 autonomous vehicle. Derive the maximum allowable **compute budget** given:
- $t_{\text{sense}} = 12$ ms
- $t_{\text{plan}} = 8$ ms
- $t_{\text{actuate}} = 4$ ms
- $t_{\text{e2e}}^{\max} = 90$ ms

Verify whether a CNN inference running at **25 fps** (frame interval = 40 ms) violates the budget. Comment on the engineering safety margin required for **ASIL-D**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **three technical challenges** of integrating heterogeneous sensors (camera + LiDAR + radar + IMU) into a unified perception pipeline. For each challenge, propose a **specific engineering solution** citing the relevant standard (IEEE, ISO, AUTOSAR, etc.).

### Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) [7 Marks] — Full Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define end-to-end latency (1 mark):

$$
t_{\text{e2e}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{compute}} + t_{\text{plan}} + t_{\text{actuate}} \leq t_{\text{e2e}}^{\max}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute and solve (2 marks):

$$
90 = 12 + t_{\text{compute}} + 8 + 4
$$

$$
t_{\text{compute}} = 90 - 24 = 66 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** CNN frame interval at 25 fps (1 mark):

$$
t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1000}{25} = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Per-frame budget (assuming radar and camera are desynchronized by ~40 ms in TDM) (1 mark):

$$
t_{\text{per frame}} = \dfrac{66}{2} = 33 \text{ ms/frame}
$$

**Step 5:** Margin calculation (1 mark):

$$
M = 33 - 40 = -7 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(NEGATIVE — violates budget)}
$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion (1 mark): The CNN **violates** the budget. Engineering recommendation: **(i)** migrate to 30 fps with a faster SoC (e.g., NVIDIA Orin); **(ii)** apply **dynamic frame skipping**; **(iii)** reserve ≥ 30 % ASIL-D margin, requiring $t_{\text{per frame}} \leq 23$ ms — mandates **hardware acceleration (NPU/GPU)**.

**Part (b) [7 Marks] — Three Challenges with Solutions:**

| # | Challenge | Engineering Solution | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (2.5 marks) | **Time desynchronization** across sensors with different sample rates (camera 30 Hz, LiDAR 10 Hz, IMU 200 Hz) | Deploy **PTP grandmaster clock** with sub-microsecond accuracy; timestamp all frames at sensor-edge | **IEEE 1588v2** |
| 2 (2.5 marks) | **Coordinate-frame misalignment** between camera (pixel), LiDAR (3D point cloud), radar (range-Doppler) | **Extrinsic calibration** using checkerboard + lidar-camera target; transform all data into **vehicle-centric ENU frame** | **ISO 23150:2021** |
| 3 (2 marks) | **Conflicting data quality** (camera fails in fog; radar fails in static scenes) | **Probabilistic sensor fusion** using **Extended Kalman Filter** with per-sensor covariance weighting | **AUTOSAR Adaptive R22-11** |

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — *Cybersecurity & Functional Safety Integration*

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 Internal Choice]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **integration challenges** between **functional safety (ISO 26262)** and **cybersecurity (ISO/SAE 21434)** in a connected ADAS vehicle. Use a real-world case study (e.g., 2015 Jeep Cherokee hack, 2022 Tesla data breach, or 2023 Cruise incident).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Freedom from Interference (FFI)** as mandated by ISO 26262. Show how **spatial isolation** (memory partitioning) and **temporal isolation** (scheduling) on a multi-core SoC achieve FFI between ASIL-D and QM software elements.

### Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) [7 Marks] — Functional Safety × Cybersecurity Conflict:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** ISO 26262 (safety) and ISO/SAE 21434 (security) are **complementary but tension-laden**. A safety mechanism (e.g., emergency brake) can be exploited as an attack vector; conversely, a security measure (e.g., cryptographic signing) adds latency that may violate a 100 ms e2e budget.

**Case Study: 2015 Jeep Cherokee Hack (Charlie Miller & Chris Valasek) (3 marks):**
- Attackers exploited the **Sprint cellular network** → OMAP chip in head unit → CAN bus injection
- They commanded **remote brake deactivation** at 110 km/h on a highway
- From ISO 26262 perspective: brake-by-wire should be ASIL-D; the attack created a **violation of the safety goal** "unintended deceleration must not occur"
- From ISO/SAE 21434 perspective: there was **no network segregation** between the infotainment domain (low ASIL) and the chassis domain (ASIL-D)

**Engineering Solutions (3 marks):**
1. **Domain isolation** via automotive firewall gateways (e.g., Continental, Bosch)
2. **SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication)** for CAN message authentication (AUTOSAR 4.4+)
3. **HSM (Hardware Security Module)** for secure boot and key storage
4. **Joint HARA + TARA analysis** at the concept phase

**Part (b) [7 Marks] — Freedom from Interference (FFI):**

**Definition (2 marks):** FFI requires that a **lower-ASIL (or QM) software element cannot interfere** with a higher-ASIL element's memory, timing, or communication. This is enforced via:

**Spatial Isolation (3 marks):**
- **MPU (Memory Protection Unit)** partitions RAM into ASIL-D and QM regions
- A QM task writing to an ASIL-D memory address triggers a **memory violation exception**, isolating the fault
- Example: ARM Cortex-R52 implements **8 memory regions** with hardware-level enforcement

**Temporal Isolation (2 marks):**
- **Time-deterministic scheduling** (e.g., AUTOSAR OS schedule tables) reserves CPU time windows
- ASIL-D tasks run in their **reserved slots**; QM tasks cannot preempt them
- WCET analysis guarantees no ASIL-D deadline miss

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ASIL with SIL** (SIL is generic IEC 61508; ASIL is automotive-specific) — **−2 marks**
> 2. **Forgetting to include sensor latency** in the e2e budget — **−1 mark**
> 3. **Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables** — causes rendering corruption; use `\vert` instead — **−0.5 marks (presentation)**
> 4. **Citing non-existent standards** (e.g., "ISO 27001 for cars") — **−1 mark**
> 5. **Forgetting PTP/IEEE 1588** when discussing time sync — **−1 mark**
> 6. **Claiming "5G solves latency"** without acknowledging V2X coverage gaps — **−2 marks**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **System Integration** is the **single largest engineering hurdle** in ADAS/AV deployment.
- ✅ The **end-to-end latency budget** is partitioned into $t_{\text{sense}}$, $t_{\text{compute}}$, $t_{\text{plan}}$, $t_{\text{actuate}}$ and **must stay under 100 ms** for L3+.
- ✅ **ASIL-D decomposition** enables redundant ECUs to share safety burden while preserving integrity targets ($\text{PMHF} \leq 10^{-8}$/h).
- ✅ **PTP (IEEE 1588v2)** provides the sub-microsecond time synchronization essential for sensor fusion.
- ✅ **CAN 2.0B saturation** ($U > 65\%$) forces migration to **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)**.
- ✅ **Mixed-criticality scheduling** on multi-core SoCs requires **hypervisors (ACRN, Jailhouse)** for spatial + temporal isolation.
- ✅ **SOTIF (ISO 21448)** addresses hazards from **intended functionality** — perception edge cases — and is **complementary to ISO 26262**.
- ✅ **ISO/SAE 21434** mandates **TARA (Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment)** alongside the safety HARA.
- ✅ **Validation** requires a **layered X-in-the-Loop strategy** (MIL → SIL → HIL → DIL → real vehicle).
- ✅ Real-world recalls (Tesla 2023, Cruise 2023) confirm that **system integration failures** dominate over single-component failures.
- ✅ Pipe symbols inside markdown tables must use `\vert` or `\mid` — never literal `|`.
- ✅ The most-tested KTU concept: **latency-budget numerical problems** and **ASIL decomposition case studies**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing and Validation - Testing Methodologies for Autonomous and ADAS Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing and Validation - Testing Methodologies for Autonomous and ADAS Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — TESTING AND VALIDATION: TESTING METHODOLOGIES FOR AUTONOMOUS AND ADAS SYSTEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT865)** and the global ADAS/AV engineering practice, **Testing and Validation** refers to the systematic, multi-layered process of verifying and validating the functional, safety, and performance behavior of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Vehicles (AV) against defined operational requirements, safety goals, and regulatory standards (such as **ISO 26262** for functional safety and **ISO 21448 — SOTIF** for Safety of the Intended Functionality).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):**
> *Testing and Validation is the structured engineering methodology that combines model-based simulation, software-in-the-loop, hardware-in-the-loop, scenario-based virtual testing, and real-world road testing to demonstrate that an ADAS/AV system performs its intended function safely across all reasonably foreseeable operating conditions, while satisfying the safety-of-intended-functionality (SOTIF) and functional-safety requirements defined in ISO 26262 and ISO 21448.*

The discipline is built on the **V-Model** of verification & validation (V&V), the **X-in-the-Loop (XiL)** continuum (MiL → SiL → HiL → ViL → DiL), and **scenario-based testing** using open-loop, closed-loop, and stochastic scenario generation frameworks (e.g., ASAM OpenSCENARIO, OpenDRIVE).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pilot-Training Ladder"

Imagine a **commercial airline pilot** learning to fly a brand-new aircraft (say, a Boeing 787). Before the pilot is allowed to carry passengers, the airline makes them climb a structured "training ladder":

| Pilot Training Stage | ADAS/AV Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Reading the flight manual & learning theories | **MiL — Model-in-the-Loop** (pure math model on PC) |
| Desktop flight simulator on a PC | **SiL — Software-in-the-Loop** (compiled C/C++ code on PC) |
| Replica cockpit with real stick & screens | **HiL — Hardware-in-the-Loop** (real ECU plugged into simulated world) |
| Driving the actual aircraft on the ground | **ViL — Vehicle-in-the-Loop** (real car + simulated traffic) |
| First supervised commercial flight | **DiL — Driver-in-the-Loop** (real human in real car) |

Just as a pilot is **never** allowed to skip straight from the manual to a passenger flight, an ADAS/AV algorithm must **never** be deployed to public roads without climbing the **X-in-the-Loop ladder**. This is the foundational philosophy of **KTU Module 4 — Testing Methodologies**.

### 1.3 Why Testing & Validation is the Hardest Problem in AV Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> A Level-5 autonomous vehicle must be tested across **~ 10 billion miles** of driving to statistically prove it is **2× safer than a human driver** (RAND Corporation, 2016 estimate). At a fleet mileage of **~ 25 million miles/day**, this would take **~ 400 years** of real-world testing. Therefore, **~ 99.9 %** of validation MUST be done in simulation.

This single fact justifies the entire testing methodology stack we are about to study.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (Board-Revision Favorites)

| Metric / Standard | Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| **ISO 26262** | Functional Safety of road vehicles — ASIL A → D |
| **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** | Safety Of The Intended Functionality |
| **ISO/SAE 21434** | Cybersecurity engineering for road vehicles |
| **UL 4600** | Safety case standard for autonomous vehicles |
| **NHTSA / Euro NCAP** | Regulatory test protocols |
| **ASAM OpenSCENARIO / OpenDRIVE** | Scenario & map description standards |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | Reliability metric |
| **Fault Tolerant Time Interval (FTTI)** | Max time before hazard → **typically 100 ms** for braking systems |
| **ASIL D probability target** | **< 10⁻⁸** failures/hour |

---

### 1.5 High-Yield Terminology Bucket (KTU Examiner Glossary)

| Term | 1-Line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **V&V** | Verification (are we building the system right?) + Validation (are we building the right system?) |
| **V-Model** | Sequential development model where each design level has a corresponding test level |
| **MiL** | Model-in-the-Loop — controller is a pure mathematical block diagram |
| **SiL** | Software-in-the-Loop — controller is auto-generated C code on a PC |
| **HiL** | Hardware-in-the-Loop — real ECU hardware running against a simulated plant |
| **ViL** | Vehicle-in-the-Loop — real vehicle on a test track + injected virtual targets |
| **DiL** | Driver-in-the-Loop — real driver in real vehicle evaluating HMI & behavior |
| **SOTIF** | Safety even when no sensor/actuator fault exists — perception limits, edge cases |
| **HARA** | Hazard Analysis & Risk Assessment (ISO 26262 trigger) |
| **FMEA / FTA** | Failure Mode & Effects Analysis / Fault Tree Analysis |
| **KPI** | Key Performance Indicator (e.g., time-to-collision, jerk, lateral accel.) |
| **Ground Truth** | Verified-correct reference state used to score the AV stack |
| **Open-Loop Test** | Recording real sensor data → replaying into AV → compare to ground truth |
| **Closed-Loop Test** | AV output affects the simulated environment (ego vehicle can crash virtual pedestrian) |
| **Scenario Catalogue** | Tagged library of test scenarios (e.g., Euro NCAP CCRs, AEB VRUs) |
| **Coverage** | Fraction of requirement / scenario space exercised by the test campaign |
| **Regression Test** | Re-running prior tests after a code change to ensure nothing broke |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-4 Anchoring Statement:**
> For board purposes, every testing methodology answer must reference **at least one** of: V-Model, X-in-the-Loop, ISO 26262, ISO 21448 (SOTIF), and scenario-based testing. Examiners explicitly look for these keywords in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The V-Model for ADAS/AV Development

The **V-Model** maps each **design decomposition** (left arm of the V) to a corresponding **test integration** (right arm of the V). It is the **dominant process backbone** in ISO 26262-conformant ADAS development.

**Left Arm — Decomposition:**
1. Vehicle-Level Requirements
2. Subsystem Requirements (Perception, Planning, Control)
3. Software / Hardware Architecture
4. Module-Level Detailed Design

**Right Arm — Integration & Testing:**
1. Module Tests (Unit)
2. Integration Tests (SiL)
3. Subsystem Tests (HiL)
4. Vehicle-Level Tests (ViL → DiL → Field)

**Bottom of V:** **Implementation** (auto-generated C/C++ from Simulink or hand-coded).

> [!NOTE]
> The **left arm** = Verification (building the system right).
> The **right arm** = Validation (building the right system).

### 2.2 The X-in-the-Loop (XiL) Continuum — Core of Testing Methodologies

| Level | Full Name | Plant | Controller | Real-Time | Typical Tool | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MiL** | Model-in-the-Loop | Math model | Math model | No | MATLAB/Simulink | Early algorithm tuning |
| **SiL** | Software-in-the-Loop | Math model | Auto-gen C/C++ | No | Simulink + VS / gcc | Software bug detection |
| **HiL** | Hardware-in-the-Loop | Real-time sim (dSPACE, NI) | Real ECU | Yes (≤1 ms) | dSPACE SCALEXIO, Vector VT System | ECU-integration, fault injection |
| **ViL** | Vehicle-in-the-Loop | Real vehicle + sim world | Real ECU stack | Real | dSPACE ViL, IPG CarMaker | Sub-system on real chassis |
| **DiL** | Driver-in-the-Loop | Real world | Real stack | Real | Test track | HMI, comfort, acceptance |

**Why "X" rather than just "HIL"?** Because no single level is sufficient. **MiL** is fast but unrealistic; **DiL** is realistic but slow & expensive. The methodology requires **progressive promotion** from cheap/fast → expensive/slow as confidence grows.

### 2.3 Scenario-Based Testing (The SOTIF Backbone)

SOTIF (ISO 21448) demands that the AV be tested against **scenarios**, not just requirements. A scenario is a 5-tuple:

$$
S = \langle A, B, C, D, E \rangle
$$

where:
- **A** = Agent (ego vehicle, target VRU, other vehicles)
- **B** = Behavior (kinematics, maneuvers)
- **C** = Conditions (weather, lighting, road surface)
- **D** = Data (sensor characteristics, occlusions)
- **E** = Environment (map, traffic rules, infrastructure)

**Scenario Generation Strategies:**
1. **Expert Knowledge Catalogue** — Euro NCAP test catalogue, hand-written.
2. **Data-Driven Replay** — Extract from real-world fleet logs (Waymo, Cruise).
3. **Stochastic Generation** — Monte Carlo sampling from parameter distributions.
4. **Falsification / Adversarial** — Search algorithms (STL, Bayesian Optimization) that find scenarios where the AV violates a specification.
5. **Generative AI / LLMs** — Modern trend: LLM-driven scenario authoring (ASAM OpenSCENARIO 2.x).

### 2.4 Coverage Metrics — KTU's Favourite Mathematical Hook

Coverage is how examiners reward you for being quantitative.

**a) Requirement Coverage (CR)**

$$
C_R \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{tested req.}}}{N_{\text{total req.}}} \;\times\; 100\,\%
$$

**b) Scenario Coverage (Combinatorial)**

If a scenario has $k$ independent parameters each with $n_i$ discrete values, the **full combinatorial space** has:

$$
N_{\text{full}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i
$$

$$
C_S \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{executed}}}{N_{\text{full}}} \;\times\; 100\,\%
$$

**c) Pairwise (2-wise) Coverage** — Industry compromise.

$$
N_{\text{pairwise}} \;=\; \sum_{i<j} n_i \cdot n_j
$$

**d) Functional Coverage (SystemVerilog / UVM style)**

$$
C_F \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of unique coverage bins hit}}{\text{Total number of defined bins}}
$$

**e) Sensor-Coverage Probability (FoV-based)**

For a sensor with **azimuthal FoV** $\alpha$ (rad) on a 360° platform with $m$ sensors:

$$
C_{\text{FoV}} \;=\; \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{m \cdot \alpha}{2\pi}\right)
$$

**f) Statistical Safety Argument (RAND / Highway Driving)**

To prove failure rate $p$ with confidence $1-\beta$ using $n$ failure-free miles:

$$
\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{-\ln(\beta)}{n}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a confidence of **95 %** ($\beta = 0.05$) and a target failure rate of **1.16 fatalities per 100 million miles** (human baseline), the required distance becomes the **10-billion-mile** number quoted earlier.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Table

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_R = N_t / N_{\text{total}}$ | Requirement coverage | Module-4 derivations |
| 2 | $N_{\text{full}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ | Combinatorial scenario space | Scenario coverage |
| 3 | $N_{\text{pairwise}} = \sum_{i<j} n_i n_j$ | Pairwise combinatorial | SOTIF testing |
| 4 | $\hat{p} = -\ln(\beta)/n$ | Failure-probability bound | Validation argument |
| 5 | $C_{\text{FoV}} = \min(1, m\alpha/2\pi)$ | Sensor coverage | Perception test design |
| 6 | $\text{ASIL} = f(S, E, C)$ | Severity × Exposure × Controllability | ISO 26262 |
| 7 | $\text{RSS Safety Check: } d_{\text{min}} \ge v_r \rho + \tfrac{1}{2}a_{\text{max,accel}} \rho^2 + (v_f + \tfrac{1}{2}a_{\text{max,brake}} \rho^2)$ | Responsibility-Sensitive Safety | AV planning tests |
| 8 | $t_{\text{TTC}} = d / v_{\text{rel}}$ | Time-to-Collision metric | AEB test scoring |
| 9 | $j_{\text{lat}} = a_{\text{lat}} \cdot \Delta t^{-1}$ | Lateral-jerk KPI | Comfort validation |
| 10 | $P_{\text{safe}} = P(\text{detect} \cap \text{plan} \cap \text{actuate})$ | Joint safety probability | Subsystem KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> Formulas 2, 3, 4, and 8 are the **four "money formulas"** most likely to appear as a numerical sub-question in the KTU board exam.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Methodology | Industry Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **MiL/SiL** | Algorithm team tuning AEB thresholds pre-sprint (Waymo, Cruise) |
| **HiL** | Bosch, Continental testing 1000s of ECU variants per week |
| **ViL** | BMW, Audi testing ADAS on test-track with dummy targets |
| **Scenario Replay** | Tesla, Comma.ai replaying fleet-collected shadow-mode data |
| **Falsification** | Aurora, Argo using S-TaLiRo tool to find adversarial scenarios |
| **SOTIF Residual Risk** | Quantified by **area of unknown-hazard region** in scenario space |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Mnemonic for KTU Module 4:**
> **"V → XiL → Scenario → Coverage → SOTIF"**
> Memorize this 5-word chain. Almost any 14-mark question can be answered by walking through these five points in order.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combinatorial Scenario-Space Explosion

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
An AEB test scenario has 4 parameters:
- Ego speed: 3 levels (30, 50, 80 km/h)
- Lead-vehicle speed: 3 levels (0, 30, 50 km/h)
- Weather: 2 levels (clear, rain)
- Time-headway setting: 2 levels (1.5 s, 2.5 s)

Determine (i) total combinatorial space, (ii) pairwise test space, (iii) % reduction achieved.

**Step 1 — Define parameters and levels.**

$$
n_1 = 3,\quad n_2 = 3,\quad n_3 = 2,\quad n_4 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Total (k-wise = full) combinatorial space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{full}} &= n_1 \times n_2 \times n_3 \times n_4 \\[2pt]
&= 3 \times 3 \times 2 \times 2 \\[2pt]
&= 36 \;\text{scenarios}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pairwise (2-wise) test count using pair-sum identity.**

$$
N_{\text{pairwise}} = \sum_{1 \le i < j \le 4} n_i \cdot n_j
$$

Expanding the six pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{pairwise}} &= (n_1 n_2) + (n_1 n_3) + (n_1 n_4) \\
&\quad + (n_2 n_3) + (n_2 n_4) + (n_3 n_4) \\[2pt]
&= (3\cdot 3) + (3\cdot 2) + (3\cdot 2) \\
&\quad + (3\cdot 2) + (3\cdot 2) + (2\cdot 2) \\[2pt]
&= 9 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 4 \\[2pt]
&= 37 \;\text{pairwise combinations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Select the minimum covering subset (orthogonal-array heuristic).**

A standard L₉ OA can cover all 37 pairs in just **9 scenarios** (industry rule-of-thumb ≈ pairwise level⁽²⁾).

**Step 5 — Compute reduction percentage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= \left(1 - \frac{N_{\text{OA}}}{N_{\text{full}}}\right)\times 100\% \\[2pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{9}{36}\right)\times 100\% \\[2pt]
&= 75\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer-line:** *"Pairwise testing reduces the test campaign from 36 to 9 scenarios — a 75 % reduction while preserving defect-detection coverage for any 2-parameter interaction (the empirical industry standard)."*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Failure-Probability Bound for AV Safety Argument

**Problem:** A fleet of test vehicles drives **25 million miles** without a fatality. What is the upper-bound on fatal-accident rate at **95 % confidence**?

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

$$
n = 25\,000\,000 \;\text{miles}, \qquad \beta = 0.05,\qquad 1-\beta = 0.95
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Chernoff-Hoeffding bound.**

$$
\hat{p} = \frac{-\ln(\beta)}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{p} &= \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{25\,000\,000} \\[2pt]
&= \frac{2.9957}{25\,000\,000} \\[2pt]
&= 1.198 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{fatalities/mile}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with human baseline.**

Human fatality rate in the US ≈ $1.13 \times 10^{-7}$ fatalities/mile (NHTSA). Therefore the AV has **not yet** met the "2× safer than human" bar; we need:

$$
n_{\text{req}} = \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{0.5 \times 1.13 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 5.30 \times 10^{10} \;\text{miles}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **53 billion mile** number is the rigorous statistical floor of the "100× more testing than humans" claim often quoted in AV marketing.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensor Field-of-View Coverage

**Problem:** A vehicle has **6 corner radars**, each with **150° azimuthal FoV**. Compute redundancy coverage.

**Step 1 — Sum the FoVs.**

$$
\text{Total FoV} = 6 \times 150° = 900°
$$

**Step 2 — Normalize against 360° envelope.**

$$
C_{\text{FoV}} = \frac{900°}{360°} = 2.5
$$

**Step 3 — Apply min() clamping.**

$$
C_{\text{FoV, usable}} = \min(1,\; 2.5) = 1.0 \quad (\text{i.e., 100 \% coverage})
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

Each direction on the vehicle is observed by **2.5 radars on average**, giving a redundancy factor of **2.5×** — essential for **ASIL D** functional-safety targets.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strengths & Weaknesses of Each XiL Level

| XiL Level | Cost | Realism | Speed | Fault Injection | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MiL | ★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Algorithm design |
| SiL | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Code-level testing |
| HiL | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ECU integration |
| ViL | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | Track testing |
| DiL | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★ | Final acceptance |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam trick:** A question may ask *"Why is HiL necessary even after SiL passes?"* Use the matrix — SiL catches software bugs, but only HiL catches **electrical, timing, and bus-load** faults of the real ECU.

---

### 3.5 SOTIF-Risk Reduction Loop (Algorithmic Flow)

$$
R_{\text{SOTIF}}(t) = R_0 - \int_{0}^{t} \alpha \cdot N_{\text{tested}}(s)\,ds
$$

where:
- $R_0$ = initial unknown-risk area
- $\alpha$ = risk-discovery rate (scenarios⁻¹)
- $N_{\text{tested}}(s)$ = cumulative tested scenarios

This shows that **residual SOTIF risk decays monotonically** with the cumulative test campaign — a powerful validation-argument structure for ISO 21448.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 V-Model for ADAS/AV Development (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Vehicle Level Requirements] --> A2[Subsystem Requirements Perception Planning Control]
    A2 --> A3[Software Hardware Architecture]
    A3 --> A4[Module Detailed Design]
    A4 --> A5[Implementation Code]
    A5 --> A6[Module Unit Test MIL SIL]
    A6 --> A7[Integration Test HiL]
    A7 --> A8[Subsystem Test ViL]
    A8 --> A9[Vehicle Acceptance Test DiL Field]
    A9 -.validation feedback.-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed feedback arrow at the bottom is the **Validation Loop** — if the vehicle acceptance test fails, requirements are revised and the V is re-climbed.

---

### 4.2 X-in-the-Loop Continuum (Progressive Realism Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MIL[MiL Math Model on PC] --> SIL[SiL Auto gen C code on PC]
    SIL --> HIL[HiL Real ECU plus Real time Plant Sim]
    HIL --> VIL[ViL Real Vehicle on Test Track plus Virtual Traffic]
    VIL --> DIL[DiL Real Driver on Public Road or Proving Ground]
```

---

### 4.3 Scenario-Based Testing Pipeline (ASAM-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Scenario Catalogue ASAM OpenSCENARIO] --> P2[Parameter Space Sampling Monte Carlo Falsification]
    P2 --> P3[Simulation Engine CARLA PreScan VTD]
    P3 --> P4[AV Stack Under Test]
    P4 --> P5[Behavior Logs]
    P5 --> P6[Scoring Engine KPIs TTC Jerk RSS]
    P6 --> P7{Requirement Pass?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Coverage Database Update]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Issue Tracker Defect Ticket]
    P9 --> P2
    P8 --> P2
```

---

### 4.4 ISO 26262 + ISO 21448 Combined V&V Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment HARA] --> Q2[Functional Safety Goals ISO 26262]
    Q1 --> Q3[SOTIF Trigger Conditions ISO 21448]
    Q2 --> Q4[ASIL Allocation QM to D]
    Q3 --> Q5[Unknown and Hazardous Area Mapping]
    Q4 --> Q6[System Design]
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q7[XiL Test Suite]
    Q7 --> Q8[Residual Risk Evaluation]
    Q8 --> Q9{Risk Acceptable?}
    Q9 -- No --> Q6
    Q9 -- Yes --> Q10[Safety Case Release]
```

---

### 4.5 Closed-Loop vs Open-Loop Test Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OLP[Open Loop: Recorded sensor log playback] --> OLA[AV Stack Output Snapshot]
    OLA --> OLB[Compare with Ground Truth]
    OLB --> OLC[Static metrics accuracy precision recall]

    CLP[Closed Loop: AV output drives simulator] --> CLA[Simulator evolves next state]
    CLA --> CLB[AV receives new sensor input]
    CLB --> CLC[Dynamic KPIs TTC crash rate mission success]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse open-loop with closed-loop. **Open-loop = no feedback** (one-shot perception test). **Closed-loop = AV actions modify the world** (planning + control test). Always specify which is being used.

---

### 4.6 Functional Coverage Database (Test Campaign Dashboard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Scenarios Executed] --> T2[Coverage Matrix Scenario x Parameter x KPI]
    T2 --> T3[Heatmap Visualization]
    T2 --> T4[Gap Analysis]
    T4 --> T5[New Scenario Generation Falsification]
    T5 --> T1
    T3 --> T6[Release Decision Gate]
```

---

### 4.7 Test Pyramid for AV Software

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Driving Tests DiL Public Roads Slow Expensive] --> B[Vehicle Track Tests ViL Fast Iter]
    B --> C[HiL ECU in Loop Fast Millions per Day]
    C --> D[SiL Software in Loop Very Fast]
    D --> E[MiL Model in Loop Fastest Lowest Cost]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]
*Define the V-Model in the context of ADAS development. How does it enforce traceability between design and testing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **V-Model** is a sequential development framework in which each design decomposition stage on the **left arm** (vehicle requirements → subsystem requirements → architecture → module design) is mirrored by a corresponding test integration stage on the **right arm** (unit → integration → subsystem → vehicle acceptance). At the bottom of the V lies the **implementation** phase. The model enforces **traceability** because every requirement on the left has a one-to-one mapped test case on the right — ensuring that no requirement is left untested and that any defect discovered in a right-arm test can be traced back to the left-arm requirement that originated it. In ADAS development, the right arm typically progresses through **MiL → SiL → HiL → ViL → DiL**, satisfying **ISO 26262** verification demands. *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1 for traceability, 0.5 for ADAS context.)*

---

#### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
*Differentiate between **Software-in-the-Loop (SiL)** and **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HiL)** testing. State one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **SiL** | **HiL** |
|---|---|---|
| Plant | Math model on PC | Real-time simulator (dSPACE/NI) |
| Controller | Auto-gen C code on PC | **Real ECU hardware** |
| Real-Time | No | Yes (≤ 1 ms) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Defect Class | Algorithm & software bugs | Electrical, timing, bus-load, sensor-IO faults |

**Limitation of SiL:** Cannot catch real-time scheduling, ECU memory, or hardware-driver bugs.
**Limitation of HiL:** Plant fidelity is approximate — full vehicle dynamics (tire slip, suspension) are simplified. *(3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Module 4 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **scenario-based testing methodology** mandated by **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** for autonomous vehicles. With the help of a block diagram, explain the closed-loop scenario test pipeline.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) ISO 21448 (SOTIF) — Core Concept (3 marks):**
SOTIF addresses hazards that arise **even when the system has no fault** — for example, perception failure in fog, or planning failure in an unseen edge case. SOTIF requires the AV to be validated across an **operational design domain (ODD)** represented as a **scenario space**. Each scenario is a tuple of agents, behaviors, conditions, data, and environment.

**(ii) Scenario Sources (2 marks):**
- Expert catalogues (Euro NCAP, NHTSA)
- Real-world fleet data replay
- Stochastic parameter sampling (Monte Carlo, Latin Hypercube)
- Falsification (adversarial search using STL / Bayesian opt.)

**(iii) Closed-Loop Test Pipeline (2 marks — from §4.3 diagram above):**

> Catalogue → Parameter Sampling → Simulator → AV Stack → Logs → Scorer → Pass/Fail → Coverage Update

The **closed-loop** nature means the AV's control outputs (steer, brake, throttle) modify the simulated world — so a planning error can lead to a virtual collision, which is detected by the scorer.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A perception test scenario has **5 parameters** with **3, 4, 2, 3, 2** discrete levels respectively. Calculate (i) the full combinatorial test count, (ii) the number of pairwise combinations, and (iii) the **minimum orthogonal-array covering-array size** if an L₁₂ OA is selected. Comment on engineering trade-offs.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total combinatorial space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{full}} &= 3 \times 4 \times 2 \times 3 \times 2 \\[2pt]
&= 144 \;\text{scenarios}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pairwise combinations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{pairwise}} &= \sum_{i<j} n_i n_j \\[2pt]
&= (3\cdot4)+(3\cdot2)+(3\cdot3)+(3\cdot2)\\
&\quad+(4\cdot2)+(4\cdot3)+(4\cdot2)\\
&\quad+(2\cdot3)+(2\cdot2)\\
&\quad+(3\cdot2)\\[2pt]
&= 12+6+9+6+8+12+8+6+4+6 \\[2pt]
&= 77 \;\text{pairs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — L₁₂ OA test count.**

$$
N_{\text{OA}} = 12 \;\text{scenarios}
$$

**Step 4 — Coverage efficiency.**

$$
C_{\text{pairwise}} = \frac{12}{77} \times \text{OA-overlap factor} \approx 95\text{–}100\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Reduction percentage vs full combinatorial.**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{12}{144}\right)\times 100\% = 91.7\,\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering trade-off comment (1 mark):** Pairwise covers **2-parameter interactions** (which catch ≈ 80 % of field defects per industry studies), but cannot detect **3-way or higher-order interaction bugs**. Therefore, **3-wise OA** is recommended for safety-critical ASIL-D functions even at higher test cost.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **X-in-the-Loop (XiL) testing continuum** for ADAS. With a labelled block diagram, describe the role of each level from MiL to DiL, and justify why all five levels are mandatory.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** XiL is the **progressive promotion** of an ADAS algorithm through layers of increasing physical realism — from pure math model on PC (MiL) to a real driver in a real vehicle (DiL). It is the **backbone methodology** of ISO 26262-conformant ADAS validation.

**Levels (3 marks — see §4.2 diagram):**
1. **MiL** — Math model of plant + controller. Fast iteration; pure algorithm design.
2. **SiL** — Auto-generated C code running on PC against a math plant. Detects software bugs (overflow, type mismatch, integration errors).
3. **HiL** — Real ECU hardware interfaced to a real-time simulator via CAN/FlexRay/Ethernet. Catches electrical/timing/bus-load faults and supports fault injection.
4. **ViL** — Real vehicle on a test track with simulated surrounding traffic (dummy targets, robot pedestrians). Verifies sub-system on actual chassis dynamics.
5. **DiL** — Real driver evaluating HMI, comfort, and acceptance on a proving ground or public road.

**Justification — Why all 5 are mandatory (2 marks):**
- MiL alone → unrealistic (no real bus, no real code, no real driver)
- DiL alone → too slow, too expensive, dangerous for unreviewed algorithms
- Each level catches a **distinct class of defects** (see Analytical Matrix in §3.4).
- Skipping levels violates ISO 26262 **"each safety requirement must be verified at the appropriate integration level"** clause.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An autonomous test fleet has driven **50 million miles without a fatality**. Using the Chernoff bound, compute the upper bound on fatal-accident rate at **99 % confidence**. Compare it with the **human baseline of 1.13 × 10⁻⁷ fatalities/mile**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$
n = 50\,000\,000,\qquad \beta = 0.01,\qquad 1-\beta = 0.99
$$

**Step 2 — Compute upper-bound rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{p} &= \frac{-\ln(\beta)}{n} \\[2pt]
&= \frac{-\ln(0.01)}{50\,000\,000} \\[2pt]
&= \frac{4.6052}{50\,000\,000} \\[2pt]
&= 9.21 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{fatalities/mile}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with human baseline.**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{\hat{p}}{p_{\text{human}}} = \frac{9.21\times 10^{-8}}{1.13\times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.815
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

At 99 % confidence, the AV is statistically **not yet as safe as a human** (only 0.815× the human rate). The fleet needs additional mileage to achieve the **"2× safer than human"** ($\hat{p} \le 5.65\times 10^{-8}$) benchmark:

$$
n_{\text{req}} = \frac{-\ln(0.01)}{5.65\times 10^{-8}} \approx 8.15\times 10^{10}\;\text{miles}
$$

> This is **why simulation-based testing is mandatory** — pure road testing cannot economically reach the required mileage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse MiL with HiL** — many students write "HiL means software-only test" — that is SiL.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the formula** in numerical questions — even if the answer is approximate, show the substitution.
> 3. **Do NOT write "V-Model is a test method"** — V-Model is a **development framework**; the test method inside it is XiL.
> 4. **Do NOT forget SOTIF** — ISO 26262 alone is **incomplete**; the examiner expects ISO 21448 mention for any perception-related question.
> 5. **Do NOT confuse open-loop vs closed-loop** — open-loop = static, closed-loop = dynamic; this is a recurring 2-mark trap.
> 6. **Do NOT omit diagrams** — for any 7-mark sub-question, a block diagram fetches **at least 1 extra mark** even if the text is weak.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **V-Model** = sequential design-test pairing; left = verification, right = validation.
- ✅ **XiL levels** = MiL → SiL → HiL → ViL → DiL; each mandatory for ISO 26262.
- ✅ **ISO 26262** = functional safety; ASIL A → D; HARA → safety goals → ASIL allocation.
- ✅ **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** = safety even without faults; scenario-based; residual-risk evaluation.
- ✅ **Open-loop** = recorded-sensor playback (static).
- ✅ **Closed-loop** = AV action affects simulated world (dynamic).
- ✅ **Scenario tuple** = ⟨A, B, C, D, E⟩ — agents, behavior, conditions, data, environment.
- ✅ **Coverage** = $C_R = N_t / N_{\text{total}}$; combinatorial space = $\prod n_i$.
- ✅ **Chernoff bound** = $\hat{p} = -\ln(\beta)/n$ — drives the "10-billion-mile" AV safety argument.
- ✅ **Falsification** = adversarial scenario search; uses STL / Bayesian optimization.
- ✅ **FoV coverage** = $\min(1, m\alpha/2\pi)$ — sensor-redundancy metric.
- ✅ **ASAM OpenSCENARIO / OpenDRIVE** = industry scenario & map description standards.
- ✅ **MTBF, FTTI, ASIL D** = **< 10⁻⁸ failures/hour** = safety benchmark numbers.
- ✅ **Test Pyramid** = base = MiL/SiL (most tests), apex = DiL (fewest, most expensive).
- ✅ **Mnemonic** — *"V → XiL → Scenario → Coverage → SOTIF"* — answers 90 % of KTU Module-4 questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation Environments and Test Tracks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulation Environments and Test Tracks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 — SIMULATION ENVIRONMENTS AND TEST TRACKS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulation Environments and Test Tracks** constitute the **Verification and Validation (V&V)** backbone of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs). Simulation environments are software frameworks that replicate vehicle dynamics, sensor behavior, road infrastructure, and traffic participants in a virtual loop (Model-in-the-Loop, Software-in-the-Loop, Hardware-in-the-Loop, Vehicle-in-the-Loop). Test tracks are controlled real-world proving grounds (e.g., MCity, AstaZero) where the developed system is subjected to standardized, repeatable, and instrumented physical trials.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework, the V&V process forms the practical bridge between *perception–planning–control* algorithms and **real-world deployment**, ensuring functional safety in accordance with **ISO 26262** and **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Flight Simulator for Cars":**
> Imagine learning to fly a Boeing 787. A pilot does **not** start by flying passengers across the Atlantic. First, they log hundreds of hours in a **flight simulator** that throws emergency engine fires, sudden thunderstorms, and bird strikes at them. Then, they taxi a real plane on a **controlled test runway** with safety nets before commercial flights.
> Autonomous vehicle development follows the **exact same philosophy**:
> 1. **Simulator (SIL / HIL)** → train, test edge cases, replay millions of accidents cheaply.
> 2. **Test Track (Proving Ground)** → validate the trained system in the *real physical world* with real sensors, real tires, and real sunlight.
> 3. **Public Road (Closed Course Pilot)** → deploy to actual users.
> Skipping steps 1 or 2 is statistically equivalent to launching an untrained pilot with paying passengers.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following benchmarks are industry-recognized for ADAS/AV V&V:

| Standard Metric | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Industry target simulation mileage** | $\mathbf{10^9}$ **–** $\mathbf{10^{10}}$ **km** | Required to demonstrate safety parity with human driving (Rand Corporation study) |
| **Real-world test track driving** | $\mathbf{10^6}$ **–** $\mathbf{10^7}$ **km** | Complement to simulation for safety case |
| **Target test-track maneuver repeatability** | $\mathbf{\pm 2\%}$ | Lateral and longitudinal path tolerance |
| **MCity facility size** | $\mathbf{32}$ **acres ($\mathbf{\approx 130{,}000}$ m²)** | First purpose-built AV proving ground |
| **Camera frame rate (typical)** | $\mathbf{30}$ **–** $\mathbf{60}$ **Hz** | Determines simulation real-time factor |
| **LiDAR rotation rate (typical)** | $\mathbf{5}$ **–** $\mathbf{20}$ **Hz** | Critical for co-simulation fidelity |
| **Euro NCAP AEB test speeds** | $\mathbf{10}$ **–** $\mathbf{80}$ **km/h** | Reference operating envelope for test tracks |

> [!NOTE]
> The unit **$10^9$ km** is not a typo — to statistically prove that an autonomous system matches the human fatality rate of roughly **1.1 fatalities per 100 million miles**, software simulation must cover distances comparable to driving around the Earth's equator **25,000 times**. This is economically and temporally impossible on real roads alone — hence the critical role of simulation environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of the V&V Simulation Loop

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes four progressively more realistic testing stages, known collectively as the **X-in-the-Loop (XiL)** paradigm.

#### 2.1.1 Model-in-the-Loop (MIL)
- **What:** Pure software model of the algorithm tested against a pure software model of the plant (vehicle + environment).
- **Why:** Cheapest, fastest, ideal for unit testing perception (e.g., YOLO detector) and control (e.g., MPC planner).
- **How:** Both algorithm and vehicle model are `.cpp` / `.py` modules; no hardware.
- **Examples:** CARLA + Python ROS node, MATLAB/Simulink plant + controller.

#### 2.1.2 Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)
- **What:** Production-grade compiled controller code (e.g., AUTOSAR C code) running against a virtual vehicle.
- **Why:** Validates that *compiled* code matches the *modeled* behavior; catches overflow, rounding, and integration bugs.
- **How:** Cross-compile ECU code → execute on host PC → exchange CAN/FlexRay signals with virtual ECU.

#### 2.1.3 Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)
- **What:** **Real ECU hardware** (e.g., dSPACE SCALEXIO, National Instruments PXI) connected to a *real-time* simulated vehicle.
- **Why:** Catches electrical noise, bus latency, ADC/DAC quantization, and processor scheduling issues.
- **How:** Real-time OS (e.g., RedHawk, VxWorks) runs vehicle dynamics at $\mathbf{1}$ **kHz**; ECU "thinks" it is in a real car.

#### 2.1.4 Vehicle-in-the-Loop (VIL)
- **What:** **Real, physical vehicle** operating in a virtual environment — augmented reality for the car.
- **Why:** Tests *real* sensors (camera glare, LiDAR cross-talk) and *real* actuators (steering torque, brake response) without crash risk.
- **How:** Real car drives on a test track; cameras/LiDAR are fed *projected* virtual traffic from a screen or surround LED walls; steering/brake commands from AV stack are applied to real car.

### 2.2 The Scenario-Based Testing Paradigm

Modern V&V does **not** enumerate every kilometer. Instead, it uses **functional scenarios** as atomic test units. A scenario is a tuple:

$$
S = \langle E, A, O, M \rangle
$$

Where:
- $E$ = **Ego vehicle** state (position, velocity, acceleration)
- $A$ = **Actors** (vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists)
- $O$ = **Environment** (weather, lighting, road geometry, time of day)
- $M$ = **Mission** (lane change, intersection crossing, emergency brake)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standards like **ASAM OpenSCENARIO** (dynamic actors) and **ASAM OpenDRIVE** (static road network) are the de-facto format for exchanging scenarios across simulators — directly examinable in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bicycle Model (Lateral Dynamics)** | $\dot{v}_y = -\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m \, v_x} \, v_y + \left(\dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r}}{m \, v_x} - v_x\right) \dot{\psi} + \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}}{m} \, \delta$ | $v_y$ lateral velocity [m/s]; $v_x$ longitudinal [m/s]; $C_{\alpha f}, C_{\alpha r}$ cornering stiffness [N/rad]; $m$ mass [kg]; $\ell_f, \ell_r$ distance from CoG [m]; $\psi$ yaw angle [rad]; $\delta$ steering [rad] | Foundation of every vehicle dynamics simulator (CarSim, CarMaker) |
| **Bicycle Model (Yaw Rate)** | $\ddot{\psi} = \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, v_y - \dfrac{\ell_f^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha f} + \ell_r^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, \dot{\psi} + \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} \, \delta$ | $I_z$ yaw moment of inertia [kg·m²] | Predicts understeer/oversteer for track testing |
| **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{rel}} \quad$ where $\quad v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{front}$ | $\Delta d$ bumper-to-bumper gap [m]; $v_{rel}$ relative speed [m/s] | Critical metric for AEB/AES test-track pass/fail |
| **Braking Distance (Kinematic)** | $d_{brake} = \dfrac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 \, \mu \, g}$ | $v_0$ initial speed [m/s]; $\mu$ road friction coefficient; $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Euro NCAP dry/wet test conditions |
| **Total Stopping Distance** | $d_{total} = v_0 \, t_{react} + \dfrac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 \, \mu \, g}$ | $t_{react}$ human/algorithm reaction time [s] | Test-track straight-line brake test metric |
| **Sensor Detection Range (Radar)** | $R_{max} = \left(\dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{min}}\right)^{1/4}$ | $P_t$ transmit power [W]; $G$ antenna gain; $\lambda$ wavelength [m]; $\sigma$ RCS [m²]; $P_{min}$ minimum detectable [W] | Calibrates radar simulation fidelity |
| **LiDAR Photogrammetry** | $N_{points} = f_{rot} \cdot N_{lines} \cdot T_{frame}$ | $f_{rot}$ rotation [Hz]; $N_{lines}$ channels; $T_{frame}$ integration [s] | Determines simulation CPU/GPU load |
| **Simulation Real-Time Factor** | $RTF = \dfrac{T_{simulated}}{T_{wall}}$ | Both in seconds. $RTF = 1$ real-time; $<1$ slower; $>1$ faster-than-real-time | Quality-of-service metric for HIL rigs |
| **Monte Carlo Coverage** | $C_{MC} = 1 - (1-p)^{N}$ | $p$ scenario failure probability; $N$ samples | Determines number of test runs to certify system |
| **Stop-Distance Margin (Safety)** | $SDM = d_{brake,ego} - d_{brake,front} - d_{gap}$ | All distances [m] | Pass criterion for AEB forward-collision test |
| **Longitudinal Acceleration** | $a_x = \dfrac{F_{x,total}}{m} = \dfrac{F_{engine} + F_{brake} - F_{aero} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}}{m}$ | $F$ forces [N] | Used in CarMaker longitudinal test scripts |
| **Aerodynamic Drag** | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, v_x^{2}$ | $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ air density; $C_d$ drag coefficient; $A_f$ frontal area [m²] | High-speed test track (e.g., Nardò) verification |
| **Sensor FOV Cone** | $\theta_{cone} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2R}\right)$ | $d$ aperture [m]; $R$ range [m] | Camera/LiDAR field of view design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *lingua franca* of ADAS V&V. The bicycle model underpins **CarMaker** and **CarSim** vehicle libraries; the TTC and stopping-distance equations are the **exact pass/fail criteria** in **Euro NCAP AEB protocols**; the radar equation calibrates the noise floor in **sensor-level simulation**; and the Monte Carlo coverage formula justifies how many test-track runs are statistically required to certify an AV.

### 2.4 Test Track Classification (KTU 2024 Viewpoint)

Test tracks are categorized by **maneuver** and **environment** they support:

1. **Handling Track** — Constant-radius circles (skidpad), lane-change (ISO 3888), moose test.
2. **Brake Test Track** — Straight, high-friction surface, water jets for wet test.
3. **High-Speed Oval** — Sustained speed validation (Nardò, Papenburg).
4. **Urban Mock-up** — Traffic lights, intersections, pedestrian crossings (MCity, AstaZero).
5. **Highway Section** — Multi-lane carriageways for ACC/LKA test.
6. **Off-Road / Rough Road** — Belgian paving, cobblestones, gravel for suspension NVH.
7. **Adverse Weather Pad** — Flooded, salted, low-adhesion zones.
8. **V2X / Connected Corridor** — RSUs, C-V2X antennas, 5G coverage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single facility rarely covers all categories. **MCity (Ann Arbor, USA)** is the gold standard for urban; **AstaZero (Sweden)** leads in active safety; **IDIADA (Spain)** is Euro NCAP's primary partner; **TRL (UK)** specializes in V2X.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bicycle Model (Lateral Dynamics)

The bicycle model is the most examinable vehicle dynamics derivation in KTU 2024 ESE. It lumps the left and right wheels of each axle into a single virtual wheel at the centerline.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram and Force Definitions**

For the front axle, lateral tire force is proportional to the slip angle $\alpha_f$:

$$
F_{y,f} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f
$$

For the rear axle, similarly:

$$
F_{y,r} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r
$$

**Step 2 — Slip Angle Geometry**

The slip angle at each axle is the angle between the wheel's heading and the wheel's velocity vector. Using small-angle approximations:

$$
\alpha_f \approx \delta - \theta_v
$$

where $\theta_v = \dfrac{v_y + \ell_f \, \dot{\psi}}{v_x}$ is the vehicle sideslip angle component at the front axle.

$$
\alpha_r \approx -\dfrac{v_y - \ell_r \, \dot{\psi}}{v_x}
$$

**Step 3 — Newton's Second Law in Lateral Direction**

$$
m \, (\dot{v}_y + v_x \, \dot{\psi}) = F_{y,f} \cos\delta + F_{y,r}
$$

For small steering angles, $\cos\delta \approx 1$, so:

$$
m \, \dot{v}_y + m \, v_x \, \dot{\psi} = C_{\alpha f} \, \alpha_f + C_{\alpha r} \, \alpha_r
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Slip Angles**

$$
m \, \dot{v}_y + m \, v_x \, \dot{\psi} = C_{\alpha f} \left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + \ell_f \, \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right) + C_{\alpha r} \left(-\dfrac{v_y - \ell_r \, \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Group by State Variables $(v_y, \dot{\psi}, \delta)$**

Rearranging and isolating $\dot{v}_y$:

$$
\dot{v}_y = -\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m \, v_x} \, v_y + \left(\dfrac{\ell_r \, C_{\alpha r} - \ell_f \, C_{\alpha f}}{m \, v_x} - v_x\right) \dot{\psi} + \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}}{m} \, \delta
$$

This is the **first governing equation** of the bicycle model used in every ADAS simulation test bench.

**Step 6 — Yaw Moment Equation**

Summing moments about the center of gravity:

$$
I_z \, \ddot{\psi} = \ell_f \, F_{y,f} - \ell_r \, F_{y,r}
$$

Substituting the slip-angle relations:

$$
I_z \, \ddot{\psi} = \ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} \left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + \ell_f \, \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right) - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r} \left(-\dfrac{v_y - \ell_r \, \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Final Yaw Equation**

$$
\ddot{\psi} = \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, v_y - \dfrac{\ell_f^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha f} + \ell_r^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, \dot{\psi} + \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} \, \delta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This two-state model $(v_y, \dot{\psi})$ is the **core plant** in HIL rigs for Lane Keep Assist (LKA) and Electronic Stability Control (ESC) test campaigns. It is also why every autonomous vehicle simulator — CARLA, CarMaker, MATLAB Driving Scenario — exposes a tunable bicycle-model block.

### 3.2 Derivation of Time-to-Collision (TTC) for AEB Tests

In an AEB test, the ego vehicle approaches a stationary target. The closing speed is $v_{rel} = v_{ego}$ (since $v_{front} = 0$).

**Step 1 — Instantaneous TTC Definition**

$$
TTC(t) = \dfrac{d_{gap}(t)}{v_{ego}(t)}
$$

where $d_{gap}(t)$ is the bumper-to-bumper distance at time $t$.

**Step 2 — TTC Under Constant Deceleration**

If the ego vehicle decelerates at $a$ (positive for braking) and the target is stationary:

$$
v_{ego}(t) = v_0 - a \, t
$$

$$
d_{gap}(t) = d_0 - v_0 \, t + \tfrac{1}{2} \, a \, t^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Time at Which $v_{ego} = 0$**

$$
t_{stop} = \dfrac{v_0}{a}
$$

**Step 4 — Distance Travelled Until Stop**

$$
d_{travel} = v_0 \, t_{stop} - \tfrac{1}{2} \, a \, t_{stop}^{\,2} = \dfrac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 \, a}
$$

**Step 5 — Pass/Fail Criterion**

AEB test passes if:

$$
d_{travel} + d_{margin} \le d_0
$$

where $d_{margin}$ is the safety buffer (typically $\mathbf{0.3}$ **m** for Euro NCAP). This equation is **directly applied** during AEB certification on a test track.

### 3.3 Derivation of Monte Carlo Coverage for Scenario Testing

A scenario is run $N$ times with stochastic perturbation (e.g., pedestrian crossing time varies by Gaussian noise). The probability that *at least one* run catches a rare failure event of base probability $p$ is:

**Step 1 — Probability of a Single Miss**

A single run *misses* the failure with probability $(1 - p)$.

**Step 2 — Probability of $N$ Consecutive Misses**

$$
P_{miss,N} = (1 - p)^{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of At Least One Hit**

$$
P_{hit,N} = 1 - (1 - p)^{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Required $N$ for 95 % Confidence**

Setting $P_{hit,N} = 0.95$:

$$
0.95 = 1 - (1 - p)^{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1 - p)^{N} = 0.05
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(1 - p)} \approx \dfrac{3.0}{p} \quad \text{for small } p
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an ADAS system where a hazardous edge case occurs with probability $p = 10^{-5}$ per test run, the test track must execute approximately $N = 300{,}000$ runs to gain 95 % confidence. This is why **scenario-based simulation**, not track tests, is the workhorse of AV certification.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Tool vs. Stage vs. Use-Case

| Tool / Platform | Loop Stage | Primary Use-Case | Open/Closed | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MATLAB / Simulink + Driving Scenario** | MIL | Algorithm prototyping | Closed | Universities, R&D |
| **CARLA** | MIL/SIL | Deep-learning perception training | **Open-source** | Open AV research, Level-4 |
| **LGSVL Simulator** | MIL/SIL | Full-stack AV validation | Open-source | Apollo, Autoware integration |
| **CarSim / TruckSim** | MIL/SIL | High-fidelity vehicle dynamics | Closed | Tier-1 suppliers (Bosch, ZF) |
| **IPG CarMaker** | MIL/SIL/HIL | OEM-grade co-simulation | Closed | BMW, Audi, Daimler |
| **PreScan (Siemens)** | SIL/HIL | Sensor + scenario simulation | Closed | ADAS development |
| **VTD (Virtual Test Drive)** | SIL/HIL | Scenario + traffic + sensors | Closed | Audi, Volkswagen |
| **dSPACE SCALEXIO** | HIL | Real-time ECU validation | Closed | Production ECU sign-off |
| **NI PXI + VeriStand** | HIL | Real-time + FPGA sensor injection | Closed | ADAS ECU |
| **AVL CRUISE M** | MIL | Longitudinal + energy analysis | Closed | Hybrid/EV powertrain + ADAS |
| **Pro-SiVIC (Oktal)** | SIL | Camera + LiDAR + radar co-sim | Closed | Sensor fusion research |
| **AstaZero (Test Track)** | VIL | Active safety, AEB, LKA | Physical | Sweden, OEM-sponsored |
| **MCity (Test Track)** | VIL | Urban AV, V2X | Physical | University of Michigan |
| **IDIADA (Test Track)** | VIL | Euro NCAP certification | Physical | Spain, certification |
| **TRL (Test Track)** | VIL | V2X, CAV pilot | Physical | UK, government-backed |
| **Nardò Ring** | Physical | High-speed endurance | Physical | Italy, prototype validation |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
> **Differentiate between Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) and Vehicle-in-the-Loop (VIL) simulation in the context of ADAS validation. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HIL | VIL |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant** | Real-time virtual vehicle model | Real physical vehicle |
| **ECU** | Real production ECU | Real production ECU |
| **Sensors** | Simulated sensor data injected into ECU | Real sensors see *real* environment (or augmented) |
| **Strength** | Catches bus-level, scheduling, electrical noise | Catches sensor physical effects, actuator response, tire-road interaction |
| **Risk** | Very low (no crash) | Moderate (real vehicle on closed track) |
| **Cost** | Moderate (HIL rig ~ €0.5M) | High (vehicle + drivers + facility) |

> In short, HIL tests the **electronic nervous system** of the car; VIL tests the **entire body** in a controlled but real environment.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
> **Explain the role of Euro NCAP AEB test protocols in autonomous vehicle validation. Why is the test-track stop distance margin used as a pass/fail criterion? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Euro NCAP defines standardized **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** test protocols to assess whether a vehicle can avoid or mitigate a frontal collision with another vehicle or vulnerable road user (pedestrian/cyclist). Tests are conducted on a **brake-test track** with controlled friction ($\mu \approx 0.9$ dry, $0.4$ wet) and a soft-prop or stationary target. The **stop-distance margin (SDM)**, defined as

$$
SDM = d_{brake,ego} - d_{brake,front} - d_{gap}
$$

must remain positive (i.e., the ego vehicle stops with a safety margin of at least **0.3 m** before contacting the target) to earn the **5-star safety rating**. SDM is used as the pass/fail criterion because it is **measurable, repeatable, independent of driver behaviour, and directly correlates with occupant and pedestrian injury severity in real-world collisions**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four levels of the X-in-the-Loop (XiL) testing framework** used in ADAS development. For each level, state the entity that is *real* and the entity that is *simulated*, and give **one** commercial tool used at that level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **bicycle-model lateral dynamics equation** for a vehicle, clearly defining each variable. Show that the steady-state yaw-rate gain $K_{ss}$ for a constant steering angle $\delta$ and constant longitudinal speed $v_x$ is given by

$$
K_{ss} = \dfrac{v_x / L}{1 + K \, v_x^{\,2}}
$$

where $K = \dfrac{m \, (\ell_r \, C_{\alpha r} - \ell_f \, C_{\alpha f})}{L^2 \, C_{\alpha f} \, C_{\alpha r}}$ is the **understeer gradient** and $L = \ell_f + \ell_r$ is the wheelbase. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — XiL Framework**

The four levels of XiL testing are:

1. **Model-in-the-Loop (MIL):** Both the controller *algorithm* and the *plant* (vehicle + environment) are pure software models. No hardware is involved.
   - *Real:* nothing (pure code).
   - *Simulated:* controller + plant.
   - *Tool example:* **MATLAB/Simulink** with Driving Scenario Designer.

2. **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL):** The *production-compiled controller* code (e.g., AUTOSAR C/C++) runs on a host PC against a *virtual plant*.
   - *Real:* compiled ECU code.
   - *Simulated:* plant.
   - *Tool example:* **dSPACE VEOS**.

3. **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL):** The *real production ECU* is connected to a *real-time simulated plant* through analogue/digital I/O and bus (CAN, FlexRay, Ethernet).
   - *Real:* ECU hardware.
   - *Simulated:* plant running in real-time OS.
   - *Tool example:* **dSPACE SCALEXIO** or **NI PXI + VeriStand**.

4. **Vehicle-in-the-Loop (VIL):** The *real vehicle* drives on a test track, but the *traffic environment* is virtual and injected into the vehicle's sensors (e.g., via projection screens or LED walls for cameras; replayed LiDAR/radar for RF sensors).
   - *Real:* full vehicle (sensors + actuators + ECU).
   - *Simulated:* surrounding traffic.
   - *Tool example:* **IPG CarMaker VIL module** or **AstaZero VIL rig**.

**Block diagram (textual representation):**

```
LEVEL 1 (MIL):  [Algo SW] <---> [Plant SW]
LEVEL 2 (SIL):  [Compiled Code] <---> [Plant SW]
LEVEL 3 (HIL):  [Real ECU HW] <---> [Real-time Plant SW via I/O]
LEVEL 4 (VIL):  [Real Vehicle] drives on track; virtual traffic injected into sensors
```

---

**Part (b) — Bicycle Model Steady-State Yaw-Rate Gain**

**Step 1 — Start from the two governing equations:**

$$
\dot{v}_y = -\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m \, v_x} \, v_y + \left(\dfrac{\ell_r \, C_{\alpha r} - \ell_f \, C_{\alpha f}}{m \, v_x} - v_x\right) \dot{\psi} + \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}}{m} \, \delta
$$

$$
\ddot{\psi} = \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, v_y - \dfrac{\ell_f^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha f} + \ell_r^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, \dot{\psi} + \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} \, \delta
$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state assumption** $\dot{v}_y = 0, \; \ddot{\psi} = 0$:

$$
0 = -\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m \, v_x} \, v_y + \left(\dfrac{\ell_r \, C_{\alpha r} - \ell_f \, C_{\alpha f}}{m \, v_x} - v_x\right) \dot{\psi}_{ss} + \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}}{m} \, \delta
$$

$$
0 = \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, v_y - \dfrac{\ell_f^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha f} + \ell_r^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha r}}{I_z \, v_x} \, \dot{\psi}_{ss} + \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} \, \delta
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the second equation for $v_y$:**

$$
v_y = \dfrac{\ell_f^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha f} + \ell_r^{\,2} \, C_{\alpha r}}{(\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r})} \, \dot{\psi}_{ss} - \dfrac{\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} \, v_x}{(\ell_f \, C_{\alpha f} - \ell_r \, C_{\alpha r})} \, \delta
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the first equation.** After algebraic elimination of $v_y$, the yaw-rate steady state becomes:

$$
\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \dfrac{v_x \, \delta}{L + K \, v_x^{\,2}}
$$

where

$$
L = \ell_f + \ell_r
$$

and

$$
K = \dfrac{m \, (\ell_r \, C_{\alpha r} - \ell_f \, C_{\alpha f})}{L^2 \, C_{\alpha f} \, C_{\alpha r}}
$$

is the **understeer gradient** (with units of rad·s²/m²).

**Step 5 — Define the steady-state yaw-rate gain $K_{ss} = \dot{\psi}_{ss}/\delta$:**

$$
K_{ss} = \dfrac{v_x / L}{1 + K \, v_x^{\,2}} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:**
> - If $K > 0$ (typical front-heavy vehicle), then as $v_x \uparrow$, $\dot{\psi}_{ss} \downarrow$ → **understeering** car, which is stable and self-correcting.
> - If $K = 0$ (neutral steer), $\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \delta \, v_x / L$, i.e., yaw rate is proportional to speed (Ackermann cornering).
> - If $K < 0$ (rear-heavy or low rear grip), $\dot{\psi}_{ss} \uparrow$ with $v_x$ → **oversteer** leading to spin-out at the critical speed $v_{crit} = \sqrt{-1/K}$.
> - This is **the most tested property on a handling track / skidpad** for any ADAS stability validation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **MCity test facility**. List **at least five distinct test-track zones** that MCity contains, and explain why each zone is required for ADAS/AV testing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle is approaching a stationary target at $v_0 = 50$ km/h. The AEB controller achieves a constant deceleration of $a = 7$ m/s². The initial gap to the target is $d_0 = 18$ m. Determine:
   (i) The time to stop.
   (ii) The total distance travelled.
   (iii) Whether the AEB test passes the Euro NCAP **0.3 m safety margin** criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — MCity Test Facility**

MCity, located in Ann Arbor, Michigan, USA, is a **32-acre purpose-built proving ground** operated by the University of Michigan's Mobility Transformation Center. It opened in 2015 and was the **first facility in the world** designed exclusively for testing connected and automated vehicles (CAVs).

The facility reproduces a full range of urban, suburban, and highway driving environments within a controlled and observable perimeter. Its five most important test-track zones are:

1. **Urban Network (5 lane-miles of roads):** Includes signalised intersections, roundabouts, bus stops, bike lanes, sidewalks, and over 20 building facades. *Required for:* V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure) and unprotected-left-turn AV tests.

2. **Highway-Style Freeway Ramps:** Curved on/off-ramps with concrete barriers. *Required for:* ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control), lane-merge, and highway pilot algorithms.

3. **Skidpad (Circular Pad):** A flat, high-friction circular pad for steady-state cornering tests. *Required for:* validating the bicycle-model understeer gradient and ESC intervention.

4. **Brake-Test Straight:** A long, high-friction straight with water-injection capability. *Required for:* AEB certification under both dry ($\mu \approx 0.9$) and wet ($\mu \approx 0.4$) conditions.

5. **Adverse-Weather and Lighting Section:** Includes a flooding pad, salted-pavement zone, and a tunnel with controllable LED lighting from 0 lux (night) to 100 000 lux (noon glare). *Required for:* SOTIF (ISO 21448) edge-case validation — sensor robustness under sun glare, rain, and darkness.

6. *(Bonus)* **V2X Connected Corridor:** RSUs, C-V2X antennas, and 5G small cells. *Required for:* cooperative ACC and platooning tests.

---

**Part (b) — AEB Numerical Problem**

**Given:** $v_0 = 50$ km/h $= 50/3.6 = 13.89$ m/s, $a = 7$ m/s², $d_0 = 18$ m, $d_{margin} = 0.3$ m.

**(i) Time to stop:**

$$
t_{stop} = \dfrac{v_0}{a} = \dfrac{13.89}{7} = 1.984 \; \text{s}
$$

**(ii) Total distance travelled:**

$$
d_{travel} = \dfrac{v_0^{\,2}}{2a} = \dfrac{13.89^{\,2}}{2 \times 7} = \dfrac{192.9}{14} = 13.78 \; \text{m}
$$

**(iii) Pass/Fail Decision:**

Stopping distance including the required margin:

$$
d_{travel} + d_{margin} = 13.78 + 0.30 = 14.08 \; \text{m}
$$

Compare to initial gap:

$$
14.08 \; \text{m} \; < \; d_0 = 18 \; \text{m}
$$

**Result:** The vehicle stops with $18 - 13.78 = 4.22$ m remaining distance, far exceeding the 0.3 m margin. **The AEB test PASSES.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error** — Failing to convert $v_0$ from km/h to m/s costs the first mark. Always write: $v_0 = 50/3.6 = 13.89$ m/s.
> 2. **Sign of acceleration** — Some students use $a = -7$ m/s² and report negative distance. State clearly that $a$ is the *magnitude* of deceleration.
> 3. **Missing margin** — The 0.3 m is the *safety buffer* the vehicle must *keep*; do not subtract it from $d_0$ before comparing.
> 4. **No units in final answer** — Always write the unit (m, s) explicitly.
> 5. **Bicycle-model derivation marks are lost** if the student skips the *steady-state assumption* (i.e., setting $\dot{v}_y = 0$ and $\ddot{\psi} = 0$). The examiner awards 2 of the 7 marks specifically for naming this assumption.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024, Module 4**

- [ ] The **V&V process** is structured as **MIL → SIL → HIL → VIL → Proving Ground → Public Road**, with each level increasing in realism and cost.
- [ ] **MIL** uses pure software on both sides; **SIL** uses compiled code; **HIL** uses a *real* ECU with a *real-time* simulated plant; **VIL** uses a *real vehicle* in a *virtual* environment.
- [ ] The **bicycle model** has two state variables: lateral velocity $v_y$ and yaw rate $\dot{\psi}$. Its understeer gradient $K$ determines if the car is understeering ($K>0$), neutral ($K=0$), or oversteering ($K<0$).
- [ ] The **steady-state yaw-rate gain** $K_{ss} = \dfrac{v_x/L}{1 + K v_x^{\,2}}$ is the **most important** closed-form result in ADAS vehicle dynamics.
- [ ] **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** = gap / closing speed is the primary trigger for AEB.
- [ ] **Stopping distance** = $v_0^{\,2} / (2a)$ under constant deceleration; the test passes if $d_{stop} + 0.3 \le d_0$.
- [ ] **Euro NCAP AEB tests** run between **10–80 km/h** on a brake-test track with a soft-prop or stationary target.
- [ ] The **Monte Carlo coverage** $1 - (1-p)^N$ dictates how many runs are required — a rare failure with $p = 10^{-5}$ needs $N \approx 300{,}000$ runs.
- [ ] **MCity** (Michigan, USA, 32 acres) is the world's first purpose-built AV proving ground; **AstaZero** (Sweden) leads in active safety; **IDIADA** (Spain) is the Euro NCAP certification partner; **Nardò** (Italy) handles high-speed endurance.
- [ ] **Open standards** for scenario exchange: **ASAM OpenSCENARIO** (dynamic actors) and **ASAM OpenDRIVE** (static road network).
- [ ] **Open-source simulators:** **CARLA**, **LGSVL**, **AirSim**; **Commercial simulators:** **CarSim**, **CarMaker (IPG)**, **PreScan (Siemens)**, **VTD (Hexagon)**.
- [ ] **HIL hardware:** **dSPACE SCALEXIO**, **NI PXI**, **Vector VT System** — all run the plant at **1 kHz** real time.
- [ ] **Sensor simulation** must reproduce the **physical effects** of cameras (FOV, lens distortion, exposure), LiDAR (ray casting, beam divergence), radar (Doppler, RCS), and GPS/IMU (drift, multipath).
- [ ] The **Real-Time Factor (RTF)** = $T_{sim} / T_{wall}$ — values near 1.0 indicate viable HIL operation.
- [ ] **Functional safety** is governed by **ISO 26262** (known hazards) and **ISO 21448 SOTIF** (unknown/edge-case hazards).
- [ ] **Radar range** $R_{max} \propto (P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma / P_{min})^{1/4}$ — the fourth-root dependence means power must be increased **16×** to double range.
- [ ] **Aerodynamic drag** $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v_x^{\,2}$ dominates the high-speed test track (Nardò > 250 km/h).
- [ ] KTU ESE 14-mark questions on this module **always** include a numerical AEB problem and a derivation of either the bicycle model or yaw-rate gain — practice both.

---
