# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`**
*Q: List and briefly explain the three strategic archetypes that classify leading autonomous vehicle projects in the industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**
1. **Vertically Integrated Robotaxi Operators** (1 mark) — Companies like Waymo, Cruise, Zoox, and Baidu Apollo Go that own the vehicle, software, fleet operations, and ride-hailing service. They target **L4–L5** within a **geo-fenced ODD**.
2. **Production-Vehicle ADAS Suppliers** (1 mark) — Companies like Mobileye, NVIDIA DRIVE, Bosch, and Continental that supply **perception/compute platforms** to OEMs. They target **L2+ to L3** features in mass-market consumer vehicles.
3. **Data-Scale Vision-First OEMs** (1 mark) — Tesla and Comma.ai leverage **consumer fleet data** to train end-to-end neural networks and push OTA software updates. The bet is that **fleet-scale data** replaces HD maps and LiDAR.

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`**
*Q: What is the Road Experience Management (REM) concept introduced by Mobileye, and how does it differ from Waymo's HD mapping approach?*

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**
- **REM definition (1.5 marks):** REM is Mobileye's **crowd-sourced HD mapping** technology where millions of consumer cars equipped with Mobileye EyeQ cameras passively collect **landmark data** (lane markings, poles, signs) and upload compressed map snippets to a central cloud. The cloud aggregates these snippets into a **centimeter-accurate HD map** that can be downloaded by autonomous vehicles.
- **Difference from Waymo (1.5 marks):** Waymo uses a **dedicated mapping fleet** of sensor-rich survey vehicles that manually drive and map each ODD. REM in contrast leverages **existing consumer vehicles** for map data, making it far more scalable. However, REM relies on **camera-only** landmarks and is therefore less geometrically rich than Waymo's multi-modal maps.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Part B]`**
*Q: Waymo and Tesla represent two fundamentally different approaches to autonomous vehicle development. Compare and contrast their engineering strategies across (a) sensor stack, mapping, and compute architecture, and (b) business model, validation methodology, and regulatory pathway. State which approach you consider more scalable for the next decade and justify with at least two quantitative arguments.*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Sensor Stack (2 marks):**
- **Waymo:** 5 LiDARs (1 long-range 360° roof, 4 short-range perimeter) + 29 cameras + 6 radars. This **multi-modal redundant** design follows the classic robotics principle of **complementary sensing** — failure of one modality (e.g., camera in glare) is covered by another (e.g., LiDAR).
- **Tesla:** 8 cameras, no LiDAR, no HD radar (HW4). Tesla's **vision-only** philosophy argues that cameras, like the human eye, are sufficient if backed by a sufficiently large neural network trained on billions of miles of fleet data.

**(ii) Mapping Strategy (2 marks):**
- **Waymo:** **HD-map dependent.** A dedicated mapping fleet first drives the ODD to build a centimeter-accurate 3D map including lane geometry, traffic-sign positions, and curb heights. The autonomous vehicle then localizes itself into this prior map.
- **Tesla:** **No HD map dependency** in the public release. The system relies on lane markings and traffic signs detected in real time, augmented by a **BEV (Bird's Eye View) transformer** that builds a 3D scene representation on the fly.

**(iii) Compute Architecture (2 marks):**
- **Waymo:** **Modular pipeline** — sensor fusion → perception → prediction → hierarchical planning → control. Each module is independently trained and validated, providing interpretability.
- **Tesla:** **End-to-end neural network** — the BEV transformer, occupancy network, and neural planner are jointly trained, sharing representations and reducing error propagation.

**(iv) Synthesis (1 mark):**
Waymo's design prioritizes **safety and interpretability** through redundancy; Tesla's design prioritizes **scalability and data efficiency** through statistical learning.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Business Model (2 marks):**
- **Waymo:** **Robotaxi service** (Waymo One) operating in Phoenix, San Francisco, Los Angeles, and Austin. Revenue is per-ride. Capital-intensive: requires fleet ownership, depot operations, and city-by-city regulatory approval.
- **Tesla:** **Consumer EV sales + OTA software revenue.** FSD is sold as a $8,000–$15,000 option (or subscription) on every Tesla. As of 2024, Tesla has sold over 6 million vehicles globally, providing the **largest fleet-scale data collection** in the industry.

**(ii) Validation Methodology (2 marks):**
- **Waymo:** **Closed-course testing** (Castle facility), **structured simulation** (Carcraft, simulation cities), and **public-road testing** with safety drivers. Total > 20 billion simulated miles + 20+ million real miles.
- **Tesla:** **Shadow-mode deployment** — the FSD neural network runs silently in millions of customer cars, comparing its decisions to human drivers. Disengagements are uploaded for retraining. **No closed-course testing facility** comparable to Waymo's Castle.

**(iii) Regulatory Pathway (1 mark):**
- **Waymo:** Requires **state-by-state robotaxi permits** (CPUC in California, ADOT in Arizona). Operations are geo-fenced and approved.
- **Tesla:** Operates under existing **Type Approval** for L2 ADAS features. FSD is classified as a **Level 2 driver assistance** system (per Tesla's own disclaimers) and does not yet require robotaxi permits.

**(iv) Scalability Verdict + Quantitative Argument (2 marks):**

**Position:** **Tesla's approach is more scalable for the next decade.**

**Quantitative Argument 1 — Data Volume:**
Tesla's fleet has driven **> 3 billion miles** on FSD-capable hardware. Waymo's cumulative real-world test miles are **< 50 million** (real + 20 billion simulated). Real-world fleet data has higher information value than simulation.

**Quantitative Argument 2 — Capital Efficiency:**
A Waymo-class sensor suite costs **~\$59,000 per vehicle** (calculated in Section 3). A Tesla-class camera suite costs **~\$240 per vehicle** — a **246× cost difference**. For a 100,000-vehicle fleet, this is **~\$5.9 billion in capital avoidance**, allowing Tesla to amortize development across a much larger production base.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Part B Internal Choice]`**
*Q: (a) Describe the architecture of Baidu Apollo and explain why it is considered the "Android of autonomous vehicles." Discuss the role of V2X in the Apollo stack. (b) Compare Mobileye's REM-based mapping with Tesla's vision-only no-HD-map approach. Which approach do you consider more robust to adverse weather and justify with technical reasoning?*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Apollo Architecture (2.5 marks):**
Baidu Apollo, launched in April 2017, is an **open-source AV software platform** that provides reference implementations of:
- **Perception:** LiDAR/camera/radar-based detection, semantic segmentation.
- **Prediction:** CNN+RNN models for trajectory prediction.
- **Planning:** EM Planner (Expectation Maximization-based motion planner).
- **Control:** PID + LQR-based vehicle controllers.
- **HD Map:** Apollo Maps with partner mapping agencies.
- **Simulation:** Apollo Dreamland (closed-loop sim) + LGSVL.

**(ii) Why "Android of AVs" (2 marks):**
Apollo follows a **modular open-source** model similar to Android. OEMs, startups, and universities can adopt the platform, modify modules, and deploy their own vehicles. Major adopters include **Geely, Toyota (with Apollo), Hyundai (partnership),** and various Chinese EV makers. Apollo reduces the **R&D barrier** to entry for AV development.

**(iii) Role of V2X (2.5 marks):**
Apollo uniquely integrates **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication:
- **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Receives SPaT (Signal Phase and Timing) from smart traffic lights, road sensors, and roadside units.
- **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Cloud-based HD map updates, real-time hazard warnings.
- **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Cooperative perception with nearby vehicles.
Apollo Go robotaxis in cities like **Wuhan, Chongqing, and Beijing** leverage China's heavy investment in **smart-city V2X infrastructure** — for example, Apollo Go vehicles at certain intersections receive signal-timing data **200 m before reaching the signal**, allowing smoother speed planning.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mobileye REM Approach (2 marks):**
- **Pipeline:** EyeQ-equipped consumer cars detect **landmarks** (signs, poles, lane markings) → compressed **map snippets** (~10 KB/km) uploaded to Mobileye cloud → cloud stitches snippets into **HD map** → downloadable by AVs.
- **Daily updates:** REM aims for **daily HD map updates** at city scale, leveraging millions of consumer vehicles.
- **Complementary:** Mobileye pairs REM with **camera + (optional) radar/LiDAR** on the vehicle side for safety redundancy.

**(ii) Tesla Vision-Only Approach (2 marks):**
- **No prior HD map dependency.** The 8 cameras detect lane markings, signs, and other road features in real time.
- **BEV Transformer + Occupancy Network** builds a 3D occupancy grid on the fly.
- **No cloud map updates** are required for the basic FSD pipeline.

**(iii) Adverse Weather Robustness Comparison (3 marks):**

| Condition | Mobileye REM + Camera + Radar/LiDAR | Tesla Vision-Only |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy rain | LiDAR (1550 nm) penetrates; radar unaffected | Cameras degraded; no LiDAR backup |
| Fog | LiDAR signal scattered but partially usable | Cameras severely degraded |
| Snow covering lane lines | Prior REM map provides fallback | No prior map; vision may fail |
| Night | LiDAR active; thermal cameras (if equipped) | Tesla cameras struggle; FSD has nighttime issues |

**Conclusion:** Mobileye's approach is **more robust to adverse weather** because (a) LiDAR operates in darkness and many weather conditions, (b) REM provides a **prior map** that can serve as a fallback when real-time vision fails, and (c) radar provides direct velocity measurement that vision cannot.

Tesla counters by arguing that **neural networks trained on adverse-weather data** can learn to recover the missing perception, but this requires vast amounts of adverse-weather training data that the fleet must collect.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss):**
> When comparing projects, students often **take a side** (e.g., "Tesla is better" or "Waymo is better") and present subjective opinion without **quantitative backing**. To get full marks, you **MUST** include at least **two quantitative arguments** (e.g., cost figures, miles driven, disengagement rates, sensor counts) and **two qualitative engineering trade-offs** (e.g., redundancy vs scalability). Marks are awarded for *justification*, not for the verdict itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Three strategic archetypes** of AV projects: **(1) Robotaxi Operators** (Waymo, Cruise, Zoox, Aurora, Apollo Go, Pony.ai), **(2) ADAS Vendors** (Mobileye, NVIDIA, Bosch, Continental), **(3) Data-Scale Vision-First OEMs** (Tesla, Comma.ai).
- ✅ **Waymo's sensor stack:** **5 LiDARs + 29 cameras + 6 radars** — multi-modal redundant design.
- ✅ **Tesla's sensor stack:** **8 cameras, no LiDAR, no HD radar (HW4)** — vision-only end-to-end NN.
- ✅ **Baidu Apollo** is **open-source** and uniquely integrates **V2X (V2I/V2N/V2V)** into the AV stack.
- ✅ **Mobileye REM** = crowd-sourced HD map from consumer cars; ~**10 KB/km** map snippets.
- ✅ **Aurora** uses **FMCW LiDAR** (acquired Blackmore) and focuses on **long-haul trucking**.
- ✅ **Zoox** (Amazon) builds a **bidirectional, purpose-built robotaxi** with no steering wheel.
- ✅ **NVIDIA Drive Orin = 254 TOPS**; **Thor (2025) ~2000 TOPS**.
- ✅ **HD map accuracy = 5–10 cm**; **V2X latency target < 10 ms**; **automotive LiDAR wavelength = 905 nm or 1550 nm**; **automotive radar = 76–77 GHz**.
- ✅ **California DMV disengagement rate** is the **only standardized cross-project safety metric**; Waymo consistently has the **lowest** disengagement rate.
- ✅ **Tesla's data advantage:** > **3 billion fleet miles**; **Waymo's simulation advantage:** > **20 billion simulated miles**.
- ✅ **Sensor cost ratio:** Waymo-class (~\$59K) vs Tesla-class (~\$240) = **~246×** difference.
- ✅ **NHTSA / Type Approval** governs L2/L3 consumer ADAS; **state-by-state permits** govern L4 robotaxi operators.
- ✅ **Cruise paused operations in late 2023** — a key example of the **gap between technical capability and regulatory/social acceptance**.
- ✅ **BEV (Bird's Eye View) Transformer** is the core neural architecture that enables Tesla's vision-only approach to estimate 3D scene geometry from 2D cameras.
- ✅ **FSD (Full Self-Driving)** by Tesla is officially classified as **L2 driver assistance** (per Tesla's own disclaimers), *not* L4 — a critical regulatory distinction.
- ✅ **End-to-end NN** (Tesla) reduces error propagation; **Modular pipeline** (Waymo) improves interpretability — the central architectural trade-off.
- ✅ **Disengagement Rate** is *not* a perfect safety measure — disengagement **policies differ** across companies.
- ✅ **Apollo Go operates commercially in Wuhan, Chongqing, Beijing** — China's leading robotaxi service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU Board Examiner's Note):**
> The Module 4 question almost always follows the pattern: *"Compare [Project A] and [Project B] across [sensing / mapping / business]."* Memorize the **4-axis comparison matrix** (Sensing, Mapping, Compute, Business) and the **2 quantitative arguments** template (Cost + Data Volume OR Cost + Disengagement Rate). This single template answers **80% of past KTU questions** on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging Trends in Autonomous Driving and ADAS Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Emerging Trends in Autonomous Driving and ADAS Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Emerging Trends in ADAS and Autonomous Driving?

The term **Emerging Trends in Autonomous Driving and ADAS Technology** refers to the next-generation technological paradigms, communication protocols, computational frameworks, and infrastructural innovations that are currently shaping the evolution of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) from simple warning systems toward fully autonomous (Level 5) vehicles. These trends are driven by the convergence of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**, **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication**, **5G networks**, **edge computing**, **high-definition (HD) mapping**, **sensor fusion**, and **smart city infrastructure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> Emerging trends in ADAS and autonomous vehicles encompass the integration of connected vehicle technology, cooperative driving strategies, AI-driven perception, over-the-air (OTA) updates, and intelligent transportation systems (ITS) that collectively enable higher levels of vehicle automation, safety, and efficiency beyond what stand-alone on-board sensors can achieve.

## 1.2 Levels of Driving Automation (SAE J3016 Standard)

The **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** has standardized automation into six levels. KTU 2024 syllabus considers this foundational.

| Level | Name | Steering/Acceleration | Monitoring of Environment | Fallback Performance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | No Automation | Driver | Driver | Driver |
| 1 | Driver Assistance | System | Driver | Driver |
| 2 | Partial Automation | System | Driver | Driver |
| 3 | Conditional Automation | System | System | Driver |
| 4 | High Automation | System | System | System |
| 5 | Full Automation | System | System | System |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor**
> Think of an emerging autonomous vehicle ecosystem as an **orchestra**. A traditional ADAS (like basic lane keeping) is a single musician playing alone. Emerging trends turn the entire vehicle into part of a *coordinated orchestra* — the car talks to other cars (**V2V**), to traffic signals (**V2I**), to pedestrians' phones (**V2P**), and to the cloud (**V2N**). The **5G network** acts as the "sheet music" being passed in real-time, while **AI** is the conductor ensuring every section plays in perfect harmony. Without these emerging trends, the vehicle is a soloist; with them, it is a symphony.

## 1.4 Key Emerging Trends — At a Glance

- **V2X Communication** (V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2D)
- **5G and Edge Computing for low-latency decisions**
- **Deep Learning & Neural Networks for perception**
- **HD Maps and Crowdsourced Mapping**
- **Sensor Fusion Evolution (LiDAR-Camera-Radar integration)**
- **Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (C-ACC)**
- **Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles**
- **Smart City and Infrastructure Integration**
- **OTA (Over-The-Air) Software Updates**
- **Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) with AI**
- **Electric Autonomous Vehicles (EV-AV Convergence)**
- **MaaS — Mobility as a Service**
- **Digital Twins for Vehicle Simulation**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember**
> - V2X latency target: **< 10 ms (for safety-critical messages)**
> - 5G URLLC latency: **< 1 ms**
> - HD map precision: **< 10 cm absolute accuracy**
> - SAE Level 5 goal: **zero human intervention under all conditions**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication

V2X is the umbrella term for the vehicular communication system where a vehicle exchanges information with entities that may affect or may be affected by the vehicle. It has the following sub-categories:

- **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)**: Cars share speed, position, and intent with nearby vehicles to prevent collisions.
- **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)**: Communication with road infrastructure such as traffic lights, road signs, and toll booths.
- **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian)**: Detection of vulnerable road users via their smartphones or wearables.
- **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network)**: Communication with cellular networks for cloud-based services (HD maps, traffic data).
- **V2D (Vehicle-to-Device)**: Connects to in-cabin devices or external diagnostic equipment.

### 2.1.1 Why V2X?

- Extends perception **beyond line-of-sight**.
- Operates in **non-line-of-sight (NLOS)** scenarios (e.g., a pedestrian hidden behind a truck).
- Reduces reliance on expensive on-board sensors.
- Enables **cooperative maneuvering** (e.g., platooning).

### 2.1.2 Latency Requirements

For safety-critical V2X messages (e.g., emergency braking), the maximum tolerable end-to-end latency is **10 ms**, and the reliability target is **99.999%** (the famous "five nines" requirement).

## 2.2 5G and Edge Computing

**5G** offers three primary service categories that map directly onto ADAS/AV needs:

- **eMBB** (Enhanced Mobile Broadband): High throughput for HD map streaming and infotainment.
- **URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication): Latency under 1 ms for safety messages.
- **mMTC** (Massive Machine-Type Communication): Connecting millions of vehicles and IoT sensors.

**Edge Computing (MEC — Multi-access Edge Computing)** brings computational resources closer to the vehicle (at the base station) to minimize the round-trip delay, which is critical for real-time perception and decision-making.

## 2.3 Artificial Intelligence and Deep Learning in Perception

Modern ADAS perception stacks are built on deep neural networks:

- **CNNs** (Convolutional Neural Networks) for object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN).
- **Semantic Segmentation** networks (U-Net, DeepLab) for free-space detection.
- **Transformers** for multi-object tracking and trajectory prediction.
- **Reinforcement Learning** for path planning and decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — Sensor Fusion**
> Modern autonomous systems do not rely on a single sensor. They **fuse** data from camera, radar, LiDAR, and ultrasonic sensors using algorithms like **Kalman Filter**, **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, and **Bayesian networks** to produce a robust world model.

## 2.4 HD Maps and Localization

HD maps contain lane geometry, road curvature, traffic signs, and 3D landmarks with centimeter-level accuracy. They are constructed via:

- **Survey-grade LiDAR** mobile mapping.
- **Crowdsourced mapping** (e.g., Tesla's vision-only fleet).

Localization against HD maps uses techniques like **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** and **particle filters**.

## 2.5 Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (C-ACC)

Unlike standard ACC that uses radar, C-ACC uses **V2V** data to receive the acceleration of the *leading vehicle's leader*, enabling tighter, more fuel-efficient platoons.

## 2.6 Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles

Connected vehicles are vulnerable to:
- **CAN bus injection attacks**
- **GPS spoofing**
- **OTA update manipulation**
- **Ransomware on infotainment systems**

Defenses include **blockchain-based authentication**, **intrusion detection systems (IDS)**, and **hardware security modules (HSM)**.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| End-to-end Latency | $t_{e2e} = t_{tx} + t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}$ | Total delay in V2X | ms |
| Communication Range | $R = \sqrt{(x_r - x_t)^2 + (y_r - y_t)^2}$ | Distance between two vehicles | m |
| Data Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{packets} \cdot L_{packet}}{T}$ | Bits transmitted per second | bps |
| SNR | $SNR = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ | Signal quality | dB |
| Path Loss (FSPL) | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss | dB |
| Cooperative Perception Gain | $G_{coop} = \dfrac{P_{det}^{V2X}}{P_{det}^{solo}}$ | Improvement via V2X | dimensionless |
| Time-to-Collision (V2V) | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | If $TTC < 1.5$ s → emergency brake | s |
| Platoon Stability | $\lambda_i = \dfrac{a_i}{a_{i-1}}$ | String stability parameter | dimensionless |
| Energy per Bit (5G) | $E_b/N_0$ | Bit energy to noise spectral density | dB |
| Localization Error | $\sigma_{loc} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}$ | 2D positioning accuracy | m |
| Reaction Time (AV) | $t_{react} = t_{sense} + t_{decide} + t_{actuate}$ | Total system response | ms |
| Kalman Gain | $K_k = P_k H^T (H P_k H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Optimal filter weighting | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> These formulas are used by OEMs (e.g., Tesla, Waymo, Bosch, Continental) to size antennas, plan sensor mounting, validate V2X protocols, and certify safety cases per **ISO 26262**.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Smart Cities** deploy V2I to dynamically adjust traffic signal phase and timing (SPaT).
- **Logistics** uses truck platooning to reduce fuel consumption by **10–15%**.
- **Ride-hailing** (Waymo, Cruise) uses MaaS to deploy robotaxis.
- **Insurance** uses UBI (Usage-Based Insurance) data from ADAS for premium calculation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Path Loss in V2X Communication

The **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** equation is fundamental for designing V2X antennas. We derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define the Isotropic Radiator
A point source radiates power uniformly in all directions. The power density $S$ at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi d^2}
$$

where $P_t$ is the transmitted power and $G_t$ is the transmitting antenna gain.

### Step 2: Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna
The effective aperture of the receiving antenna is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4 \pi}
$$

The received power is the product of the power density and the effective aperture:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e
$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4 \pi} \\
P_r &= P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Rearrange into Path Loss Form

$$
\frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{(4 \pi d)^2}{G_t G_r \lambda^2}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Decibels (dB)

$$
PL_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_t}{P_r}\right)
$$

Assuming $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic) and substituting $\lambda = c / f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PL_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(4 \pi) + 20 \log_{10}(d) - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{c}{f}\right) \\
PL_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4 \pi}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, the constant evaluates to approximately **32.44** (when $d$ is in km and $f$ in MHz).

$$
\boxed{PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44}
$$

> This equation is used to size V2X transmitter power in DSRC (5.9 GHz) and C-V2X (5.9 GHz) deployments.

## 3.2 Derivation: Time-to-Collision in V2V Emergency Braking

TTC is the time available before a collision assuming current relative kinematics are maintained.

### Step 1: Relative Distance and Velocity
Let the ego vehicle be at $x_e$ and the lead vehicle at $x_l$, both moving along a straight line.

$$
\Delta x = x_l - x_e \quad \text{(bumper-to-bumper distance)}
$$

$$
\Delta v = v_l - v_e \quad \text{(relative velocity)}
$$

### Step 2: Time-to-Collision Definition
The TTC is the time $\tau$ such that $\Delta x(\tau) = 0$, assuming constant velocities.

$$
\Delta x + \Delta v \cdot \tau = 0
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\tau$

$$
\tau = -\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta v}
$$

Since $\Delta x > 0$ and $\Delta v < 0$ in a closing scenario, $\tau$ is positive.

$$
\boxed{TTC = \frac{\Delta x}{|\Delta v|}} \quad \text{when closing}
$$

### Step 4: Decision Threshold (KTU Standard)
If $TTC < 1.5$ s, the system initiates **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)**. If $TTC < 0.6$ s, the system applies **maximum braking force** and may pre-tension seatbelts.

## 3.3 Derivation: String Stability of a Vehicle Platoon

A platoon is **string stable** if disturbances do not amplify as they propagate upstream.

### Step 1: Spacing Policy (Constant Time Headway)
The desired spacing of vehicle $i$ from vehicle $i-1$ is:

$$
d_i = d_0 + h \cdot v_i
$$

where $d_0$ is the standstill gap, $h$ is the time headway, and $v_i$ is the velocity of vehicle $i$.

### Step 2: Control Law (C-ACC)
The acceleration command is:

$$
a_i = k_1 (d_i - d_{desired}) + k_2 (v_{i-1} - v_i) + a_{i-1}
$$

The crucial term is the inclusion of $a_{i-1}$ (leader's acceleration) via V2V.

### Step 3: Transfer Function Analysis
Taking the Laplace transform, the transfer function from $a_{i-1}$ to $a_i$ is:

$$
H(s) = \frac{k_1 h s + k_1}{s^2 + (k_2 + k_1 h) s + k_1}
$$

### Step 4: String Stability Condition
String stability requires the $H_{\infty}$ norm of $H(s)$ to be less than or equal to 1:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert \le 1 \quad \forall \omega
$$

For typical values $k_1, k_2 > 0$, this holds when the V2V delay is **< 50 ms** — a key reason **5G URLLC** is targeted for platooning.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Communication Technologies

| Parameter | DSRC (802.11p) | C-V2X (4G LTE) | C-V2X (5G NR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Latency | ~20 ms | ~20 ms | **< 5 ms** |
| Range | ~300 m | ~400 m | ~500 m |
| Bandwidth | 10 MHz (5.9 GHz) | Up to 20 MHz | Up to 100 MHz |
| Reliability | 95% | 99% | **99.999%** |
| Deployment | Mature (US/EU) | Wide (China) | Emerging |
| Best Use | Basic safety msgs | Infotainment | Cooperative driving |

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Example

**Problem**: Two vehicles approach an intersection. Vehicle A is at 50 m and closing at 15 m/s. Vehicle B is at 0 m and moving at 5 m/s in the same direction. Compute TTC.

**Solution**:

$$
\Delta x = 50 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta v = 5 - 15 = -10 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
TTC = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ s}
$$

Since 5 s > 1.5 s, no emergency braking is required. However, AEB preparation (pre-charge brakes) may begin.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 V2X Communication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    egoCar["Ego Vehicle"]
    otherCar["Other Vehicle V2V"]
    infra["Traffic Signal V2I"]
    ped["Pedestrian Phone V2P"]
    cloud["Cloud Server V2N"]
    rsU["Roadside Unit RSU"]
    edge["Edge Server MEC"]
    
    egoCar -- V2V message --> otherCar
    egoCar -- V2I message --> infra
    egoCar -- V2P alert --> ped
    egoCar -- Cellular 5G --> cloud
    infra -- Aggregated data --> rsU
    rsU -- Low latency link --> edge
    cloud -- HD Map update --> egoCar
    edge -- Real time perception --> egoCar
    otherCar -- Cooperative maneuver --> egoCar
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The ego vehicle is at the center, communicating bidirectionally with other vehicles, infrastructure, pedestrians, and the cloud. The Roadside Unit (RSU) and Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) server form the low-latency layer.

## 4.2 Autonomous Vehicle Software Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensors["Sensor Layer Camera LiDAR Radar GPS IMU"]
    perception["Perception Module Object Detection Tracking Segmentation"]
    localization["Localization Module HD Map Matching Particle Filter"]
    prediction["Prediction Module Trajectory Forecasting Behavior Modeling"]
    planning["Planning Module Route Behavior Motion"]
    control["Control Module Steering Throttle Brake"]
    v2x["V2X Module Communication Security"]
    
    sensors --> perception
    sensors --> localization
    perception --> prediction
    localization --> planning
    prediction --> planning
    planning --> control
    v2x --> perception
    v2x --> planning
    control --> drive["Vehicle Actuators"]
```

## 4.3 Cooperative Driving Scenario — Highway Platooning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lead["Lead Vehicle V2V Tx"]
    follower1["Follower 1 V2V Rx and Tx"]
    follower2["Follower 2 V2V Rx and Tx"]
    follower3["Follower 3 V2V Rx"]
    
    lead -- Acceleration data --> follower1
    follower1 -- Acceleration data --> follower2
    follower2 -- Acceleration data --> follower3
    follower1 -. HD map from cloud .-> follower1
    follower2 -. HD map from cloud .-> follower2
```

## 4.4 ADAS Feature Evolution Trend

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Level 1 Basic ADAS AEB LDW"]
    B["Level 2 Partial ACC LKA"]
    C["Level 3 Conditional Traffic Jam Pilot"]
    D["Level 4 High Geo fenced Robotaxi"]
    E["Level 5 Full Ubiquitous AV"]
    
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    A -. V2X assists .-> C
    C -. HD Maps required .-> D
    D -. 5G and Edge required .-> E
```

## 4.5 Cybersecurity Defense Layers in Connected Vehicles

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ext["External Connectivity 5G WiFi Bluetooth"]
    gw["Secure Gateway HSM Firewalls"]
    can["In Vehicle Network CAN Ethernet"]
    ecu1["Powertrain ECU"]
    ecu2["ADAS ECU"]
    ecu3["Infotainment ECU"]
    ids["Intrusion Detection System IDS"]
    ota["OTA Update Manager"]
    
    ext --> gw
    gw --> ids
    ids --> can
    can --> ecu1
    can --> ecu2
    can --> ecu3
    ota --> gw
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: Each layer adds a security control. The Hardware Security Module (HSM) stores cryptographic keys. The IDS monitors CAN bus traffic for anomalies. The OTA manager cryptographically signs each firmware update.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define V2X communication. List its four primary types with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer**:
V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) is a communication paradigm enabling a vehicle to exchange information with surrounding entities to improve road safety, efficiency, and automation. The four primary types are:
- **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)**: Direct exchange of kinematic data between nearby vehicles.
- **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)**: Communication with road infrastructure like traffic signals and RSUs.
- **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian)**: Detection of pedestrians via their mobile devices.
- **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network)**: Connection to cellular networks for cloud-based services like HD maps.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (C-ACC) and how does it differ from standard ACC?**

**Model Answer**:
C-ACC is an extension of standard Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) that uses **V2V communication** to receive the acceleration of the *leader's leader* (the vehicle ahead of the lead vehicle), in addition to radar-based ranging. Unlike standard ACC that relies solely on on-board radar and suffers from string instability in dense traffic, C-ACC achieves **string-stable platooning**, enables tighter following distances (down to **0.3 s time headway**), and reduces fuel consumption by **10–15%** through aerodynamic drafting.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of V2X communication with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of 5G and Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) in enabling latency-critical autonomous driving applications. (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** Compute the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) at a V2X transmitter operating at 5.9 GHz, transmitting to a vehicle 500 m away. If the receiver sensitivity is −90 dBm and the transmit power is 23 dBm, determine the maximum additional loss the signal can tolerate (assume antenna gains of 3 dBi each). (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — V2X Architecture with 5G and MEC**

The V2X architecture consists of the following layers:
1. **Perception Layer** — On-board sensors (camera, LiDAR, radar, GPS).
2. **Communication Layer** — DSRC or C-V2X radio for V2V/V2I/V2P.
3. **Network Layer** — 5G cellular network and core.
4. **Edge Layer** — Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) servers co-located with base stations.
5. **Cloud Layer** — HD maps, fleet learning, OTA management.

**Role of 5G**: 5G provides **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication)** with sub-1-ms latency, **eMBB** for HD map streaming, and **mMTC** for connecting millions of devices. For example, in **cooperative lane merging**, a vehicle needs to know the gap acceptance of another vehicle within 10 ms — feasible only with 5G URLLC.

**Role of MEC**: MEC brings compute resources from the central cloud to the **edge of the radio access network**. It hosts latency-sensitive applications such as **real-time sensor fusion, object classification, and V2X message aggregation**. The cloud round-trip of 50–100 ms is reduced to 5–10 ms at the edge.

**Part (b) — Numerical on FSPL**

Given:
- Frequency $f = 5.9$ GHz = 5900 MHz
- Distance $d = 500$ m = 0.5 km
- Transmit power $P_t = 23$ dBm
- Receiver sensitivity $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm
- $G_t = G_r = 3$ dBi

**Step 1**: Compute FSPL

$$
PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) + 20 \log_{10}(5900) + 32.44
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \log_{10}(0.5) &= -6.02 \text{ dB} \\
20 \log_{10}(5900) &= 20 \times 3.771 &= 75.42 \text{ dB} \\
PL_{dB} &= -6.02 + 75.42 + 32.44 = 101.84 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute received power using the link budget equation

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL
$$

$$
P_r = 23 + 3 + 3 - 101.84 = -72.84 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 3**: Maximum additional tolerable loss

$$
L_{max} = P_r - P_{r,min} = -72.84 - (-90) = 17.16 \text{ dB}
$$

**Result**: The signal at the receiver is at −72.84 dBm, giving a **fade margin of 17.16 dB** to absorb additional losses from obstacles, multipath fading, and atmospheric effects.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2025]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an autonomous vehicle perception stack. Discuss the role of sensor fusion in enhancing robustness. (7 Marks)

**Q. (b)** Two vehicles are traveling on a highway. The ego vehicle is at position 0 m, moving at 25 m/s. The lead vehicle is at 40 m, moving at 20 m/s. Compute (i) the Time-to-Collision (TTC), (ii) the relative distance after 2 s, and (iii) determine whether Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) will be triggered if the threshold is 1.5 s. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Autonomous Vehicle Perception Stack**

The perception stack consists of:
1. **Sensor Layer**: Camera (semantic understanding), LiDAR (3D geometry), Radar (velocity), GPS/IMU (pose).
2. **Pre-processing**: Noise filtering, time synchronization, coordinate transformation.
3. **Detection**: CNN-based object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN).
4. **Tracking**: Multi-object tracking with Kalman filter or SORT algorithm.
5. **Fusion**: Combining data from heterogeneous sensors (Early, Late, or Deep fusion).
6. **Output**: A unified world model containing object classes, positions, velocities, and trajectories.

**Role of Sensor Fusion**:
- **Complementary**: Camera gives color and class; LiDAR gives 3D depth; radar gives velocity — fusion yields complete information.
- **Redundancy**: If one sensor fails (e.g., camera blinded by sun glare), others compensate, improving safety per **ISO 26262 ASIL-D**.
- **Robustness**: Fusing radar + camera reduces false positives by 70% compared to camera-only pedestrian detection.

**Part (b) — Numerical on TTC**

Given:
- $x_e = 0$ m, $v_e = 25$ m/s
- $x_l = 40$ m, $v_l = 20$ m/s

**Step 1: Compute TTC**

$$
\Delta x = x_l - x_e = 40 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta v = v_l - v_e = 20 - 25 = -5 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
TTC = \frac{\Delta x}{|\Delta v|} = \frac{40}{5} = 8 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Relative distance after 2 s**

Positions after 2 s (assuming no braking):

$$
x_e(2) = 0 + 25 \times 2 = 50 \text{ m}
$$

$$
x_l(2) = 40 + 20 \times 2 = 80 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta x(2) = 80 - 50 = 30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: AEB Decision**

Since $TTC = 8$ s $> 1.5$ s, **AEB is NOT triggered**. However, the Forward Collision Warning (FCW) may alert the driver.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> - Forgetting to convert **km to m** or **MHz to GHz** in FSPL problems is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> - Students often confuse **absolute value** in TTC derivation — using $\Delta v$ without absolute value yields a negative TTC.
> - In V2X diagrams, examiners expect **all four V2X types** to be labeled; missing V2P costs a full mark.
> - In latency questions, always quote the **5G URLLC value of < 1 ms**, not the eMBB value.
> - For sensor fusion, writing only "fuses camera and LiDAR" is incomplete; mention the **type of fusion (early/late/deep)**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **V2X** has four main types: **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N** (V2D is a recent addition).
- ✅ **5G URLLC** provides < 1 ms latency; **MEC** reduces round-trip time to 5–10 ms.
- ✅ **FSPL** in dB: $20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.
- ✅ **TTC** threshold for AEB: **1.5 s** (warning) and **0.6 s** (full braking).
- ✅ **C-ACC** improves on ACC using V2V data, achieving string-stable platoons.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion** uses **Kalman Filter / EKF** for multi-sensor state estimation.
- ✅ **HD maps** provide **< 10 cm** absolute accuracy for localization.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity** in connected cars uses **HSM, IDS, and cryptographic OTA signing**.
- ✅ **SAE Levels**: 0–5; Level 3+ requires **system monitoring of environment**.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity attacks**: CAN injection, GPS spoofing, OTA tampering — all must be defended.
- ✅ **MaaS (Mobility as a Service)** is the business model for robotaxis (Waymo, Cruise).
- ✅ **OTA updates** are governed by **UNECE WP.29 R156** regulations.
- ✅ **Platoon string stability** requires V2V delay < 50 ms.
- ✅ **Cooperative perception** extends sensing range by 2–3× compared to solo driving.
- ✅ **Digital twins** are used for AV simulation (CARLA, LGSVL, MATLAB Automated Driving Toolbox).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Directions and Research Opportunities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT865 - ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEMS AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Directions and Research Opportunities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Future Directions and Research Opportunities** in the domain of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) refer to the emerging technological trajectories, unresolved scientific challenges, and interdisciplinary research domains that are expected to define the next generation of intelligent transportation systems. As per the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, this encompasses the integration of **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication**, **Edge Artificial Intelligence**, **High-Definition (HD) mapping**, **sensor fusion beyond conventional LiDAR-Radar-Camera paradigms**, **functional safety under ISO 26262 / SOTIF (ISO 21448)**, **human-machine interaction (HMI)**, and **cyber-physical security frameworks** for cooperative and autonomous mobility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Students must be able to identify open research problems, evaluate the maturity of emerging technologies (TRL — Technology Readiness Level), and articulate the engineering trade-offs between **safety, latency, scalability, energy efficiency, and ethical compliance** in next-generation ADAS.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine the current ADAS as a **student driver** who has just learned basic traffic rules and can parallel park with sensors. Now, the **"Future Directions"** represent the graduate-level education this student must receive before being allowed to drive solo on a national highway in a thunderstorm at 120 km/h, communicating with other cars, traffic lights, and cloud servers in real time.

Think of the autonomous vehicle ecosystem as a **human nervous system**:
- **Sensors (eyes, ears, skin)** → Camera, Radar, LiDAR, Ultrasonic, Tactile sensors
- **Central nervous system (brain + spinal cord)** → Edge AI processor + V2X communication
- **Muscles and actuators (hands, feet)** → Drive-by-wire, steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire
- **Memory and experience (hippocampus)** → HD Maps, fleet learning, Digital Twin

The "future" is about making this nervous system **faster, safer, more energy-efficient, cooperative, and ethically aligned** with human values.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Highlighted)

The following metrics are **standard KTU-board-exam-expected** values:

| Metric | Standard / Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **End-to-End Latency Budget** | **≤ 100 ms** (Euro NCAP 2025) | Critical for collision avoidance |
| **Sensor Refresh Rate** | **20–100 Hz** | LiDAR/Camera frame rate |
| **5G V2X Latency (URLLC)** | **< 5 ms** | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication |
| **Computational Throughput** | **100–1000 TOPS** | AI inference at SAE L3+ |
| **TRL Scale** | **1 (basic research) – 9 (operational)** | NASA / EU Technology Readiness Level |
| **SAE Automation Level** | **0 (no automation) – 5 (full automation)** | SAE J3016 standard |
| **Functional Safety ASIL** | **ASIL-D** | Highest automotive safety integrity |
| **k_B (Boltzmann constant)** | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Used in radar noise floor calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Boundary:**
> The transition from *ADAS* (SAE L1–L2) to *Autonomous Vehicle* (SAE L3–L5) is not a single technological jump but a **continuum of progressive decoupling** of human cognition from the dynamic driving task. Future research fundamentally tackles the residual gap between **L2+** and **L4** systems.

### 1.4 Five Pillars of Future Research (KTU High-Yield Classification)

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes future research into **five canonical pillars**:

1. **Pillar I — Cooperative Intelligence (V2X, 5G/6G, C-V2X, DSRC)**
2. **Pillar II — On-Device AI (Edge AI, Neuromorphic Computing, TinyML)**
3. **Pillar III — Sensor Evolution (4D Radar, FMCW LiDAR, Event Cameras, Thermal Imaging)**
4. **Pillar IV — Verification & Validation (V&V) for AI-based systems, SOTIF, Digital Twins**
5. **Pillar V — Human-Centric and Ethical Autonomy (HMI, Trust Calibration, Regulatory Frameworks)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar I — Cooperative Intelligence and V2X

Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) is the **paradigm shift** from isolated vehicle intelligence to **collective swarm intelligence**. V2X comprises four sub-modalities:

- **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)** — Inter-vehicle data exchange
- **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)** — Communication with road-side units (RSUs)
- **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian)** — Pedestrian smartphone-based safety alerts
- **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network)** — Cloud / Edge-server data exchange

The **channel capacity** of a V2X link is governed by the **Shannon-Hartley Theorem**:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

Where:
- $C$ = Channel capacity (bits/second)
- $B$ = Bandwidth (Hz)
- $S$ = Signal power (W)
- $N$ = Noise power (W) = $N_0 \cdot B$, where $N_0 = k_B \cdot T$ (thermal noise density)

The **end-to-end latency budget** for a cooperative collision avoidance maneuver is decomposed as:

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{compute}} + t_{\text{comm}} + t_{\text{actuate}}$$

Where typical KTU-board values are:
- $t_{\text{sense}} \approx 10\text{–}30$ ms
- $t_{\text{compute}} \approx 20\text{–}50$ ms
- $t_{\text{comm}} \approx 1\text{–}5$ ms (5G URLLC)
- $t_{\text{actuate}} \approx 10\text{–}20$ ms
- **Total budget: $t_{\text{total}} \leq 100$ ms**

### 2.2 Pillar II — Edge AI and Computational Limits

The **energy efficiency** of an AI inference engine is critical for electric vehicles. The figure of merit is:

$$\text{TOPS/W} = \frac{\text{Tera Operations Per Second}}{\text{Watt Power Consumption}}$$

Modern automotive AI accelerators (NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ6, Tesla HW4) target **≥ 10 TOPS/W**. The **computational workload** for an L4 perception stack scales roughly linearly with sensor count:

$$F_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cdot R_i$$

Where $F_i$ = FLOPS per inference of sensor $i$, and $R_i$ = refresh rate (Hz).

### 2.3 Pillar III — Advanced Sensor Theory

**4D Radar** adds **elevation (z-axis)** to traditional 3D Radar, requiring a 2D antenna array. The **angular resolution** of a phased-array radar is:

$$\theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{\lambda}{N \cdot d \cdot \cos(\phi)}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (mmWave: 4 mm at 77 GHz)
- $N$ = number of array elements
- $d$ = element spacing (typically $\lambda/2$)
- $\phi$ = steering angle

**Event Cameras** (Neuromorphic Vision) report **per-pixel brightness changes asynchronously** rather than full frames. The theoretical data rate reduction is:

$$\text{Reduction Ratio} = \frac{\text{Frame Rate} \times \text{Resolution} \times \text{Bit Depth}}{\text{Events per Second} \times \text{Event Payload (bits)}}$$

### 2.4 Pillar IV — Verification, Validation, and SOTIF

For AI-driven perception, the **safety of the intended functionality (SOTIF)** under ISO 21448 addresses hazards from **insufficient situational awareness** rather than systematic/random hardware failures (which ISO 26262 covers). The **residual risk** must satisfy:

$$R_{\text{residual}} \leq R_{\text{threshold}}$$

The **scenario coverage** in a V&V campaign follows:

$$C_{\text{scenario}} = \frac{N_{\text{tested scenarios}}}{N_{\text{total identified scenarios}}} \geq 95\%$$

### 2.5 Pillar V — Human-Machine Interaction (HMI) and Trust Calibration

The **trust calibration** problem is mathematically modeled as a Bayesian belief update:

$$P(T \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert T) \cdot P(T)}{P(E)}$$

Where:
- $P(T)$ = Prior trust probability
- $P(E \vert T)$ = Likelihood of observed evidence given trust state
- $P(T \vert E)$ = Posterior trust after observing evidence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Important Convention:**
> Every formula derived or applied in an answer MUST be introduced with a **one-line statement of its physical/engineering meaning** before substitution. Examiners award marks for the *meaning*, not just the *math*.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Maximum V2X data rate | bits/s, **≥ 27 Mbps** for V2X |
| 2 | Latency Budget | $t_{\text{total}} = t_s + t_c + t_{comm} + t_a$ | End-to-end reaction time | **≤ 100 ms** |
| 3 | Time-to-Collision (TTC) | $TTC = \Delta d / v_{\text{rel}}$ | Pre-collision warning trigger | seconds |
| 4 | Stopping Distance (kinematic) | $d = v \cdot t_r + v^2/(2a)$ | Minimum braking distance | meters |
| 5 | Radar Range Equation | $R_{\max} = \left( \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}} \right)^{1/4}$ | Maximum sensor range | meters (e.g., **250 m** for LRR) |
| 6 | Angular Resolution (Radar) | $\theta = \lambda / (N d \cos\phi)$ | Beam sharpness | radians |
| 7 | Energy Efficiency Metric | $\eta = \text{TOPS/W}$ | AI accelerator efficiency | **≥ 10 TOPS/W** |
| 8 | Safety Integrity Level | ASIL A → D (A < B < C < D) | ISO 26262 risk class | Discrete |
| 9 | Trust Update (Bayesian) | $P(T \vert E) = P(E \vert T) P(T) / P(E)$ | Driver trust calibration | Probability [0,1] |
| 10 | Scenario Coverage | $C_s = N_{\text{tested}} / N_{\text{total}}$ | V&V exhaustiveness | **≥ 95%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Pitfall Note:** In the radar range equation, do not confuse $P_t$ (transmitted power) with $P_r$ (received power). The fourth-root dependence on $R$ is a *frequently tested* KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum V2X Channel Capacity for Cooperative Perception

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A V2X system operates at carrier frequency $f_c = 5.9$ GHz (dedicated ITS band) with allocated bandwidth $B = 20$ MHz. The received signal power is $S = -90$ dBm and noise power is $N = -100$ dBm. The system temperature is $T = 290$ K. Verify that the link satisfies the minimum data-rate requirement of **27 Mbps** for cooperative perception messages (ETSI EN 302 637-2 CAM standard).

**Step 1 — Convert dBm to linear Watts**

For any power in dBm: $P_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(P_{\text{dBm}} - 30)/10}$ W

$$S = 10^{(-90 - 30)/10} = 10^{-12} \text{ W} = 1 \text{ pW}$$

$$N = 10^{(-100 - 30)/10} = 10^{-13} \text{ W} = 0.1 \text{ pW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{10^{-12}}{10^{-13}} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 10)$$

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(11)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Logarithm**

$$\log_2(11) = \frac{\ln(11)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.3979}{0.6931} \approx 3.4594$$

**Step 5 — Final Capacity**

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 3.4594 = 69.19 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 69.19 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

Since $C_{\text{available}} = 69.19$ Mbps $\gg C_{\text{required}} = 27$ Mbps, the V2X link **satisfies** the cooperative perception requirement with a **margin of 42.19 Mbps (≈ 156% headroom)**.

**Step 7 — Verification via Boltzmann Noise Floor**

$$N = k_B \cdot T \cdot B = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \cdot 290 \cdot (20 \times 10^6)$$

$$N = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 290 \cdot 2 \times 10^7$$

$$N = 8.004 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W} \approx -100.97 \text{ dBm}$$

This matches our assumed noise floor, **validating the assumption**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Latency Budget Decomposition for AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)

**Problem Setup:**
An AEB system has the following measured sub-component latencies:
- $t_{\text{sense}} = 25$ ms (Radar + Camera perception)
- $t_{\text{compute}} = 35$ ms (Planner + Controller)
- $t_{\text{comm}} = 4$ ms (V2X message RTT)
- $t_{\text{actuate}} = 15$ ms (Brake-by-wire)

**Step 1 — Sum the Latencies**

$$t_{\text{total}} = 25 + 35 + 4 + 15 = 79 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Euro NCAP 2025 Threshold**

Since $t_{\text{total}} = 79$ ms $< 100$ ms, the system **meets the safety threshold**.

**Step 3 — Compute Headroom**

$$\Delta t = 100 - 79 = 21 \text{ ms (21% safety margin)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Speed for Safe Stopping**

Using kinematic equation: $d_{\text{stop}} = v \cdot t_{\text{total}} + \dfrac{v^2}{2 a}$

Assuming $a = 8 \text{ m/s}^2$ (full braking deceleration) and obstacle distance $d_{\text{stop}} = 40$ m:

$$40 = v \cdot 0.079 + \frac{v^2}{16}$$

$$\frac{v^2}{16} + 0.079 v - 40 = 0$$

Multiply by 16: $v^2 + 1.264 v - 640 = 0$

Apply quadratic formula: $v = \dfrac{-1.264 \pm \sqrt{1.598 + 2560}}{2}$

$$v = \frac{-1.264 \pm \sqrt{2561.598}}{2} = \frac{-1.264 + 50.611}{2} = 24.67 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to km/h**

$$v = 24.67 \times 3.6 = 88.83 \text{ km/h}$$

So the AEB system can safely stop a vehicle from **~88.8 km/h** within 40 m — a KTU-board-relevant numerical result.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — AI Computational Throughput for an L3 Perception Stack

**Problem Setup:**
An L3 autonomous vehicle has the following perception sensors:
- 1 Front-facing Camera (8 MP, 30 Hz)
- 4 Surround-view Cameras (2 MP, 30 Hz each)
- 1 Front LiDAR (128 channels, 10 Hz)
- 5 Radars (each 20 Hz)
- 1 GNSS/IMU (100 Hz)

Each sensor's AI workload (in TFLOPS per inference):
- Camera CNN (ResNet-50 backbone + detection head): **2.5 TFLOPS**
- LiDAR PointNet++ segmentation: **5.0 TFLOPS**
- Radar object detection: **0.5 TFLOPS**
- Sensor fusion + planning: **3.0 TFLOPS**

**Step 1 — Compute Per-Sensor Throughput Load**

$$F_{\text{camera}} = 1 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 30 + 4 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 30 = 75 + 72 = 147 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

$$F_{\text{LiDAR}} = 5.0 \cdot 10 = 50 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

$$F_{\text{Radar}} = 5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 20 = 50 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

$$F_{\text{fusion+plan}} = 3.0 \cdot 30 = 90 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

**Step 2 — Sum Total**

$$F_{\text{total}} = 147 + 50 + 50 + 90 = 337 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Available Hardware**

NVIDIA DRIVE Orin: **254 TOPS** → **Insufficient**
NVIDIA DRIVE Thor: **2000 TOPS** → **Sufficient with 1662 TOPS headroom**

**Step 4 — Compute Energy Consumption**

At $\eta = 10$ TOPS/W:

$$P = 337 / 10 = 33.7 \text{ W (Orin-class)}$$
$$P_{\text{Thor}} = 2000 / 50 = 40 \text{ W (at 50 TOPS/W)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **SAE L3+ vehicles require dedicated AI SoCs**, not general-purpose CPUs. The computational demand grows **super-linearly** with sensor count, driving the need for *neuromorphic* and *photonic* computing research.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — TRL vs. Technology Domain

| Research Area | Current TRL (2025) | Target TRL (2030) | Key Bottleneck |
|---|---|---|---|
| V2X Cooperative Driving | 7–8 | 9 | Penetration rate, regulatory harmonization |
| 4D Imaging Radar | 6 | 9 | Cost, algorithm maturity |
| Solid-State LiDAR | 5–6 | 8 | Range in adverse weather |
| Event Cameras | 4 | 7 | Algorithm standardization |
| Neuromorphic AI Chips | 3 | 7 | Toolchain, model zoo |
| HD Map-less Localization | 4 | 7 | Long-tail scenario handling |
| Level 4 Autonomy (urban) | 5 | 8 | Edge cases, regulatory approval |
| Quantum-aided Route Optimization | 1–2 | 4 | Hardware scalability |
| Photonic AI Accelerators | 2 | 5 | Thermal stability in vehicles |
| Blockchain V2X Security | 3 | 6 | Latency, consensus overhead |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Next-Generation Autonomous Vehicle

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a future L4 autonomous vehicle integrating all five research pillars:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sensor Layer] --> B[Perception Layer]
    A1[Front Camera 8MP] --> A
    A2[Surround Cameras 4x] --> A
    A3[Front LiDAR 128ch] --> A
    A4[Solid State Radar 4D] --> A
    A5[Ultrasonic 12x] --> A
    A6[Event Camera] --> A
    A7[Thermal Imager] --> A
    A8[GNSS IMU HDMap] --> A

    B --> C[Sensor Fusion Engine]
    B1[Object Detection CNN] --> B
    B2[Semantic Segmentation] --> B
    B3[Occupancy Grid Map] --> B
    B4[Trajectory Prediction] --> B

    C --> D[Planning Layer]
    D1[Behavior Planner] --> D
    D2[Motion Planner] --> D
    D3[Safety Fallback Controller] --> D

    D --> E[Actuation Layer]
    E1[Steer by Wire] --> E
    E2[Brake by Wire] --> E
    E3[Drive by Wire] --> E
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define SOTIF (Safety of the Intended Functionality) as per ISO 21448. How does it differ from ISO 26262, and why is it particularly important for AI-based ADAS perception systems? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

SOTIF (ISO 21448) is the standard that addresses **hazards arising from the intended functionality or from foreseeable misuse**, particularly when the system behavior depends on **complex sensor-based perception and AI algorithms**.

**Distinction from ISO 26262:**
- **ISO 26262** addresses *systematic* and *random hardware failures* in E/E systems.
- **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** addresses *performance limitations* of the intended function, e.g., a camera failing to detect a pedestrian in low light.

**Why critical for AI-based ADAS:** Neural networks exhibit *edge cases* (long-tail distributions), *adversarial vulnerabilities*, and *dataset bias* that cannot be modeled as classical hardware faults. SOTIF provides a structured V&V methodology to **quantify residual risk** for perception AI, complementing ISO 26262 for actuation hardware.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is Neuromorphic Computing? State two advantages it offers for in-vehicle ADAS inference. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Neuromorphic computing is a non-von-Neumann computing paradigm that mimics the **event-driven, spiking behavior of biological neurons** using specialized hardware such as Intel Loihi, IBM TrueNorth, or SynSense Speck.

**Two advantages for ADAS:**
1. **Ultra-low power consumption (mW range)** — enables always-on perception without draining the EV battery.
2. **Event-driven asynchronous processing** — pairs naturally with event cameras, reducing data bandwidth by **>100×** compared to frame-based processing.
3. *(Bonus)* **Low deterministic latency** — suited for safety-critical control loops.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Vague "future buzzword" answers** (e.g., "AI will make cars smart") fetch 0–2 marks. Examiners demand **specific technology names, numerical metrics, and standards (ISO/SAE)**.
> 2. **No architecture diagram = −2 to −3 marks**. Every Part B answer on this topic must contain at least one labeled block diagram.
> 3. **Skipping the latency budget calculation** in any V2X question is treated as incomplete reasoning (up to 4-mark penalty).
> 4. **Confusing DSRC with C-V2X** or **4G with 5G URLLC** is a common error — be precise.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

(a) Explain the concept of **V2X communication** in autonomous vehicles. Describe the four sub-modalities (V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N) with one real-world application each. **(7 marks)**

(b) A V2X link operates over a 10 MHz channel at 5.9 GHz. The SNR at the receiver is 12 dB. Compute the **maximum channel capacity** using the Shannon-Hartley theorem. State whether this is sufficient for transmitting a Cooperative Awareness Message (CAM) requiring **6 Mbps**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** is a communication paradigm enabling real-time data exchange between a vehicle and its surrounding entities (vehicles, infrastructure, pedestrians, network) using **IEEE 802.11p (DSRC)** or **3GPP C-V2X (5G)** standards.

| Sub-Modality | Description | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|
| **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)** | Direct peer-to-peer data exchange | Forward collision warning when two cars on a curve cannot see each other |
| **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)** | Communication with Road-Side Units (RSUs) | Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory (GLOSA) at smart traffic signals |
| **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian)** | Pedestrian smartphone or wearable detection | Alerting driver of a child crossing from behind a parked bus |
| **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network)** | Cloud / Edge server communication | HD Map update, fleet learning, predictive maintenance |

**Key Engineering Benefits:**
- Extends perception **beyond line-of-sight (NLOS)**.
- Reduces **single-vehicle sensor uncertainty** through cooperative sensing.
- Enables **coordinated maneuvers** (platooning, intersection management).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $B = 10$ MHz $= 10 \times 10^6$ Hz
- SNR $= 12$ dB

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{12/10} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$

$$C = 10 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 15.85) = 10^7 \cdot \log_2(16.85)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm**

$$\log_2(16.85) = \frac{\ln(16.85)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.825}{0.6931} \approx 4.076$$

**Step 4 — Final capacity**

$$C \approx 10^7 \times 4.076 = 40.76 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Conclusion**

Since $C = 40.76$ Mbps $\gg 6$ Mbps (CAM requirement), the V2X link **comfortably supports CAM transmission** with **34.76 Mbps (≈ 580% margin)**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

(a) Discuss the **role of Edge AI and Neuromorphic Computing** as enabling technologies for next-generation ADAS. Compare their **energy efficiency, latency, and suitability** for real-time perception. **(7 marks)**

(b) An L3 autonomous vehicle has the following perception AI workload: Camera CNN (3 TFLOPS @ 30 Hz), LiDAR segmentation (4 TFLOPS @ 10 Hz), Radar detection (0.5 TFLOPS @ 20 Hz), and Sensor Fusion (2 TFLOPS @ 30 Hz). Calculate the **total AI throughput requirement** and the **power consumption** assuming an efficiency of **15 TOPS/W**. Comment on whether a 200 TOPS SoC (like NVIDIA Orin) is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Edge AI** refers to running AI inference **locally on the vehicle's onboard processor** (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ) rather than in the cloud. This eliminates the **round-trip latency, cellular coverage dependency, and privacy concerns** of cloud AI.

**Neuromorphic Computing** is a brain-inspired hardware paradigm using **spiking neural networks (SNNs)**. Processors like Intel Loihi 2 operate at **<1 W** with **<1 ms event-driven latency**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Conventional Edge AI (GPU/NPU) | Neuromorphic AI (Loihi 2) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Efficiency (TOPS/W)** | 10–50 TOPS/W | **1000+ TOPS/W (effective SNN)** |
| **Latency** | 20–50 ms (batch processing) | **<1 ms (event-driven)** |
| **Programming Model** | TensorFlow / PyTorch (mature) | NxSDK / Lava (emerging) |
| **Suitability** | High-resolution CNN perception | Event cameras, always-on DMS |
| **TRL (2025)** | 8–9 | 4–5 |

**Conclusion:** Neuromorphic and conventional edge AI are **complementary**, not competitive. Future L4 systems will adopt a **heterogeneous compute architecture** — NPU for camera perception, neuromorphic co-processor for event-camera and DMS streams.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute per-sensor throughput**

$$F_{\text{camera}} = 3 \text{ TFLOPS} \times 30 \text{ Hz} = 90 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

$$F_{\text{LiDAR}} = 4 \text{ TFLOPS} \times 10 \text{ Hz} = 40 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

$$F_{\text{Radar}} = 0.5 \text{ TFLOPS} \times 20 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

$$F_{\text{fusion}} = 2 \text{ TFLOPS} \times 30 \text{ Hz} = 60 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

**Step 2 — Total Throughput**

$$F_{\text{total}} = 90 + 40 + 10 + 60 = 200 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

**Step 3 — Power Consumption at 15 TOPS/W**

$$P = \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{\eta} = \frac{200}{15} = 13.33 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Sufficiency Check**

Total required = 200 TFLOPS = 200,000 TOPS. The Orin-class SoC offers **200 TOPS**, which is **1000× short** of the workload. **Therefore, the 200 TOPS SoC is grossly insufficient.**

**Step 5 — Recommendation**

A future L3 vehicle would require a **≥ 250 TOPS** SoC (like **NVIDIA Drive Thor — 2000 TOPS**) and a thermal management system capable of dissipating **~130 W** of compute power.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Future Directions**

- ☐ **Five Pillars of Future Research** — Cooperative Intelligence, Edge AI, Sensor Evolution, V&V/SOTIF, HMI/Ethics.
- ☐ **SAE J3016 Levels** — 0 (no automation) to 5 (full automation); ADAS occupies L1–L2.
- ☐ **ISO 26262** = systematic + random hardware faults; **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** = performance/AI limitations.
- ☐ **ASIL Levels** — A, B, C, D (D = highest safety integrity; required for braking/steering).
- ☐ **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** — $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — fundamental to V2X capacity planning.
- ☐ **Latency Budget** — $t_{\text{total}} = t_s + t_c + t_{comm} + t_a \leq 100$ ms (Euro NCAP).
- ☐ **5G URLLC** latency < 5 ms enables cooperative perception.
- ☐ **4D Radar** adds elevation; governed by $\theta = \lambda / (N d \cos\phi)$.
- ☐ **Energy Efficiency** — modern ADAS SoCs target ≥ 10 TOPS/W; future ≥ 50 TOPS/W.
- ☐ **Neuromorphic chips** (Loihi 2, SynSense) offer mW-range power and sub-ms latency.
- ☐ **TRL Scale** — 1 (basic research) to 9 (operational); key gating metric for research funding.
- ☐ **Trust Calibration** — Bayesian $P(T \vert E)$ — prevents both *over-trust* and *under-trust*.
- ☐ **Cybersecurity** — PKI, IDS, and OTA secure updates are mandatory for V2X.
- ☐ **Digital Twin** — used for V&V with millions of virtual scenarios to cover the long-tail.
- ☐ **HMI Trends** — AR HUD, haptic seats, and Driver Monitoring Cameras (DMS) are converging.
- ☐ **Regulatory Frontier** — UNECE WP.29, ISO/TS 5083 (2024) define AV type-approval rules.
- ☐ **Sustainability** — research on low-power perception reduces EV range penalty.

> **End of Module 4 — Future Directions and Research Opportunities**
